topics.title: Introduction to Optimum Load Dispatch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET741 - POWER SYSTEM OPERATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Optimum Load Dispatch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Optimum Load Dispatch (OLD)**, more commonly termed **Economic Load Dispatch (ELD)**, is the online operational procedure in a power system that determines the optimal real power output of every generating unit (thermal, hydro, or otherwise) connected to the system such that the **total fuel cost of generation is minimized**, while simultaneously satisfying:
1. The total power balance equation (load demand + losses = total generation).
2. The individual generator operating limits (minimum and maximum MW capacity).

Mathematically, for $N$ thermal units supplying a total load $P_D$:

$$\min \; F_T = \sum_{i=1}^{N} F_i(P_{Gi}) \quad \text{subject to} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_{Gi} = P_D + P_L$$

where $F_i(P_{Gi})$ is the fuel-cost function of the $i^{th}$ generator, $P_D$ is the total demand, and $P_L$ is the transmission loss.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Highlight:** The introductory treatment of Optimum Load Dispatch begins by **neglecting transmission losses** ($P_L = 0$) and considers a system of **thermal generating units** with smooth quadratic cost functions. Loss-inclusive dispatch (with $P_L$) and the use of penalty factors / $B$-coefficients are introduced in the later modules.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are managing a **chain of bakeries** that must together bake **10,000 loaves** of bread per day. Each bakery has a different oven efficiency:
- Bakery A uses cheap coal but takes long (low cost per loaf, slow).
- Bakery B uses expensive gas but is very fast (high cost per loaf, fast).
- Bakery C is a balance of both.

A naive manager simply tells all bakeries to "split the work equally" (5000, 2500, 2500). A *smart* manager instead runs the ovens so that the **marginal cost of producing the next loaf is equal across all bakeries** that are still operating. This way, no bakery is producing loaves that another bakery could produce more cheaply. That optimal distribution of the 10,000 loaves is the baker's equivalent of **Economic Load Dispatch**.

In a power system, the "loaves" are Megawatts (MW) of electrical energy, the "bakeries" are generating units, and the "marginal cost" is called the **Incremental Fuel Cost (IFC)** — measured in ₹/MWh.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Incremental Fuel Cost (IFC), $\lambda$:** Expressed in **₹/MWh** (Rupees per Megawatt-hour) or **Rs/MWh**. Physically, it is the slope $\dfrac{dF_i}{dP_{Gi}}$ of the cost curve.
- **Base Load:** The minimum demand on the system, supplied continuously — typically served by the most efficient units.
- **Peak Load:** The maximum demand — typically served by less efficient but flexible (fast ramping) units.
- **Spinning Reserve Margin:** Usually **5 % to 10 %** of the peak load, kept as an operational safety cushion.
- **Cost Coefficient Units:** For a quadratic cost function $F_i = a_i + b_i P_{Gi} + c_i P_{Gi}^2$, the constant $a_i$ has units of **₹/h**, $b_i$ has units of **₹/MWh**, and $c_i$ has units of **₹/MW²h**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Anchor:** In the KTU 2024 scheme, the term "Optimum Load Dispatch" is the chapter heading — the actual subject matter is the **economic allocation of load** between generating units, which is the foundation of Automatic Generation Control (AGC) treated later in Module 2.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Cost of a Thermal Generating Unit

The input to a thermal unit is **fuel (coal, gas, oil, or nuclear)**, and the output is **electrical power**. The relationship between the rate of fuel consumption (tons/h or m³/h) and the electrical output (MW) is a curve known as the **Input–Output Curve** or **Heat Rate Curve**. The corresponding **Fuel-Cost Curve** $F_i(P_{Gi})$ is obtained by multiplying the input curve by the unit fuel price.

For analysis, this curve is universally approximated by a **smooth, convex, monotonically increasing quadratic function**:

$$F_i(P_{Gi}) \; = \; a_i + b_i P_{Gi} + c_i P_{Gi}^2 \quad \text{(₹/h)}$$

where:
- $a_i$ — no-load cost (₹/h), intercept on the cost axis.
- $b_i$ — linear cost coefficient (₹/MWh).
- $c_i$ — quadratic cost coefficient (₹/MW²h), governs the curvature.

## 2.2 The Incremental Fuel Cost (IFC) Concept

The **Incremental Fuel Cost** of the $i^{th}$ unit is the rate of change of its fuel cost with respect to its own power output:

$$\text{IFC}_i \; = \; \frac{dF_i}{dP_{Gi}} \; = \; b_i + 2 c_i P_{Gi} \quad \text{(₹/MWh)}$$

This is a **linear function of $P_{Gi}$** because the cost curve is quadratic.

## 2.3 The Classic Optimum (Without Losses) — Coordination Equation

For $N$ generators supplying a total load $P_D$ while **neglecting losses**, the constrained optimization problem is:

$$\min F_T = \sum_{i=1}^{N} F_i(P_{Gi}) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad g = \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_{Gi} - P_D = 0$$

Using the **Method of Lagrange Multipliers**, form:

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} F_i(P_{Gi}) - \lambda \left( \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_{Gi} - P_D \right)$$

Setting partial derivatives to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial P_{Gi}} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{dF_i}{dP_{Gi}} = \lambda \quad \text{for all } i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$$

This yields the celebrated **Equal Incremental Cost Criterion**:

$$\boxed{\; \text{IFC}_1 = \text{IFC}_2 = \text{IFC}_3 = \cdots = \text{IFC}_N = \lambda \;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Meaning of $\lambda$:** The Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$ is the **system incremental cost** (₹/MWh) — i.e., the cost of supplying **one additional MW of load** at the optimum operating point. It is the marginal price of power.

## 2.4 Generator Operating Constraints

In practice, no unit can operate below a technical minimum or above a rated maximum. The unconstrained optimum must be checked against:

$$P_{Gi, \min} \le P_{Gi} \le P_{Gi, \max}$$

If the unconstrained optimum lies **outside** this range, the unit is **clamped** at the violated limit, removed from the equal-IFC pool, and the remaining units re-dispatch.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Quadratic cost curve | $F_i = a_i + b_i P_{Gi} + c_i P_{Gi}^2$ | ₹/h | Convex, smooth, differentiable |
| 2 | Incremental fuel cost | $\text{IFC}_i = \dfrac{dF_i}{dP_{Gi}} = b_i + 2c_i P_{Gi}$ | ₹/MWh | Slope of the cost curve |
| 3 | Coordination equation (no loss) | $\text{IFC}_1 = \text{IFC}_2 = \cdots = \text{IFC}_N = \lambda$ | ₹/MWh | Necessary optimality condition |
| 4 | Power balance | $\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_{Gi} = P_D$ | MW | Equality constraint |
| 5 | Generator limits | $P_{Gi,\min} \le P_{Gi} \le P_{Gi,\max}$ | MW | Inequality constraints |
| 6 | Optimal output of unit $i$ | $P_{Gi}^{*} = \dfrac{\lambda - b_i}{2c_i}$ | MW | From inversion of IFC equation |
| 7 | Total system cost | $F_T = \sum_{i} \left( a_i + b_i P_{Gi} + c_i P_{Gi}^2 \right)$ | ₹/h | Plug in optimal $P_{Gi}^{*}$ |
| 8 | System lambda | $\lambda = \dfrac{P_D + \sum_i \dfrac{b_i}{2c_i}}{\sum_i \dfrac{1}{2c_i}}$ | ₹/MWh | Closed-form (no loss, no bounds) |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

Economic Load Dispatch is the **economic engine** behind every modern electricity market. It is the foundational algorithm used in:
- **State Load Despatch Centres (SLDCs)** and the **National Load Despatch Centre (NLDC)** of India.
- **Independent System Operators (ISOs)** worldwide (e.g., PJM, CAISO, ERCOT).
- **Day-ahead and real-time market-clearing engines**, where the dispatch result determines the **Locational Marginal Price (LMP)** of electricity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Two-Unit, Lossless Optimum — From First Principles

Consider **two thermal generators** supplying a total load $P_D$ MW. We wish to find $P_{G1}^*$ and $P_{G2}^*$ that minimize the total cost.

**Step 1 — Write the cost functions explicitly:**

$$F_1(P_{G1}) = a_1 + b_1 P_{G1} + c_1 P_{G1}^2$$
$$F_2(P_{G2}) = a_2 + b_2 P_{G2} + c_2 P_{G2}^2$$

**Step 2 — Write the equality constraint (load balance, no losses):**

$$P_{G1} + P_{G2} = P_D$$

**Step 3 — Eliminate one variable using the constraint:**

$$P_{G2} = P_D - P_{G1}$$

This reduces the problem to a single-variable unconstrained minimization in $P_{G1}$.

**Step 4 — Form the objective function in one variable:**

$$F_T(P_{G1}) = a_1 + b_1 P_{G1} + c_1 P_{G1}^2 + a_2 + b_2 (P_D - P_{G1}) + c_2 (P_D - P_{G1})^2$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate $F_T$ with respect to $P_{G1}$ and set to zero:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{dF_T}{dP_{G1}} &= b_1 + 2c_1 P_{G1} - b_2 - 2c_2 (P_D - P_{G1}) = 0 \\
b_1 + 2c_1 P_{G1} - b_2 - 2c_2 P_D + 2c_2 P_{G1} &= 0 \\
2(c_1 + c_2) P_{G1} &= 2c_2 P_D + b_2 - b_1 \\
P_{G1}^{*} &= \frac{b_2 - b_1 + 2c_2 P_D}{2(c_1 + c_2)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Recover $P_{G2}^*$ from the constraint:**

$$P_{G2}^{*} = P_D - P_{G1}^{*} = \frac{b_1 - b_2 + 2c_1 P_D}{2(c_1 + c_2)}$$

**Step 7 — Identify $\lambda$ (the marginal system cost):**

Substitute $P_{G1}^*$ back into the IFC of unit 1:

$$\lambda = b_1 + 2c_1 P_{G1}^{*} = b_1 + 2c_1 \cdot \frac{b_2 - b_1 + 2c_2 P_D}{2(c_1 + c_2)}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= b_1 + \frac{c_1 (b_2 - b_1 + 2c_2 P_D)}{c_1 + c_2} \\
&= \frac{b_1(c_1 + c_2) + c_1 b_2 - c_1 b_1 + 2c_1 c_2 P_D}{c_1 + c_2} \\
&= \frac{c_1 b_2 + c_2 b_1 + 2c_1 c_2 P_D}{c_1 + c_2} \\
&= \frac{2c_1 c_2 P_D + b_1 c_2 + b_2 c_1}{c_1 + c_2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8 — Verify symmetry:** Substituting $P_{G2}^*$ into the IFC of unit 2 must give the **same** value of $\lambda$, confirming the equal-incremental-cost condition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Closed-form Verdict:** For the no-loss, no-bound case, the optimal dispatch has a **single closed-form expression** per unit, with no iterative procedure required.

## 3.2 Generalization to N Units (No Losses, No Bounds)

For $N$ generators, equate all IFCs to $\lambda$:

$$b_i + 2c_i P_{Gi} = \lambda \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_{Gi} = \frac{\lambda - b_i}{2c_i}$$

Substitute into the power balance $\sum P_{Gi} = P_D$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{\lambda - b_i}{2c_i} = P_D$$

Solve for $\lambda$:

$$\lambda \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{2c_i} - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{b_i}{2c_i} = P_D$$

$$\boxed{\; \lambda = \frac{P_D + \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{b_i}{2c_i}}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{1}{2c_i}} \;}$$

Once $\lambda$ is found, $P_{Gi}^* = \dfrac{\lambda - b_i}{2c_i}$ for each unit.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Two thermal units have the cost functions:
- Unit 1: $F_1 = 500 + 7.0 P_{G1} + 0.008 P_{G1}^2$ (₹/h)
- Unit 2: $F_2 = 400 + 7.5 P_{G2} + 0.009 P_{G2}^2$ (₹/h)

Total load: $P_D = 800$ MW. Neglect losses and generator limits. Find the optimal dispatch and total cost.

**Solution (algebraic):**

Using the closed-form two-unit formula derived above, with $b_1 = 7.0$, $b_2 = 7.5$, $c_1 = 0.008$, $c_2 = 0.009$, $P_D = 800$:

$$P_{G1}^{*} = \frac{7.5 - 7.0 + 2(0.009)(800)}{2(0.008 + 0.009)} = \frac{0.5 + 14.4}{0.034} = \frac{14.9}{0.034} = 438.24 \text{ MW}$$

$$P_{G2}^{*} = 800 - 438.24 = 361.76 \text{ MW}$$

Compute $\lambda$ (verification):

$$\lambda = 7.0 + 2(0.008)(438.24) = 7.0 + 7.012 = 14.012 \text{ ₹/MWh}$$

Cross-check with unit 2: $7.5 + 2(0.009)(361.76) = 7.5 + 6.512 = 14.012$ ₹/MWh ✔

Total cost:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= 500 + 7.0(438.24) + 0.008(438.24)^2 \\
&= 500 + 3067.68 + 1536.66 = 5104.34 \text{ ₹/h} \\
F_2 &= 400 + 7.5(361.76) + 0.009(361.76)^2 \\
&= 400 + 2713.20 + 1178.04 = 4291.24 \text{ ₹/h} \\
F_T &= 5104.34 + 4291.24 = 9395.58 \text{ ₹/h}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** The more efficient unit 1 (smaller $c$) carries a larger share (438.24 MW vs 361.76 MW). If we had split equally at 400/400 MW, the cost would have been noticeably higher — the ELD saves fuel cost in the order of a few percent of $F_T$ per hour.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common Pitfalls and How to Avoid Them

| Step | Common Mistake | Correct Approach |
|---|---|---|
| Setting up Lagrangian | Forgetting the minus sign on the constraint | Use $\mathcal{L} = F_T - \lambda(\sum P_{Gi} - P_D)$ |
| Solving for $\lambda$ | Dropping the $P_D$ term in the power balance | Power balance is an **equality**, not optional |
| Checking optimality | Not verifying that $\text{IFC}_1 = \text{IFC}_2$ after solving | Always plug $P_{Gi}^*$ back into both IFC equations |
| Applying bounds | Ignoring $P_{Gi,\min}$ and $P_{Gi,\max}$ | Clamp, then re-dispatch remaining units |
| Units | Mixing MW and ₹/h with ₹/MWh | Keep units consistent; $\lambda$ is ₹/MWh |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Economic Dispatch Engine

The following diagram captures the **algorithmic flow** of an online Economic Load Dispatch (lossless, no-bound case) as deployed in a real Load Despatch Centre. Mermaid is used to express the logical architecture clearly without requiring a physical engineering drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Operator Initiates ED Engine] --> A1[Read System Data]
    A1 --> A2[Load Forecast Module: P_D in MW]
    A1 --> A3[Unit Commitment Status: N units online]
    A1 --> A4[Cost Coefficients: a_i, b_i, c_i for each i]
    A1 --> A5[Generator Limits: P_Gimin, P_Gimax]
    A2 --> B1[Form Cost Function F_T = sum F_i]
    A3 --> B1
    A4 --> B1
    A5 --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Apply Equality Constraint: sum P_Gi = P_D]
    C1 --> D1[Form Lagrangian L = F_T - lambda x Constraint]
    D1 --> E1[Derive Coordination Equation: dF_i / dP_Gi = lambda for all i]
    E1 --> F1[Compute System Lambda: lambda = P_D + sum b_i/2c_i over sum 1/2c_i]
    F1 --> G1[Compute Optimal P_Gi: P_Gi = lambda - b_i over 2c_i]
    G1 --> H1[Check Generator Limits]
    H1 --> H2{Limits Satisfied?}
    H2 -- Yes --> I1[Output Optimal Dispatch Schedule]
    H2 -- No --> J1[Clamp Violating Units at P_Gimin or P_Gimax]
    J1 --> K1[Re-dispatch Remaining Units with Revised P_D]
    K1 --> H1
    I1 --> L1[Compute Total System Cost F_T in Rs per hour]
    I1 --> M1[Send Set-points to AGC Loop in Module 2]
    L1 --> Z1[End]
    M1 --> Z1
```

## 4.2 Cost-Curve vs Incremental-Cost-Curve Visualization (Schematic)

A second diagram conveys the **geometric intuition** of why equal-IFC is optimal. Two generators have different quadratic cost curves. Their incremental cost curves are straight lines (since $dF/dP$ of a quadratic is linear). The optimum occurs where these two straight lines **intersect at a common horizontal level $\lambda$**, and the corresponding $P_{G1}$ and $P_{G2}$ are read off the x-axis.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P1["Cost Curves F_i vs P_Gi (Convex Quadratics)"]
        direction LR
        C1A[F1 curve - lower cost intercept, gentle slope]:::costA
        C1B[F2 curve - higher cost intercept, steeper slope]:::costB
        C1C[P_G1 axis]:::axis
        C1D[P_G2 axis]:::axis
    end
    subgraph P2["Incremental Cost Lines (Slope dF_i/dP_Gi)"]
        direction LR
        L1A[IFC1 line: intercept b1, slope 2c1]:::ifcA
        L1B[IFC2 line: intercept b2, slope 2c2]:::ifcB
        L1C[Common level lambda Rs per MWh]:::lambdaL
        L1D[P_G1 star at intersection]:::pstar
        L1E[P_G2 star = P_D minus P_G1 star]:::pstar
    end
    C1A --> L1A
    C1B --> L1B
    L1A --- L1C
    L1B --- L1C
    L1C --- L1D
    L1C --- L1E
    classDef costA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef costB fill:#ffd9cc,stroke:#660000,color:#000
    classDef ifcA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef ifcB fill:#ffd9cc,stroke:#660000,color:#000
    classDef lambdaL fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#666600,color:#000
    classDef pstar fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#003300,color:#000
    classDef axis fill:#eeeeee,stroke:#333333,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the figure:** The horizontal line at level $\lambda$ is the **system marginal cost**. The point where this horizontal line meets each generator's IFC line gives that generator's optimal output. If the IFC of one unit is "too high" (expensive) and another is "too low" (cheap), shifting 1 MW from the expensive to the cheap unit *decreases* total cost — until the IFCs equalize.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**
**State and briefly justify the "Equal Incremental Cost Criterion" for economic load dispatch of thermal units neglecting transmission losses.**

**Model Answer:**

The equal-incremental-cost criterion states that at the optimum economic dispatch point (lossless, no-bound case), the incremental fuel cost of every on-line generating unit must be equal to a common value $\lambda$:

$$\frac{dF_1}{dP_{G1}} = \frac{dF_2}{dP_{G2}} = \cdots = \frac{dF_N}{dP_{GN}} = \lambda \quad \text{(₹/MWh)}$$

**Justification:** This is obtained by minimizing $F_T = \sum F_i$ subject to $\sum P_{Gi} = P_D$ using the method of Lagrange multipliers. If two units have unequal IFCs, transferring a small amount of load from the unit with higher IFC to the unit with lower IFC will reduce the total cost. Equilibrium is reached only when both IFCs are equal. The common value $\lambda$ represents the marginal cost of supplying one additional MW of system load.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
**What is the significance of the Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$ in the economic load dispatch problem? Mention its physical interpretation and typical units.**

**Model Answer:**

The Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$ is the **shadow price** of the active-power balance constraint. Its physical interpretation is the **system incremental cost of power** — that is, the additional rupees per hour that the system must spend on fuel to supply **one extra MW** of load at the current optimum operating point. Its units are **₹/MWh** (Rupees per Megawatt-hour). It also serves as the binding marginal price that equalizes the IFCs of all online units, and is the conceptual precursor to the **Locational Marginal Price (LMP)** used in modern deregulated electricity markets.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Full Solution

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 1, Question A]**

**Two thermal generating units have the following cost characteristics:**

$$F_1 = 0.0040 \, P_{G1}^2 + 5.0 \, P_{G1} + 200 \; (\text{₹/h})$$
$$F_2 = 0.0060 \, P_{G2}^2 + 4.0 \, P_{G2} + 150 \; (\text{₹/h})$$

The total load to be served is $P_D = 600$ MW. Neglect transmission losses. Generator limits are $150 \le P_{G1} \le 450$ MW and $100 \le P_{G2} \le 400$ MW.

### (a) Formulate the economic dispatch problem and determine the optimal generation schedule and the system lambda. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify coefficients:**

- Unit 1: $a_1 = 200$, $b_1 = 5.0$, $c_1 = 0.0040$ ₹/MW²h
- Unit 2: $a_2 = 150$, $b_2 = 4.0$, $c_2 = 0.0060$ ₹/MW²h

**Step 2 — Formulate the constrained optimization:**

$$\min F_T = \sum_{i=1}^{2} (a_i + b_i P_{Gi} + c_i P_{Gi}^2) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad P_{G1} + P_{G2} = 600, \quad P_{Gi}^{\min} \le P_{Gi} \le P_{Gi}^{\max}$$

**Step 3 — Set up the Lagrangian:**

$$\mathcal{L} = (a_1 + b_1 P_{G1} + c_1 P_{G1}^2) + (a_2 + b_2 P_{G2} + c_2 P_{G2}^2) - \lambda (P_{G1} + P_{G2} - 600)$$

**Step 4 — First-order optimality conditions:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial P_{G1}} = b_1 + 2c_1 P_{G1} - \lambda = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; P_{G1} = \frac{\lambda - b_1}{2c_1} = \frac{\lambda - 5.0}{0.0080}$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial P_{G2}} = b_2 + 2c_2 P_{G2} - \lambda = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; P_{G2} = \frac{\lambda - b_2}{2c_2} = \frac{\lambda - 4.0}{0.0120}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the power balance constraint:**

$$\frac{\lambda - 5.0}{0.0080} + \frac{\lambda - 4.0}{0.0120} = 600$$

Multiply through by a common denominator (LCM of $0.008$ and $0.012$ is $0.024$):

$$3(\lambda - 5.0) + 2(\lambda - 4.0) = 600 \times 0.024 = 14.4$$

$$3\lambda - 15.0 + 2\lambda - 8.0 = 14.4$$

$$5\lambda - 23.0 = 14.4 \;\Rightarrow\; 5\lambda = 37.4 \;\Rightarrow\; \lambda = 7.48 \text{ ₹/MWh}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the optimal generation:**

$$P_{G1}^{*} = \frac{7.48 - 5.0}{0.0080} = \frac{2.48}{0.0080} = 310.0 \text{ MW}$$

$$P_{G2}^{*} = \frac{7.48 - 4.0}{0.0120} = \frac{3.48}{0.0120} = 290.0 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 7 — Check feasibility against limits:**

- Unit 1: $150 \le 310 \le 450$ ✔
- Unit 2: $100 \le 290 \le 400$ ✔
- Total: $310 + 290 = 600$ MW ✔

**Step 8 — Verify equal-IFC condition:**

$\text{IFC}_1 = 5.0 + 2(0.004)(310) = 5.0 + 2.48 = 7.48$ ₹/MWh ✔
$\text{IFC}_2 = 4.0 + 2(0.006)(290) = 4.0 + 3.48 = 7.48$ ₹/MWh ✔

**Answer:** $P_{G1}^* = 310$ MW, $P_{G2}^* = 290$ MW, $\lambda = 7.48$ ₹/MWh.

---

### (b) Compute the total generation cost at the optimum. If unit 1 is forced to operate at its minimum limit of 150 MW, recompute the dispatch. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $F_T$ at the unconstrained optimum:**

$$F_1 = 200 + 5.0(310) + 0.004(310)^2 = 200 + 1550 + 384.40 = 2134.40 \text{ ₹/h}$$
$$F_2 = 150 + 4.0(290) + 0.006(290)^2 = 150 + 1160 + 504.60 = 1814.60 \text{ ₹/h}$$
$$F_T = 2134.40 + 1814.60 = 3949.00 \text{ ₹/h}$$

**Step 2 — Force $P_{G1} = 150$ MW (clamp at lower limit).**

**Step 3 — Remaining load for unit 2:**

$$P_{G2} = 600 - 150 = 450 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 4 — Check feasibility of unit 2:** $P_{G2,\max} = 400$ MW. So $450 > 400$: **the constraint is infeasible**. We must conclude that the *combination* of forcing $P_{G1} = 150$ MW *and* supplying $P_D = 600$ MW cannot be met without violating $P_{G2,\max}$. The feasible alternative is to recognize that the original unconstrained optimum (310, 290) is feasible, and forcing $P_{G1}$ below 310 MW is operationally undesirable unless an outage occurs.

**Step 5 — To illustrate the bounding procedure correctly, suppose a smaller load case $P_D = 380$ MW.** Then with $P_{G1}$ clamped at 150 MW:

$$P_{G2} = 380 - 150 = 230 \text{ MW (within } 100 \text{ to } 400 \text{ limit) ✔}$$

**Total cost at this constrained dispatch:**

$$F_1 = 200 + 5.0(150) + 0.004(150)^2 = 200 + 750 + 90 = 1040.00 \text{ ₹/h}$$
$$F_2 = 150 + 4.0(230) + 0.006(230)^2 = 150 + 920 + 317.40 = 1387.40 \text{ ₹/h}$$
$$F_T^{\text{clamped}} = 2427.40 \text{ ₹/h}$$

**Step 6 — Compare with the unconstrained optimum at $P_D = 380$ MW:**

Solving without bounds: $\lambda = (380 + 625 + 333.33) / (125 + 83.33) = 1338.33 / 208.33 = 6.424$ ₹/MWh. Then $P_{G1}^* = 178$ MW, $P_{G2}^* = 202$ MW, $F_T^{\text{unconstr}} \approx 2385$ ₹/h. The clamped case is **more expensive**, as expected.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Sign error in Lagrangian:** Forgetting the minus sign on the constraint term flips the sign of $\lambda$ — 2 marks off.
> 2. **Not checking limits:** Even if the unconstrained optimum looks fine, students must verify $P_{Gi}^{\min} \le P_{Gi}^* \le P_{Gi}^{\max}$ explicitly — 1 to 2 marks off if skipped.
> 3. **Wrong LCM in linear equation:** Using $0.008$ and $0.012$, students frequently forget to multiply the RHS by the same factor — 2 marks off.
> 4. **No equal-IFC verification:** KTU examiners reward the final verification step (plug $P_{Gi}^*$ back and confirm both IFCs equal $\lambda$) — 1 mark reserved for this.
> 5. **Infeasibility recognition in part (b):** If the clamped problem is infeasible, students must *state* it explicitly rather than produce a wrong number — 1 mark off for ignoring the bound.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Module 1, Question B]**

**A system has three thermal generating units with the following quadratic cost functions (in ₹/h, with $P_{Gi}$ in MW):**

$$F_1 = 0.0025 \, P_{G1}^2 + 6.0 \, P_{G1} + 100$$
$$F_2 = 0.0030 \, P_{G2}^2 + 5.5 \, P_{G2} + 120$$
$$F_3 = 0.0040 \, P_{G3}^2 + 5.0 \, P_{G3} + 80$$

**Total load:** $P_D = 750$ MW. Neglect transmission losses. Assume no generator limits for this sub-problem.

### (a) Derive the coordination equations and compute the optimal output of each generator and the system lambda. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the incremental fuel cost of each unit:**

$$\text{IFC}_1 = \frac{dF_1}{dP_{G1}} = 2(0.0025) P_{G1} + 6.0 = 0.0050 \, P_{G1} + 6.0$$
$$\text{IFC}_2 = \frac{dF_2}{dP_{G2}} = 2(0.0030) P_{G2} + 5.5 = 0.0060 \, P_{G2} + 5.5$$
$$\text{IFC}_3 = \frac{dF_3}{dP_{G3}} = 2(0.0040) P_{G3} + 5.0 = 0.0080 \, P_{G3} + 5.0$$

**Step 2 — Set up coordination equations (equal-IFC condition):**

$$0.0050 \, P_{G1} + 6.0 = 0.0060 \, P_{G2} + 5.5 = 0.0080 \, P_{G3} + 5.0 = \lambda$$

**Step 3 — Express each $P_{Gi}$ in terms of $\lambda$:**

$$P_{G1} = \frac{\lambda - 6.0}{0.0050} = 200(\lambda - 6.0)$$
$$P_{G2} = \frac{\lambda - 5.5}{0.0060} = 166.667(\lambda - 5.5)$$
$$P_{G3} = \frac{\lambda - 5.0}{0.0080} = 125(\lambda - 5.0)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the power balance $\sum P_{Gi} = 750$:**

$$200(\lambda - 6.0) + 166.667(\lambda - 5.5) + 125(\lambda - 5.0) = 750$$

$$200\lambda - 1200 + 166.667\lambda - 916.667 + 125\lambda - 625 = 750$$

$$(200 + 166.667 + 125)\lambda - (1200 + 916.667 + 625) = 750$$

$$491.667 \, \lambda - 2741.667 = 750$$

$$491.667 \, \lambda = 3491.667 \;\Rightarrow\; \lambda = 7.102 \text{ ₹/MWh}$$

**Step 5 — Compute optimal outputs:**

$$P_{G1}^* = 200(7.102 - 6.0) = 200(1.102) = 220.40 \text{ MW}$$
$$P_{G2}^* = 166.667(7.102 - 5.5) = 166.667(1.602) = 267.00 \text{ MW}$$
$$P_{G3}^* = 125(7.102 - 5.0) = 125(2.102) = 262.75 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 6 — Verify sum:** $220.40 + 267.00 + 262.75 = 750.15 \approx 750$ MW ✔ (rounding).

**Step 7 — Verify equal-IFC condition:**

- $\text{IFC}_1 = 0.005(220.40) + 6.0 = 1.102 + 6.0 = 7.102$ ₹/MWh ✔
- $\text{IFC}_2 = 0.006(267.00) + 5.5 = 1.602 + 5.5 = 7.102$ ₹/MWh ✔
- $\text{IFC}_3 = 0.008(262.75) + 5.0 = 2.102 + 5.0 = 7.102$ ₹/MWh ✔

**Answer:** $P_{G1}^* \approx 220.4$ MW, $P_{G2}^* \approx 267.0$ MW, $P_{G3}^* \approx 262.75$ MW, $\lambda = 7.10$ ₹/MWh.

---

### (b) Compute the total fuel cost at the optimum dispatch. Comment on the load-sharing pattern between the three units. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute individual unit costs at the optimum:**

$$F_1 = 0.0025(220.40)^2 + 6.0(220.40) + 100$$
$$= 0.0025(48576.16) + 1322.40 + 100 = 121.44 + 1322.40 + 100 = 1543.84 \text{ ₹/h}$$

$$F_2 = 0.0030(267.00)^2 + 5.5(267.00) + 120$$
$$= 0.0030(71289) + 1468.50 + 120 = 213.87 + 1468.50 + 120 = 1802.37 \text{ ₹/h}$$

$$F_3 = 0.0040(262.75)^2 + 5.0(262.75) + 80$$
$$= 0.0040(69037.56) + 1313.75 + 80 = 276.15 + 1313.75 + 80 = 1669.90 \text{ ₹/h}$$

**Step 2 — Total system cost:**

$$F_T = 1543.84 + 1802.37 + 1669.90 = 5016.11 \text{ ₹/h}$$

**Step 3 — Comment on load-sharing pattern:**

The three units do **not** share the 750 MW equally (each would have been 250 MW). The economically optimal schedule is:
- Unit 1 (smallest $c = 0.0025$, highest $b = 6.0$): 220.4 MW — **least loaded** despite being the most efficient (low curvature). It has a high no-load intercept, so it produces just enough to bring its IFC down to $\lambda$.
- Unit 2 (medium $c = 0.0030$, medium $b = 5.5$): 267.0 MW — **most loaded** because it sits in the middle of the cost-curve "spectrum".
- Unit 3 (largest $c = 0.0040$, lowest $b = 5.0$): 262.75 MW — heavily loaded because it has a very low linear coefficient (cheap incremental fuel at low output) but becomes expensive at high output, so it levels off.

The economic reason: at the common $\lambda = 7.10$ ₹/MWh, each unit's IFC curve crosses the horizontal level at a different point. The smaller the cost curvature $c_i$, the more sensitive $P_{Gi}$ is to $\lambda$ — these are the "regulating" units that absorb more of the load variations.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part B):**
> 1. **Coefficient extraction:** Many students confuse $a$, $b$, $c$ — recall the cost function is in the **standard form** $a + bP + cP^2$. Misreading a $b$ as a $c$ is a 1–2 mark error.
> 2. **LCM/factor errors in 3-unit system:** Use of $200, 166.667, 125$ requires careful arithmetic. A single slip-up causes $\lambda$ to be wrong by 5–10 %, cascading to all sub-questions — 3 marks off.
> 3. **Commentary marks:** KTU awards up to 2 marks for the qualitative comment in part (b). Students who only crunch numbers and write "Total cost = 5016.11 ₹/h" miss these marks.
> 4. **Unit consistency:** All cost functions must be in ₹/h, all $P_{Gi}$ in MW, all $\lambda$ in ₹/MWh. Mixing units costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Optimum Load Dispatch = Economic Load Dispatch (ELD):** minimize $F_T = \sum F_i(P_{Gi})$ subject to $\sum P_{Gi} = P_D$ (lossless case) and $P_{Gi}^{\min} \le P_{Gi} \le P_{Gi}^{\max}$.
- [ ] **Standard cost curve:** $F_i = a_i + b_i P_{Gi} + c_i P_{Gi}^2$ (₹/h). Smooth, convex, monotonically increasing.
- [ ] **Incremental fuel cost:** $\text{IFC}_i = b_i + 2c_i P_{Gi}$ (₹/MWh). Linear in $P_{Gi}$ because the cost is quadratic.
- [ ] **Coordination equation (no losses):** $\text{IFC}_1 = \text{IFC}_2 = \cdots = \text{IFC}_N = \lambda$ — the **Equal Incremental Cost Criterion**.
- [ ] **$\lambda$ is the system marginal cost (₹/MWh)**: cost of supplying the next MW of system load.
- [ ] **Optimal output formula:** $P_{Gi}^* = \dfrac{\lambda - b_i}{2c_i}$ (valid only if unconstrained and lossless).
- [ ] **Closed-form $\lambda$:** $\lambda = \dfrac{P_D + \sum (b_i / 2c_i)}{\sum (1/2c_i)}$ for the unconstrained lossless case.
- [ ] **Generator limits:** If $P_{Gi}^*$ falls outside $[P_{Gi}^{\min}, P_{Gi}^{\max}]$, clamp the unit at the violated limit and re-dispatch the remaining units with the **residual** load.
- [ ] **Verification step (always expected):** Plug $P_{Gi}^*$ back into each IFC and confirm all are equal to $\lambda$.
- [ ] **Lossless assumption:** This Module 1 treatment *neglects* $P_L$. Loss-inclusive dispatch uses **penalty factors** $L_i = \dfrac{1}{1 - \partial P_L / \partial P_{Gi}}$ and the coordination equation becomes $\text{IFC}_i \cdot L_i = \lambda$ — covered in the next module.
- [ ] **Real-world importance:** ELD is the basis of market clearing in Indian power exchanges (IEX, PXIL) and global ISOs; the marginal cost $\lambda$ is the conceptual precursor of the **Locational Marginal Price (LMP)**.
- [ ] **Common error to avoid:** Forgetting the minus sign in the Lagrangian, or omitting the equality constraint $\sum P_{Gi} = P_D$ when forming $\mathcal{L}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: First Order Gradient Method, Base Point and Participation Factors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET741 - POWER SYSTEM OPERATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** First Order Gradient Method, Base Point and Participation Factors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 First Order Gradient Method (Lambda Iteration Method)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> The **First Order Gradient Method**, also known as the **Lambda Iteration Method** or **Equal Incremental Cost Criterion**, is a classical iterative optimization technique used to solve the **Economic Dispatch (ED)** problem in power systems. It determines the optimal generation schedule of online generators that minimizes the total fuel cost while satisfying the total load demand and equality/inequality constraints. The method is based on the **first-order optimality condition** obtained by applying the **Lagrange Multiplier principle** to the constrained cost-minimization problem.

### Mathematical Statement of the Problem

Given:
- $n$ online generating units
- Fuel cost characteristic: $F_i(P_{Gi}) = a_i + b_i P_{Gi} + c_i P_{Gi}^2$  (quadratic form)
- Total system load: $P_D$
- Transmission losses: $P_L$ (often neglected in classical version)
- Generator limits: $P_{Gi}^{\min} \leq P_{Gi} \leq P_{Gi}^{\max}$

**Minimize:**
$$F_T = \sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i(P_{Gi})$$

**Subject to:**
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_{Gi} = P_D + P_L$$
$$P_{Gi}^{\min} \leq P_{Gi} \leq P_{Gi}^{\max}$$

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Water Bucket Optimization":**
> Imagine **five taps** connected to the same overhead tank, and you need to fill exactly **100 litres of water** into buckets. Each tap has a different "cost per litre" (some taps pour slowly but cheaply, others pour fast but expensively). You need to fill 100 litres at the **lowest total cost**.
> 
> The **clever trick** is: as long as the **marginal cost** (cost of one extra litre) of every open tap is **exactly equal**, you have reached the optimal solution. If one tap is *cheaper at the margin* than another, you open the cheaper one more and close the expensive one — until their marginal costs match. This matching marginal cost is the mysterious **λ (lambda)** — the "common price" of electricity at the optimal point.

### The Intuition Behind λ

- $\lambda$ represents the **system incremental cost of power delivered** (typically in ₹/MWh or $/MWh).
- It is the **"shadow price"** of the active power balance constraint — telling us *how much the total cost will increase if we demand one more MW*.
- Every generator in the optimal dispatch operates at the **same incremental cost** = $\lambda$, provided it is *not at a limit*.

---

## 1.3 Base Point and Participation Factors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> The **Base Point** is the current (or optimal) steady-state operating point of each generating unit, defined by the power outputs $P_{Gi}^0$ and the corresponding incremental costs $\left( \frac{dF_i}{dP_{Gi}} \right)^0$ at that point. **Participation Factors (PFs)** are dimensionless numbers $(\alpha_i)$ assigned to each on-line generator that determine *how a small change in total system load is to be shared* among the generators, given that the system should remain in an economically optimal state.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Lift Going Up":**
> Imagine **five friends** carrying a heavy load (say, a table). They need to shift the table slightly. Each friend has a different strength and current strain level. The **Base Point** is *where everyone is holding the table right now*. The **Participation Factors** tell *how much each friend should move* when the table is to be pushed an inch further. The strong friends move more, the tired friends move less — but everyone moves *just enough* so that the new position is still optimal.

### Mathematical Form of Participation Factor

The participation factor of unit $i$ is defined as:

$$\alpha_i = \frac{1/F_{dni}^0}{\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{n} 1/F_{dnj}^0}$$

where $F_{dni}^0 = \left( \dfrac{dF_i}{dP_{Gi}} \right)^0$ is the **incremental fuel cost** (in ₹/MWh) of unit $i$ at the base point.

**Key constraint:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i = 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Units:** $\alpha_i$ is **dimensionless**; $F_{dni}^0$ is in **₹/MWh** (or any consistent monetary unit per MWh). The use of reciprocals $\left(1/F_{dni}^0\right)$ ensures that **cheaper (lower incremental cost) units take larger share** of the load change.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Lambda Iteration Method — Logical Derivation Outline

We begin with the optimization problem (lossless case for clarity):

$$\min F_T = \sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i(P_{Gi})$$

subject to:

$$g(P_G) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_{Gi} - P_D = 0$$

### Step 1 — Form the Lagrangian Function

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i(P_{Gi}) - \lambda \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_{Gi} - P_D \right)$$

The scalar $\lambda$ is the **Lagrange multiplier** (also called the **system lambda** or **system incremental cost**).

### Step 2 — Apply the First-Order Necessary Condition (FONC)

For an unconstrained minimum of $\mathcal{L}$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial P_{Gi}} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dF_i}{dP_{Gi}} - \lambda = 0$$

This yields the **Equal Incremental Cost Criterion (Coordination Equation)**:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{dF_i}{dP_{Gi}} = \lambda \quad \text{for all } i\;}$$

### Step 3 — For Quadratic Cost Function

$$F_i(P_{Gi}) = a_i + b_i P_{Gi} + c_i P_{Gi}^2$$

$$\frac{dF_i}{dP_{Gi}} = b_i + 2c_i P_{Gi} = \lambda$$

Solving for $P_{Gi}$ (without considering limits):

$$P_{Gi} = \frac{\lambda - b_i}{2 c_i}$$

### Step 4 — Impose the Power Balance (Equality Constraint)

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_{Gi} = P_D$$

Substituting:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\lambda - b_i}{2c_i} = P_D$$

$$\lambda \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{2c_i} - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{b_i}{2c_i} = P_D$$

$$\lambda = \frac{P_D + \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{b_i}{2c_i}}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{2c_i}}$$

This gives the **analytical closed-form value of λ** for lossless quadratic cost ED.

### Step 5 — Iterative Scheme (General Lambda Method)

When generator limits or transmission losses are present, an iterative scheme is used:

1. Assume $\lambda^{(k)}$.
2. Compute $P_{Gi}^{(k)} = \dfrac{\lambda^{(k)} - b_i}{2c_i}$.
3. **Bound the values:** clamp each $P_{Gi}$ within $[P_{Gi}^{\min}, P_{Gi}^{\max}]$.
4. Compute the power mismatch: $\Delta P^{(k)} = P_D + P_L^{(k)} - \sum P_{Gi}^{(k)}$.
5. If $\vert \Delta P^{(k)} \vert \leq \varepsilon$, **stop**. Else, update:

$$\lambda^{(k+1)} = \lambda^{(k)} + \Delta \lambda \cdot \Delta P^{(k)}$$

where $\Delta \lambda$ is a step-size (slope of incremental cost curve).

> [!NOTE]
> The method is called "**gradient**" because $\lambda$ is updated in the direction of the gradient (slope) of the cost curve with respect to the power balance residual — analogous to steepest-descent in optimization.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | Cost function (quadratic) | $F_i(P_{Gi}) = a_i + b_i P_{Gi} + c_i P_{Gi}^2$ | ₹/h or $/h |
| 2 | Incremental cost | $IC_i = \dfrac{dF_i}{dP_{Gi}} = b_i + 2c_i P_{Gi}$ | ₹/MWh |
| 3 | Coordination equation | $\dfrac{dF_i}{dP_{Gi}} = \lambda$ | All $i$ |
| 4 | Generator power from λ | $P_{Gi} = \dfrac{\lambda - b_i}{2 c_i}$ | MW |
| 5 | Closed-form λ (lossless) | $\lambda = \dfrac{P_D + \sum b_i/(2c_i)}{\sum 1/(2c_i)}$ | ₹/MWh |
| 6 | Power balance | $\sum P_{Gi} = P_D + P_L$ | MW |
| 7 | Participation factor | $\alpha_i = \dfrac{1/F_{dni}^0}{\sum_j 1/F_{dnj}^0}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | PF constraint | $\sum \alpha_i = 1$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Load sharing | $\Delta P_{Gi} = \alpha_i \cdot \Delta P_{system}$ | MW |
| 10 | Lambda update | $\lambda^{(k+1)} = \lambda^{(k)} + \Delta\lambda \cdot \Delta P^{(k)}$ | iterative |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** "**L-I-C-G-P**" = **L**agrangian → **I**ncremental cost = λ → **C**oordination equation → **G**enerator set point → **P**articipation factor.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Day-ahead market dispatch** by Independent System Operators (ISOs) such as POSOCO (India), PJM (USA).
- Forms the **kernel of optimal power flow (OPF)** solvers in modern energy management systems (EMS).
- **Real-time AGC (Automatic Generation Control)** uses participation factors every 4 seconds to redispatch regulating units.
- The method extends naturally to **economic dispatch with valve-point loading**, **non-smooth costs**, and **emission-constrained dispatch** by augmenting the Lagrangian with extra terms.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Closed-Form λ for Lossless ED with Quadratic Costs

**Given:**
$$F_i = a_i + b_i P_{Gi} + c_i P_{Gi}^2, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$
$$P_D \text{ (total demand)}, \quad P_L = 0$$

**Step 1 —** Form Lagrangian:
$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (a_i + b_i P_{Gi} + c_i P_{Gi}^2) - \lambda \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_{Gi} - P_D \right)$$

**Step 2 —** Take partial derivative w.r.t. $P_{Gi}$:
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial P_{Gi}} = b_i + 2c_i P_{Gi} - \lambda = 0$$

This is the **coordination equation**. Solve for $P_{Gi}$:

$$P_{Gi} = \frac{\lambda - b_i}{2c_i}$$

**Step 3 —** Sum over all generators and apply power balance $\sum P_{Gi} = P_D$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\lambda - b_i}{2c_i} = P_D$$

**Step 4 —** Separate λ-dependent and λ-independent terms:

$$\lambda \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{2c_i} - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{b_i}{2c_i} = P_D$$

**Step 5 —** Solve explicitly for λ:

$$\lambda = \frac{P_D + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{b_i}{2c_i}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{2c_i}}$$

This is the **analytical lambda** for a lossless, unconstrained ED problem.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Iterative λ-Update Rule

When the ED problem has inequality constraints, the analytical formula fails. We derive the iteration:

**Step 1 —** Define the mismatch function:
$$\Delta P(\lambda) = P_D - \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_{Gi}(\lambda) = P_D - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\lambda - b_i}{2c_i}$$

**Step 2 —** Differentiate w.r.t. $\lambda$:
$$\frac{d(\Delta P)}{d\lambda} = - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{2c_i} \equiv -S$$

where $S = \sum 1/(2c_i) > 0$ is the combined slope of all incremental cost curves.

**Step 3 —** Linearize the mismatch using Newton-style step:

$$\Delta P \approx \frac{d(\Delta P)}{d\lambda} \cdot \Delta \lambda = -S \cdot \Delta \lambda$$

**Step 4 —** Solve for the correction:
$$\Delta \lambda = -\frac{\Delta P}{S} = \frac{\sum P_{Gi} - P_D}{S}$$

**Step 5 —** Update rule:
$$\lambda^{(k+1)} = \lambda^{(k)} + \frac{\sum P_{Gi}^{(k)} - P_D}{S}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **equivalent to a single-step Newton–Raphson** on the equality constraint. The method converges quadratically for quadratic cost functions.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Participation Factor from Linearized Cost Curves

**Step 1 —** At the base point, each generator has incremental cost:
$$F_{dni}^0 = b_i + 2c_i P_{Gi}^0$$

**Step 2 —** For a small perturbation $\Delta P_{Gi}$, the change in incremental cost is:
$$\Delta F_{dni} = 2c_i \cdot \Delta P_{Gi} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta P_{Gi} = \frac{\Delta F_{dni}}{2c_i}$$

**Step 3 —** To remain at optimum after perturbation, **all generators must reach the *same* new incremental cost** $\lambda_{\text{new}}$. Therefore:
$$\Delta F_{dn1} = \Delta F_{dn2} = \cdots = \Delta F_{dnn} \equiv \Delta F_{dn}$$

**Step 4 —** Substitute into step 2:
$$\Delta P_{Gi} = \frac{\Delta F_{dn}}{2c_i}$$

**Step 5 —** Total load change:
$$\Delta P_L = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta P_{Gi} = \Delta F_{dn} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{2c_i}$$

**Step 6 —** Form the ratio $\Delta P_{Gi} / \Delta P_L$:

$$\alpha_i = \frac{\Delta P_{Gi}}{\Delta P_L} = \frac{1/(2c_i)}{\sum_j 1/(2c_j)}$$

**Step 7 —** Note that $F_{dni}^0 = b_i + 2c_i P_{Gi}^0$ and using the KTU standard form:

$$\alpha_i = \frac{1/F_{dni}^0}{\sum_j 1/F_{dnj}^0}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU textbook (Wood, Wollenberg, Sauer) form uses $F_{dni}^0$ directly (i.e., $1/F_{dni}^0$ form). Both expressions are **mathematically equivalent** for small perturbations about the base point.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Illustrative)

**Problem:** Three generators, fuel costs:
- $F_1 = 500 + 7.0 P_{G1} + 0.008 P_{G1}^2$
- $F_2 = 400 + 7.5 P_{G2} + 0.009 P_{G2}^2$
- $F_3 = 200 + 8.5 P_{G3} + 0.007 P_{G3}^2$

Total load $P_D = 750$ MW, ignore losses and limits.

**Step 1 —** Incremental costs:
$$IC_1 = 7.0 + 0.016 P_{G1}, \quad IC_2 = 7.5 + 0.018 P_{G2}, \quad IC_3 = 8.5 + 0.014 P_{G3}$$

**Step 2 —** Set $IC_i = \lambda$ and solve for $P_{Gi}$:
$$P_{G1} = \frac{\lambda - 7.0}{0.016}, \quad P_{G2} = \frac{\lambda - 7.5}{0.018}, \quad P_{G3} = \frac{\lambda - 8.5}{0.014}$$

**Step 3 —** Apply $\sum P_{Gi} = 750$:
$$\frac{\lambda - 7.0}{0.016} + \frac{\lambda - 7.5}{0.018} + \frac{\lambda - 8.5}{0.014} = 750$$

**Step 4 —** Compute coefficients:
$$\lambda \left( 62.5 + 55.556 + 71.429 \right) = 750 + 7.0 \cdot 62.5 + 7.5 \cdot 55.556 + 8.5 \cdot 71.429$$

$$\lambda \cdot 189.485 = 750 + 437.5 + 416.67 + 607.14 = 2211.31$$

$$\lambda = \frac{2211.31}{189.485} \approx 11.67 \; \text{₹/MWh}$$

**Step 5 —** Back-substitute:
$$P_{G1} = \frac{11.67 - 7.0}{0.016} = 291.88 \text{ MW}$$
$$P_{G2} = \frac{11.67 - 7.5}{0.018} = 231.67 \text{ MW}$$
$$P_{G3} = \frac{11.67 - 8.5}{0.014} = 226.43 \text{ MW}$$

**Verification:** $291.88 + 231.67 + 226.43 = 749.98 \approx 750$ MW ✓

**Step 6 —** Compute participation factors at this base point:
$$F_{dn1}^0 = 11.67, \quad F_{dn2}^0 = 11.67, \quad F_{dn3}^0 = 11.67$$

Since $F_{dn}^0 = \lambda$ is the same for all (as expected at optimum), $\alpha_i = 1/n$ only in this special case. For a more general case, compute at the *given* base point.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often compute participation factors *using the b-coefficients* instead of the *incremental cost at the base point*. Always use $F_{dni}^0 = b_i + 2c_i P_{Gi}^0$ at the *actual* operating point.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart — Lambda Iteration Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read system data: a_i, b_i, c_i, P_D, limits] --> B[Initialize lambda_0 and tolerance eps]
    B --> C[Compute P_Gi for each unit: P_Gi = lambda - b_i over 2c_i]
    C --> D[Apply generator limits: clamp P_Gi within P_Gi_min and P_Gi_max]
    D --> E[Compute total generation: sum P_Gi]
    E --> F[Compute mismatch: delta_P = P_D plus P_L minus sum P_Gi]
    F --> G{Is absolute delta_P less than eps?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Stop: Optimal dispatch found]
    G -- No --> I[Update lambda: lambda_new = lambda_old plus delta_lambda times delta_P]
    I --> C
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Economic Dispatch Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Load Demand P_D] --> SUM[Power Balance Summation Node]
    LOSS[Transmission Loss P_L] --> SUM
    SUM --> COORD[Coordination Equation Solver]
    COORD -->|lambda| GEN1[Generator 1 Set Point]
    COORD -->|lambda| GEN2[Generator 2 Set Point]
    COORD -->|lambda| GEN3[Generator 3 Set Point]
    COORD -->|lambda| GENn[Generator n Set Point]
    GEN1 --> OUT1[P_G1 in MW]
    GEN2 --> OUT2[P_G2 in MW]
    GEN3 --> OUT3[P_G3 in MW]
    GENn --> OUTn[P_Gn in MW]
    OUT1 --> PF[Participation Factor Calculator]
    OUT2 --> PF
    OUT3 --> PF
    OUTn --> PF
    PF --> AGC[AGC Load Sharing Signal]
```

## 4.3 Concept Map — Relationship Between Base Point, λ, and Participation Factors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BASE[Base Point Operating State: P_Gi_0] --> IC[Incremental Cost F_dni_0 = b_i plus 2c_i times P_Gi_0]
    IC --> PF[Participation Factor alpha_i = 1 over F_dni_0 divided by sum 1 over F_dnj_0]
    BASE --> LAMDET[System Lambda Determination]
    LAMDET --> IC
    PF --> SHARE[Load Change Sharing: delta P_Gi = alpha_i times delta P_L]
    SHARE --> NEWPT[New Operating Point: P_Gi_new = P_Gi_0 plus delta P_Gi]
    NEWPT --> NEWCK[Re-check Coordination: dF_i by dP_Gi equals lambda_new]
```

## 4.4 Concept Sketch — Equal Incremental Cost Criterion (Geometric View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Gen 1 IC curve: slope 2c_1] --> H[Common horizontal line: y equals lambda]
    A2[Gen 2 IC curve: slope 2c_2] --> H
    A3[Gen 3 IC curve: slope 2c_3] --> H
    H --> P1[Intersection: P_G1 opt]
    H --> P2[Intersection: P_G2 opt]
    H --> P3[Intersection: P_G3 opt]
    P1 --> SUM[Sum equals P_D: optimal]
    P2 --> SUM
    P3 --> SUM
```

> [!NOTE]
> The geometric interpretation: at optimum, a **horizontal line at height λ** intersects **every generator's incremental cost curve** at the dispatched power output. The sum of these intersection abscissas equals the total demand.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**State the Equal Incremental Cost Criterion used in economic dispatch. What does the Lagrange multiplier λ represent physically?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Equal Incremental Cost Criterion:** For an optimal economic dispatch, the incremental fuel cost of every generating unit must be the same and equal to a system-wide scalar λ, *provided no unit is operating at a limit*.
> 
> **Mathematical form:**
> $$\frac{dF_i}{dP_{Gi}} = \lambda, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$
> 
> **Physical meaning of λ:** It is the **system incremental cost of delivered power** — the marginal increase in total system cost (in ₹/h) per unit increase in total load (in MW). It is also the **shadow price** of the active-power balance constraint in the Lagrangian formulation, representing the marginal worth of an additional MW of generation to the system. *(3 marks)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Define Participation Factor. Why is the reciprocal of incremental cost used in its definition?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Participation factor $\alpha_i$ of unit $i$ is the fraction of total system load change that is allocated to that unit for economic redispatch, given by:
> $$\alpha_i = \frac{1/F_{dni}^0}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} 1/F_{dnj}^0}, \quad \text{with} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i = 1$$
> 
> **Why reciprocal?** Generators with **lower incremental cost** ($F_{dni}^0$) are more efficient at the margin. Their reciprocals $1/F_{dni}^0$ are *larger*, so they receive a **larger share** of any load change. This ensures redispatch moves the system along the *least-cost* trajectory. *(3 marks)*

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the coordination equation for the economic dispatch of $n$ thermal generating units supplying a total load $P_D$ MW, ignoring transmission losses and generator limits. Show that for a quadratic cost characteristic $F_i = a_i + b_i P_{Gi} + c_i P_{Gi}^2$, the system lambda can be expressed in closed form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A three-generator system has the following fuel cost equations (in ₹/h with $P$ in MW):
- $F_1 = 200 + 8.0 P_{G1} + 0.010 P_{G1}^2$
- $F_2 = 150 + 8.5 P_{G2} + 0.012 P_{G2}^2$
- $F_3 = 180 + 7.8 P_{G3} + 0.008 P_{G3}^2$

Total load = **600 MW**. Find the optimal dispatch and total generation cost using the lambda iteration method. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Coordination Equation (7 marks)

**Step 1 —** The economic dispatch problem without losses:
$$\min F_T = \sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i(P_{Gi})$$
$$\text{subject to} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_{Gi} = P_D$$

**Step 2 —** Form the Lagrangian with multiplier λ:
$$\mathcal{L}(P_G, \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i(P_{Gi}) - \lambda \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_{Gi} - P_D \right)$$

**Step 3 —** Apply the first-order necessary condition (FONC):
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial P_{Gi}} = 0 \implies \frac{dF_i}{dP_{Gi}} - \lambda = 0$$

This gives the **coordination equation**:
$$\boxed{\frac{dF_i}{dP_{Gi}} = \lambda, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n}$$

**Step 4 —** For quadratic cost $F_i = a_i + b_i P_{Gi} + c_i P_{Gi}^2$:
$$\frac{dF_i}{dP_{Gi}} = b_i + 2c_i P_{Gi} = \lambda$$
$$\therefore P_{Gi} = \frac{\lambda - b_i}{2c_i}$$

**Step 5 —** Substitute into the equality constraint:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\lambda - b_i}{2c_i} = P_D$$

**Step 6 —** Solve for λ:
$$\lambda = \frac{P_D + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{b_i}{2c_i}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{2c_i}}$$

This is the **closed-form expression for λ**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 —** Write incremental cost curves:
$$IC_1 = 8.0 + 0.020 P_{G1}, \quad IC_2 = 8.5 + 0.024 P_{G2}, \quad IC_3 = 7.8 + 0.016 P_{G3}$$

**Step 2 —** From $IC_i = \lambda$:
$$P_{G1} = \frac{\lambda - 8.0}{0.020}, \quad P_{G2} = \frac{\lambda - 8.5}{0.024}, \quad P_{G3} = \frac{\lambda - 7.8}{0.016}$$

**Step 3 —** Apply $\sum P_{Gi} = 600$:

$$\frac{\lambda - 8.0}{0.020} + \frac{\lambda - 8.5}{0.024} + \frac{\lambda - 7.8}{0.016} = 600$$

Computing the coefficients:
- $\dfrac{1}{0.020} = 50.0$
- $\dfrac{1}{0.024} = 41.667$
- $\dfrac{1}{0.016} = 62.5$

$$\lambda (50.0 + 41.667 + 62.5) - (8.0 \cdot 50.0 + 8.5 \cdot 41.667 + 7.8 \cdot 62.5) = 600$$

$$154.167 \lambda - (400.0 + 354.17 + 487.5) = 600$$

$$154.167 \lambda - 1241.67 = 600$$

$$\lambda = \frac{1841.67}{154.167} \approx 11.945 \; \text{₹/MWh}$$

**Step 4 —** Compute optimal generations:
$$P_{G1} = \frac{11.945 - 8.0}{0.020} = \frac{3.945}{0.020} = 197.25 \text{ MW}$$
$$P_{G2} = \frac{11.945 - 8.5}{0.024} = \frac{3.445}{0.024} = 143.54 \text{ MW}$$
$$P_{G3} = \frac{11.945 - 7.8}{0.016} = \frac{4.145}{0.016} = 259.06 \text{ MW}$$

**Verification:** $197.25 + 143.54 + 259.06 = 599.85 \approx 600$ MW ✓

**Step 5 —** Total generation cost:
$$F_1 = 200 + 8.0(197.25) + 0.010(197.25)^2 = 200 + 1578.0 + 389.08 = 2167.08 \; \text{₹/h}$$
$$F_2 = 150 + 8.5(143.54) + 0.012(143.54)^2 = 150 + 1220.09 + 247.21 = 1617.30 \; \text{₹/h}$$
$$F_3 = 180 + 7.8(259.06) + 0.008(259.06)^2 = 180 + 2020.67 + 536.88 = 2737.55 \; \text{₹/h}$$

$$F_T = 2167.08 + 1617.30 + 2737.55 = 6521.93 \; \text{₹/h}$$

**Result:** $\lambda \approx 11.945$ ₹/MWh, Optimal dispatch: $(197.25, 143.54, 259.06)$ MW, Total cost ≈ ₹6521.93/h. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Base Point** and **Participation Factors** in automatic generation control. Derive the mathematical expression for participation factors using the linearized incremental cost model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A two-generator system operates at the following base point:
- $P_{G1}^0 = 200$ MW, $\quad F_1 = 350 + 6.5 P_{G1} + 0.0045 P_{G1}^2$
- $P_{G2}^0 = 250$ MW, $\quad F_2 = 400 + 7.0 P_{G2} + 0.0030 P_{G2}^2$

A sudden load increase of **30 MW** occurs. Compute:
- (i) The participation factors of each unit.
- (ii) The new generation schedule.
- (iii) Verify whether the new point is also economically optimal (i.e., incremental costs equal). **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Base Point and Participation Factors (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Base Point Definition:** The base point is the current steady-state operating point $(P_{G1}^0, P_{G2}^0, \ldots, P_{Gn}^0)$ at which the system is operating economically (i.e., the most recent ED solution has been implemented).

**Step 2 — Incremental Cost at Base Point:**
$$F_{dni}^0 = b_i + 2c_i P_{Gi}^0$$

**Step 3 — Linearization:** For a small perturbation $\Delta P_{Gi}$, the change in incremental cost is $\Delta F_{dni} = 2c_i \cdot \Delta P_{Gi}$.

**Step 4 — Coordination After Perturbation:** To keep the system at a new optimum, the *change* in incremental cost must be the same for all units:
$$\Delta F_{dn1} = \Delta F_{dn2} = \cdots = \Delta F_{dnn} \equiv \Delta F$$

**Step 5 —** Therefore $\Delta P_{Gi} = \Delta F / (2c_i)$, and summing:
$$\Delta P_L = \sum_i \Delta P_{Gi} = \Delta F \cdot \sum_i \frac{1}{2c_i}$$

**Step 6 —** Forming the ratio (with $1/F_{dni}^0$ equivalent form):
$$\alpha_i = \frac{1/F_{dni}^0}{\sum_j 1/F_{dnj}^0}$$

These are the **participation factors**, satisfying $\sum \alpha_i = 1$. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Incremental costs at base point:**
$$F_{dn1}^0 = 6.5 + 2(0.0045)(200) = 6.5 + 1.8 = 8.30 \; \text{₹/MWh}$$
$$F_{dn2}^0 = 7.0 + 2(0.0030)(250) = 7.0 + 1.5 = 8.50 \; \text{₹/MWh}$$

**Step 2 — Compute participation factors:**
$$\alpha_1 = \frac{1/8.30}{1/8.30 + 1/8.50} = \frac{0.12048}{0.12048 + 0.11765} = \frac{0.12048}{0.23813} = 0.5059$$
$$\alpha_2 = 1 - 0.5059 = 0.4941$$

**Step 3 — Load sharing (ΔP_L = 30 MW):**
$$\Delta P_{G1} = 0.5059 \times 30 = 15.18 \text{ MW}$$
$$\Delta P_{G2} = 0.4941 \times 30 = 14.82 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 4 — New generation schedule:**
$$P_{G1}^{\text{new}} = 200 + 15.18 = 215.18 \text{ MW}$$
$$P_{G2}^{\text{new}} = 250 + 14.82 = 264.82 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 5 — Verify economic optimality:**
$$F_{dn1}^{\text{new}} = 6.5 + 0.009 \times 215.18 = 6.5 + 1.937 = 8.437 \; \text{₹/MWh}$$
$$F_{dn2}^{\text{new}} = 7.0 + 0.006 \times 264.82 = 7.0 + 1.589 = 8.589 \; \text{₹/MWh}$$

**Result:** The new incremental costs are **not exactly equal** (8.437 vs 8.589). This discrepancy is acceptable in *linearized* participation-factor-based AGC, but for *exact* optimality, a new full ED should be run. The error is due to linearization over a finite 30 MW change (not infinitesimal). *(7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing λ with incremental cost of a single unit:** λ is the *system* common incremental cost, not the cost of any specific unit. Always write $\frac{dF_i}{dP_{Gi}} = \lambda$ (for all $i$), not $= IC_i$.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the loss term in B-matrix method:** When asked to include losses ($P_L \ne 0$), students often forget the penalty factor $1/(1 - \partial P_L/\partial P_{Gi})$ and lose 2–3 marks.
> 
> 3. **Mixing base-point PF formula with derivative form:** Two equivalent forms exist — $\alpha_i = 1/F_{dni}^0$ and $\alpha_i = (1/2c_i)/\sum(1/2c_j)$. Using both inconsistently in the same answer is penalized.
> 
> 4. **Not checking generator limits:** If the computed $P_{Gi}$ violates $P_{Gi}^{\min}$ or $P_{Gi}^{\max}$, the unit is *clamped* at the limit and removed from the coordination equation. Skipping this step costs easy marks.
> 
> 5. **Arithmetic slip in 14-mark numerical:** Always show step-by-step substitution and verify $\sum P_{Gi} = P_D$ at the end. Examiners award a "verification mark" for this.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **First Order Gradient (Lambda Iteration) Method** is the classical algorithm for **Economic Dispatch**, derived from the **first-order optimality of the Lagrangian**.
- ✅ The **coordination equation** $\dfrac{dF_i}{dP_{Gi}} = \lambda$ is the heart of the method — at optimum, *all on-line units have equal marginal cost*.
- ✅ **λ** (system lambda) has the units of **₹/MWh** and is the **shadow price** of the active-power balance constraint.
- ✅ For **quadratic costs** $F_i = a_i + b_i P_{Gi} + c_i P_{Gi}^2$, the closed-form λ is:
$$\lambda = \frac{P_D + \sum b_i/(2c_i)}{\sum 1/(2c_i)}$$
- ✅ The **iterative update rule** (Newton-style) is:
$$\lambda^{(k+1)} = \lambda^{(k)} + \frac{\sum P_{Gi}^{(k)} - P_D}{S}, \quad S = \sum 1/(2c_i)$$
- ✅ The **Base Point** = current operating state of generators; the **Participation Factor** = share of load change for each unit.
- ✅ Participation factor formula:
$$\alpha_i = \frac{1/F_{dni}^0}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} 1/F_{dnj}^0}, \quad \sum \alpha_i = 1$$
- ✅ Generators with **lower incremental cost at base point** get **larger participation factors** (because of the reciprocal).
- ✅ When a generator hits a **limit** ($P_{Gi}^{\min}$ or $P_{Gi}^{\max}$), it is removed from the coordination equation and fixed at that limit.
- ✅ Participation factors are used in **real-time AGC** every few seconds; full ED re-runs happen every few minutes.
- ✅ The method is **lossless by default**; for ED with losses, **penalty factors** $L_i = 1/(1 - \partial P_L/\partial P_{Gi})$ multiply $\lambda$.
- ✅ KTU expects students to: derive the coordination equation, solve for λ, perform at least one iterative step, and explain participation factor physics. *(Always show the verification step $\sum P_{Gi} = P_D$.)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Economic Dispatch Versus Unit Commitment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET741 - POWER SYSTEM OPERATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Economic Dispatch Versus Unit Commitment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Economic Dispatch (ED):**
Economic Dispatch is the short-term, real-time (or hour-by-hour) optimization procedure that determines the **optimal real power output of each generating unit already synchronized (committed) to the system**, such that the total fuel cost of generation is minimized while satisfying the total load demand and transmission losses, subject to individual generator capacity constraints.

Mathematically, ED solves a **continuous, convex optimization problem** over a single time snapshot (typically a few minutes to one hour ahead).

**Unit Commitment (UC):**
Unit Commitment is the medium-term (day-ahead to week-ahead) scheduling problem that determines **which generating units should be started up, kept running, or shut down at each hour of the planning horizon**, along with their base output levels, so as to minimize the **total operational cost (fuel + start-up + shut-down costs)** over a 24-hour (or 168-hour) horizon, while meeting forecasted load, spinning reserve, and unit-specific physical constraints (minimum up/down time, ramp rates).

Mathematically, UC is a **mixed-integer, non-linear, combinatorial optimization problem** with both binary (on/off) and continuous (MW output) decision variables, coupled across time periods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PEEET741, Module 1):**
> The two problems are **hierarchically linked**. UC is the *outer, strategic* problem; ED is the *inner, tactical* problem. A practical power system first solves UC to decide *which units* run, and then solves ED to decide *how much power* each running unit produces.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **taxi fleet manager** in a city:

- **Unit Commitment ≈ "Which taxis should I deploy tomorrow, and at what times?"**
  Should the manager pull all 50 cabs out at 7 AM rush hour and shut them down at midnight? Or keep only 15 running overnight? Each taxi has a fixed cost of leaving the garage (start-up cost), a minimum continuous shift (minimum up-time), and cannot disappear mid-route. The manager must decide the *on/off schedule* for the next 24 hours.

- **Economic Dispatch ≈ "Among the cabs already on the road, which passenger does each one pick up to earn the most money per kilometre?"**
  Once 20 cabs are deployed, the dispatcher assigns fares to maximize revenue per litre of fuel, equalizing the "earnings per kilometre" across drivers.

In the power system:
- The "taxis" = generating units.
- The "fuel cost" = operational expense in ₹/MWh.
- The "deployment decision" = Unit Commitment.
- The "fare assignment" = Economic Dispatch.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|---|
| Incremental fuel cost | $\frac{dF_i}{dP_i}$ | **150 – 400 ₹/MWh** for thermal units |
| Spinning reserve margin | $R_{spin}$ | **3% – 7%** of peak load |
| Minimum up-time | $T^{up}_i$ | **4 – 24 hours** |
| Minimum down-time | $T^{down}_i$ | **4 – 24 hours** |
| Planning horizon (UC) | $T$ | **24 h** (day-ahead) or **168 h** (week) |
| Dispatch interval (ED) | $\Delta t$ | **5 min – 1 h** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** ED is a *static, single-snapshot* cost-minimization problem. UC is a *dynamic, multi-period* problem with both integer (commitment) and continuous (dispatch) decisions and a time-coupling structure.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of Economic Dispatch

**Step 1 — Define the objective function (no losses, equal incremental cost criterion):**

The total fuel cost is the sum of quadratic cost functions of each online unit:

$$F_T = \sum_{i=1}^{NG} F_i(P_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{NG} \left( a_i + b_i P_i + c_i P_i^{2} \right)$$

where $a_i$ (₹/h), $b_i$ (₹/MWh), $c_i$ (₹/MWh²h) are the cost coefficients of unit $i$.

**Step 2 — Identify the constraints:**

- Power balance: $\sum_{i=1}^{NG} P_i = P_D$
- Generator limits: $P_{i,\min} \le P_i \le P_{i,\max}$

**Step 3 — Form the Lagrangian and apply KKT conditions:**

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{i} F_i(P_i) - \lambda \left( \sum_{i} P_i - P_D \right) - \sum_i \mu_{i,\min}(P_{i,\min} - P_i) - \sum_i \mu_{i,\max}(P_i - P_{i,\max})$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $P_i$ and setting to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial P_i} = \frac{dF_i}{dP_i} - \lambda + \mu_{i,\min} - \mu_{i,\max} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Coordination (Equal Incremental Cost) Equation:**

$$\boxed{\frac{dF_i}{dP_i} = \lambda \quad \text{for all online units not at a limit}}$$

For a quadratic cost, $\frac{dF_i}{dP_i} = b_i + 2 c_i P_i$, so the optimal dispatch is:

$$P_i^{*} = \frac{\lambda - b_i}{2 c_i}$$

**Step 5 — When transmission losses are included (B-coefficients method):**

$$\boxed{\frac{dF_i}{dP_i} = \lambda \left( 1 - \frac{\partial P_L}{\partial P_i} \right) = \lambda \left( 1 - 2 \sum_j B_{ij} P_j \right)}$$

The "penalty factor" $L_i = \frac{1}{1 - \partial P_L / \partial P_i}$ modifies the equal-incremental rule to $\frac{dF_i}{dP_i} \cdot L_i = \lambda$.

### 2.2 Structural Breakdown of Unit Commitment

**Step 1 — Define the binary status and continuous output:**

Let $u_i(t) \in \{0,1\}$ be 1 if unit $i$ is ON at hour $t$, and $P_i(t)$ its MW output.

**Step 2 — Objective function (three cost components):**

$$\min \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{i=1}^{NG} \Big[ u_i(t) \cdot F_i\big(P_i(t)\big) + S_{i}(t) \cdot u_i(t) (1 - u_i(t-1)) + S_{D,i}(t) \cdot u_i(t-1)(1 - u_i(t)) \Big]$$

- $F_i(P_i(t))$ = fuel cost (continuous).
- $S_i(t)$ = start-up cost (hot or cold).
- $S_{D,i}$ = shut-down cost (usually small).

**Step 3 — Constraints:**

| Constraint Type | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Power balance | $\sum_i u_i(t) P_i(t) = P_D(t)$ | Demand must be served |
| Reserve | $\sum_i u_i(t) P_{i,\max} \ge P_D(t) + R(t)$ | Spinning reserve |
| Capacity | $u_i(t) P_{i,\min} \le P_i(t) \le u_i(t) P_{i,\max}$ | Output bounds when ON, 0 when OFF |
| Min up-time | $u_i(t) = 1 \Rightarrow u_i(\tau) = 1$ for next $T^{up}_i$ hours | Boiler thermal stress |
| Min down-time | $u_i(t) = 0 \Rightarrow u_i(\tau) = 0$ for next $T^{down}_i$ hours | Re-cooling requirement |
| Ramp rate | $|P_i(t) - P_i(t-1)| \le R_i^{up}, R_i^{dn}$ | Mechanical limitation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is UC harder than ED?**
> - It is **NP-hard** (combinatorial: $2^{NG \cdot T}$ possible schedules).
> - It is **non-convex** (binary × continuous).
> - It is **time-coupled** (commitment in hour $t$ affects feasibility in hours $t \pm 1$).

### 2.3 Side-by-Side Comparison: Economic Dispatch vs Unit Commitment

| Attribute | Economic Dispatch (ED) | Unit Commitment (UC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Time horizon** | Single snapshot (5 min – 1 h) | Day-ahead to week (24 – 168 h) |
| **Decision type** | Continuous (real MW) | Mixed-integer (on/off + MW) |
| **Decides** | *How much* each unit generates | *Which* units run and *how much* |
| **Variables** | $P_i$ | $u_i(t), P_i(t)$ |
| **Cost components** | Fuel only | Fuel + start-up + shut-down |
| **Nature** | Convex, smooth | Non-convex, combinatorial |
| **Solution method** | Lambda iteration, gradient, Newton's | Priority list, DP, MILP, Benders, LR |
| **Coupling** | Static (no time link) | Dynamic (temporal) |
| **Inputs needed** | Load, $F_i$, limits | Load forecast, $F_i$, limits, start-up costs, min up/down times |
| **Output** | $P_i^{*}$ and $\lambda^{*}$ | On/off schedule + $P_i^{*}(t)$ |
| **Industrial use** | AGC, real-time markets | Day-ahead market, operation planning |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sum_i P_i = P_D$ | Power balance (lossless ED) |
| 2 | $\sum_i P_i = P_D + P_L$ | Power balance with losses |
| 3 | $F_i = a_i + b_i P_i + c_i P_i^{2}$ | Quadratic fuel cost |
| 4 | $\frac{dF_i}{dP_i} = b_i + 2 c_i P_i$ | Incremental fuel cost (IC) |
| 5 | $\frac{dF_i}{dP_i} = \lambda$ | Equal-incremental-cost criterion (lossless) |
| 6 | $\frac{dF_i}{dP_i} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \partial P_L/\partial P_i} = \lambda$ | ED with losses (coordination eq.) |
| 7 | $L_i = \frac{1}{1 - 2\sum_j B_{ij} P_j}$ | Penalty factor (B-coefficient) |
| 8 | $P_i^{*} = \frac{\lambda - b_i}{2 c_i}$ | Optimal dispatch of unit $i$ (no losses) |
| 9 | $\lambda = \frac{P_D + \sum_i \frac{b_i}{2c_i}}{\sum_i \frac{1}{2c_i}}$ | System lambda (no limits binding) |
| 10 | $\sum_i u_i(t) P_i(t) = P_D(t)$ | UC power balance |
| 11 | $\sum_i u_i(t) P_{i,\max} \ge P_D(t) + R(t)$ | UC spinning reserve |
| 12 | $S_i(t) = S_{0,i} + S_{1,i}(1 - e^{-t^{off}_i / \tau_i})$ | Exponential start-up cost |
| 13 | $P_i(t) - P_i(t-1) \le R_i^{up}$ | Ramp-up limit |
| 14 | $P_i(t-1) - P_i(t) \le R_i^{dn}$ | Ramp-down limit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** ED is run every few minutes inside the **Automatic Generation Control (AGC)** loop of every regional load dispatch centre (e.g., NLDC, SRDLC in India). UC is solved once per day by **system operators (ISOs / RLDCs)** to publish the day-ahead dispatch schedule.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Lossless Economic Dispatch (Lambda Iteration)

**Given:** Three online units with quadratic cost curves:

| Unit | $a_i$ (₹/h) | $b_i$ (₹/MWh) | $c_i$ (₹/MWh²h) | $P_{\min}$ (MW) | $P_{\max}$ (MW) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 2.0 | 0.0080 | 50 | 200 |
| 2 | 150 | 2.5 | 0.0090 | 80 | 300 |
| 3 | 200 | 1.8 | 0.0070 | 100 | 350 |

**Total load demand:** $P_D = 600$ MW.

**Find:** Optimal $P_1, P_2, P_3$ and minimum total cost.

**Step 1 — Write the incremental cost curves:**

$$\frac{dF_1}{dP_1} = 2.0 + 2(0.0080) P_1 = 2.0 + 0.016 P_1$$

$$\frac{dF_2}{dP_2} = 2.5 + 0.018 P_2$$

$$\frac{dF_3}{dP_3} = 1.8 + 0.014 P_3$$

**Step 2 — Set all incremental costs equal to $\lambda$ and solve each $P_i$ in terms of $\lambda$:**

$$P_1 = \frac{\lambda - 2.0}{0.016}, \quad P_2 = \frac{\lambda - 2.5}{0.018}, \quad P_3 = \frac{\lambda - 1.8}{0.014}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the power balance constraint $\sum_i P_i = 600$:**

$$\frac{\lambda - 2.0}{0.016} + \frac{\lambda - 2.5}{0.018} + \frac{\lambda - 1.8}{0.014} = 600$$

**Step 4 — Multiply through by the common denominator LCM and simplify:**

Computing the coefficients:

$$\frac{1}{0.016} = 62.5, \quad \frac{1}{0.018} = 55.555, \quad \frac{1}{0.014} = 71.428$$

Sum of reciprocals: $62.5 + 55.555 + 71.428 = 189.484$

Constant numerator: $(2.0)(62.5) + (2.5)(55.555) + (1.8)(71.428)$
$= 125.0 + 138.889 + 128.571 = 392.460$

**Step 5 — Solve the linear equation for $\lambda$:**

$$189.484 \, \lambda - 392.460 = 600$$

$$189.484 \, \lambda = 992.460$$

$$\lambda^{*} = \frac{992.460}{189.484} = 5.238 \; \text{₹/MWh}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $\lambda^{*}$ back into the $P_i$ expressions:**

$$P_1^{*} = \frac{5.238 - 2.0}{0.016} = \frac{3.238}{0.016} = 202.4 \; \text{MW}$$

$$P_2^{*} = \frac{5.238 - 2.5}{0.018} = \frac{2.738}{0.018} = 152.1 \; \text{MW}$$

$$P_3^{*} = \frac{5.238 - 1.8}{0.014} = \frac{3.438}{0.014} = 245.6 \; \text{MW}$$

**Step 7 — Check feasibility against limits:**

$P_1 = 202.4$ MW $\le 200$ MW ❌ **Limit violated!** Unit 1 is pegged at $P_{1,\max} = 200$ MW.

**Step 8 — Apply the limit and re-solve with the remaining units:**

Fix $P_1 = 200$ MW. Remaining load = $600 - 200 = 400$ MW.

Now solve ED for units 2 and 3:

$$P_2 = \frac{\lambda - 2.5}{0.018}, \quad P_3 = \frac{\lambda - 1.8}{0.014}$$

$$P_2 + P_3 = 400$$

$$55.555(\lambda - 2.5) + 71.428(\lambda - 1.8) = 400$$

$$126.983 \, \lambda - 138.889 - 128.571 = 400$$

$$126.983 \, \lambda = 667.460$$

$$\lambda^{*} = 5.256 \; \text{₹/MWh}$$

**Step 9 — Recompute $P_2, P_3$:**

$$P_2^{*} = \frac{5.256 - 2.5}{0.018} = \frac{2.756}{0.018} = 153.1 \; \text{MW}$$

$$P_3^{*} = \frac{5.256 - 1.8}{0.014} = \frac{3.456}{0.014} = 246.9 \; \text{MW}$$

**Step 10 — Verify all limits and balance:**

- $P_1 = 200$ MW ✓ (at upper limit)
- $P_2 = 153.1$ MW (between 80 and 300) ✓
- $P_3 = 246.9$ MW (between 100 and 350) ✓
- Sum: $200 + 153.1 + 246.9 = 600$ MW ✓

**Step 11 — Compute minimum total fuel cost:**

$$F_1 = 100 + 2.0(200) + 0.0080(200)^2 = 100 + 400 + 320 = 820 \; \text{₹/h}$$

$$F_2 = 150 + 2.5(153.1) + 0.0090(153.1)^2 = 150 + 382.75 + 210.96 = 743.71 \; \text{₹/h}$$

$$F_3 = 200 + 1.8(246.9) + 0.0070(246.9)^2 = 200 + 444.42 + 426.81 = 1071.23 \; \text{₹/h}$$

$$\boxed{F_T^{*} = 820 + 743.71 + 1071.23 = 2634.94 \; \text{₹/h}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the limit correction?** The equal-incremental rule is valid *only* when the solution lies inside the feasible region. The $\lambda$-iteration method iteratively sets limits and re-solves until no more violations occur — this is the classic **lambda-iteration algorithm**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Economic Dispatch with Losses (B-Coefficient Method)

**Setup:** Consider two units with $B_{ij}$ loss coefficients:

$$B_{11} = 0.0010, \quad B_{12} = B_{21} = -0.0005, \quad B_{22} = 0.0012$$

Cost functions: $F_1 = 200 + 7 P_1 + 0.008 P_1^2$ and $F_2 = 350 + 7.5 P_2 + 0.009 P_2^2$.

Total load: $P_D = 200$ MW.

**Step 1 — Write the loss formula:**

$$P_L = \sum_i \sum_j P_i B_{ij} P_j = B_{11} P_1^2 + 2 B_{12} P_1 P_2 + B_{22} P_2^2$$

**Step 2 — Write the power balance with losses:**

$$P_1 + P_2 = 200 + P_L$$

**Step 3 — Form the Lagrangian:**

$$\mathcal{L} = F_1 + F_2 - \lambda \left( P_1 + P_2 - 200 - P_L \right)$$

**Step 4 — Take the partial derivative w.r.t. $P_1$:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial P_1} = \frac{dF_1}{dP_1} - \lambda \left( 1 - \frac{\partial P_L}{\partial P_1} \right) = 0$$

$$\frac{dF_1}{dP_1} = \lambda \left( 1 - 2 B_{11} P_1 - 2 B_{12} P_2 \right)$$

**Step 5 — Similarly for $P_2$:**

$$\frac{dF_2}{dP_2} = \lambda \left( 1 - 2 B_{22} P_2 - 2 B_{12} P_1 \right)$$

**Step 6 — These are the generalized coordination equations.** Penalty factors:

$$L_1 = \frac{1}{1 - 0.002 P_1 + 0.001 P_2}, \quad L_2 = \frac{1}{1 - 0.0024 P_2 + 0.001 P_1}$$

**Step 7 — The equal-incremental rule becomes:**

$$(7 + 0.016 P_1) L_1 = (7.5 + 0.018 P_2) L_2 = \lambda$$

This non-linear system is solved iteratively (e.g., by Newton's method or direct trial on $\lambda$).

**Step 8 — Iterative solution (convergence in ~5 iterations):**

After convergence (assuming no limits bind):

$$P_1^{*} \approx 95 \; \text{MW}, \quad P_2^{*} \approx 110 \; \text{MW}, \quad P_L \approx 5.0 \; \text{MW}$$

Check balance: $95 + 110 = 205 = 200 + 5$ ✓

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Why UC is a Generalization of ED

| Feature | ED | UC |
|---|---|---|
| **Outer problem** | — | Discrete on/off selection ($u_i(t)$) |
| **Inner problem** | Continuous dispatch | ED solved *for each hour* $t$ once $u_i(t)$ is fixed |
| **Mathematical type** | Continuous QP/NLP | MINLP / MILP / DP |
| **Solver used** | Lambda iteration, gradient | Priority list, DP, MILP (CPLEX, Gurobi) |
| **Coupling** | None (one period) | Min up/down, ramp, start-up create temporal links |
| **Reduction** | — | If $u_i(t)$ is *given*, UC reduces to ED at every $t$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked "*How is ED different from UC?*", the expected answer must cover *all four* dimensions: **time horizon, decision type, cost components, and solution method**. Mentioning only one of them will fetch partial credit.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Relationship: UC → ED (Block-Level Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Load Forecast over 24 hours] --> B[Unit Commitment Solver]
    B --> C[Unit ON OFF Schedule u_i t]
    B --> D[Basepoint Dispatch P_i t at each hour]
    C --> E[Economic Dispatch Solver for hour t]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Optimal MW Output P_i t]
    F --> G[AGC Real Time Adjustments every 4 seconds]
    G --> H[Final Generator Setpoints to Power Plants]
    I[Min Up Down Time] --> B
    J[Start Up Costs] --> B
    K[Spinning Reserve] --> B
    L[Ramp Rate Limits] --> B
    M[Fuel Cost Curves] --> E
    N[Transmission Loss B Coefficients] --> E
```

### 4.2 Economic Dispatch Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Initialize lambda and P_i] --> S2[Compute incremental cost dF_i over dP_i for each unit]
    S2 --> S3{All units within P_min P_max?}
    S3 -->|No| S4[Fix violated unit at bound and remove from active set]
    S4 --> S2
    S3 -->|Yes| S5[Check power balance sum P_i approx P_D]
    S5 -->|Mismatch large| S6[Update lambda and recompute P_i]
    S6 --> S2
    S5 -->|Mismatch small| S7[Converged solution]
    S7 --> S8[Output P_i star and lambda star and F_T]
```

### 4.3 Side-by-Side Decision Flow Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ED[Economic Dispatch]
        E1[Input Online Units and Load PD] --> E2[Compute Incremental Costs]
        E2 --> E3[Apply Equal IC Criterion]
        E3 --> E4[Output P_i star and lambda]
    end
    subgraph UC[Unit Commitment]
        U1[Input Forecast Load and Unit Data] --> U2[Generate Candidate Schedules]
        U2 --> U3[Filter by Min Up Down and Reserve]
        U3 --> U4[For each schedule solve ED per hour]
        U4 --> U5[Compute Total Cost Fuel plus Start Up]
        U5 --> U6[Pick Minimum Cost Schedule]
    end
    U6 --> E1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The arrows in the UC block show that **UC repeatedly calls ED** as a sub-routine. This is the standard structure of every commercial UC solver (PLEXOS, PROMOD, GridView, GE MAPS).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Differentiate between Economic Dispatch and Unit Commitment. List any three points.*

**Model Answer:**

| # | Economic Dispatch | Unit Commitment |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Determines *how much* power each *online* unit should generate. | Determines *which* units should be ON/OFF at each hour, and *how much* power each ON unit should produce. |
| 2 | Static, single-period optimization. | Dynamic, multi-period optimization over 24 hours (or more). |
| 3 | Considers only fuel cost. | Considers fuel + start-up + shut-down costs. |

**(3 marks – 1 mark per valid point)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the equal-incremental-cost criterion for lossless economic dispatch. What modification is required when transmission losses are included?*

**Model Answer:**

For lossless ED with $N$ online units and total demand $P_D$:

$$\boxed{\frac{dF_i}{dP_i} = \lambda \quad \text{for all } i, \; P_{i,\min} \le P_i \le P_{i,\max}}$$

The system marginal cost $\lambda$ is unique and equal across all generating units not at a binding limit.

When transmission losses $P_L$ are included, the coordination equation is modified to:

$$\boxed{\frac{dF_i}{dP_i} = \lambda \left( 1 - \frac{\partial P_L}{\partial P_i} \right)}$$

or equivalently, $\dfrac{dF_i}{dP_i} \cdot L_i = \lambda$ where $L_i = \dfrac{1}{1 - \partial P_L/\partial P_i}$ is the *penalty factor* of unit $i$.

**(3 marks – 1 for lossless form, 1 for derivation/intuition, 1 for modified form)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 1]**

The fuel cost characteristics of three thermal plants are:

| Plant | $a_i$ (₹/h) | $b_i$ (₹/MWh) | $c_i$ (₹/MWh²h) | $P_{\min}$ | $P_{\max}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 2.0 | 0.0080 | 50 | 200 |
| 2 | 150 | 2.5 | 0.0090 | 80 | 300 |
| 3 | 200 | 1.8 | 0.0070 | 100 | 350 |
