**(a)** Neglecting transmission losses and generator limits, determine the economic dispatch for a total load of **600 MW** using the equal-incremental-cost criterion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Now include generator limits. If Plant 1's output is restricted to a maximum of 200 MW, recompute the optimal dispatch. Also compute the total minimum fuel cost. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Step 1 — Incremental cost of each plant:**

$$IC_1 = 2.0 + 0.016 P_1, \quad IC_2 = 2.5 + 0.018 P_2, \quad IC_3 = 1.8 + 0.014 P_3$$

**Step 2 — Set $IC_1 = IC_2 = IC_3 = \lambda$:**

$$P_1 = \frac{\lambda - 2.0}{0.016}, \quad P_2 = \frac{\lambda - 2.5}{0.018}, \quad P_3 = \frac{\lambda - 1.8}{0.014}$$

**Step 3 — Apply power balance $P_1 + P_2 + P_3 = 600$:**

$$62.5(\lambda - 2.0) + 55.556(\lambda - 2.5) + 71.429(\lambda - 1.8) = 600$$

$$189.485 \lambda = 600 + 125 + 138.89 + 128.57 = 992.46$$

$$\boxed{\lambda^{*} = 5.238 \; \text{₹/MWh}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute optimal outputs:**

$$P_1^{*} = \frac{5.238 - 2.0}{0.016} = 202.4 \; \text{MW}$$

$$P_2^{*} = \frac{5.238 - 2.5}{0.018} = 152.1 \; \text{MW}$$

$$P_3^{*} = \frac{5.238 - 1.8}{0.014} = 245.6 \; \text{MW}$$

**Sum check:** $202.4 + 152.1 + 245.6 = 600.1 \approx 600$ MW ✓

**Total fuel cost (no limits):**

$$F_1 = 100 + 2.0(202.4) + 0.008(202.4)^2 = 100 + 404.8 + 327.77 = 832.57 \; \text{₹/h}$$

$$F_2 = 150 + 2.5(152.1) + 0.009(152.1)^2 = 150 + 380.25 + 208.23 = 738.48 \; \text{₹/h}$$

$$F_3 = 200 + 1.8(245.6) + 0.007(245.6)^2 = 200 + 442.08 + 422.21 = 1064.29 \; \text{₹/h}$$

$$F_T = 832.57 + 738.48 + 1064.29 = 2635.34 \; \text{₹/h}$$

---

**Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Step 1 — Check feasibility:** $P_1 = 202.4$ MW > $P_{1,\max} = 200$ MW ⇒ **violation**.

**Step 2 — Fix $P_1 = 200$ MW.** Remaining load = $600 - 200 = 400$ MW for plants 2 and 3.

**Step 3 — Solve ED for plants 2 and 3 only:**

$$P_2 = \frac{\lambda - 2.5}{0.018}, \quad P_3 = \frac{\lambda - 1.8}{0.014}$$

$$55.556(\lambda - 2.5) + 71.429(\lambda - 1.8) = 400$$

$$126.985 \lambda - 138.89 - 128.57 = 400$$

$$126.985 \lambda = 667.46$$

$$\boxed{\lambda^{*} = 5.256 \; \text{₹/MWh}}$$

**Step 4 — Final dispatch:**

$$P_1 = 200 \; \text{MW (capped)}, \quad P_2 = \frac{5.256 - 2.5}{0.018} = 153.1 \; \text{MW}, \quad P_3 = \frac{5.256 - 1.8}{0.014} = 246.9 \; \text{MW}$$

**Sum:** $200 + 153.1 + 246.9 = 600$ MW ✓

**Step 5 — Total minimum cost:**

$$F_1 = 100 + 2.0(200) + 0.008(200)^2 = 820.0 \; \text{₹/h}$$

$$F_2 = 150 + 2.5(153.1) + 0.009(153.1)^2 = 743.71 \; \text{₹/h}$$

$$F_3 = 200 + 1.8(246.9) + 0.007(246.9)^2 = 1071.23 \; \text{₹/h}$$

$$\boxed{F_T^{*} = 820.0 + 743.71 + 1071.23 = 2634.94 \; \text{₹/h}}$$

**Note:** $F_T$ dropped from 2635.34 to 2634.94 ₹/h by respecting the limit. **(7 marks – 3 marks for setup, 2 marks for $\lambda$ computation, 2 marks for cost computation)**

---

**Question B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** Define Unit Commitment. List and explain **four major constraints** encountered in the UC problem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-unit system has the following hourly data. Use the **Priority List method** to commit units for hours 1 to 3.

| Hour | Load (MW) | Spinning Reserve (MW) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 400 | 50 |
| 2 | 700 | 80 |
| 3 | 1100 | 120 |

Unit data:

| Unit | $P_{\max}$ (MW) | $P_{\min}$ (MW) | Operating Cost at $P_{\max}$ (₹/h) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 400 | 100 | 5000 |
| 2 | 500 | 150 | 6000 |
| 3 | 600 | 200 | 7500 |

**(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Unit Commitment is the scheduling problem that determines the **on/off status and generation level of each thermal unit at each hour of the planning horizon** (typically 24 h), so as to **minimize the total operating cost** (fuel + start-up + shut-down) while satisfying forecasted load, spinning reserve, and unit physical constraints.

**Four Major Constraints:**

1. **Power balance (Demand constraint):** $\sum_i u_i(t) P_i(t) = P_D(t)$ — Generated MW must exactly match the load in every hour.

2. **Spinning reserve:** $\sum_i u_i(t) P_{i,\max} \ge P_D(t) + R(t)$ — Online capacity must exceed load by a reserve margin to cover outages/ramp events.

3. **Minimum up-time:** Once started, a unit cannot be de-committed for at least $T^{up}_i$ hours, due to boiler thermal stresses and start-up economics.

4. **Minimum down-time:** Once shut down, a unit cannot be re-started for at least $T^{down}_i$ hours, due to re-cooling requirements of the boiler/turbine.

*(Other acceptable constraints: ramp rate, crew constraints, fuel limits.)*

**(7 marks – 2 for definition, 1.25 per constraint)**

---

**Solution to Q-B(b): Priority List Method**

**Step 1 — Form the priority list by ranking units in order of *increasing* average cost at $P_{\max}$:**

Average cost at full load = (Total cost at $P_{\max}$) / $P_{\max}$:

- Unit 1: $5000 / 400 = 12.50$ ₹/MWh
- Unit 2: $6000 / 500 = 12.00$ ₹/MWh
- Unit 3: $7500 / 600 = 12.50$ ₹/MWh

**Priority order (cheapest first):** Unit 2 → Unit 1 → Unit 3.

**Step 2 — Commit units hour-by-hour:**

**Hour 1: $P_D = 400$, $R = 50$, total requirement = 450 MW.**

Try Unit 2 alone: $P_{\max,2} = 500$ MW $\ge 450$ MW ✓ Commit Unit 2. (We may also commit Unit 1 to supply 400 MW $\ge 450$? No, $400 < 450$ ✗ — must add another unit.)

Try Units 2 + 1: $500 + 400 = 900$ MW $\ge 450$ ✓ Commit both.

*Final Hour 1:* **Units 1 and 2 ON**, $P_1 + P_2 = 400$ MW.

**Hour 2: $P_D = 700$, $R = 80$, total = 780 MW.**

Units 1 + 2 ON: $900$ MW $\ge 780$ ✓ Sufficient — no need to start Unit 3.

*Final Hour 2:* **Units 1 and 2 ON**, $P_1 + P_2 = 700$ MW.

**Hour 3: $P_D = 1100$, $R = 120$, total = 1220 MW.**

Units 1 + 2 ON: $900$ MW $\not\ge 1220$ ✗ Must start Unit 3.

Units 1 + 2 + 3 ON: $400 + 500 + 600 = 1500$ MW $\ge 1220$ ✓

*Final Hour 3:* **All three units ON**, $P_1 + P_2 + P_3 = 1100$ MW.

**Step 3 — Final Commitment Schedule:**

| Hour | Unit 1 | Unit 2 | Unit 3 | Total Capacity (MW) | Load + Reserve (MW) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ON | ON | OFF | 900 | 450 |
| 2 | ON | ON | OFF | 900 | 780 |
| 3 | ON | ON | ON | 1500 | 1220 |

**(7 marks – 2 for priority list, 3 for hour-by-hour commitment, 2 for final schedule table)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to check generator limits** after the unconstrained $\lambda$-iteration solution. The most frequent deduction is **–2 marks** for not verifying $P_{i,\min} \le P_i \le P_{i,\max}$ and re-solving when a limit binds.
> 2. **Confusing lossless and lossy ED equations.** In lossy ED, the equation is $\frac{dF_i}{dP_i} = \lambda(1 - \partial P_L/\partial P_i)$, *not* $\frac{dF_i}{dP_i} = \lambda$. The penalty factor is **multiplied**, not added.
> 3. **Ignoring start-up cost in UC.** Many students consider only fuel cost in UC — UC's *defining feature* is the inclusion of start-up/shut-down costs and time-coupling.
> 4. **Mixing up UC and ED terminology.** "Economic dispatch decides which units to start" is **wrong**; that is UC. "Unit commitment decides output levels" is **wrong**; that is ED.
> 5. **No verification of spinning reserve** in UC problems — KTU examiners will deduct marks if you don't confirm $\sum P_{i,\max} \ge P_D + R$ in every hour.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **ED** is a *single-period*, *continuous*, *convex* optimization. **UC** is *multi-period*, *mixed-integer*, *non-convex*.
- **ED objective:** minimize $\sum F_i(P_i)$ subject to $\sum P_i = P_D$ and $P_{i,\min} \le P_i \le P_{i,\max}$.
- **ED key result (lossless):** $\frac{dF_i}{dP_i} = \lambda$ for all online units.
- **ED with losses:** $\frac{dF_i}{dP_i} \cdot L_i = \lambda$ where $L_i = \frac{1}{1 - \partial P_L/\partial P_i}$.
- **ED solution method:** Lambda iteration / gradient method / Newton's method.
- **UC objective:** minimize $\sum_t \sum_i [u_i(t) F_i + S_i \cdot \Delta u_i^{up} + S_{D,i} \cdot \Delta u_i^{dn}]$.
- **UC constraints:** power balance, spinning reserve, min up/down time, ramp rates, crew constraints.
- **UC methods:** Priority list, Dynamic Programming, Lagrangian Relaxation, MILP, Benders Decomposition, Genetic Algorithms.
- **B-coefficients:** $P_L = \sum \sum P_i B_{ij} P_j$; valid only for *quadratic loss approximation* around an operating point.
- **Hierarchical coupling:** UC calls ED as an inner sub-problem for every candidate hourly schedule.
- **Always check** generator limits and re-solve $\lambda$ if any $P_i$ is at a boundary.
- **KTU 2024 typical weightage:** Module 1 carries ~20–25% of the syllabus; ED/UC comparison is a **guaranteed 3-mark Part A question** and frequently a **14-mark Part B** topic.
- **Industrial relevance:** ED → AGC loop (seconds); UC → Day-ahead market (24 h horizon).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Unit Commitment (UC)

**Unit Commitment** is the process of determining the **ON/OFF status** (commitment schedule) of each generating unit in a power system for a scheduled time horizon (typically **24 hours**, divided into hourly intervals), while satisfying system load demand, spinning reserve requirements, and individual generator operational constraints, with the objective of **minimizing the total operating cost** (fuel cost + start-up cost + shutdown cost).

Mathematically, the UC problem can be stated as:

$$\min \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ F_i(P_{it}) \cdot U_{it} + S_{it} \cdot U_{it}(1-U_{i,t-1}) + SD_{it} \cdot U_{i,t-1}(1-U_{it}) \right]$$

Subject to:
- **Power balance:** $\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_{it} \cdot U_{it} = P_{Dt} + P_{Lt}$
- **Spinning reserve:** $\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_{i}^{max} \cdot U_{it} \geq P_{Dt} + R_t$
- **Generation limits:** $P_{i}^{min} \cdot U_{it} \leq P_{it} \leq P_{i}^{max} \cdot U_{it}$
- **Minimum up/down time constraints**
- **Ramp rate constraints**

Where:
- $U_{it}$ = 1 if unit *i* is ON at hour *t*, else 0
- $F_i(P_{it})$ = fuel cost function of unit *i*
- $S_{it}, SD_{it}$ = start-up and shut-down costs
- $P_{Dt}$ = system load demand at hour *t*
- $P_{Lt}$ = transmission losses
- $R_t$ = spinning reserve requirement
- $T$ = scheduling horizon (**24 hours**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Unit Commitment is a **Mixed-Integer Combinatorial Optimization** problem. The binary nature of $U_{it}$ makes it an **NP-hard** problem. Exact solutions become computationally infeasible beyond ~10 units. Hence, approximate/heuristic methods are emphasized in KTU examinations.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a **fleet manager of delivery trucks** for a 24-hour logistics company. Each truck has:
- A **fixed rental cost** (start-up cost)
- **Fuel efficiency** that varies with load
- A **minimum continuous usage rule** (cannot run for just 10 minutes)
- A **cooling-off rule** before reuse (minimum down time)
- A **maximum cargo capacity**

You must decide, **hour by hour**, which trucks to keep running so that:
1. Total cargo demand is met
2. A backup truck is always ready (spinning reserve)
3. Total fuel + rental cost is minimum

This is exactly the Unit Commitment problem — only with **generators**, **megawatts**, and **rupees**.

## 1.3 Solution Methods — Classification Overview

The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes the following major solution techniques:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Pillars of UC Solution Methods:**
> 1. **Priority List Method** — Heuristic (fast, approximate)
> 2. **Dynamic Programming (DP)** — Sequential optimization
> 3. **Lagrangian Relaxation (LR)** — Dual-based decomposition

| Method | Type | Accuracy | Speed | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Priority List | Heuristic | Low–Medium | **Very Fast** | Small systems, online decisions |
| Dynamic Programming | Numerical | High | Slow | Medium systems (≤ 10 units) |
| Lagrangian Relaxation | Mathematical | Medium–High | Moderate | Large systems (≥ 30 units) |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cost Function Components

### 2.1.1 Fuel Cost (Running Cost)
For unit *i*, the quadratic fuel cost is:

$$F_i(P_i) = a_i + b_i P_i + c_i P_i^2 \quad (\text{Rs/hour})$$

Where:
- $a_i$ = no-load cost (Rs/hr)
- $b_i$ = linear incremental cost (Rs/MWh)
- $c_i$ = quadratic incremental cost coefficient (Rs/MW²hr)

### 2.1.2 Incremental Fuel Cost
For economic dispatch, the derivative of $F_i(P_i)$:

$$\frac{dF_i}{dP_i} = b_i + 2 c_i P_i \quad (\text{Rs/MWh})$$

### 2.1.3 Start-Up Cost
**Hot Start** (unit was down for less than cold-start hours):

$$S_i^{hot} = H_{SC_i} \quad (\text{Rs})$$

**Cold Start** (unit was down for more than cold-start hours):

$$S_i^{cold} = C_{SC_i} \quad (\text{Rs})$$

Typically, $C_{SC_i} > H_{SC_i}$.

### 2.1.4 Shut-Down Cost
$$SD_{it} = \text{constant (Rs per shut-down event)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In many KTU problems, **shut-down cost is neglected** for simplicity, while start-up cost is included.

## 2.2 Constraints in Unit Commitment

### 2.2.1 System Constraints

**Power Balance Constraint:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_{it} \cdot U_{it} = P_{Dt} \quad (\text{for all } t = 1, 2, \ldots, T)$$

**Spinning Reserve Constraint:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_{i}^{max} \cdot U_{it} \geq P_{Dt} + R_t$$

### 2.2.2 Unit Constraints

**Generation Limits:**

$$P_i^{min} \cdot U_{it} \leq P_{it} \leq P_i^{max} \cdot U_{it}$$

**Minimum Up-Time (MUT):**

$$\sum_{\tau=t-T_i^{up}+1}^{t} U_{i\tau} \geq T_i^{up} \cdot (U_{it} - U_{i,t-1})$$

**Minimum Down-Time (MDT):**

$$\sum_{\tau=t-T_i^{down}+1}^{t} (1-U_{i\tau}) \geq T_i^{down} \cdot (U_{i,t-1} - U_{it})$$

**Ramp Rate Limits:**

$$P_{it} - P_{i,t-1} \leq UR_i \quad \text{(up-ramp)}$$

$$P_{i,t-1} - P_{it} \leq DR_i \quad \text{(down-ramp)}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fuel cost (quadratic) | $F_i = a_i + b_i P_i + c_i P_i^2$ | Rs/hour |
| 2 | Incremental cost | $IC_i = b_i + 2c_i P_i$ | Rs/MWh |
| 3 | Start-up cost (Hot/Cold) | $S_i^{hot} < S_i^{cold}$ | Rs |
| 4 | Power balance | $\sum P_{it} U_{it} = P_{Dt}$ | MW (equality) |
| 5 | Spinning reserve | $\sum P_i^{max} U_{it} \geq P_{Dt} + R_t$ | MW (inequality) |
| 6 | Generation limits | $P_i^{min} \leq P_{it} \leq P_i^{max}$ | MW |
| 7 | Full-Load Average Cost (FLAC) | $FLAC_i = \dfrac{F_i(P_i^{max})}{P_i^{max}}$ | Rs/MWh |
| 8 | Minimum Up-Time | $T_i^{up}$ (constant) | Hours |
| 9 | Minimum Down-Time | $T_i^{down}$ (constant) | Hours |
| 10 | DP Recursion | $F_n^*(P_n) = \min[FC_n(P_n) + F_{n-1}^*(P_{n-1})]$ | Rs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FLAC (Full-Load Average Cost)** is the *yardstick* for the Priority List Method. Units with **lower FLAC** are loaded first. This is the most frequently tested concept in KTU for the Priority List method.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In actual power system operation:
- **ISOs (Independent System Operators)** like PJM, CAISO, MISO run UC every day at 10 AM for the next 24 hours.
- The output directly drives the **Automatic Generation Control (AGC)** systems.
- A typical UC problem for 1000+ generators with 1-minute resolution requires advanced methods (LR, MILP, Benders Decomposition).
- **Cost savings** of even 0.5% translate to **crores of rupees annually** in a regional grid.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Method 1 — Priority List Method (PLM)

### 3.1.1 Concept

The Priority List Method constructs a **commitment order** of units based on their **Full-Load Average Cost (FLAC)**, then schedules them in that order while checking load + reserve constraints at every hour.

### 3.1.2 Derivation of FLAC

Starting from the quadratic fuel cost:

$$F_i(P_i) = a_i + b_i P_i + c_i P_i^2$$

The average cost per MW at full load ($P_i = P_i^{max}$) is:

$$FLAC_i = \frac{F_i(P_i^{max})}{P_i^{max}}$$

Substituting $P_i = P_i^{max}$:

$$FLAC_i = \frac{a_i + b_i P_i^{max} + c_i (P_i^{max})^2}{P_i^{max}}$$

$$FLAC_i = \frac{a_i}{P_i^{max}} + b_i + c_i P_i^{max} \quad (\text{Rs/MWh})$$

**Lower FLAC → higher priority → committed earlier.**

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Algorithm

**Step 1:** Compute $FLAC_i$ for all $N$ units using the formula above.

**Step 2:** Sort units in **ascending order of FLAC** → forms the priority list.

**Step 3:** Initialize hour $t = 1$.

**Step 4:** For each hour $t$, load units in priority order until:

$$\sum_{i \in committed} P_i^{max} \geq P_{Dt} + R_t$$

**Step 5:** Perform **Economic Dispatch** among committed units (using equal λ method or λ-iteration).

**Step 6:** Apply economic dispatch to find optimal $P_{it}$ for each committed unit.

**Step 7:** Move to hour $t+1$, repeat from Step 4.

**Step 8:** Check minimum up/down time constraints; adjust commitment if violated.

### 3.1.4 Numerical Illustration

**Given Data:**

| Unit | $a_i$ (Rs/h) | $b_i$ (Rs/MWh) | $c_i$ (Rs/MW²h) | $P_i^{min}$ (MW) | $P_i^{max}$ (MW) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1000 | 7.0 | 0.0080 | 100 | 500 |
| 2 | 950  | 7.5 | 0.0090 | 100 | 400 |
| 3 | 900  | 8.0 | 0.0070 |  50 | 300 |
| 4 | 850  | 8.5 | 0.0075 |  50 | 200 |

**Step 1: Compute FLAC for each unit**

$$FLAC_1 = \frac{1000}{500} + 7.0 + 0.0080 \times 500 = 2.00 + 7.0 + 4.00 = 13.00 \, \text{Rs/MWh}$$

$$FLAC_2 = \frac{950}{400} + 7.5 + 0.0090 \times 400 = 2.375 + 7.5 + 3.60 = 13.475 \, \text{Rs/MWh}$$

$$FLAC_3 = \frac{900}{300} + 8.0 + 0.0070 \times 300 = 3.00 + 8.0 + 2.10 = 13.10 \, \text{Rs/MWh}$$

$$FLAC_4 = \frac{850}{200} + 8.5 + 0.0075 \times 200 = 4.25 + 8.5 + 1.50 = 14.25 \, \text{Rs/MWh}$$

**Step 2: Priority List (ascending FLAC)**

| Priority | Unit | FLAC (Rs/MWh) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Unit 1 | 13.00 |
| 2 | Unit 3 | 13.10 |
| 3 | Unit 2 | 13.475 |
| 4 | Unit 4 | 14.25 |

**Step 3: Hour-wise Commitment (Assume $P_D = 750$ MW, $R = 50$ MW)**

Required generation + reserve = **800 MW**.

Cumulative capacity:
- After Unit 1: 500 MW → Insufficient
- After Unit 3: 500 + 300 = 800 MW → **Sufficient** ✓

So Units **1 and 3** are committed.

**Step 4: Economic Dispatch between Units 1 and 3**

Using equal incremental cost: $\lambda_1 = \lambda_3$

$$b_1 + 2c_1 P_1 = b_3 + 2c_3 P_3$$

$$7.0 + 0.016 P_1 = 8.0 + 0.014 P_3$$

$$0.016 P_1 - 0.014 P_3 = 1.0$$

With $P_1 + P_3 = 750$ MW:

$$P_3 = 750 - P_1$$

Substituting:

$$0.016 P_1 - 0.014(750 - P_1) = 1.0$$

$$0.016 P_1 - 105 + 0.014 P_1 = 1.0$$

$$0.030 P_1 = 106.0$$

$$P_1 = 3533.33 \text{ MW} \quad \text{(infeasible — exceeds } P_1^{max}\text{)}$$

This indicates Unit 3 alone may suffice. Let us verify:

$$P_1^{min} = 100, \quad P_3^{min} = 50$$

If $P_1 = 500$ (max), $P_3 = 250$ MW → feasible ($50 \leq 250 \leq 300$) ✓

So Unit 1 at max, Unit 3 balances: $P_1^* = 500$ MW, $P_3^* = 250$ MW.

Verify incremental costs:
- $\lambda_1 = 7.0 + 0.016 \times 500 = 15.0$ Rs/MWh
- $\lambda_3 = 8.0 + 0.014 \times 250 = 11.5$ Rs/MWh

Since $\lambda_1 > \lambda_3$, transfer load from Unit 1 to Unit 3 by raising Unit 3 to its max:
- $P_3 = 300$, $P_1 = 450$ → feasible
- $\lambda_1 = 7.0 + 0.016 \times 450 = 14.2$
- $\lambda_3 = 8.0 + 0.014 \times 300 = 12.2$

Still unequal. Unit 3 at max, so Unit 1 alone decreases:
- $P_1 = 450, P_3 = 300$ is the optimum within bounds.

**Optimal dispatch:** $P_1^* = 450$ MW, $P_3^* = 300$ MW.

## 3.2 Method 2 — Dynamic Programming (DP) Approach

### 3.2.1 Concept

DP solves UC by **decomposing** the 24-hour horizon into **sequential stages**. At each stage, a state represents the **cumulative capacity** or the **set of committed units**.

### 3.2.2 Forward DP Recursion

$$F_n^*(P_n) = \min_{P_n} \left[ FC_n(P_n) + F_{n-1}^*(P_{n-1}) + S_n(P_n - P_{n-1}) \right]$$

Where:
- $F_n^*(P_n)$ = minimum cost from stage 1 to stage $n$ with state $P_n$
- $FC_n(P_n)$ = fuel cost of running units to produce $P_n$
- $S_n$ = start-up cost transition term
- $P_n$ = state variable (e.g., number of committed units or total capacity)

### 3.2.3 DP Algorithm Steps

**Step 1:** Initialize. Stage $n = 1$, $F_0^* = 0$, and enumerate feasible states (combinations of units that can meet load $P_{D1}$ with reserve).

**Step 2:** For each feasible state at stage $n$, compute:
- Fuel cost (via economic dispatch on committed units)
- Start-up cost (if state changes from previous stage)
- Add the minimum cumulative cost from stage $n-1$.

**Step 3:** Track the optimal cumulative cost and the optimal path.

**Step 4:** Increment $n$ by 1, repeat.

**Step 5:** At the final stage, retrieve the optimal path as the commitment schedule.

### 3.2.4 State Space Definition

In the **truncated-state DP** (most common KTU form):
- States = $S_n \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, N\}$ where $S_n$ = number of committed units at hour $n$.
- Each state $S_n$ has a representative dispatch (e.g., top $S_n$ units at full load, or dispatched economically).

## 3.3 Method 3 — Lagrangian Relaxation (LR) — Conceptual Derivation

### 3.3.1 Idea

LR **duplicates the system constraints** (power balance and reserve) into the objective using Lagrange multipliers ($\lambda, \mu$), then decomposes the problem into $N$ independent sub-problems (one per unit).

### 3.3.2 Lagrangian Formulation

The Lagrangian of the UC problem:

$$\mathcal{L}(P, U, \lambda, \mu) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{t=1}^{T} [F_i(P_{it}) U_{it} + S_{it}] + \sum_{t=1}^{T} \lambda_t \left( P_{Dt} - \sum_i P_{it} U_{it} \right) + \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mu_t \left( P_{Dt} + R_t - \sum_i P_i^{max} U_{it} \right)$$

Rearranging:

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left[ F_i(P_{it}) - \lambda_t P_{it} - \mu_t P_i^{max} \right] U_{it} + \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left[ \lambda_t P_{Dt} + \mu_t (P_{Dt} + R_t) \right] + \sum_{i,t} S_{it}$$

### 3.3.3 Dual Function and Subgradient Update

The dual function is:

$$q(\lambda, \mu) = \min_{P, U} \mathcal{L}(P, U, \lambda, \mu)$$

The dual problem is:

$$\max_{\lambda, \mu \geq 0} q(\lambda, \mu)$$

The subgradient update rule for $\lambda_t$:

$$\lambda_t^{(k+1)} = \lambda_t^{(k)} + \alpha^{(k)} \left( P_{Dt} - \sum_i P_{it}^* U_{it}^* \right)$$

And for $\mu_t$:

$$\mu_t^{(k+1)} = \mu_t^{(k)} + \alpha^{(k)} \left( P_{Dt} + R_t - \sum_i P_i^{max} U_{it}^* \right)$$

Where $\alpha^{(k)}$ is a step size that diminishes with iteration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** For LR, you only need to **write the Lagrangian formulation** and explain the dual decomposition. Iterative subgradient updates are typically not numerically required in exam answers.

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Aspect | Priority List | Dynamic Programming | Lagrangian Relaxation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Methodology | Heuristic ranking | Sequential optimization | Dual decomposition |
| Optimality | Sub-optimal | Near-optimal | Near-optimal |
| Computation Time | $O(N \log N + NT)$ | $O(N^2 T)$ | $O(NT \cdot k)$ where $k$ = iterations |
| Scalability | Excellent | Limited (≤ 10 units) | Excellent (1000+ units) |
| Constraint Handling | Approximate | Exact | Exact (relaxed) |
| KTU Exam Frequency | **Very High** | **Very High** | Moderate |
| Typical Marks | 7–14 | 7–14 | 3–7 |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Overall UC Solution Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Load Forecast for 24 hours] --> inputA[Input: Generator Data, Costs, Limits]
    inputA --> methodChoice{Select Solution Method}
    methodChoice --> plm[Priority List Method]
    methodChoice --> dp[Dynamic Programming]
    methodChoice --> lr[Lagrangian Relaxation]
    plm --> flac1[Compute FLAC for all units]
    flac1 --> sort1[Sort Units by Ascending FLAC]
    sort1 --> sched1[Commit units per hour to meet Load + Reserve]
    sched1 --> ed1[Economic Dispatch among committed]
    ed1 --> output1[Output: Commitment + Dispatch Schedule]
    dp --> stateInit[Initialize State Space at Hour 1]
    stateInit --> recurse[DP Recursion across stages]
    recurse --> ed2[Economic Dispatch at each stage]
    ed2 --> optPath[Backtrack Optimal Path]
    optPath --> output2[Output: Optimal Commitment]
    lr --> lagForm[Formulate Lagrangian]
    lagForm --> decompose[Decompose into N sub-problems]
    decompose --> subgrad[Subgradient Iteration on lambda, mu]
    subgrad --> recon[Reconstruct Primal Feasible Solution]
    recon --> output3[Output: Near-Optimal Schedule]
    output1 --> validate[Validate MUT, MDT, Reserve]
    output2 --> validate
    output3 --> validate
    validate --> done[Final Hour-wise Unit Status and Dispatch]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Unit Commitment Problem
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Unit Commitment (UC) is the scheduling of generating units to determine their ON/OFF status over a scheduled time horizon (typically 24 hours, hourly intervals) such that the total operating cost (fuel + start-up + shut-down) is **minimized**, while satisfying load demand, spinning reserve, and individual generator constraints (generation limits, minimum up/down times, ramp rates).

Mathematically:

$$\min \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ F_i(P_{it}) U_{it} + S_{it} U_{it}(1-U_{i,t-1}) \right]$$

Subject to power balance, reserve, and unit-level constraints.

UC is a **combinatorial, mixed-integer, non-linear, non-convex** optimization problem classified as **NP-hard**.

---

### Question 2: What is FLAC and why is it used in Priority List Method?
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Full-Load Average Cost (FLAC)** is the average fuel cost per MW when a unit operates at its maximum capacity. It is given by:

$$FLAC_i = \frac{F_i(P_i^{max})}{P_i^{max}} = \frac{a_i}{P_i^{max}} + b_i + c_i P_i^{max}$$

**Units:** Rs/MWh.

**Why used:**
- A unit with low FLAC is more economical at full load.
- It serves as a **ranking index** for deciding which unit to commit first.
- The Priority List Method sorts units in **ascending FLAC** order, so cheaper units are committed first.
- It is **computationally trivial** to compute (no iterative algorithm required), making it ideal for fast, heuristic scheduling.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: Priority List Method with Numerical Computation

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

> Consider a power system with **three generating units** having the following data:

| Unit | $a_i$ (Rs/h) | $b_i$ (Rs/MWh) | $c_i$ (Rs/MW²h) | $P_i^{min}$ (MW) | $P_i^{max}$ (MW) | $S_i$ Start-up (Rs) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 800 | 7.5 | 0.0050 | 100 | 400 | 500 |
| 2 | 700 | 8.0 | 0.0060 |  80 | 350 | 400 |
| 3 | 600 | 8.5 | 0.0075 |  50 | 250 | 300 |

> System load for hour 10 is **700 MW** and spinning reserve is **50 MW**. Use **Priority List Method** to determine the commitment and economic dispatch.

#### Part (a) — Form the Priority List [7 Marks]

**Solution:**

Compute FLAC for each unit:

$$FLAC_1 = \frac{800}{400} + 7.5 + 0.0050 \times 400 = 2.00 + 7.5 + 2.00 = 11.50 \, \text{Rs/MWh}$$

$$FLAC_2 = \frac{700}{350} + 8.0 + 0.0060 \times 350 = 2.00 + 8.0 + 2.10 = 12.10 \, \text{Rs/MWh}$$

$$FLAC_3 = \frac{600}{250} + 8.5 + 0.0075 \times 250 = 2.40 + 8.5 + 1.875 = 12.775 \, \text{Rs/MWh}$$

**Priority List (Ascending FLAC):**

| Priority | Unit | FLAC (Rs/MWh) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Unit 1 | 11.50 |
| 2 | Unit 2 | 12.10 |
| 3 | Unit 3 | 12.775 |

#### Part (b) — Hour 10 Commitment & Economic Dispatch [7 Marks]

**Required generation:** $700 + 50 = 750$ MW.

Cumulative capacity:
- Unit 1 alone: 400 MW → insufficient
- Units 1+2: $400 + 350 = 750$ MW → **sufficient** ✓

So Units **1 and 2** are committed. Unit 3 is OFF.

**Economic Dispatch (equal λ):**

$$b_1 + 2c_1 P_1 = b_2 + 2c_2 P_2$$

$$7.5 + 0.010 P_1 = 8.0 + 0.012 P_2$$

$$0.010 P_1 - 0.012 P_2 = 0.5 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

Power balance: $P_1 + P_2 = 700$ → $P_2 = 700 - P_1$ ...(ii)

Substituting (ii) into (i):

$$0.010 P_1 - 0.012(700 - P_1) = 0.5$$

$$0.010 P_1 - 8.4 + 0.012 P_1 = 0.5$$

$$0.022 P_1 = 8.9$$

$$P_1 = 404.55 \, \text{MW}$$

This exceeds $P_1^{max} = 400$ MW, so cap at max: $P_1 = 400$ MW.

Then $P_2 = 700 - 400 = 300$ MW. Feasible ($80 \leq 300 \leq 350$) ✓.

**Optimal Dispatch:** $P_1^* = 400$ MW, $P_2^* = 300$ MW.

**Total Cost:**

$$F_1 = 800 + 7.5 \times 400 + 0.005 \times 400^2 = 800 + 3000 + 800 = 4600 \, \text{Rs/h}$$

$$F_2 = 700 + 8.0 \times 300 + 0.006 \times 300^2 = 700 + 2400 + 540 = 3640 \, \text{Rs/h}$$

$$\text{Total Fuel Cost} = 4600 + 3640 = 8240 \, \text{Rs/h}$$

Plus start-up costs (if units were OFF in the previous hour): assume hot start costs apply.

$$\text{Start-up Cost} = 500 + 400 = 900 \, \text{Rs (one-time)}$$

---

### Question B: Dynamic Programming Approach to Unit Commitment

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

> Consider **three generating units** to be scheduled over **3 hours**. The hourly loads are: $P_{D1} = 400$ MW, $P_{D2} = 700$ MW, $P_{D3} = 500$ MW. Spinning reserve is taken as **10%** of load. Use the **Dynamic Programming Method** with the **truncated state** approach to determine the optimal commitment. Each unit has $P^{max} = 250$ MW, $P^{min} = 100$ MW. Cost data is identical for all units: $F_i(P_i) = 500 + 8P_i + 0.01 P_i^2$ Rs/h. Start-up cost is **300 Rs** per unit.

#### Part (a) — Define States and Feasibility [7 Marks]

**Solution:**

**State variable:** $S_t$ = number of committed units at hour $t$.

**Possible states:** $S_t \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$.

**Feasibility check (must satisfy load + reserve):**

Reserve = 10% of load.
- Hour 1: load 400 MW, reserve 40 MW → need 440 MW capacity.
  - $S_1 = 1$: $250 \times 1 = 250 < 440$ → infeasible
  - $S_1 = 2$: $500 \geq 440$ → **feasible**
  - $S_1 = 3$: $750 \geq 440$ → feasible
- Hour 2: load 700 MW, reserve 70 MW → need 770 MW.
  - $S_2 = 2$: $500 < 770$ → infeasible
  - $S_2 = 3$: $750 \geq 770$ → **feasible** (marginal)
- Hour 3: load 500 MW, reserve 50 MW → need 550 MW.
  - $S_3 = 2$: $500 < 550$ → infeasible
  - $S_3 = 3$: $750 \geq 550$ → **feasible**

**Feasible states:**

| Hour | Feasible $S_t$ |
|---|---|
| 1 | 2, 3 |
| 2 | 3 |
| 3 | 2, 3 |

#### Part (b) — DP Recursion and Optimal Path [7 Marks]

**Fuel cost per unit (full load):**

$$F_i(250) = 500 + 8 \times 250 + 0.01 \times 250^2 = 500 + 2000 + 625 = 3125 \, \text{Rs/h}$$

Since all units are identical, total cost = (number of committed units) × 3125 Rs/h (assume full load).

**Start-up cost:** $S = 300$ Rs per unit turned ON.

**Stage costs (Fuel + start-up):**

| Transition | Description | Cost (Rs) |
|---|---|---|
| $0 \rightarrow 2$ units | Start 2 units | $2 \times 300 = 600$ |
| $0 \rightarrow 3$ units | Start 3 units | $3 \times 300 = 900$ |
| $1 \rightarrow 2$ units | Start 1 more | $300$ |
| $2 \rightarrow 3$ units | Start 1 more | $300$ |
| Same state (no start-up) | No cost | $0$ |

**DP Recursion:**

$$F_t^*(S_t) = \min_{S_{t-1}} \left[ F_{t-1}^*(S_{t-1}) + \text{Fuel cost at hour } t + \text{Start-up cost} \right]$$

**Stage 1 (Hour 1, feasible states: 2, 3):**

$F_1^*(2) = 2 \times 3125 = 6250$ Rs (assume no prior cost)

$F_1^*(3) = 3 \times 3125 = 9375$ Rs

**Stage 2 (Hour 2, only feasible state: 3):**

$F_2^*(3) = \min \left[ F_1^*(2) + \text{Fuel}_2(3) + \text{Startup}(2 \rightarrow 3) \right]$

$$= 6250 + 9375 + 300 = 15925 \, \text{Rs}$$

$F_1^*(3)$ cannot transition since $S_2 = 3$ only.

Optimal predecessor of $S_2 = 3$ is $S_1 = 2$.

**Stage 3 (Hour 3, feasible states: 2, 3):**

Case $S_3 = 2$:

$$F_3^*(2) = F_2^*(3) + \text{Fuel}_3(2) + \text{Startup}(3 \rightarrow 2)$$

Shutdown transition (3→2) does not incur start-up cost (units stay ON, just decommit one). 

$$F_3^*(2) = 15925 + 6250 + 0 = 22175 \, \text{Rs}$$

Case $S_3 = 3$:

$$F_3^*(3) = F_2^*(3) + \text{Fuel}_3(3) + \text{Startup}(3 \rightarrow 3)$$

$$F_3^*(3) = 15925 + 9375 + 0 = 25300 \, \text{Rs}$$

**Optimal final cost:**

$$F^* = \min(F_3^*(2), F_3^*(3)) = \min(22175, 25300) = 22175 \, \text{Rs}$$

**Optimal Path:**

| Hour | Units Committed |
|---|---|
| 1 | 2 units |
| 2 | 3 units |
| 3 | 2 units |

**Total Cost = Rs 22,175**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **FLAC formula miscalculation:** Students often forget to add $a_i / P_i^{max}$. KTU strictly awards partial credit for showing the full substitution.
> 2. **Spinning reserve inclusion:** Reserve MUST be added to load during commitment check. Missing reserve is the **#1 reason for full-mark loss** in priority list problems.
> 3. **Economic dispatch bounds:** After solving equal-λ, ALWAYS verify $P_i^{min} \leq P_i \leq P_i^{max}$. If violated, cap at the bound and re-dispatch.
> 4. **DP state enumeration:** Students forget that infeasible states (those not satisfying reserve) must be excluded BEFORE starting recursion. Including them creates paths with no merit but pollutes the optimal search.
> 5. **Unit-3 decommit in DP:** When transitioning from 3 units to 2 units, start-up cost is **zero** because the unit remains ON but is decommitted for dispatch purposes (no fresh start-up event). This is the most common conceptual error.
> 6. **Sign convention in Lagrangian update:** The subgradient step uses +α for power deficit (load > generation). Reversing signs gives invalid dual ascent.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Unit Commitment Solution Methods**

- **Unit Commitment** = Decide ON/OFF status of each generator over a 24-hour horizon to minimize total cost.
- The total cost includes **fuel cost + start-up cost + shut-down cost**.
- **Constraints:** Power balance (equality), spinning reserve (inequality), unit limits, minimum up/down time, ramp rates.
- **Priority List Method (PLM):**
  - Compute **FLAC** = $(a_i / P_i^{max}) + b_i + c_i P_i^{max}$.
  - Sort units **ascending by FLAC** → forms the priority list.
  - Commit units in priority order until load + reserve is met.
  - Dispatch via **equal incremental cost** rule.
  - **Pros:** Very fast, simple. **Cons:** Sub-optimal.
- **Dynamic Programming (DP):**
  - Decompose 24-hour problem into 24 stages.
  - State = number of committed units (or capacity level).
  - Recursion: $F_n^* = \min[\text{Fuel} + F_{n-1}^* + \text{Start-up}]$.
  - **Pros:** Near-optimal for small systems. **Cons:** Combinatorial explosion for large $N$.
- **Lagrangian Relaxation (LR):**
  - Dualize system constraints using $\lambda_t$ (power balance) and $\mu_t$ (reserve).
  - Decompose into $N$ independent unit-wise sub-problems.
  - Solve dual via **subgradient ascent**.
  - **Pros:** Scales to 1000+ units. **Cons:** Dual gap may exist; primal feasibility recovery needed.
- **FLAC** is the cornerstone for Priority List ranking — always compute and rank before committing.
- The committed set must satisfy: $\sum P_i^{max} \geq P_D + R$ at every hour.
- Economic Dispatch among committed units uses equal incremental cost: $\lambda = b_i + 2c_i P_i$ for all committed units.
- **Hot start cost < Cold start cost** (a unit recently shut down is cheaper to restart).
- After solving for $P_i$, always check feasibility: $P_i^{min} \leq P_i \leq P_i^{max}$.
- Minimum up-time $T^{up}$ and minimum down-time $T^{down}$ prevent excessive cycling.
- **Lagrangian subgradient step:** $\lambda \leftarrow \lambda + \alpha(\text{load} - \text{generation})$.
- KTU exams often test a **3-unit, 3-hour to 6-hour** PLM/DP problem — practice with full state enumeration.
- Always state the **objective function** explicitly before solving — KTU awards marks for formulation clarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Priority-List Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET741 - POWER SYSTEM OPERATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Priority-List Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Priority-List Method (PLM)** is a deterministic, heuristic scheduling technique used in **Unit Commitment (UC)** problems of power systems. In this approach, the available generating units are first ranked (ordered) in a **merit order** based on their **Average Full-Load Cost (AFLC)** or **Incremental Fuel Cost at full load**, and then committed in that order until the system load demand and spinning reserve requirements are satisfied.

Mathematically, the average full-load cost of unit *i* is given by:

$$AFLC_i = \frac{F_i(P_{i,\max})}{P_{i,\max}} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{Rs}}{\text{MWh}} \right]$$

where $F_i(P_{i,\max})$ is the total fuel cost of unit *i* when operating at its maximum capacity $P_{i,\max}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Priority-List Methods form the foundational introductory module of advanced optimization techniques (Dynamic Programming, Lagrangian Relaxation) studied later in the course. Expect 1 to 2 direct questions on this topic in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are a **home cook preparing dinner for 30 guests**. You have five stoves (generators), each running on a different fuel type — LPG, induction, firewood, electric coil, and solar backup. Each stove has a different cost-per-meal cooked. The smartest approach is to:

1. **Rank** all stoves from cheapest-cooking-cost to most expensive.
2. **Turn on** only the cheapest stoves first.
3. Keep adding more stoves (in that priority order) until all 30 guests' meals are covered.
4. If demand drops, **switch off** the most expensive stoves first (reverse priority).

**Priority-List Method does exactly this for power plants.** The cheapest plants (renewables, hydro, high-efficiency gas) are turned on first, and expensive peaking units (diesel, oil) are only used as a last resort. Since power cannot be stored economically in bulk, the grid must be ready to produce exactly the right amount at every hour — and PLM provides a fast, simple first-cut schedule for this.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Spinning Reserve Requirement:** Typically **7.5 % to 10 %** of the peak load, OR equals the capacity of the **largest single unit online**.
- **Minimum Up-Time ($UT_i$):** Minimum hours a unit must stay ON once started (e.g., **4 hours** for a thermal unit).
- **Minimum Down-Time ($DT_i$):** Minimum hours a unit must stay OFF once shut down (e.g., **4 hours**).
- **Ramp Rate Limits:** Maximum MW/min a unit can change output (typically **1 % to 5 %** of capacity per minute).
- **Start-up Cost ($ST_i$):** Cost to bring a unit from cold/hot state to synchronization, ranges from **₹50,000 to ₹5,00,000 per start** for thermal units.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme ESE problems, numerical values of $P_{\max}$, fuel cost coefficients ($a$, $b$, $c$), and reserve margins are usually provided. Read the question carefully — students often confuse *per-MW average cost* with *incremental cost* during valuation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Logic of Priority-List Method

The PLM is built upon **two sequential sub-problems** that must be solved in every hour *t* of the scheduling horizon:

1. **Unit Commitment (UC):** Decide the ON/OFF status ($u_i^t \in \{0,1\}$) of every generating unit.
2. **Economic Dispatch (ED):** Decide how much MW each committed unit should generate, subject to the load balance constraint.

The PLM collapses the UC problem into a simple **ranked list** based on AFLC, leaving ED to be solved via the classical **Lambda-Iteration (Equal Incremental Cost)** method.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logical Flow

1. **Input Data Acquisition:** For each unit *i*, collect the quadratic fuel cost function:
$$F_i(P_i) = a_i + b_i P_i + c_i P_i^2 \quad \left[ \text{Rs/hr} \right]$$
the operating limits $P_{i,\min}$ and $P_{i,\max}$, the start-up/shut-down costs, the minimum up/down times, and the load forecast $P_D^t$ for each hour.

2. **Compute Average Full-Load Cost (AFLC):** For each unit, evaluate the average cost at full load.
$$AFLC_i = \frac{a_i}{P_{i,\max}} + b_i + c_i P_{i,\max}$$

3. **Build the Priority List:** Sort all units in **ascending order of AFLC**. The unit with the lowest AFLC sits at the top of the list. This list is **fixed for the entire scheduling horizon** in the basic PLM.

4. **Hour-by-Hour Commitment:**
   - Start with all units OFF.
   - Beginning from the top of the priority list, commit units one by one.
   - After each commitment, check whether the cumulative available capacity satisfies the load **plus** the spinning reserve constraint.
   - Stop when both constraints are met for that hour.

5. **De-commitment (If over-generation):** If the committed capacity exceeds the requirement, de-commit the **last-committed (most expensive)** unit that is not required to satisfy the reserve.

6. **Economic Dispatch:** For the final committed set, solve the lambda-iteration problem:
$$\min \sum_{i=1}^{N} F_i(P_i) \quad \text{subject to} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i = P_D$$
   using the equal-incremental-cost condition:
$$\frac{dF_i}{dP_i} = \lambda \quad \Rightarrow \quad b_i + 2c_i P_i = \lambda$$

7. **Apply Time Coupling Constraints:** Verify minimum up-time, minimum down-time, and crew constraints; if violated, force-commit or force-de-commit the offending unit and repeat from Step 6.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Symbol Meaning | Units / Boundary |
|---|----------------|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | $F_i(P_i) = a_i + b_i P_i + c_i P_i^2$ | Quadratic fuel cost of unit *i* | Rs/hr |
| 2 | $IC_i(P_i) = \frac{dF_i}{dP_i} = b_i + 2c_i P_i$ | Incremental fuel cost | Rs/MWh |
| 3 | $AFLC_i = \frac{a_i}{P_{i,\max}} + b_i + c_i P_{i,\max}$ | Average full-load cost (priority metric) | Rs/MWh |
| 4 | $\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i^t = P_D^t$ | Power balance (load demand equality) | MW |
| 5 | $\sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i^t P_{i,\max} \geq P_D^t + SR^t$ | Spinning reserve inequality | MW |
| 6 | $P_{i,\min} \leq P_i^t \leq P_{i,\max}$ | Generator capacity limits | MW |
| 7 | $P_i^t - P_i^{t-1} \leq UR_i$ | Up-ramp rate limit | MW/hr |
| 8 | $P_i^{t-1} - P_i^t \leq DR_i$ | Down-ramp rate limit | MW/hr |
| 9 | $ST_i^{\text{hot}}$, $ST_i^{\text{cold}}$ | Hot / Cold start-up cost | Rs |
| 10 | $u_i^t \in \{0, 1\}$ | Binary commitment status (1 = ON, 0 = OFF) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The boundary condition for the spinning reserve is **NOT** a strict equality. Always use $\geq$ (greater than or equal to). Many students lose marks by writing $=$ instead of $\geq$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Day-Ahead Market Scheduling (DAM):** Indian Power Exchanges (IEX, PXIL) use PLM-like merit-order dispatch as the preliminary clearing mechanism.
- **National Load Despatch Centre (NLDC) / State LDCs:** PLM is used to pre-screen the unit schedule before running heavy optimization like Lagrangian Relaxation.
- **Industrial Cogeneration Plants:** Captive power plants use priority lists among their diesel/gas/turbine sets for internal load dispatch.
- **Microgrid Energy Management Systems (EMS):** PLM serves as the baseline controller in islanded microgrids before AI-based dispatch is applied.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Average Full-Load Cost (AFLC)

**Starting Point:** The total fuel cost function for a thermal unit *i* is given as a quadratic polynomial in active power output:

$$F_i(P_i) = a_i + b_i P_i + c_i P_i^2$$

where:
- $a_i$ = no-load cost (Rs/hr)
- $b_i$ = linear cost coefficient (Rs/MWh)
- $c_i$ = quadratic cost coefficient (Rs/MW²hr)

**Step 1 — Evaluate total cost at maximum loading:**

To find the AFLC, we substitute $P_i = P_{i,\max}$ into the cost function. This yields the **total hourly cost of running the unit at its nameplate capacity**.

$$F_i(P_{i,\max}) = a_i + b_i P_{i,\max} + c_i P_{i,\max}^2$$

**Step 2 — Divide by the maximum capacity:**

The average cost per MWh is obtained by dividing the total cost by the energy produced in one hour at full load, which is $P_{i,\max}$ MWh. This gives the merit-order metric.

$$AFLC_i = \frac{F_i(P_{i,\max})}{P_{i,\max}}$$

$$AFLC_i = \frac{a_i + b_i P_{i,\max} + c_i P_{i,\max}^2}{P_{i,\max}}$$

**Step 3 — Split the numerator term-by-term:**

Each term in the numerator is independently divided by $P_{i,\max}$ to expose the contributions of no-load, linear, and quadratic cost components.

$$AFLC_i = \frac{a_i}{P_{i,\max}} + \frac{b_i P_{i,\max}}{P_{i,\max}} + \frac{c_i P_{i,\max}^2}{P_{i,\max}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the algebra:**

The middle term cancels cleanly, and the last term reduces by one power of $P_{i,\max}$.

$$AFLC_i = \frac{a_i}{P_{i,\max}} + b_i + c_i P_{i,\max}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The no-load cost $a_i$ is **spread** across fewer MWh for a smaller unit, making small units *appear* expensive on AFLC even if their incremental cost is low. This is a known flaw of pure PLM, which motivates the use of incremental-cost ranking in some variants.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Economic Dispatch Sub-Problem (Lambda Method)

**Step 1 — Form the Lagrangian:**

The classical Economic Dispatch (ED) for *N* committed units minimizing total cost subject to a single equality constraint (power balance) uses the Lagrangian:

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} F_i(P_i) - \lambda \left( \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i - P_D \right)$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to each $P_i$:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial P_i} = \frac{dF_i}{dP_i} - \lambda = 0 \quad \forall i$$

This produces the famous **equal-incremental-cost condition**:

$$IC_i(P_i^*) = b_i + 2c_i P_i^* = \lambda$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the optimal dispatch of each unit:**

$$P_i^* = \frac{\lambda - b_i}{2c_i}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the power balance constraint:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i^* = P_D \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{\lambda - b_i}{2c_i} = P_D$$

**Step 5 — Isolate the system lambda ($\lambda$):**

$$\lambda = \frac{P_D + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{b_i}{2c_i}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{2c_i}}$$

**Step 6 — Check capacity limit violations:**

If $P_i^* < P_{i,\min}$, fix $P_i = P_{i,\min}$ and recompute $\lambda$ from the remaining units. If $P_i^* > P_{i,\max}$, fix $P_i = P_{i,\max}$ and recompute. This is the **iterative** part of the lambda method.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Style)

**Problem Statement:** Three units have the following data. Build the priority list, determine the committed set for a load of **700 MW** with spinning reserve = **largest unit online**, and dispatch economically.

| Unit | $P_{\min}$ (MW) | $P_{\max}$ (MW) | $a_i$ (Rs/hr) | $b_i$ (Rs/MWh) | $c_i$ (Rs/MW²hr) |
|------|-----------------|-----------------|---------------|----------------|-------------------|
| 1    | 50              | 200             | 1000          | 7.0            | 0.0080            |
| 2    | 80              | 300             | 1500          | 7.5            | 0.0090            |
| 3    | 100             | 400             | 2000          | 8.5            | 0.0070            |

**Step A — Compute AFLC for each unit:**

For Unit 1:

$$AFLC_1 = \frac{1000}{200} + 7.0 + 0.0080 \times 200 = 5.00 + 7.0 + 1.60 = 13.60 \; \text{Rs/MWh}$$

For Unit 2:

$$AFLC_2 = \frac{1500}{300} + 7.5 + 0.0090 \times 300 = 5.00 + 7.5 + 2.70 = 15.20 \; \text{Rs/MWh}$$

For Unit 3:

$$AFLC_3 = \frac{2000}{400} + 8.5 + 0.0070 \times 400 = 5.00 + 8.5 + 2.80 = 16.30 \; \text{Rs/MWh}$$

**Step B — Sort units by ascending AFLC:**

The resulting **priority list** is: **Unit 1 → Unit 2 → Unit 3**.

**Step C — Check commitment for 700 MW load:**

- Cumulative capacity with Unit 1 alone = **200 MW** (insufficient).
- Cumulative capacity with Units 1 + 2 = **500 MW** (insufficient).
- Cumulative capacity with Units 1 + 2 + 3 = **900 MW** (sufficient, with 200 MW margin).

All three units must be committed.

**Step D — Spinning reserve check:**

Largest unit online = **400 MW (Unit 3)**. Reserve required = 400 MW. Committed capacity (900 MW) − Load (700 MW) = **200 MW**. This is less than 400 MW, but the **priority-list method often uses % of load reserve**; assume 7.5 % of 700 = **52.5 MW** is also required. The 200 MW margin easily covers both. ✓

**Step E — Economic Dispatch (Lambda Iteration):**

All three units are within limits. Use the equal-IC condition:

$$P_i^* = \frac{\lambda - b_i}{2c_i}$$

Power balance:

$$\frac{\lambda - 7.0}{0.016} + \frac{\lambda - 7.5}{0.018} + \frac{\lambda - 8.5}{0.014} = 700$$

Let $A = \frac{1}{2c_i}$ values: $A_1 = 62.5$, $A_2 = 55.556$, $A_3 = 71.429$. Sum $A = 189.484$.

$$\sum \frac{b_i}{2c_i} = \frac{7.0}{0.016} + \frac{7.5}{0.018} + \frac{8.5}{0.014} = 437.5 + 416.667 + 607.143 = 1461.31$$

$$\lambda = \frac{700 + 1461.31}{189.484} = \frac{2161.31}{189.484} = 11.406 \; \text{Rs/MWh}$$

**Step F — Compute individual dispatches:**

$$P_1^* = \frac{11.406 - 7.0}{0.016} = 275.4 \; \text{MW} \quad (\text{clipped to } 200 \; \text{MW — violates } P_{\max}!)$$

Since Unit 1 exceeds $P_{\max} = 200$ MW, we **fix** $P_1 = 200$ MW and **re-solve** for Units 2 and 3 with the remaining load of $700 - 200 = 500$ MW.

New equation (only Units 2 and 3):

$$A_2 + A_3 = 55.556 + 71.429 = 126.985$$

$$\frac{b_2}{2c_2} + \frac{b_3}{2c_3} = 416.667 + 607.143 = 1023.81$$

$$\lambda = \frac{500 + 1023.81}{126.985} = 12.004 \; \text{Rs/MWh}$$

$$P_2^* = \frac{12.004 - 7.5}{0.018} = 250.2 \; \text{MW} \quad (\text{within } [80, 300] \; \text{OK})$$

$$P_3^* = \frac{12.004 - 8.5}{0.014} = 250.3 \; \text{MW} \quad (\text{within } [100, 400] \; \text{OK})$$

**Final Optimal Dispatch:**

$$P_1 = 200 \; \text{MW}, \quad P_2 = 250.2 \; \text{MW}, \quad P_3 = 250.3 \; \text{MW}$$

Total generation = **700.5 MW** (≈ 700 MW, within numerical tolerance).

**Total Fuel Cost:**

$$F_1 = 1000 + 7.0(200) + 0.0080(200)^2 = 1720 \; \text{Rs/hr}$$

$$F_2 = 1500 + 7.5(250.2) + 0.0090(250.2)^2 = 3390.2 \; \text{Rs/hr}$$

$$F_3 = 2000 + 8.5(250.3) + 0.0070(250.3)^2 = 4564.6 \; \text{Rs/hr}$$

$$F_{\text{total}} = 1720 + 3390.2 + 4564.6 = 9674.8 \; \text{Rs/hr}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Priority-List Method

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the entire algorithmic architecture — from raw data ingestion to final economic dispatch and time-coupling validation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Data Acquisition] --> A1[Fuel cost coefficients a b c]
    A --> A2[Generator limits Pmin Pmax]
    A --> A3[Startup cost and min up down times]
    A --> A4[Hourly load forecast PDt]
    A1 --> B[Step 1 Compute AFLC for every unit]
    A2 --> B
    A3 --> B
    A4 --> B
    B --> C[Step 2 Sort units in ascending AFLC]
    C --> D[Step 3 Build fixed merit order priority list]
    D --> E[Step 4 Hour by hour commitment loop]
    E --> F{Is cumulative capacity greater than or equal to PDt plus SRt}
    F -- No --> G[Commit next unit from priority list]
    G --> F
    F -- Yes --> H[Step 5 Run Economic Dispatch via lambda iteration]
    H --> I{Any unit violates Pmin or Pmax}
    I -- Yes --> J[Clip violating unit and re solve lambda]
    J --> I
    I -- No --> K[Step 6 Apply minimum up time and down time checks]
    K --> L{Any time coupling constraint violated}
    L -- Yes --> M[Force commit or force decommit violating unit]
    M --> H
    L -- No --> N[Step 7 Record final schedule and total cost for hour t]
    N --> O{All 24 hours of horizon covered}
    O -- No --> P[Increment t to t plus 1 hour]
    P --> E
    O -- Yes --> Q[Output complete 24 hour unit commitment and dispatch schedule]
```

## 4.2 Comparative Position of PLM Among UC Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Unit Commitment Solution Methods] --> R2[Exact Optimization]
    R1 --> R3[Heuristic Methods]
    R1 --> R4[Metaheuristic Methods]
    R2 --> S1[Dynamic Programming]
    R2 --> S2[Branch and Bound]
    R2 --> S3[Mixed Integer Linear Programming]
    R3 --> S4[Priority List Method]
    R3 --> S5[Lagrangian Relaxation]
    R4 --> S6[Genetic Algorithm]
    R4 --> S7[Particle Swarm Optimization]
    R4 --> S8[Simulated Annealing]
    S4 -.-> T1[Fast but Suboptimal]
    S5 -.-> T2[Slower but Near Optimal]
    S6 -.-> T3[Slowest but Globally Robust]
```

## 4.3 Logical State Diagram of a Unit in PLM

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OFF
    OFF --> STARTING : Commit from priority list
    STARTING --> ON : Synchronization complete
    ON --> ON : Continue operation respect Pmin Pmax
    ON --> COOLDOWN : De-commit reverse priority
    COOLDOWN --> OFF : Minimum down time elapsed
    ON --> FORCED_ON : Minimum up time not satisfied
    FORCED_ON --> COOLDOWN : Up time satisfied and load permits
    OFF --> FORCED_OFF : Minimum down time not satisfied
    FORCED_OFF --> OFF : Down time satisfied
    OFF --> [*]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the Priority-List Method used in unit commitment problems. What is the metric used to rank generating units?**

**Model Answer:**

The Priority-List Method is a heuristic scheduling technique in which available generating units are ranked in a fixed order of merit and committed sequentially to meet load demand plus spinning reserve.

The ranking metric is the **Average Full-Load Cost (AFLC)** defined as:

$$AFLC_i = \frac{a_i}{P_{i,\max}} + b_i + c_i P_{i,\max} \quad \text{in Rs per MWh}$$

Units with the lowest AFLC are committed first because they deliver energy most cheaply at full load. The list remains fixed across the entire 24-hour scheduling horizon in the basic PLM.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**State any two limitations of the Priority-List Method.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Sub-optimality:** Because the list is fixed, PLM cannot adapt to varying load levels. During off-peak hours, a large cheap unit is committed first even though a smaller expensive unit could serve the load at lower total cost.

2. **Ignores start-up costs:** PLM may frequently cycle units ON/OFF across hours without accounting for the significant start-up cost penalty, leading to an uneconomical daily schedule.

3. (Bonus) It does not handle ramp-rate constraints or minimum up/down time rigorously without manual intervention.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** A 3-unit system has the following data. Construct the priority list and determine the unit commitment status for a load demand of **550 MW** with spinning reserve equal to the capacity of the largest committed unit. **\[7 Marks\]**

| Unit | $P_{\min}$ | $P_{\max}$ | $a_i$ | $b_i$ | $c_i$ |
|------|------------|------------|-------|-------|-------|
| 1    | 50         | 150        | 700   | 6.8   | 0.0075 |
| 2    | 80         | 250        | 1000  | 7.2   | 0.0085 |
| 3    | 100        | 350        | 1800  | 8.0   | 0.0060 |

**(b)** For the committed units in part (a), perform the Economic Dispatch using the lambda-iteration method and compute the total fuel cost. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Priority List Construction:**

Compute AFLC for each unit using the formula:

$$AFLC_i = \frac{a_i}{P_{i,\max}} + b_i + c_i P_{i,\max}$$

For Unit 1:

$$AFLC_1 = \frac{700}{150} + 6.8 + 0.0075 \times 150 = 4.667 + 6.8 + 1.125 = 12.59 \; \text{Rs/MWh}$$

For Unit 2:

$$AFLC_2 = \frac{1000}{250} + 7.2 + 0.0085 \times 250 = 4.0 + 7.2 + 2.125 = 13.325 \; \text{Rs/MWh}$$

For Unit 3:

$$AFLC_3 = \frac{1800}{350} + 8.0 + 0.0060 \times 350 = 5.143 + 8.0 + 2.10 = 15.243 \; \text{Rs/MWh}$$

**Priority list (ascending AFLC): Unit 1 → Unit 2 → Unit 3**

**Cumulative capacity check for 550 MW load + reserve:**

- Step 1: Commit Unit 1 → 150 MW available. Need 550 + reserve. Insufficient.
- Step 2: Commit Unit 2 → 150 + 250 = 400 MW. Still insufficient.
- Step 3: Commit Unit 3 → 400 + 350 = 750 MW. Sufficient.

**Spinning Reserve Check:** Largest committed unit = Unit 3 = 350 MW. Reserve margin = 750 − 550 = **200 MW** < 350 MW. Since PLM may not strictly enforce this, we note the deficit but in standard textbook problems, all three units are **committed**.

**Part (b) — Economic Dispatch:**

Equal-IC condition:

$$b_i + 2c_i P_i = \lambda \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_i = \frac{\lambda - b_i}{2c_i}$$

Power balance:

$$\frac{\lambda - 6.8}{0.015} + \frac{\lambda - 7.2}{0.017} + \frac{\lambda - 8.0}{0.012} = 550$$

Compute the coefficients:

$$A_1 = 66.667, \quad A_2 = 58.824, \quad A_3 = 83.333, \quad \sum A = 208.824$$

$$B_1 = \frac{6.8}{0.015} = 453.33, \quad B_2 = \frac{7.2}{0.017} = 423.53, \quad B_3 = \frac{8.0}{0.012} = 666.67, \quad \sum B = 1543.53$$

$$\lambda = \frac{550 + 1543.53}{208.824} = \frac{2093.53}{208.824} = 10.026 \; \text{Rs/MWh}$$

Individual dispatches:

$$P_1 = \frac{10.026 - 6.8}{0.015} = 215.1 \; \text{MW} \quad (\text{exceeds } P_{1,\max} = 150; \text{ clip to } 150)$$

**Re-solve** with $P_1 = 150$ MW, remaining load = 400 MW, using only Units 2 and 3:

$$A_2 + A_3 = 58.824 + 83.333 = 142.157$$

$$B_2 + B_3 = 423.53 + 666.67 = 1090.20$$

$$\lambda = \frac{400 + 1090.20}{142.157} = 10.485 \; \text{Rs/MWh}$$

$$P_2 = \frac{10.485 - 7.2}{0.017} = 193.24 \; \text{MW} \quad (\text{within } [80, 250])$$

$$P_3 = \frac{10.485 - 8.0}{0.012} = 207.08 \; \text{MW} \quad (\text{within } [100, 350])$$

**Final Dispatch:** $P_1 = 150$ MW, $P_2 = 193.24$ MW, $P_3 = 207.08$ MW (Total ≈ 550.32 MW).

**Total Fuel Cost:**

$$F_1 = 700 + 6.8(150) + 0.0075(150)^2 = 700 + 1020 + 168.75 = 1888.75 \; \text{Rs/hr}$$

$$F_2 = 1000 + 7.2(193.24) + 0.0085(193.24)^2 = 1000 + 1391.33 + 317.55 = 2708.88 \; \text{Rs/hr}$$

$$F_3 = 1800 + 8.0(207.08) + 0.0060(207.08)^2 = 1800 + 1656.64 + 257.27 = 3713.91 \; \text{Rs/hr}$$

$$\boxed{F_{\text{total}} = 1888.75 + 2708.88 + 3713.91 = 8311.54 \; \text{Rs/hr}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step algorithm of the Priority-List Method for solving the unit commitment problem. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Discuss **four** major limitations of the Priority-List Method and briefly state how Dynamic Programming overcomes them. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step Algorithm:**

1. **Step 1 — Data Collection:** Collect fuel cost coefficients, generator limits, start-up costs, minimum up/down times, and the 24-hour load forecast.

2. **Step 2 — AFLC Computation:** For each unit *i*, compute the Average Full-Load Cost:
$$AFLC_i = \frac{a_i}{P_{i,\max}} + b_i + c_i P_{i,\max}$$

3. **Step 3 — Priority List Formation:** Sort all units in ascending order of AFLC. This list is the fixed merit order for the entire horizon.

4. **Step 4 — Hourly Commitment:** For each hour *t*, sequentially commit units from the top of the priority list until the cumulative committed capacity meets:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i^t P_{i,\max} \geq P_D^t + SR^t$$

5. **Step 5 — De-commitment if Over-committed:** If excess capacity is observed and reserve is over-satisfied, de-commit the most recently added (most expensive) unit.

6. **Step 6 — Economic Dispatch:** For the final committed set, apply the lambda-iteration method to share load optimally at equal incremental cost.

7. **Step 7 — Time-Coupling Verification:** Check minimum up-time and down-time constraints. If violated, force-commit or force-de-commit the affected unit and re-dispatch.

8. **Step 8 — Cost Aggregation:** Sum the fuel cost, start-up cost, and shut-down cost over all 24 hours to obtain the daily operating cost.

**Part (b) — Four Limitations and DP Solution:**

1. **Sub-optimal commitment due to fixed ranking:**
   The list is static, ignoring load-level variations. *DP solution:* Dynamic Programming enumerates all feasible ON/OFF combinations for the time horizon, guaranteeing the global optimum for the chosen stage size.

2. **Ignores start-up cost transitions:**
   PLM does not differentiate between hot start, warm start, and cold start costs, leading to frequent uneconomical cycling. *DP solution:* DP explicitly includes $ST_i^{\text{hot}}$, $ST_i^{\text{warm}}$, $ST_i^{\text{cold}}$ as state-dependent costs.

3. **Cannot rigorously enforce minimum up/down times:**
   PLM requires manual override and re-iteration when these constraints are violated. *DP solution:* DP embeds the time-coupling constraint directly into the state space, automatically forbidding infeasible transitions.

4. **Computationally expensive for large systems via manual overrides:**
   The iterative re-commitment loop becomes cumbersome with many units. *DP solution:* DP's forward dynamic programming (priority-list enhanced DP) reduces the state space drastically, making it tractable for systems with up to 100 units.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** $AFLC_i$ (used for commitment ranking) with $IC_i = b_i + 2c_i P_i$ (used for dispatch). Mixing these two in numerical problems is the **#1 cause of lost marks** in KTU UC papers.
> 2. **Always clip** generators to $P_{\max}$ and re-solve $\lambda$ if any unit exceeds its limit. A one-shot lambda computation that yields $P_i > P_{i,\max}$ will fetch **zero marks** for the ED sub-question.
> 3. For spinning reserve questions, write the inequality with $\geq$ symbol, not $=$.
> 4. In part (a) of Question A type, **show the AFLC computation in a separate small table** — examiners award step-marks generously for tabular clarity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **PLM is a heuristic**, not an optimizer; it is fast but **sub-optimal**.
- **Ranking metric:** Average Full-Load Cost (AFLC), not incremental cost.
- **AFLC formula:** $AFLC_i = \frac{a_i}{P_{i,\max}} + b_i + c_i P_{i,\max}$ — memorize this.
- **Spinning reserve** is satisfied via $\geq$ (greater than or equal to), and is typically 7.5–10 % of load OR equal to the largest unit.
- **ED sub-problem** uses the **equal incremental cost (lambda) condition**: $b_i + 2c_i P_i = \lambda$.
- **If $P_i > P_{i,\max}$** in ED, fix it at $P_{i,\max}$ and **recompute $\lambda$** — this is non-negotiable.
- **Time-coupling constraints** (min up/down time, start-up cost) are **not handled** natively by PLM and need manual checks.
- **PLM is the precursor** to **Priority-List Dynamic Programming (PLDP)** studied in Module 2 of this course.
- **Practical use case:** Indian Power Exchange (IEX) day-ahead market clearing uses a similar merit-order framework.
- **Examiner expects:** Tabular AFLC computation, sorted list, capacity check, ED with lambda iteration, and final cost.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Constrained Unit Commitment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET741 - POWER SYSTEM OPERATION AND CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Security Constrained Unit Commitment # Security Constrained Unit Commitment (SCUC) ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Security Constrained Unit Commitment (SCUC)** is an advanced extension of the classical Unit Commitment (UC) problem in power system operation that determines the optimal on/off status and generation levels of available generating units over a scheduling horizon (typically 24 hours to 1 week), **subject to both operational (system-wide) constraints and security (contingency) constraints**. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, SCUC integrates the economic dispatch of generating units with **N-1 security criteria** and **transmission network constraints**, ensuring that the committed schedule is feasible not only in the normal (intact network) state but also under any credible contingency such as the outage of a generator, transmission line, or transformer. Mathematically, SCUC is formulated as a **large-scale, mixed-integer, non-linear optimization problem** with: - **Binary decision variables** $u_i(t) \in \{0,1\}$ for unit start-up/shut-down status - **Continuous decision variables** $P_i(t)$ for power output levels - **Coupling security constraints** derived from network sensitivity factors > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** SCUC is the industry-standard formulation used by all Independent System Operators (ISOs) such as PJM Interconnection, CAISO, ERCOT, and India's **NLDC/POSOCO** for day-ahead and real-time market clearing. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are the **head chef of a large hotel kitchen** preparing breakfast for 1,000 guests over the next 24 hours. You must: 1. **Decide which ovens (generators) to turn ON** at which hours (Unit Commitment). 2. **Decide how much each oven will cook** (Economic Dispatch). 3. **Ensure backup plans** — if one oven suddenly fails (contingency), there should be enough cooking capacity in the *already-running* ovens to handle the load (Spinning Reserve). 4. **Respect kitchen pipeline capacity** — you cannot route all food through a single serving line (Transmission Limits). **SCUC is the kitchen manager's plan that handles (1) through (4) simultaneously**, ensuring no blackout occurs even when something unexpected breaks. | KTU Term | Kitchen Analogy | |---|---| | Generator Unit | Cooking Oven | | Unit Commitment | Oven ON/OFF schedule | | Economic Dispatch | Cooking load per oven | | Spinning Reserve | Idle-but-hot backup ovens | | Contingency | Sudden oven failure | | Transmission Limit | Pipeline / serving line capacity | | N-1 Criterion | Plan survives any single failure | > [!NOTE] > **Key Standard Metric:** The **N-1 Security Criterion** requires that the loss of any *single* major component (generator, line, transformer) must NOT cause system-wide load shedding. This is enforced by the **NERC TPL-001-4** and **CEA Grid Standards** (India). ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Base Power:** $S_{base} = 100 \text{ MVA}$ (standard in KTU problems) - **Frequency:** $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$ (India, Europe) / $60 \text{ Hz}$ (USA) - **Spinning Reserve Margin:** Typically **5% to 7%** of peak load OR **largest online unit** + 5%, whichever is greater - **Ramp Rate:** $R_i$ in **MW/min** (KTU convention: usually reported as MW/h) - **Minimum Up-Time / Down-Time:** Given in **hours** - **Startup Cost:** ₹ / MW (or in monetary units per start) # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Problem Architecture SCUC is built on **three coupled layers** of decision-making: ### Layer 1 — Commitment (Binary) Decides $u_i(t) \in \{0,1\}$ — which unit is ON at hour $t$. ### Layer 2 — Dispatch (Continuous) Decides $P_i(t)$ — power output of committed unit. ### Layer 3 — Security (Coupling) Enforces that the Layer 1 + Layer 2 decision survives every credible **N-1 contingency** $k \in K$. ## 2.2 SCUC vs Conventional UC — What's New? | Feature | Conventional UC | Security Constrained UC (SCUC) | |---|---|---| | System Intact? | Yes | Yes | | Reserve Type | Spinning only | Spinning + Operating + Contingency | | Network Limits | Ignored (single-bus) | Enforced via DC/AC power flow | | Generator Outage | Not modelled | Explicitly modelled as contingency | | Line Outage | Not modelled | Explicitly modelled | | Complexity | NP-hard mixed integer | NP-hard, much larger instance | | Solver | DP / LR | Benders Decomposition / MILP | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why SCUC exists:** The August 2003 North-East Blackout (USA) and July 2012 India blackout demonstrated that **economically optimal but insecure schedules** cause cascading failures. SCUC is the mathematical fix. ## 2.3 Mathematical Formulation of SCUC ### Objective Function Minimize total operating cost: $$\min \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{i=1}^{N_g} \left[ u_i(t) \cdot F_i(P_i(t)) + S_i(t) \cdot u_i(t)(1-u_i(t-1)) + S_{i0} \cdot u_i(t-1)(1-u_i(t)) \right]$$ Where: - $F_i(P_i(t)) = a_i + b_i P_i(t) + c_i P_i^2(t)$ — fuel cost (quadratic) - $S_i(t)$ — hot start-up cost - $S_{i0}$ — cold start-up cost - $u_i(t)$ — binary commitment variable - $T = 24$ hours typically ### Constraints — System Level **Power Balance (Normal State):** $$\sum_{i=1}^{N_g} u_i(t) P_i(t) = P_D(t), \quad \forall t$$ **Spinning Reserve Requirement (Normal):** $$\sum_{i=1}^{N_g} u_i(t) \overline{P_i} \geq P_D(t) + R(t), \quad \forall t$$ **Power Balance (Under Contingency k):** $$\sum_{i=1}^{N_g} u_i(t) P_i^k(t) = P_D(t) - P_k^{lost}, \quad \forall t, \forall k \in K$$ **Spinning Reserve (Under Contingency k):** $$\sum_{i=1}^{N_g} u_i(t) \overline{P_i} \geq P_D(t) - P_k^{lost} + R(t), \quad \forall t, \forall k \in K$$ ### Constraints — Unit Level **Generation Limits:** $$u_i(t) \cdot \underline{P_i} \leq P_i(t) \leq u_i(t) \cdot \overline{P_i}$$ **Ramp Rate Limits:** $$P_i(t) - P_i(t-1) \leq RU_i \cdot u_i(t-1) + \underline{P_i} \cdot (u_i(t) - u_i(t-1)) + \overline{P_i} \cdot (1 - u_i(t))$$ **Minimum Up-Time:** $$\sum_{\tau=t-UT_i+1}^{t} u_i(\tau) \geq UT_i \cdot [u_i(t) - u_i(t-1)]$$ **Minimum Down-Time:** $$\sum_{\tau=t-DT_i+1}^{t} [1-u_i(\tau)] \geq DT_i \cdot [u_i(t-1) - u_i(t)]$$ ### Network Security Constraints (DC Power Flow) **Line Flow Limit (Normal):** $$\vert \sum_{i=1}^{N_g} \alpha_{l,i} P_i(t) - \sum_{j=1}^{N_d} \alpha_{l,j} P_{D_j}(t) \vert \leq \overline{F_l}$$ **Line Flow Limit (Contingency k):** $$\vert \sum_{i \neq i_k} \alpha_{l,i} P_i^k(t) - \sum_{j=1}^{N_d} \alpha_{l,j} P_{D_j}(t) \vert \leq \overline{F_l} \cdot \gamma_k$$ Where $\alpha_{l,i}$ are the **Power Transfer Distribution Factors (PTDF)** and $\gamma_k$ is the **contingency derating factor** (typically 1.0 for short-term emergencies). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Meaning | Unit | Constraint Type | |---|---|---|---| | $u_i(t)$ | Unit $i$ ON/OFF at hour $t$ | binary $\{0,1\}$ | Commitment | | $P_i(t)$ | Output of unit $i$ at hour $t$ | MW | Dispatch | | $F_i(\cdot)$ | Fuel cost function | ₹/h | Objective | | $S_i(t), S_{i0}$ | Hot / cold start-up cost | ₹ | Objective | | $\underline{P_i}, \overline{P_i}$ | Min / max output | MW | Unit limit | | $RU_i, RD_i$ | Ramp up / down rate | MW/h | Unit dynamics | | $UT_i, DT_i$ | Min up / down time | h | Unit dynamics | | $R(t)$ | Reserve at hour $t$ | MW | System security | | $\alpha_{l,i}$ | PTDF of line $l$ w.r.t bus $i$ | per unit | Network security | | $K$ | Set of credible contingencies | set | Security | | $\lambda(t)$ | System marginal price (LMP) | ₹/MWh | Dual variable | | $B_{ij}$ | Loss coefficient | 1/MW | B-matrix | | $\gamma_k$ | Contingency derating factor | per unit | Security | | $S_{base}$ | Base MVA | 100 MVA | Normalization | ## 2.5 Engineering Utility - **Day-Ahead Market Clearing** — ISO schedules 24-h generation respecting N-1 - **Real-Time Security Assessment** — adjusts dispatch to keep system secure - **Resource Adequacy Planning** — long-term capacity built to meet N-1 - **Renewable Integration** — SCUC now includes stochastic scenarios for wind/solar uncertainty # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Power Transfer Distribution Factor (PTDF) The **PTDF** $\alpha_{l,i}$ measures the change in active power flow on line $l$ for a unit change in injection at bus $i$ (with reference bus balancing). **Step 1 — Start with the DC Power Flow Equation:** $$P = B \cdot \theta$$ Where $P$ is the bus power injection vector, $B$ is the network susceptance matrix, and $\theta$ is the bus voltage angle vector. **Step 2 — Solve for Angles:** $$\theta = B^{-1} \cdot P$$ **Step 3 — Power Flow on Line $l$ between buses $m$ and $n$:** $$F_l = \frac{\theta_m - \theta_n}{x_l} = \frac{1}{x_l} (e_m^T - e_n^T) B^{-1} P$$ **Step 4 — Differentiate w.r.t. Injection at Bus $i$:** $$\alpha_{l,i} = \frac{\partial F_l}{\partial P_i} = \frac{1}{x_l} (e_m^T - e_n^T) B^{-1} e_i$$ where $e_i$ is the $i$-th standard basis vector. **Step 5 — Compact Form:** $$\alpha_{l,i} = \frac{1}{x_l} \left[ B^{-1} \right]_{m,i} - \left[ B^{-1} \right]_{n,i}$$ > [!NOTE] > **PTDF Interpretation:** If generator at bus $i$ injects 1 MW, line $l$ carries $\alpha_{l,i}$ MW. PTDFs are **dimensionless** and sum to zero across all buses (with a slack reference). ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Lagrange Multiplier for System Lambda The system marginal price (SMP) $\lambda(t)$ emerges as the **dual variable** of the power balance constraint. Starting from Lagrangian: $$L = \sum_i F_i(P_i) + \lambda(t) \left[ P_D(t) - \sum_i P_i(t) \right]$$ Taking the **first-order optimality condition** (KKT): $$\frac{\partial L}{\partial P_i} = \frac{dF_i}{dP_i} - \lambda(t) = 0$$ For quadratic cost $F_i(P_i) = a_i + b_i P_i + c_i P_i^2$: $$\frac{dF_i}{dP_i} = b_i + 2c_i P_i = \lambda(t)$$ Solving for optimal dispatch: $$P_i^*(t) = \frac{\lambda(t) - b_i}{2c_i}$$ This is the **coordination equation** of economic dispatch, with $\lambda(t)$ found from: $$\sum_{i \in C(t)} P_i^*(t) = P_D(t) \implies \sum_{i \in C(t)} \frac{\lambda(t) - b_i}{2c_i} = P_D(t)$$ Solving for $\lambda(t)$: $$\lambda(t) = \frac{2 P_D(t) + \sum_{i \in C(t)} \frac{b_i}{c_i}}{\sum_{i \in C(t)} \frac{1}{c_i}}$$ where $C(t)$ is the set of committed units at hour $t$. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Spinning Reserve for N-1 Security For a system of $N_g$ committed units with capacities $\overline{P_i}$, the **largest-unit contingency reserve** requirement is: $$R_{N-1}(t) = \max_{i \in C(t)} \overline{P_i} + \alpha_{margin} \cdot P_D(t)$$ Total available spinning reserve: $$R_{avail}(t) = \sum_{i \in C(t)} \left[ \overline{P_i} - P_i(t) \right]$$ For **N-1 security** under all contingencies $k$: $$R_{avail}(t) \geq R_{N-1}(t), \quad \forall t$$ If the unit $i_k$ is lost in contingency $k$: $$P_D(t) \leq \sum_{i \neq i_k} \overline{P_i} \quad \text{(capacity adequacy)}$$ And ramp constraints must allow pick-up within **10 minutes** (primary reserve response per Indian Electricity Grid Code). ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Benders Decomposition for SCUC Since SCUC is computationally intractable as a single MILP, **Benders Decomposition** splits it into: - **Master Problem:** Unit commitment (binary) — decides $u_i(t)$ - **Subproblem:** Economic dispatch + security check — for fixed $u_i(t)$ **Master Objective:** $$\min_{u} \sum_{t,i} \left[ u_i(t) F_i(\tilde{P_i}) + S_i(t) \right] + \alpha$$ Subject to: unit constraints, where $\alpha$ is the Benders cut variable. **Subproblem (for fixed $u^*$):** $$\min_{P, \theta, F} \sum_{t,i} u_i^*(t) F_i(P_i(t))$$ Subject to: power balance, line limits, contingency constraints. **Benders Cut Generated:** If subproblem is infeasible: $$\alpha \geq \pi^T (A u - b)$$ where $\pi$ is the **dual of the violated constraint**. Master problem is re-solved with this cut added. Iterates until convergence. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Benders Cut Logic:** Each iteration *learns* a new contingency violation, and the master problem is told "do not commit such-and-such pattern that violates this constraint." This makes SCUC tractable for **thousands of buses** and **millions of contingencies**. ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** 3-unit system, 2-hour horizon, load: $P_D(1) = 400$ MW, $P_D(2) = 700$ MW. **Unit Data:** | Unit | $\overline{P_i}$ (MW) | $\underline{P_i}$ (MW) | $a_i$ (₹/h) | $b_i$ (₹/MWh) | $c_i$ (₹/MW²h) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 400 | 100 | 1000 | 8.0 | 0.0050 | | 2 | 300 | 80 | 800 | 7.5 | 0.0060 | | 3 | 200 | 50 | 600 | 7.0 | 0.0075 | **Reserve requirement:** Largest online unit + 5% of load. **Hour 1 (Load 400 MW):** Likely Units 1 + 2 = 700 MW capacity, sufficient. - Constraint: $\overline{P_1} + \overline{P_2} = 700 \geq 400 + 0.05(400) + 300 = 720$ → INFEASIBLE - Solution: Commit Units 1 + 2 + 3 (cap = 900 MW), reserve = 500 MW ✓ **Hour 2 (Load 700 MW):** Units 1 + 2 + 3, cap = 900 MW, reserve = 200 MW. - Need: $700 + 0.05(700) + 300 = 1035$ → still tight. - Optimal dispatch via lambda: $$\lambda(2) = \frac{2(700) + \frac{8.0}{0.005} + \frac{7.5}{0.006} + \frac{7.0}{0.0075}}{\frac{1}{0.005} + \frac{1}{0.006} + \frac{1}{0.0075}}$$ Computing numerator: $$1400 + 1600 + 1250 + 933.33 = 5183.33$$ Denominator: $$200 + 166.67 + 133.33 = 500$$ $$\lambda(2) = 5183.33 / 500 = 10.367 \text{ ₹/MWh}$$ Optimal generation: $$P_1^* = \frac{10.367 - 8.0}{2(0.005)} = 236.7 \text{ MW}$$ $$P_2^* = \frac{10.367 - 7.5}{2(0.006)} = 239.0 \text{ MW}$$ $$P_3^* = \frac{10.367 - 7.0}{2(0.0075)} = 224.4 \text{ MW}$$ Verification: $236.7 + 239.0 + 224.4 = 700.1 \approx 700$ MW ✓ (small rounding) > [!NOTE] > All units are within their limits (Pmin ≤ P* ≤ Pmax), so the dispatch is feasible. **N-1 check:** If unit 1 trips, remaining cap = 500 MW, shortfall = 200 MW → not N-1 secure. Need additional unit or demand response. # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 SCUC Functional Block Architecture The following Mermaid block diagram shows the architecture of a typical SCUC implementation, mapping the interactions between the commitment solver, dispatch solver, and security analyzer. ```mermaid flowchart TD inputA[Input Data:
Load Forecast] --> masterA inputB[Unit Parameters:
Cost and Limits] --> masterA inputC[Network Topology:
B-matrix] --> masterA inputD[Contingency List:
N-1 Set] --> masterA masterA[Master Problem:
MILP Unit Commitment
Decision: u_i_t] masterA --> cutGen[Benders Cut Generator] cutGen --> masterA masterA --> subNode subNode[Subproblem:
Economic Dispatch
+ DC Power Flow] subNode --> chk1{Feasible?} chk1 -- Yes --> optimal[Optimal SCUC Solution] chk1 -- No --> cutGen subNode --> secChk[Security Analyzer:
PTDF-based N-1 Check] secChk --> chk1 optimal --> out1[Day-Ahead Schedule] optimal --> out2[LMP at each bus] optimal --> out3[Commitment Pattern] optimal --> out4[Reserve Allocation] ``` ## 4.2 SCUC Decision Flow Sequence ```mermaid flowchart LR start([Start]) --> load[Load Forecast P_D_t] load --> uc[Conventional UC
Priority Order] uc --> econ[Economic Dispatch] econ --> secA{Line Limits OK?} secA -- No --> bump[Bump Generation
at Slack Bus] secA -- Yes --> secB{Contingency N-1 OK?} secB -- No --> add[Add Constraint
to Benders Master] add --> uc secB -- Yes --> final[Commit Final Schedule] final --> out([Output]) bump --> secA ``` ## 4.3 Three-Layer Optimization Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB Layer1[Layer 1: Commitment Layer
Binary u_i_t ∈ 0,1
Decide ON/OFF schedule] Layer2[Layer 2: Dispatch Layer
Continuous P_i_t
Decide MW output] Layer3[Layer 3: Security Layer
Contingency k in K
Enforce N-1 feasibility] Layer1 -- sends u to --> Layer2 Layer2 -- sends P to --> Layer3 Layer3 -- feasibility feedback --> Layer1 Layer2 -- cost feedback --> Layer1 Layer1 --> obj1[Minimize Total Cost] Layer2 --> obj1 Layer3 --> obj1 ``` ## 4.4 Data Flow in Benders Decomposition ```mermaid flowchart TB init[Initialize:
u_i_t = 0] --> solveM[Solve Master Problem] solveM --> fixM[Fix u_i_t = u_hat] fixM --> solveS[Solve Subproblem:
Minimize dispatch cost] solveS --> chkS{Subproblem
Feasible?} chkS -- No --> genCut[Generate Infeasibility Cut] chkS -- Yes --> chkOpt{Optimal
Reached?} chkOpt -- No --> genOpt[Generate Optimality Cut] chkOpt -- Yes --> done([Final Solution]) genCut --> solveM genOpt --> solveM ``` > [!NOTE] > The above block diagrams map the **functional architecture** of SCUC solvers as typically deployed in modern ISO Energy Management Systems (EMS). Each block corresponds to a software module — for example, PJM's e-terra platform uses analogous modules. # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: Define Security Constrained Unit Commitment.
