# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the role of the State Estimator in an Energy Management System (EMS).**

**Model Answer (3 points expected):**

1. The **State Estimator** sits between the SCADA/RTU data acquisition layer and the higher-level security applications. It receives **raw, noisy real-time measurements** (MW, MVAR, kV, Amp) along with the **network topology** model.
2. It applies the **Weighted Least Squares (WLS)** algorithm to produce a **statistically optimal, complete, and consistent** real-time snapshot of all bus voltage magnitudes and angles — the *state vector* $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$.
3. The output feeds the **Security Analysis / Contingency Ranking** module and the **Network Database** used by the operator; in parallel a **Bad Data Processor** flags gross measurement errors using the $\chi^2$ test and largest normalised residual method.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: State the conditions for the observability of a power network.**

**Model Answer:**

1. The network is **algebraically observable** if the measurement Jacobian $\mathbf{H}$ (or $\mathbf{H}_{dc}$ in DC SE) has **full column rank**, i.e., $\text{rank}(\mathbf{H}) = n = N - 1$, where $N$ is the number of buses and one bus is chosen as the reference.
2. **Topological observability**: a spanning tree must exist in the augmented graph formed by adding the reference bus as a node and treating every power-injection measurement as a pseudo-edge between its bus and the reference.
3. In practice, the **redundancy ratio** $\eta = m/n$ should be $\ge 1$ for observability and $\ge 2$ for reliable bad data detection.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Derive the Weighted Least Squares (WLS) state estimation formulation for a power system. Starting from the measurement model $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}) + \mathbf{e}$, show the derivation of the normal (Gauss-Newton) equations and discuss the role of the weighting matrix. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the $\chi^2$-test based **bad data detection** procedure. State the hypothesis, define the test statistic, and discuss how the threshold is selected. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

1. Measurement model: $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}) + \mathbf{e}$, with $E[\mathbf{e}] = \mathbf{0}$, $E[\mathbf{e}\mathbf{e}^{T}] = \mathbf{R}$, $\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{R}^{-1}$. **(1 mark)**
2. WLS objective: minimise $J(\mathbf{x}) = [\mathbf{z} - \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x})]^{T}\mathbf{W}[\mathbf{z} - \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x})]$. **(1 mark)**
3. Differentiate, set gradient to zero: $\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{W}[\mathbf{z} - \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x})] = \mathbf{0}$. **(1 mark)**
4. Linearise: $\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}^{\nu+1}) \approx \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}^{\nu}) + \mathbf{H}\Delta\mathbf{x}^{\nu}$, substitute to obtain the **normal equation**: $\mathbf{G}\Delta\mathbf{x}^{\nu} = \mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{W}\Delta\mathbf{z}^{\nu}$, where $\mathbf{G} = \mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{H}$. **(2 marks)**
5. The weighting matrix $\mathbf{W}$ gives **higher importance to more accurate (lower-variance) measurements**, producing an unbiased, minimum-variance estimator. Converged solution: $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = (\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{z}$. **(2 marks)**

**(b) Chi-square Bad Data Detection (7 Marks):**

1. Under $\mathcal{H}_0$ (no bad data), the WLS objective at convergence follows a chi-square distribution: $J(\hat{\mathbf{x}}) = \mathbf{r}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{r} \sim \chi^{2}_{K}$, with $K = m - n$ degrees of freedom. **(2 marks)**
2. Choose significance level $\alpha$ (typically $0.05$). Compute the threshold $\chi^{2}_{K, \alpha}$ from chi-square tables. **(1 mark)**
3. Decision rule: if $J(\hat{\mathbf{x}}) > \chi^{2}_{K, \alpha}$, reject $\mathcal{H}_0$ — bad data is **present**; if $J(\hat{\mathbf{x}}) \le \chi^{2}_{K, \alpha}$, accept $\mathcal{H}_0$ — no bad data. **(2 marks)**
4. The threshold balances **false alarm** probability ($\alpha$) against **missed detection** probability ($1 - \text{power}$). Higher $\alpha$ → more false alarms, fewer missed bad data. The redundancy ratio $\eta$ should be $\ge 2$ for the test to be meaningful. **(2 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (WLS Derivation):** Students commonly **omit the Jacobian $\mathbf{H}$ definition** in step 3 of part (a), writing the normal equation with $\mathbf{h}$ instead of $\mathbf{H}$. This costs 1 full mark. Always explicitly state $\mathbf{H} = \partial \mathbf{h}/\partial \mathbf{x}$. Also, do not confuse $\mathbf{W}$ with $\mathbf{R}$; remember $\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{R}^{-1}$.

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Develop the **DC state estimation** model for a power system. Show the structure of the measurement Jacobian using the bus admittance matrix, and explain the construction of the flow and injection measurement rows. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Largest Normalised Residual (LNR) test** for bad data identification. Derive the residual covariance matrix and show how the normalisation is performed. Discuss its limitations and the enhancement known as **Hypothesis Testing Identification (HTI)**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) DC State Estimation (7 Marks):**

1. DC model assumptions: (i) voltage magnitudes are unity, (ii) line resistances are negligible, (iii) angle differences are small. State vector $\mathbf{x} = \boldsymbol{\theta} \in \mathbb{R}^{N-1}$. **(1 mark)**
2. **Power flow on line $i$–$j$:** $P_{ij} = B_{ij}(\theta_i - \theta_j)$. The corresponding row in $\mathbf{H}_{dc}$ has $+B_{ij}$ at column $i$ and $-B_{ij}$ at column $j$ (with the reference column removed). **(2 marks)**
3. **Power injection at bus $i$:** $P_i = \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} B_{ij}\theta_j$. The row is the $i$-th row of the bus admittance matrix $\mathbf{B}$ (with the reference row/column removed). **(2 marks)**
4. Solution: $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = (\mathbf{H}_{dc}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{H}_{dc})^{-1}\mathbf{H}_{dc}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{z}$. The DC estimator is **non-iterative** — a single matrix solve gives the state. **(2 marks)**

**(b) LNR Test and HTI (7 Marks):**

1. The residual vector is $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{z} - \mathbf{H}\hat{\mathbf{x}}$. Its covariance is $\boldsymbol{\Omega} = \mathbf{W}^{-1} - \mathbf{H}\mathbf{G}^{-1}\mathbf{H}^{T}$. **(2 marks)**
2. The $i$-th normalised residual is $r_i^{N} = \vert r_i \vert / \sqrt{\Omega_{ii}}$. The measurement with the **largest $r_i^{N}$** is identified as the bad data candidate and is **removed**; the WLS is then re-run. **(2 marks)**
3. **Limitations of LNR**:
   - **Smearing effect**: when bad data is present, residuals of *good* measurements become inflated (the "smearing" or "masking" effect).
   - Fails when **multiple interacting bad data** are present — only one is identified at a time.
   - Fails to identify the bad data type when it is a **topology error**. **(2 marks)**
4. **HTI enhancement** (Rousseaux / Van Cutsem): instead of picking the single largest, test *every subset* of measurements for compatibility. The subset that minimises a weighted residual norm is the "clean" set. This handles multiple bad data and topology errors. **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (LNR Test):** The most common mistake is writing the residual covariance as $\boldsymbol{\Omega} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{G}^{-1}\mathbf{H}^{T}$ — the **sign is wrong**. It is $\mathbf{W}^{-1} - \mathbf{H}\mathbf{G}^{-1}\mathbf{H}^{T}$, i.e., a *subtraction*. Forgetting the $\mathbf{W}^{-1}$ term costs 1 mark. Also, when the LNR test triggers, students often forget to **re-run the WLS** after removing the bad data; mention this loop explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullet List — Print and Memorise**

- **Measurement model**: $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}) + \mathbf{e}$; $\mathbf{e}$ is zero-mean Gaussian with covariance $\mathbf{R}$; $\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{R}^{-1}$.
- **WLS objective**: minimise $[\mathbf{z} - \mathbf{h}]^{T}\mathbf{W}[\mathbf{z} - \mathbf{h}]$.
- **Gauss-Newton normal equation**: $\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{H} \, \Delta \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{W} \Delta \mathbf{z}$; **gain matrix** $\mathbf{G} = \mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{H}$.
- **Convergence**: iterate until $\vert \Delta \mathbf{x} \vert_\infty < \epsilon$.
- **DC state estimate**: $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = (\mathbf{H}_{dc}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{H}_{dc})^{-1}\mathbf{H}_{dc}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{z}$ — non-iterative, linear.
- **DC flow row**: $P_{ij} \Rightarrow$ row $[+B_{ij}, -B_{ij}]$ in $\mathbf{H}_{dc}$.
- **DC injection row**: $P_i \Rightarrow$ $i$-th row of $\mathbf{B}$ matrix in $\mathbf{H}_{dc}$.
- **Chi-square detection**: $J(\hat{\mathbf{x}}) \sim \chi^{2}_{m-n}$; reject $\mathcal{H}_0$ if $J > \chi^{2}_{K,\alpha}$ with $\alpha = 0.05$.
- **Residual covariance**: $\boldsymbol{\Omega} = \mathbf{W}^{-1} - \mathbf{H}\mathbf{G}^{-1}\mathbf{H}^{T}$ (note the **minus sign**!).
- **Largest Normalised Residual (LNR)**: $r_i^{N} = \vert r_i \vert / \sqrt{\Omega_{ii}}$; identify the largest, remove it, re-estimate.
- **Observability (algebraic)**: $\text{rank}(\mathbf{H}) = n = N - 1$.
- **Observability (topological)**: augmented graph (injections → pseudo-edges to reference) must contain a spanning tree.
- **Redundancy ratio**: $\eta = m/n$; need $\eta \ge 1$ for observability, ideally $\eta \ge 2$ for reliable BDD.
- **Smearing / masking effect**: bad data contaminates residuals of neighbouring good measurements — LNR may misidentify.
- **HTI (Hypothesis Testing Identification)**: enumerates measurement subsets; cleanest subset is the one with smallest weighted residual norm.
- **Pipeline order**: Telemetry → Topology → Observability → State Estimation → Bad Data Detection → Bad Data Identification → Security Database.
- **Reference bus**: angle fixed to zero; state vector has dimension $N - 1$.
- **Sign convention in DC flow**: $P_{ij}$ is the flow *from* $i$ *to* $j$; with $B_{ij} > 0$, $P_{ij} > 0$ means power flows from $i$ to $j$.
- **Units**: $\theta$ in radians (p.u. for voltages and powers); $B$ in p.u. susceptance.
- **Indian context**: state estimators run at NLDC / RLDCs / SLDCs every 1–5 minutes; mandated by **CEA Grid Standards 2010**.

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generation Outage** is the event in which a generating unit (or a portion of its rated capacity) becomes unavailable to supply power to the grid, either due to a forced (unscheduled) failure or a planned (scheduled) maintenance activity.

In the language of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (Power System Operation & Control)** syllabus, generation outages are classified into two principal categories:

1. **Forced Outages** — Unscheduled, sudden, and involuntary removal of a generating unit from service caused by equipment failure, tripping of protection relays, fuel supply interruption, or unforeseen auxiliary system breakdown.
2. **Planned (Scheduled) Outages** — Pre-arranged, scheduled withdrawals of generating units for preventive maintenance, overhauling, inspection, testing, or refurbishment during periods when the unit is not economically critical.

> [!NOTE]
> **Outage Rate (OR)** = (Duration of outage hours / Total period hours) — a dimensionless fraction bounded between **0** and **1**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **bus fleet serving a city**. Each bus is a generating unit. A **forced outage** is like a bus suddenly breaking down on the highway — passengers are stranded, and the operator must scramble a replacement. A **planned outage** is like a bus being taken to the depot every Sunday for routine servicing — the operator knows in advance and arranges substitute transport.

The system operator's job is to keep enough **standby buses (spinning reserve)** so that even if several buses fail simultaneously, the city still runs smoothly.

---

## 1.3 Key Outage Performance Indices

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Forced Outage Rate (FOR)** — Probability that a unit will be unavailable when needed.
> - **Planned Outage Rate (POR)** — Fraction of time a unit is out for scheduled maintenance.
> - **Equivalent Forced Outage Rate (EFOR)** — Includes partial derating events; more realistic than FOR.
> - **Availability (A)** — Probability the unit is available, **A = 1 − FOR**.
> - **Unavailability (U)** — **U = FOR** for a two-state model.
> - **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** — Average running hours before a forced outage.
> - **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** — Average hours needed to restore the unit.
> - **Capacity Factor (CF)** — Ratio of actual energy produced to maximum possible energy.

These indices are typically recorded over long periods (one to five years) by generating companies and reported to regulatory bodies such as the **Central Electricity Regulatory Commission (CERC)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two-State Availability Model

The simplest and most widely used model in reliability evaluation of a generating unit is the **two-state Markov model**, where the unit is either **UP (Available)** or **DOWN (Forced Out)**.

The two-state model is the foundation for building outage probability tables for generation system reliability studies.

---

## 2.2 Spinning Reserve and Outage Considerations

When the largest generating unit (or any unit) suffers a forced outage, the system frequency drops instantly. To prevent under-frequency load shedding, **spinning reserve** must be carried. The KTU syllabus stresses that **spinning reserve is directly related to outage probability**.

A common operating rule:
> The spinning reserve must be greater than or equal to the capacity of the largest online unit, **OR** the sum of the FOR-weighted probabilities of capacity deficiency above the largest unit.

---

## 2.3 Capacity Outage Probability Table (COPT)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Capacity Outage Probability Table (COPT)** is a tabular listing of all possible capacity outage states of a generating system along with their associated probabilities of occurrence.

The COPT is the heart of generation reliability evaluation and is built **one unit at a time** using a **recursive convolution** technique. It is then used to compute the **Loss of Load Probability (LOLP)** and **Expected Energy Not Served (EENS)**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **high-yield** for KTU ESE, Module 3. Master the recursive convolution — it is the most frequently asked topic.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forced Outage Rate | $FOR = \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu}$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Availability | $A = 1 - FOR = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Mean Time To Failure | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | hours |
| 4 | Mean Time To Repair | $MTTR = \dfrac{1}{\mu}$ | hours |
| 5 | Recursive COPT formula | $P(X) = (1-U_{k}) \, P'(X) + U_{k} \, P'(X - C_{k})$ | Probability of exactly $X$ MW out |
| 6 | Equivalent Load | $L_{eq}(t) = L(t) + C_{out}(t)$ | MW |
| 7 | LOLP | $LOLP = \sum P(X) \; \big\vert \; X \geq L$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Expected Load Not Served | $ELNS = \sum_{X \geq L} (X - L) \, P(X)$ | MW |
| 9 | EENS | $EENS = ELNS \times 8760$ | MWh/year |
| 10 | Spinning Reserve Margin | $SR = \sum C_{online} - P_{peak}$ | MW |

**Where:**
- $\lambda$ = failure rate (failures/hour)
- $\mu$ = repair rate (repairs/hour)
- $U_{k}$ = unavailability (FOR) of the $k$-th unit being added
- $C_{k}$ = capacity of the $k$-th unit being added
- $P'(X)$ = COPT probability **before** adding unit $k$
- $P(X)$ = COPT probability **after** adding unit $k$
- $L$ = system peak load
- $C_{out}$ = capacity on outage at hour $t$

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

Generation outage analysis is essential for:

- **Generation expansion planning** — how much new capacity to add.
- **Reserve margin determination** — how much spinning + standby reserve to maintain.
- **Outage scheduling** — coordinating maintenance of multiple units.
- **Reliability pricing in deregulated markets** — capacity payments.
- **Compliance with Grid Code reliability standards** (e.g., the Indian Electricity Grid Code specifies an **n−1** contingency criterion).

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Two-State Outage Probability

Consider a single generating unit with:

- Failure rate (transition from UP → DOWN): $\lambda$ failures per hour
- Repair rate (transition from DOWN → UP): $\mu$ repairs per hour

In steady-state (long-run equilibrium), let:

- $P_{up}$ = probability the unit is UP
- $P_{down}$ = probability the unit is DOWN (on forced outage)

By flow balance at steady state:

$$\lambda \, P_{up} = \mu \, P_{down}$$

This equation states that **the rate of leaving the UP state equals the rate of entering the UP state**. Solving the system:

$$P_{up} + P_{down} = 1$$

$$\lambda \, P_{up} = \mu \, (1 - P_{up})$$

$$P_{up} \, (\lambda + \mu) = \mu$$

$$P_{up} = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$$

$$P_{down} = 1 - P_{up} = \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu}$$

**Identifying with operating indices:**

$$FOR = P_{down} = \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu}, \quad\quad A = P_{up} = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$$

$$MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}, \quad\quad MTTR = \dfrac{1}{\mu}$$

**Physical meaning:** If a unit fails once every 1000 hours on average and takes 50 hours to repair on average, then:

$$FOR = \dfrac{1/1000}{1/1000 + 1/50} = \dfrac{0.001}{0.001 + 0.02} = 0.0476$$

This unit is unavailable about **4.76 %** of the time — a typical value for a well-maintained thermal unit.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Recursive COPT Formula

We want to add a **new unit of capacity $C_k$ and forced outage rate $U_k$** to an existing system whose COPT is already known as $P'(X)$. The new unit is either **available** (with probability $1 - U_k$) or **on forced outage** (with probability $U_k$).

**Case A — New unit is AVAILABLE** (probability $1 - U_k$): The system outage state remains exactly $X$ MW; contribution = $(1 - U_k) \, P'(X)$.

**Case B — New unit is ON FORCED OUTAGE** (probability $U_k$): The system outage state increases by $C_k$ MW, becoming $X - C_k$ in the new table from $X$ in the old; contribution = $U_k \, P'(X - C_k)$.

By the **total probability theorem**, the new probability of being in state $X$ MW out is:

$$\boxed{\,P(X) = (1 - U_k) \, P'(X) \; + \; U_k \, P'(X - C_k)\,}$$

This is the **recursive convolution** formula. The starting condition for a fresh system (no units) is:

$$P'(0) = 1, \quad\quad P'(X) = 0 \; \text{for} \; X > 0$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Building a COPT

**Problem:** A generating station has **3 units** with the following capacities and forced outage rates. Build the COPT.

| Unit | Capacity $C_k$ (MW) | Forced Outage Rate $U_k$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 25 | 0.05 |
| 2 | 30 | 0.04 |
| 3 | 40 | 0.06 |

---

### Step 1 — Add Unit 1 (25 MW, $U_1 = 0.05$)

$$P_1(0) = (1 - 0.05) \, P'(0) + 0.05 \, P'(-25) = 0.95 \times 1 + 0 = 0.95$$

$$P_1(25) = 0.05 \, P'(0) + (1 - 0.05) \, P'(-25) = 0.05 \times 1 + 0 = 0.05$$

| Outage State $X$ (MW) | Probability $P_1(X)$ |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0.95 |
| 25 | 0.05 |

(Sum = 1.000 ✓)

---

### Step 2 — Add Unit 2 (30 MW, $U_2 = 0.04$)

$$P_2(0) = 0.96 \times P_1(0) + 0.04 \times P_1(-30) = 0.96 \times 0.95 = 0.912$$

$$P_2(25) = 0.96 \times P_1(25) + 0.04 \times P_1(-5) = 0.96 \times 0.05 = 0.048$$

Wait — there is also a contribution from the case where Unit 1 was out AND Unit 2 is up. Correcting:

$$P_2(25) = 0.96 \times P_1(25) + 0.04 \times P_1(25 - 30) = 0.96 \times 0.05 + 0.04 \times P_1(-5)$$

Since $P_1(-5) = 0$:

$$P_2(25) = 0.96 \times 0.05 + 0 = 0.048$$

$$P_2(30) = 0.96 \times P_1(30) + 0.04 \times P_1(0) = 0 + 0.04 \times 0.95 = 0.038$$

$$P_2(55) = 0.96 \times P_1(55) + 0.04 \times P_1(25) = 0 + 0.04 \times 0.05 = 0.002$$

| Outage State $X$ (MW) | Probability $P_2(X)$ |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0.912 |
| 25 | 0.048 |
| 30 | 0.038 |
| 55 | 0.002 |

(Sum = 1.000 ✓)

---

### Step 3 — Add Unit 3 (40 MW, $U_3 = 0.06$)

$$P_3(0) = 0.94 \times 0.912 + 0 = 0.8573$$

$$P_3(25) = 0.94 \times 0.048 + 0.06 \times P_2(-15) = 0.0451 + 0 = 0.0451$$

$$P_3(30) = 0.94 \times 0.038 + 0.06 \times P_2(-10) = 0.0357 + 0 = 0.0357$$

$$P_3(40) = 0.94 \times P_2(40) + 0.06 \times P_2(0) = 0 + 0.06 \times 0.912 = 0.0547$$

$$P_3(55) = 0.94 \times P_2(55) + 0.06 \times P_2(15) = 0.94 \times 0.002 + 0.06 \times 0 = 0.00188$$

$$P_3(65) = 0.94 \times P_2(65) + 0.06 \times P_2(25) = 0 + 0.06 \times 0.048 = 0.00288$$

$$P_3(70) = 0.94 \times P_2(70) + 0.06 \times P_2(30) = 0 + 0.06 \times 0.038 = 0.00228$$

$$P_3(95) = 0.94 \times P_2(95) + 0.06 \times P_2(55) = 0 + 0.06 \times 0.002 = 0.00012$$

**Final COPT (3 units):**

| Outage State $X$ (MW) | Probability $P_3(X)$ |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0.8573 |
| 25 | 0.0451 |
| 30 | 0.0357 |
| 40 | 0.0547 |
| 55 | 0.00188 |
| 65 | 0.00288 |
| 70 | 0.00228 |
| 95 | 0.00012 |

(Sum ≈ 1.000 ✓)

---

### Step 4 — Interpretation

If the system peak load is, say, $L = 50$ MW, then **loss of load** occurs whenever capacity outage $X \geq 50$ MW:

$$LOLP = P_3(55) + P_3(65) + P_3(70) + P_3(95)$$

$$LOLP = 0.00188 + 0.00288 + 0.00228 + 0.00012 = 0.00716$$

This means about **0.716 %** of the time, the available capacity cannot meet the 50 MW peak load — illustrating directly why **spinning reserve is mandatory**.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Two-State Markov Model of a Generating Unit

This diagram shows the fundamental **UP ↔ DOWN** transitions used in COPT construction.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> sUP
    sUP : UP Available
    sUP --> sDOWN : Failure occurs at rate lambda
    sDOWN : DOWN Forced Outage
    sDOWN --> sUP : Repair completed at rate mu
    sDOWN --> [*] : Permanent retirement
```

**Reading the diagram:** A unit starts in the UP state. When it fails, it transitions to the DOWN state with rate $\lambda$. After repair at rate $\mu$, it returns to UP. The COPT construction uses only the steady-state probabilities of these two states.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of Generation Outage Analysis

The following diagram maps the complete analytical pipeline used in KTU-level generation reliability studies, from raw failure data to LOLP/EENS indices.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Historical Operating Data of Units] --> nodeB[Compute Unit-Level Indices lambda mu MTTF MTTR]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Derive FOR and Availability per unit]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Initialize COPT with P 0 equals 1]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Pick next unit k with Capacity Ck and FOR Uk]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Apply Recursive Formula P X equals 1 minus Uk times P prime X plus Uk times P prime X minus Ck]
    nodeF --> nodeG{All units added?}
    nodeG -- No --> nodeE
    nodeG -- Yes --> nodeH[Final Capacity Outage Probability Table]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Form Equivalent Load Duration Curve]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Compute LOLP and EENS]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Determine Spinning Reserve and Operating Margin]
```

**Block explanation:**

- **nodeA → nodeB**: The journey begins with raw data — number of failures, repair hours — collected from plant logs.
- **nodeC**: From these counts, the FOR is derived for each unit individually.
- **nodeD → nodeG**: The COPT is built incrementally, one unit per iteration, applying the recursive formula at each step.
- **nodeH**: The completed table maps every possible outage magnitude to its probability.
- **nodeI**: The COPT is convolved with the load duration curve to form the **equivalent load** used in composite system reliability.
- **nodeJ → nodeK**: Reliability indices (LOLP, EENS) are computed, driving reserve-setting decisions.

---

## 4.3 Outage Classification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Unit Becomes Unavailable] --> nodeB{Is the event scheduled in advance?}
    nodeB -- Yes --> nodeC[Planned Outage]
    nodeB -- No --> nodeD[Forced Outage]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Sub-types Routine Maintenance Overhaul Inspection Refurbishment]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Sub-types Equipment Trip Relay Operation Fuel Failure Auxiliary Breakdown]
    nodeF --> nodeG{Does partial capacity remain available?}
    nodeG -- Yes --> nodeH[Derated Outage]
    nodeG -- No --> nodeI[Full Forced Outage]
```

This tree directly answers the KTU exam question **"Classify generation outages"** with diagram-backed reasoning.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Forced Outage Rate (FOR) and Planned Outage Rate (POR). How are they related to unit availability?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Forced Outage Rate (FOR)** is the long-run average fraction of time a generating unit is unavailable due to **unscheduled, involuntary failures**. Mathematically,
>
> $$FOR = \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu}$$
>
> where $\lambda$ is the failure rate and $\mu$ is the repair rate.
>
> **Planned Outage Rate (POR)** is the long-run average fraction of time a unit is withdrawn from service for **pre-scheduled maintenance**. The **unit availability** is the probability that the unit is in a state capable of generating:
>
> $$A = 1 - (FOR + POR)$$
>
> (in the simplified two-outage-state model). Together, FOR + POR + Available-time = 1.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is a Capacity Outage Probability Table (COPT)? State its use.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **Capacity Outage Probability Table (COPT)** is a discrete probability distribution listing **every possible magnitude of generation capacity on outage** (in MW) and the **corresponding probability** of that outage magnitude occurring.
>
> It is constructed using the **recursive convolution formula**:
>
> $$P(X) = (1 - U_k) \, P'(X) + U_k \, P'(X - C_k)$$
>
> **Uses:** (i) computing **Loss of Load Probability (LOLP)**, (ii) computing **Expected Energy Not Served (EENS)**, (iii) setting **spinning reserve requirements**, and (iv) **generation expansion planning**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**(a)** Derive the recursive formula used to build the Capacity Outage Probability Table. Explain the meaning of every term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A generating station has **4 units** with the following data. Build the COPT and determine the LOLP if the system peak load is **90 MW**. **(7 marks)**

| Unit | Capacity (MW) | FOR |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 20 | 0.04 |
| 2 | 25 | 0.05 |
| 3 | 30 | 0.06 |
| 4 | 40 | 0.03 |

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of the Recursive COPT Formula (7 marks)

Let us add a new unit $k$ of capacity $C_k$ MW and forced outage rate $U_k$ to an existing system whose COPT $P'(X)$ is already known.

The new unit has two mutually exclusive states:
- **Available**, with probability $1 - U_k$.
- **On forced outage**, with probability $U_k$.

**Case 1 — New unit is AVAILABLE (prob. $1 - U_k$):** The capacity on outage is unchanged at $X$ MW. The probability that the existing system was already at state $X$ is $P'(X)$. The combined probability is $(1 - U_k) P'(X)$.

**Case 2 — New unit is ON OUTAGE (prob. $U_k$):** The capacity on outage increases by $C_k$ MW. If the existing system was at state $X - C_k$ with probability $P'(X - C_k)$, then the new state becomes $X$. The contribution is $U_k \, P'(X - C_k)$.

By the **total probability theorem**, adding the two disjoint cases:

$$\boxed{\,P(X) = (1 - U_k) \, P'(X) + U_k \, P'(X - C_k)\,}$$

**Meaning of terms:**
- $P(X)$ = new probability of being in outage state $X$ MW
- $P'(X)$ = old probability of outage state $X$ MW (before adding unit $k$)
- $U_k$ = forced outage rate of unit $k$
- $C_k$ = capacity of unit $k$
- $P'(X - C_k)$ = old probability of outage state $X - C_k$ (taken as 0 if $X < C_k$)

---

#### Part (b) — Building the COPT and Computing LOLP (7 marks)

**Step 1: Add Unit 1 — 20 MW, $U_1 = 0.04$**

| $X$ (MW) | $P_1(X)$ |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0.96 |
| 20 | 0.04 |

(Sum = 1.000 ✓)

**Step 2: Add Unit 2 — 25 MW, $U_2 = 0.05$**

$$P_2(0) = 0.95 \times 0.96 = 0.9120$$

$$P_2(20) = 0.95 \times 0.04 = 0.0380$$

$$P_2(25) = 0.05 \times 0.96 = 0.0480$$

$$P_2(45) = 0.05 \times 0.04 = 0.0020$$

| $X$ (MW) | $P_2(X)$ |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0.9120 |
| 20 | 0.0380 |
| 25 | 0.0480 |
| 45 | 0.0020 |

(Sum = 1.000 ✓)

**Step 3: Add Unit 3 — 30 MW, $U_3 = 0.06$**

$$P_3(0) = 0.94 \times 0.9120 = 0.8573$$

$$P_3(20) = 0.94 \times 0.0380 = 0.0357$$

$$P_3(25) = 0.94 \times 0.0480 = 0.0451$$

| 0 | 0.8573 |

(Sum = 0.99996 ≈ 1.000 ✓)

**Step 4: Add Unit 4 — 40 MW, $U_4 = 0.03$**

Only the cases where $X - 40 \geq 0$ contribute:

$$P_4(0) = 0.97 \times 0.8573 = 0.8316$$

$$P_4(20) = 0.97 \times 0.0357 = 0.03463$$

$$P_4(25) = 0.97 \times 0.0451 = 0.04375$$

$$P_4(30) = 0.97 \times 0.0547 = 0.05306$$

$$P_4(40) = 0.03 \times 0.8573 = 0.02572$$

$$P_4(45) = 0.97 \times 0.00188 = 0.00182$$

$$P_4(50) = 0.97 \times 0.00228 = 0.00221$$

$$P_4(55) = 0.97 \times 0.00288 = 0.00279$$

$$P_4(60) = 0.03 \times 0.0357 = 0.00107$$

$$LOLP = P_4(90) + P_4(95) + P_4(115)$$

This corresponds to approximately **1.39 hours per year** of expected loss-of-load condition — an acceptably reliable system.

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 — Alternative]

**(a)** Explain the **two-state Markov model** of a generating unit. Derive expressions for FOR, availability, MTTF and MTTR in terms of failure rate $\lambda$ and repair rate $\mu$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 200 MW unit has experienced **5 forced outages** during the last year, with **total forced outage hours = 720 hours** and **total operating hours = 8040 hours**. Compute its FOR, availability, MTTF and MTTR. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Two-State Markov Model (7 marks)

A generating unit is modeled as having only two states:

- **State UP** — the unit is synchronised and capable of generating.
- **State DOWN** — the unit is on forced outage.

Transitions:
- UP → DOWN at failure rate $\lambda$ (failures per hour)
- DOWN → UP at repair rate $\mu$ (repairs per hour)

Let $P_{up}$ and $P_{down}$ be the steady-state probabilities.

**Equilibrium (flow balance) equations:**

$$\lambda \, P_{up} = \mu \, P_{down}$$

$$P_{up} + P_{down} = 1$$

**Solving simultaneously:**

$$P_{up} = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}, \quad\quad P_{down} = \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu}$$

**Identifying:**

$$FOR = P_{down} = \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu}$$

$$Availability = P_{up} = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$$

$$MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}, \quad\quad MTTR = \dfrac{1}{\mu}$$

**Practical meaning:** MTTF is the average continuous run-hours between failures; MTTR is the average hours to bring the unit back online. Both are critical inputs to maintenance scheduling and reserve planning.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Number of forced outages in the year = **5**
- Total forced outage hours = **720 h**
- Total operating (running) hours = **8040 h**

**Step 1 — Compute failure rate $\lambda$:**

$$\lambda = \dfrac{\text{Number of failures}}{\text{Operating hours}} = \dfrac{5}{8040}$$

$$\lambda = 6.22 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hour}$$

**Step 2 — Compute repair rate $\mu$:**

$$\mu = \dfrac{\text{Number of repairs}}{\text{Outage hours}} = \dfrac{5}{720}$$

$$\mu = 6.944 \times 10^{-3} \text{ repairs/hour}$$

**Step 3 — Compute FOR:**

$$FOR = \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu} = \dfrac{6.22 \times 10^{-4}}{6.22 \times 10^{-4} + 6.944 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$FOR = \dfrac{6.22 \times 10^{-4}}{7.566 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0822$$

$$\boxed{FOR \approx 8.22\%}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Availability:**

$$A = 1 - FOR = 1 - 0.0822 = 0.9178$$

$$\boxed{A \approx 91.78\%}$$

**Step 5 — Compute MTTF:**

$$MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1}{6.22 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{MTTF \approx 1608 \text{ hours}}$$

**Step 6 — Compute MTTR:**

$$MTTR = \dfrac{1}{\mu} = \dfrac{1}{6.944 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\boxed{MTTR \approx 144 \text{ hours}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This unit runs on average for **1608 hours** between failures but requires **144 hours** (6 days) to repair each time. Its availability of **91.78 %** is moderately good but below the industry benchmark of **~95 %** for modern thermal units, suggesting that either the repair crew efficiency or the spare parts inventory needs improvement.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalise heavily:**
>
> 1. **Reversing the recursion** — Many students mistakenly write $P(X) = U_k P'(X) + (1 - U_k) P'(X - C_k)$. The correct formula multiplies $(1-U_k)$ by $P'(X)$ (unit available → same outage state) and $U_k$ by $P'(X - C_k)$ (unit out → outage increased by $C_k$). **−1 to −2 marks for this swap.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the initial condition** — The COPT must start with $P(0) = 1$, not $P(0) = 0$. Examiners check this rigorously.
>
> 3. **Not summing to 1** — Every intermediate COPT must sum to unity. If yours does not, you have either dropped a state or mis-applied the formula. KTU values this verification step.
>
> 4. **Wrong LOLP summation range** — LOLP is $\sum P(X)$ for $X \geq L$, NOT $X > L$. State this explicitly in the answer.
>
> 5. **Units inconsistency in MTTF/MTTR** — Always state **hours**. Examiners deduct marks if units are missing or wrong.
>
> 6. **Confusing EFOR with FOR** — EFOR includes partial deratings. If a question says "the unit derated to 60 % capacity for 200 hours", that is an EFOR event, not a full FOR event.
>
> 7. **Not drawing the two-state diagram** when derivation is asked. Examiners reward visual reasoning with **1 mark** in derivation questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist — pin this before the exam!**

- **Two outage types:** Forced (unscheduled) and Planned (scheduled). Know at least **two sub-types** of each.
- **Two-state Markov model:** UP ↔ DOWN with rates $\lambda$ and $\mu$. Always draw the diagram before deriving.
- **Master the four indices:** $FOR = \lambda/(\lambda+\mu)$, $A = \mu/(\lambda+\mu)$, $MTTF = 1/\lambda$, $MTTR = 1/\mu$.
- **The recursive convolution formula** is the most important tool:
  
  $$P(X) = (1 - U_k) \, P'(X) + U_k \, P'(X - C_k)$$
  
  Apply it **one unit at a time**, in any order — the final COPT is order-independent.
- **Initial seed:** $P'(0) = 1$, all others zero.
- **Verification:** After every step, ensure $\sum P(X) = 1$ to the fourth decimal.
- **LOLP** = $\sum P(X)$ for **all $X \geq L$** where $L$ is the peak load.
- **EENS** = $\sum (X - L) \, P(X)$ for $X \geq L$, multiplied by **8760 hours** for annual MWh.
- **Spinning reserve rule of thumb:** must be $\geq$ capacity of the largest single online unit, OR $\geq$ LOLP-derived requirement, whichever is higher.
- **State-space limitation:** COPT grows combinatorially — for $n$ units, the number of distinct outage states can be up to $n+1$, but with different capacities, it grows more. For very large systems, use **Monte Carlo simulation** instead.
- **Outage scheduling** must coordinate multiple units so that **total capacity on planned outage at any hour** does not compromise reliability — typically done using **levelized reserve method**.
- **Industry benchmarks:** Modern thermal units target $FOR \approx 3$–5 %, availability $\geq 95$ %. Values outside this range indicate ageing equipment or poor O&M.
- **KTU-specific exam hints:** Always (i) define FOR, (ii) state the recursive formula, (iii) draw the two-state diagram if derivation is asked, and (iv) verify $\sum P = 1$. Examiners award marks for these four habits.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmission Outages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET741 - POWER SYSTEM OPERATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transmission Outages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transmission Outage (KTU 2024 Definition):** A **transmission outage** is defined as the intentional or unintentional removal of one or more transmission system elements (lines, transformers, generators, bus-bars, or breakers) from service, which alters the topology of the power network and may restrict the system's ability to deliver power from generation to load while satisfying operational security constraints.

In the context of **Power System Operation and Control**, transmission outages are broadly classified as:

1. **Planned (Scheduled) Outages** — pre-arranged, coordinated removals carried out for preventive maintenance, construction, or refurbishment.
2. **Forced (Unscheduled / Emergency) Outages** — unscheduled, automatic or manual removals triggered by equipment failure, protection operation, or unforeseen events.
3. **Maintenance Outages** — short-duration pre-approved outages for routine inspection/testing.
4. **Permanent Outages** — outages where the element is decommissioned indefinitely.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 scheme, transmission outages are studied in conjunction with **security analysis**, **contingency analysis**, and **reliability assessment**. The goal is to plan, rank, and operate the system such that no single contingency violates operational security limits.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **national highway network** carrying goods from factories (generators) to cities (loads).

- If one highway lane is **blocked for roadwork** (planned outage), traffic must be rerouted through parallel roads.
- If a bridge **collapses suddenly** (forced outage), congestion builds up and some cities may not receive supplies on time.
- The **Highway Authority** must pre-plan lane closures, post diversions, and keep reserve capacity — exactly what a power system operator does with **transmission outages**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why it matters to a power engineer:** A single unconsidered line outage can cause **cascading failure**, **voltage collapse**, or **overloading** of healthy lines — culminating in blackouts (e.g., the 2003 North-East US blackout originated from unassessed transmission contingencies).

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **System Frequency (India):** **50 Hz**
- **Standard Voltage Levels (India):** **400 kV, 220 kV, 132 kV, 66 kV**
- **Planning Reserve Margin (PRM):** typically **15–20 %** of annual peak load
- **N − 1 Contingency Criterion** (the global operational norm).
- **Forced Outage Rate (FOR)** is a dimensionless probability, range **[0, 1]**.

> [!CAUTION]
> In KTU 2024 papers, candidates often confuse **LOLP (probability)** with **LOLE (expectation in hours/day or days/year)**. Memorise the units — they are tested.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic Flow — How a Transmission Outage is Handled

A transmission outage is processed in four engineering stages:

1. **Detection / Scheduling** — Identify the element to be removed (manually or by EMS/State-Estimator).
2. **Pre-outage Security Analysis** — Run **AC / DC load-flow** for the post-outage topology.
3. **Contingency Ranking & Screening** — Rank violations by **Performance Index (PI)**.
4. **Corrective / Preventive Action** — Reschedule generation, switch lines, or load-shed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering rationale:** Every transmission element has a finite probability of failure. Operators must *guarantee* that even after the *worst single contingency*, the system remains within **thermal**, **voltage**, and **stability** limits.

### 2.2 Reliability Indices — The Quantitative Heart of the Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners love numerical problems on **LOLE**, **LOLP**, and **FOR**. Master the table below.

#### 2.2.1 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Index | Formula (LaTeX-safe) | Unit | Description |
|---|-------|----------------------|------|-------------|
| 1 | Failure Rate | $\lambda$ | failures/yr | Mean number of failures per year |
| 2 | Repair Rate | $\mu$ | repairs/yr | Mean number of repairs per year |
| 3 | Mean Time To Failure | $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ | hours | Average up-time |
| 4 | Mean Time To Repair | $\text{MTTR} = 1/\mu$ | hours | Average down-time |
| 5 | Availability | $A = \text{MTTF}/(\text{MTTF}+\text{MTTR})$ | dimensionless | Probability of being operational |
| 6 | Unavailability | $U = \text{MTTR}/(\text{MTTF}+\text{MTTR}) = \text{FOR}$ | dimensionless | Probability of being down |
| 7 | Forced Outage Rate | $\text{FOR} = \lambda/(\lambda+\mu)$ | dimensionless | Same as unavailability for exponential model |
| 8 | Loss of Load Probability | $\text{LOLP} = \sum_i P_i(\text{loss of load})$ | dimensionless | Probability of load exceeding available capacity |
| 9 | Loss of Load Expectation | $\text{LOLE} = \sum_i P_i \cdot t_i$ | hours/yr or days/yr | Expected time when load exceeds capacity |
| 10 | Expected Energy Not Supplied | $\text{EENS} = \sum_i E_i \cdot P_i$ | MWh/yr | Expected unsupplied energy |
| 11 | Expected Demand Not Supplied | $\text{EDNS} = \sum_i C_i \cdot P_i$ | MW | Expected power shortage |
| 12 | System Well-being | $\{\text{Healthy, Marginal, At-Risk}\}$ | categorical | Operating state classification |
| 13 | Performance Index | $\text{PI} = \sum_i w_i (\Delta f_i)^2$ | — | Used in contingency ranking |
| 14 | Composite Customer Damage Function | $\text{CCDF} = \sum_j C_j \cdot L_j$ | ₹/kW | Cost of interruption |
| 15 | Energy Index of Reliability | $\text{EIR} = 1 - \text{EENS}/E_{\text{demand}}$ | dimensionless | Reliability ratio |

> **Note on LaTeX:** All vertical bars / absolute values are written using `\vert` to keep the markdown table intact.

#### 2.2.2 Real-world Utility

- **LOLE** is used by **CEA / POSOCO** as the deterministic reliability benchmark (typically **0.2 %** of annual peak, i.e., ~17.5 hours/year).
- **FOR** is the primary input for probabilistic production costing and outage-scheduling models.
- **PI-based ranking** is built into every modern **Energy Management System (EMS)** for online contingency screening.

### 2.3 The N − 1 Security Criterion

The **N − 1 criterion** is the global operational gold standard:

> *At any instant, the loss of any single transmission element (line, transformer, generator, or shunt device) shall not result in:*
> 1. *Overloading of any remaining element beyond its emergency rating.*
> 2. *Bus voltage falling below emergency limits.*
> 3. *Loss of stability.*

**Types of N − 1 Studies Performed by Operators:**

- **DC Power-Flow Contingency Analysis** (fast screening using B-coefficients).
- **AC Power-Flow Contingency Analysis** (full Newton–Raphson solve for each contingency).
- **Transient Security Assessment (TSA)** for stability.

### 2.4 Outage Planning Hierarchy

1. **Long-term (5–20 years)** — System expansion planning.
2. **Annual Outage Plan** — Maintenance of major EHV transformers, lines, and bus reactors.
3. **Monthly / Weekly Outage Schedule** — Coordinated by **RLDCs (Regional Load Despatch Centres)**.
4. **Daily / Real-time Outages** — Approved by the System Operator based on security analysis.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Weightage:** The 14-mark Module 3 question is *almost always* a numerical on **LOLE / LOLP / EENS calculation** OR a descriptive question on **N − 1 / outage classification / planning procedure**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Two-State Markov Model for a Transmission Element

Every transmission element (line, transformer) can be modelled as a **two-state (Up / Down) Markov process**.

> **States:** *Up* (available) and *Down* (forced out).
> **Transition rates:** *λ* from Up → Down, *μ* from Down → Up.

Let **P_up(t)** = probability of being in Up state, **P_down(t)** = probability of being in Down state.

The Chapman–Kolmogorov equations are:

$$
\frac{dP_{\text{up}}(t)}{dt} = -\lambda \, P_{\text{up}}(t) + \mu \, P_{\text{down}}(t)
$$

$$
\frac{dP_{\text{down}}(t)}{dt} = \lambda \, P_{\text{up}}(t) - \mu \, P_{\text{down}}(t)
$$

**Step 1 — Apply steady-state condition** (operating horizon is long):

$$
\frac{dP_{\text{up}}(t)}{dt} = 0, \quad \frac{dP_{\text{down}}(t)}{dt} = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Use the constraint** that the element is in *some* state:

$$
P_{\text{up}} + P_{\text{down}} = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the steady-state balance**:

$$
-\lambda P_{\text{up}} + \mu P_{\text{down}} = 0
$$

Substituting $P_{\text{down}} = 1 - P_{\text{up}}$:

$$
-\lambda P_{\text{up}} + \mu(1 - P_{\text{up}}) = 0
$$

$$
P_{\text{up}}(\lambda + \mu) = \mu
$$

$$
P_{\text{up}} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}, \qquad P_{\text{down}} = \frac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify availability & unavailability**:

$$
A = P_{\text{up}} = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}}, \qquad U = P_{\text{down}} = \text{FOR}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** This derivation is the **foundation of every probabilistic reliability study** in power systems. Every LOLE, LOLP, and EENS computation begins from this two-state result.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Loss of Load Expectation (LOLE)

Consider a load duration curve approximated by *n* discrete blocks.

- Let $L_i$ be the load in block *i*.
- Let $C_i$ be the **available generating capacity** (installed capacity × availability).
- Let $p_i$ be the probability of having that available capacity.

**Step 1 — Compute the probability of capacity outage** (loss of load event) in block *i*:

$$
P_i(\text{loss of load}) = P(C_i < L_i)
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by the duration** $t_i$ of the block:

$$
\text{LOLE}_i = P_i(\text{loss of load}) \cdot t_i
$$

**Step 3 — Sum over all blocks** to obtain the annual LOLE:

$$
\text{LOLE} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i(\text{loss of load}) \cdot t_i \quad \text{[hours/year]}
$$

> If $t_i$ is in days, the result is in **days/year** (the standard IEC form).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU answers:** State the **unit** of the answer explicitly. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark if the unit is missing.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Expected Energy Not Supplied (EENS)

EENS integrates *how much* energy is curtailed, not just *how long* curtailment lasts.

**Step 1 — For each block *i*, compute the energy curtailed**:

$$
E_i = (L_i - C_i) \cdot t_i \quad \text{(MWh)}
$$

Only when $C_i < L_i$ (otherwise $E_i = 0$).

**Step 2 — Weight by the probability** of the capacity outage:

$$
\text{EENS}_i = E_i \cdot P_i
$$

**Step 3 — Sum over all blocks**:

$$
\text{EENS} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i \cdot P_i \quad \text{[MWh/year]}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **EENS** is the basis for calculating **Interruption Cost (IC)** using CCDF:
> $$ \text{IC} = \text{CCDF} \times \text{EENS} $$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Reliability Tool to Outage Type

| Outage Type | Outage Time Frame | Reliability Tool Used | Decision Outcome |
|-------------|-------------------|------------------------|------------------|
| Permanent Decommissioning | Years | Long-term expansion planning | New line/transformer addition |
| Scheduled Maintenance | Days–Weeks | Annual outage plan, N − 1 check | Outage approved or shifted |
| Forced Outage (Line) | Hours | Real-time contingency analysis | Re-dispatch, load-shed |
| Forced Outage (Transformer) | Hours | Contingency analysis + VAR support | Switch capacitor / reactor |
| Cascading Outage | Minutes | Dynamic security assessment | UFLS / islanding |
| Weather-driven Outage | Variable | Probabilistic risk assessment | Hardening, tree-trimming |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering value:** The same physical event (a line going down) triggers *different* analytical tools depending on whether it is scheduled, forced, or cascading. KTU questions test this **mapping knowledge** frequently.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Transmission Outage Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Start: Element Removed?]) --> nodeB{Scheduled or Unscheduled?}
    nodeB -- Scheduled --> nodeC{Type of Work}
    nodeB -- Unscheduled --> nodeD{Trigger Source}
    nodeC -- Routine --> nodeE[Maintenance Outage]
    nodeC -- Capital Work --> nodeF[Construction Outage]
    nodeC -- Refurbishment --> nodeG[Planned Long Outage]
    nodeD -- Equipment Fault --> nodeH[Forced Outage]
    nodeD -- Protection Trip --> nodeI[Emergency Outage]
    nodeD -- Weather --> nodeJ[Storm Outage]
    nodeH --> nodeK{Consequence}
    nodeK -- Lines Overloaded --> nodeL[PI Ranking]
    nodeK -- Voltage Drop --> nodeM[VAR Re-dispatch]
    nodeK -- Cascading --> nodeN[UFLS / Islanding]
    nodeL --> nodeO[Corrective Action]
    nodeM --> nodeO
    nodeN --> nodeO
    nodeO([System Restored])
```

### 4.2 Outage Planning & Reliability Assessment Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Outage Request] --> B[Security Analysis Module]
    B --> C[DC Power Flow]
    B --> D[AC Power Flow]
    B --> E[Stability Analysis]
    C --> F{Security OK?}
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F -- Yes --> G[Approve Outage]
    F -- No --> H[Modify Outage Plan]
    H --> I[Re-schedule Generation]
    H --> J[Shift Outage Date]
    H --> K[Postpone to Next Window]
    G --> L[Execute in Real Time]
    L --> M[Log MTTF / MTTR]
    M --> N[Update FOR Database]
    N --> O[Reliability Indices: LOLE / LOLP / EENS]
    O --> P[Long-term Planning Feedback]
```

### 4.3 Operational State Diagram — Healthy / Marginal / At-Risk

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Healthy
    Healthy --> Marginal: Load approaches operating limit
    Marginal --> AtRisk: Post-contingency violation imminent
    AtRisk --> Marginal: Re-dispatch successful
    Marginal --> Healthy: Load relieved
    AtRisk --> Emergency: N-1 violation occurs
    Emergency --> AtRisk: Corrective action taken
    Emergency --> [*]: System Collapse (avoided)
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual interpretation:** The system engineer continuously monitors the *state* of the grid. Any transmission outage forces an immediate re-evaluation of which state the system is in. KTU Module 3 questions often ask to *classify* the system into one of these three states given load, FOR, and reserve data.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the term **Forced Outage Rate (FOR)** of a transmission line and write the formula relating it to MTTF and MTTR.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Forced Outage Rate (FOR) is the long-run probability that a transmission element will be unavailable due to forced (unscheduled) outages.
>
> $$ \text{FOR} = \frac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}} $$
>
> where $\lambda$ is the failure rate, $\mu$ the repair rate, **MTTF** the Mean Time To Failure, and **MTTR** the Mean Time To Repair. FOR is dimensionless.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Distinguish between **Loss of Load Probability (LOLP)** and **Loss of Load Expectation (LOLE)**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **LOLP** is a *dimensionless probability* — the probability that the available generating capacity is insufficient to meet the load at a given instant.
> - **LOLE** is the *expected duration* (in hours/yr or days/yr) over a period during which the load exceeds available capacity. $\text{LOLE} = \sum P_i \cdot t_i$.
> - LOLP answers *"how likely?"*; LOLE answers *"for how long?"*.

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module carries one 14-mark question. The question has internal choice — students answer **either OR**.

---

#### **Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 – Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **classification of transmission outages** with suitable examples. What are the planning and operational implications of each type? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 220 kV transmission line has a **MTTF of 4380 hours** and **MTTR of 20 hours**. Compute (i) the failure rate λ, (ii) the repair rate μ, (iii) the FOR, and (iv) the availability. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Classification (7 marks):**

| Type | Trigger | Notice | Example |
|------|---------|--------|---------|
| Planned | Pre-approved maintenance | Weeks–months | Annual tower-painting |
| Forced | Sudden fault | None | Lightning trip |
| Maintenance | Inspection/testing | Days | Oil filtration |
| Emergency | Operator action | Minutes | Manual trip for safety |

*Planning implications:* planned outages must pass N − 1 security check; forced outages trigger real-time corrective action; maintenance outages balance cost vs. availability.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** MTTF = 4380 h, MTTR = 20 h.

**Step 1 — Failure rate:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTTF}} = \frac{1}{4380} = 2.283 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per hour}
$$

**Step 2 — Repair rate:**

$$
\mu = \frac{1}{\text{MTTR}} = \frac{1}{20} = 0.05 \text{ per hour}
$$

**Step 3 — FOR (unavailability):**

$$
\text{FOR} = \frac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{2.283 \times 10^{-4}}{2.283 \times 10^{-4} + 0.05} = 4.55 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 4 — Availability:**

$$
A = 1 - \text{FOR} = 0.9955 \;\; (99.55\%)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question A:** Students forget to **state the unit** of λ and μ (per hour). Also, do not confuse **FOR (probability)** with **MTTF (hours)** — they are dimensionally distinct. Marks deducted: typically 1 mark each for unit and dimension.

---

#### **Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Actual Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **three-state system well-being model** (Healthy, Marginal, At-Risk) used in transmission outage studies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A small isolated system has **two identical 50 MW generators**. The load duration curve for the system is given below. Each generator has **FOR = 0.02**. Determine the **annual LOLE** in hours/year.

| Load (MW) | Duration (h/yr) |
|-----------|-----------------|
| 90 | 1000 |
| 70 | 3000 |
| 50 | 3000 |
| 30 | 2000 |
| 10 | 1000 |

##### Model Solution

**(a) Three-state system well-being model (7 marks):**

(See the state diagram in Section 4.3.)

- **Healthy:** All constraints satisfied even after a credible contingency. No operating limit is approached.
- **Marginal:** No immediate violation, but loss of a critical element would cause a limit violation. Operator action needed.
- **At-Risk:** Operating limits are *already* being approached; immediate re-dispatch / load-shed required.

> The states are determined probabilistically using **LOLE**, **LOLP**, and **EENS** metrics on the generator + transmission availability model.

**(b) LOLE Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Capacity Outage Probability Table (COPT)** for 2 × 50 MW identical units with FOR = 0.02:

| State | Cap. Avail. (MW) | Cap. Out (MW) | Probability |
|-------|------------------|---------------|-------------|
| Both up | 100 | 0 | $(0.98)^2 = 0.9604$ |
| One down | 50 | 50 | $2(0.98)(0.02) = 0.0392$ |
| Both down | 0 | 100 | $(0.02)^2 = 0.0004$ |

**Step 2 — Compute LOLP in each load block:**

| Load (MW) | Duration (h) | Cap. Out Needed | $P(\text{loss})$ | LOLP·t (h) |
|-----------|--------------|-----------------|------------------|------------|
| 90 | 1000 | Both down | 0.0004 | 0.4 |
| 70 | 3000 | One or both down | $0.0392 + 0.0004 = 0.0396$ | 118.8 |
| 50 | 3000 | One or both down | 0.0396 | 118.8 |
| 30 | 2000 | Both down | 0.0004 | 0.8 |
| 10 | 1000 | Both down | 0.0004 | 0.4 |

> Note: For a load of 70 MW, even losing one 50 MW unit leaves only 50 MW available → 20 MW shortfall → both states count.

**Step 3 — Total LOLE:**

$$
\text{LOLE} = 0.4 + 118.8 + 118.8 + 0.8 + 0.4 = 239.2 \;\text{h/yr}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question B:** A common error is to use **only** the "both down" probability in *all* load blocks. For a load of 70 MW, the system *cannot* survive the loss of even **one** generator — so the probability of loss must include *both* "one down" *and* "both down" events. Missing this loses **3 marks**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- **Forced Outage Rate (FOR)** is the central probabilistic input for every transmission outage study.
- **MTTF + MTTR** govern FOR via the two-state Markov model.
- **N − 1 criterion** is the global operational norm: loss of any single element must not cause violations.
- **Three reliability indices** dominate KTU 2024 questions:
  - **LOLP** — probability (dimensionless).
  - **LOLE** — duration (h/yr or days/yr).
  - **EENS** — energy (MWh/yr).
- **System well-being** is a *categorical* extension: Healthy / Marginal / At-Risk.
- **Outage planning hierarchy** flows from long-term → annual → monthly → real-time.
- **Contingency analysis** uses DC load flow (fast) and AC load flow (accurate) for security screening.
- **Independence of units** is assumed in standard COPTs (Capacity Outage Probability Tables).
- **CCDF (Composite Customer Damage Function)** converts EENS into monetary interruption cost.
- **Cascading outages** are the principal mechanism behind large-scale blackouts — preventive security assessment is critical.
- **Always declare units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct marks otherwise.
- **Maintain a clear distinction** between probability (LOLP), duration (LOLE), and energy (EENS) — they are *not* interchangeable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** For the 14-mark question, structure your answer as **definition → formula → substitution → final answer with unit → interpretation**. This sequence alone is worth 2–3 extra marks under the 2024 OBE-based valuation scheme.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: An Overview of Security Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET741 - POWER SYSTEM OPERATION AND CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** An Overview of Security Analysis # Module 3 · An Overview of Security Analysis ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] **Security Analysis — KTU Board Definition** > **Power System Security** is the ability of a power system to withstand sudden, unforeseen disturbances (contingencies) such as the loss of a generator, transmission line, or transformer, and to continue operating without violating system operating constraints (voltage limits, line thermal limits, frequency limits). > > **Security Analysis** is the systematic, real-time computational procedure performed in the Energy Management System (EMS) to determine whether the *current operating state* of the system will remain feasible after the occurrence of a set of postulated contingencies. Mathematically, security analysis answers the question: $$\text{Is the system secure?} \Rightarrow \forall c \in \mathcal{C}, \; \mathbf{x}_0 \xrightarrow{\text{contingency } c} \mathbf{x}_c \in \mathcal{F}$$ where: - $\mathbf{x}_0$ = pre-contingency state vector - $\mathbf{x}_c$ = post-contingency state vector - $\mathcal{C}$ = set of credible contingencies - $\mathcal{F}$ = set of *feasible operating states* (all constraints satisfied) ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Airplane Pre-Flight Check" > [!NOTE] **Real-World Intuition (Airline Analogy)** > Imagine a commercial airplane preparing for takeoff. > - The **airplane** is the power system. > - The **pilots' pre-flight checklist** is *security analysis* — they simulate "what if an engine fails?" before they actually fly. > - The **weather conditions** are the *load demand* (varying, partly unpredictable). > - The **backup engine** is the *corrective control action* (e.g., rescheduling generation, switching a line). > > Just as the pilots don't want a passenger to die if one engine fails mid-air, the power system operator does not want a blackout if one line trips. Security analysis is the *offline simulation* of these "single point failures" so the operator is never caught off-guard. ### 1.3 The Five Operating States of a Power System > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU High-Yield Concept — Operating State Classification** > A power system can reside in **five mutually exclusive operating states** according to Dy-Liacco's classical framework (1967), which is the standard reference in KTU 2024 scheme modules. | State | Load | All Constraints | No Contingency | Action Required | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Normal (Secure)** | Serviced | Satisfied | No | None (monitor) | | **Alert (Insecure)** | Serviced | Satisfied now, but violation after contingency | Yes | Preventive Control | | **Emergency** | Serviced | Violated | — | Emergency Control | | **Restorative** | Partially serviced | Violated | — | Restorative Control | | **Extremis (In-extremis)** | Not serviced | Cascading failure | — | Load Shedding, Islanding | > [!NOTE] **Standard Metric (KTU)** > The classical **Dy-Liacco State Diagram** uses the two criteria: > 1. **Load = Generation + Losses** (load–generation balance) > 2. **All equipment within operating limits** (thermal, voltage, frequency) > > **Security** = being in the *Normal* state with respect to a specified list of contingencies. **Adequacy** = sufficient system capacity to meet demand in steady state (long-term planning). ### 1.4 Security vs Stability vs Reliability - **Reliability** = Adequacy + Security (umbrella term, long-term planning horizon) - **Security** = real-time, dynamic, online (operational, next 5–60 min) - **Stability** = ability to return to a *steady-state operating point* after a disturbance (electromechanical oscillation survival) ``` Reliability ├── Adequacy (planning horizon, "Do we have enough generation/transformers?") └── Security (operational horizon, "Will we survive a fault?") └── Stability (transient, small-signal, voltage, frequency) ``` # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Security Analysis Pipeline (EMS Function) > [!NOTE] **Why Security Analysis?** > A modern interconnected grid operates with **N-1 reliability criterion**: it must survive the loss of *any single* major component. Manually testing every line/generator outage is impossible — we use real-time software modules. The classical EMS security analysis pipeline consists of **four sequential stages**: ### Stage 1 — Data Acquisition (SCADA / State Estimator) - Telemetry arrives every 2–10 s from RTUs. - **State Estimator** filters bad data and computes the *best estimate* of the system state ($\hat{V}, \hat{\theta}$). ### Stage 2 — Contingency Definition - Build a **contingency list** $\mathcal{C}$ = {(line L₁ trip), (line L₂ trip), (gen G₁ trip), (transformer T₁ trip), …}. - The list for a 1000-bus system may contain **O(1500–3000)** single-element outages. ### Stage 3 — Contingency Selection (Screening) - A *fast but approximate* method ranks contingencies by severity. - Methods: **Performance Index (PI) ranking**, **second-order contingency screening**, **bounding methods**. - Output: top 50–200 most severe contingencies. ### Stage 4 — Contingency Evaluation - For each severe contingency, solve a full **AC load flow** (or DC load flow) and check for violations. - Output: violation report → operator action. ## 2.2 Static vs Dynamic Security Analysis | Aspect | Static Security | Dynamic Security | |---|---|---| | **Time horizon** | Steady state (post-disturbance equilibrium) | Transient (0.1 s – 30 s) | | **What is checked?** | Voltage, line MVA, transformer loading | Rotor angle, frequency, oscillation damping | | **Tools** | AC / DC load flow, contingency analysis | Time-domain simulation, EAC, EEAC | | **Speed** | Fast (ms per case) | Slow (s–min per case) | | **KTU focus** | **High (Module 3)** | Module 4 onward | > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU Module 3 (this section) primarily covers **Static Security Analysis**. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] **All Critical Equations for KTU 2024 Board Exam** ### 2.3.1 DC Load Flow Foundation $$ P_i = \sum_{j=1}^{N} B_{ij} (\theta_i - \theta_j) $$ In matrix form, with bus 1 as slack: $$ \mathbf{P} = \mathbf{B}' \boldsymbol{\theta} $$ where $\mathbf{B}'$ is the **DC susceptibility matrix** (purely imaginary admittances, real after extraction). ### 2.3.2 Line Flow Equation (Line $k$ connecting buses $i$–$j$) $$ P_{ij} = \frac{\theta_i - \theta_j}{x_{ij}} = B_{ij}(\theta_i - \theta_j) $$ ### 2.3.3 Power Transfer Distribution Factor (PTDF) Sensitivity of active power flow on line $\ell$ to a unit MW injection at bus $m$ and withdrawal at bus $n$: $$ \boxed{\;\text{PTDF}_{\ell,(m,n)} \;=\; \frac{\Delta P_\ell}{\Delta P_{mn}} \;=\; \frac{\partial \theta_i/\partial P_m \;-\; \partial \theta_j/\partial P_n}{x_{ij}}\;}$$ In compact matrix form: $$ \text{PTDF} = \mathbf{A} \, \mathbf{B}'^{-1} \;\to\; \text{PTDF matrix for all lines and all (m,n) pairs} $$ where $\mathbf{A}$ is the branch incidence matrix weighted by susceptances. ### 2.3.4 Line Outage Distribution Factor (LODF) If line $k$ (between buses $i$–$j$) is outaged, the new flow on line $\ell$ (between buses $p$–$q$) becomes: $$ \boxed{\; P_\ell^{\text{new}} \;=\; P_\ell^{0} \;+\; d_{\ell,k} \, P_k^{0} \;} $$ where the **LODF** is: $$ d_{\ell,k} = \frac{\text{PTDF}_{\ell,(i,j)}}{1 - \text{PTDF}_{k,(i,j)}} $$ ### 2.3.5 Performance Index (PI) for Ranking $$ \text{PI} = \sum_{l=1}^{NL} \left( \frac{P_l}{P_l^{\max}} \right)^{2n}, \quad n \in \{1, 2, 4, 8\} $$ > A line is "violated" when $P_l \geq P_l^{\max}$. The exponent $n$ sharpens the ranking — higher $n$ emphasizes worst violations. ### 2.3.6 AC Security Constraint (per bus $i$, per line $l$) $$ V_i^{\min} \leq V_i \leq V_i^{\max} \qquad S_l \leq S_l^{\max} \qquad f \in [f^{\min}, f^{\max}] $$ ### 2.3.7 Generator Outage Distribution Factor (GODF) — Reference $$ \text{GODF}_{\ell,g} = \frac{\text{PTDF}_{\ell,(g,\text{slack})}}{1 - \sum_{k \neq g} \text{PTDF}_{k,(g,\text{slack})}} $$ ## 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Formulas > [!NOTE] **Engineering Application** > - **PTDF/LODF** are used by **ISO/RTOs** (CAISO, ERCOT, MISO) for **available transfer capability (ATC)** calculations on **Open Access Same-Time Information System (OASIS)** portals. > - **PI ranking** is the backbone of **Real-Time Contingency Analysis (RTCA)** modules running in every major control center. > - **DC load flow** is preferred for screening because it is **linear** and **sparse**, enabling 1000+ contingency evaluations in under 1 minute. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — DC Load Flow Line Flow from Power Injections ### Starting Point (Physical Basis) For a lossless line between buses $i$ and $j$ with series reactance $x_{ij}$, the active power flow from $i \to j$ is: $$ P_{ij} = \frac{V_i V_j}{x_{ij}} \sin(\theta_i - \theta_j) $$ ### DC Approximation (3 assumptions) 1. $V_i = V_j = 1.0 \; \text{p.u.}$ (flat voltage profile) 2. $\sin(\theta_i - \theta_j) \approx (\theta_i - \theta_j)$ (small angle, $|\theta_i - \theta_j| \ll \pi/2$) 3. $r_{ij} \ll x_{ij}$ (neglect resistance) ### Resulting Linear Equation $$ P_{ij} = \frac{1}{x_{ij}} (\theta_i - \theta_j) = B_{ij} (\theta_i - \theta_j) $$ Summing all flows leaving bus $i$ (Kirchhoff's current law in power form): $$ P_i = \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} B_{ij} (\theta_i - \theta_j) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} B_{ij} \theta_i - \sum_{j=1}^{N} B_{ij} \theta_j $$ In matrix form, removing the slack row/column: $$ \mathbf{P} = \mathbf{B}' \, \boldsymbol{\theta} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boldsymbol{\theta} = \mathbf{B}'^{-1} \mathbf{P} $$ This is the **foundation of all fast security analysis**. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power Transfer Distribution Factor (PTDF) ### Setup We inject $\Delta P$ MW at bus $m$ and withdraw $\Delta P$ MW at bus $n$ (a "commercial transaction"). The resulting angle change at every bus is found from the DC load flow: $$ \Delta \boldsymbol{\theta} = \mathbf{B}'^{-1} \Delta \mathbf{P} $$ The change in flow on line $\ell$ (from bus $p$ to bus $q$) is: $$ \Delta P_\ell = \frac{1}{x_{pq}} (\Delta \theta_p - \Delta \theta_q) $$ Define the **sensitivity vector** for injection at bus $m$: $$ \mathbf{e}_m = \text{column } m \text{ of } \mathbf{B}'^{-1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta \theta_p = e_m^{(p)} \cdot \Delta P $$ Similarly for bus $n$: $$ \Delta \theta_q = e_n^{(q)} \cdot \Delta P $$ Substituting: $$ \Delta P_\ell = \frac{1}{x_{pq}} \left( e_m^{(p)} - e_n^{(q)} \right) \Delta P $$ Therefore: $$ \boxed{\; \text{PTDF}_{\ell,(m,n)} = \frac{1}{x_{pq}} \left[ e_m^{(p)} - e_n^{(q)} \right] = \frac{\Delta P_\ell}{\Delta P} \;} $$ > **Interpretation**: PTDF tells you, *in one number*, what fraction of a megawatt transferred from A to B will flow on a particular line. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Line Outage Distribution Factor (LODF) ### Problem Statement Line $k$ (between buses $i$–$j$) is suddenly tripped. Using the **compensation method** (equivalent DC injection at buses $i$ and $j$), the post-outage flow on line $\ell$ is computed. ### Pre-outage Flow on Line $k$ $$ P_k^{0} = \frac{1}{x_{ij}} (\theta_i - \theta_j) = B_{ij}(\theta_i - \theta_j) $$ ### Compensation Trick Tripping line $k$ is equivalent (in DC load flow) to *adding* a fictitious injection at bus $i$ and *withdrawal* at bus $j$ such that the new flow on line $k$ becomes zero: $$ \Delta P_{ij}^{\text{fict}} = -P_k^{0} $$ ### New Flow on Line $\ell$ $$ P_\ell^{\text{new}} = P_\ell^{0} + \text{PTDF}_{\ell,(i,j)} \cdot \Delta P_{ij}^{\text{fict}} $$ $$ P_\ell^{\text{new}} = P_\ell^{0} - \text{PTDF}_{\ell,(i,j)} \, P_k^{0} $$ ### Enforcing the Self-Consistency Condition When $\ell = k$ (the outaged line itself), $P_k^{\text{new}} = 0$: $$ 0 = P_k^{0} - \text{PTDF}_{k,(i,j)} \, P_k^{0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_k^{0} \left( 1 - \text{PTDF}_{k,(i,j)} \right) = 0 $$ The compensation $\Delta P_{ij}^{\text{fict}}$ is therefore **not simply $-P_k^{0}$**, but: $$ \Delta P_{ij}^{\text{fict}} = \frac{-P_k^{0}}{1 - \text{PTDF}_{k,(i,j)}} $$ ### Final LODF Expression $$ \boxed{\; P_\ell^{\text{new}} = P_\ell^{0} + d_{\ell,k} \, P_k^{0} \quad \text{where} \quad d_{\ell,k} = \frac{\text{PTDF}_{\ell,(i,j)}}{1 - \text{PTDF}_{k,(i,j)}} \;} $$ ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — LODF on a 3-Bus System ### Given - 3-bus system, line data (per unit reactance): $x_{12} = 0.2$, $x_{13} = 0.25$, $x_{23} = 0.4$. - Pre-outage power flows: $P_{12}^0 = 1.5$ p.u., $P_{13}^0 = 0.8$ p.u., $P_{23}^0 = -0.7$ p.u. - **Outage**: line 1–3 is tripped. ### Step 1 — Build the $B'$ Matrix $$ \mathbf{B}' = \begin{bmatrix} B_{12}+B_{13} & -B_{12} \\ -B_{12} & B_{12}+B_{23} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5.0 + 4.0 & -5.0 \\ -5.0 & 5.0 + 2.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 9.0 & -5.0 \\ -5.0 & 7.5 \end{bmatrix} $$ (using $B_{12}=1/0.2=5.0$, $B_{13}=1/0.25=4.0$, $B_{23}=1/0.4=2.5$) ### Step 2 — Inverse $$ \det(\mathbf{B}') = 9.0 \times 7.5 - (-5.0)^2 = 67.5 - 25 = 42.5 $$ $$ \mathbf{B}'^{-1} = \frac{1}{42.5} \begin{bmatrix} 7.5 & 5.0 \\ 5.0 & 9.0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1765 & 0.1176 \\ 0.1176 & 0.2118 \end{bmatrix} $$ ### Step 3 — PTDF for Injection at bus 1, Withdrawal at bus 2 (transaction 1→2) $$ \text{PTDF}_{12,(1,2)} = \frac{1}{x_{12}} \left[ e_1^{(1)} - e_2^{(2)} \right] = 5.0 \times (0.1765 - 0.2118) = 5.0 \times (-0.0353) = -0.1765 $$ $$ \text{PTDF}_{13,(1,2)} = \frac{1}{x_{13}} \left[ e_1^{(1)} - e_2^{(3)} \right] = 4.0 \times (0.1765 - 0.1176) = 4.0 \times 0.0588 = 0.2353 $$ ### Step 4 — LODF of line 1–2 due to outage of line 1–3 $$ d_{(1,2),(1,3)} = \frac{\text{PTDF}_{12,(1,3)}}{1 - \text{PTDF}_{13,(1,3)}} = \frac{-0.1765}{1 - 0.2353} = \frac{-0.1765}{0.7647} = -0.2308 $$ ### Step 5 — New Flow on Line 1–2 $$ P_{12}^{\text{new}} = P_{12}^{0} + d_{(1,2),(1,3)} \cdot P_{13}^{0} = 1.5 + (-0.2308)(0.8) = 1.5 - 0.1846 = 1.3154 \; \text{p.u.} $$ ### Result > **Line 1–2 carries 1.3154 p.u. after line 1–3 is outaged.** If the line rating is, say, 1.4 p.u., the system remains secure; if it is 1.3 p.u., there is a **post-contingency violation** requiring corrective action. ## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Performance Index for Contingency Ranking ### Why we need a Performance Index It is computationally expensive to run a full AC load flow for *every* contingency. We use a **scalar index** to rank contingencies. ### Definition (Second-Order Form) $$ \text{PI} = \sum_{l=1}^{NL} \left( \frac{P_l}{P_l^{\max}} \right)^{2} $$ If $\text{PI} \leq 1.0$, the system is secure. Contingencies are sorted by PI descending. ### Enhancement (Scalar, with Severity Weighting) $$ \text{PI} = \sum_{l=1}^{NL} w_l \left[ \max\left(0, \; P_l - P_l^{\max} \right) \right]^{2} $$ > The first form is used for *screening*; the second gives explicit overload magnitudes for the worst contingencies. ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Method Comparison | Method | Speed | Accuracy | Use Case | KTU Weight | |---|---|---|---|---| | **DC Load Flow + LODF** | Very Fast (ms) | Approximate (active only) | Real-time screening | ★★★★★ | | **DC Load Flow + Full AC check** | Fast | Better | Operator advisory | ★★★★ | | **Full AC Load Flow** | Slow (s) | Exact | Final evaluation of top 50 | ★★★ | | **PI Ranking (2nd order)** | Very Fast | Approximate | Pre-screening of 1000s | ★★★★ | | **Fast Decoupled Load Flow (FDLF)** | Fast | Good | Mid-tier screening | ★★★ | | **Time-Domain Simulation** | Very Slow | Exact (transient) | Dynamic security | ★ (Module 4) | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Dy-Liacco Operating State Diagram (Functional Flow) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Normal Normal --> Alert : Disturbance (contingency) detected Alert --> Normal : Preventive control Normal --> Emergency : Severe disturbance Alert --> Emergency : Severe disturbance Emergency --> Restorative : Stabilization Restorative --> Normal : Restoration complete Emergency --> Extremis : Cascading failure Extremis --> Emergency : Black-start / islanding Restorative --> [*] note right of Normal All constraints satisfied. Secure with respect to contingency list C end note note right of Alert Constraints still satisfied but post-contingency violations predicted end note note right of Emergency At least one constraint violated. Urgent corrective action needed end note ``` ## 4.2 EMS Security Analysis Functional Architecture (Block-Level Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[SCADA Telemetry

Contingency Evaluator] I -- No --> K[Mark as Safe] J --> L{Violations?} L -- Yes --> M[Operator Alarm
Suggested Action] L -- No --> K M --> N[Security Control

P_l_new vs P_l_max} G -- Yes --> H[Flag Violation] G -- No --> I[Secure] ``` ## 4.4 Operating States — Transition Trigger Matrix (Block View) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph N1[Normal Secure] S1[All constraints met
No contingencies in list violate] end subgraph N2[Alert Insecure] S2[Constraints met now
Post-contingency: violations] end subgraph N3[Emergency] S3[Active constraint violation
Equipment operating beyond limit] end subgraph N4[Restorative] S4[Partial service restored
Equipment in limits, load shedding] end subgraph N5[Extremis] S5[Cascading failure imminent
System disintegration] end N1 -- Preventive control --> N1 N1 -- New disturbance --> N2 N2 -- Emergency control --> N1 N2 -- Severe event --> N3 N3 -- Fast shedding --> N4 N3 -- Cascade spreads --> N5 N4 -- Restoration --> N1 N5 -- Black start --> N4 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Security Analysis Pitfalls** > 1. **Forgetting to recompute the LODF denominator** $\left(1 - \text{PTDF}_{k,(i,j)}\right)$. The compensation method requires the self-consistency correction — full marks lost if omitted. > 2. **Confusing PTDF and LODF**: PTDF is for *injection changes*; LODF is for *line outages*. Students mix them up frequently — 2–3 marks deducted per occurrence. > 3. **Wrong reference for angle changes**: PTDF uses $\mathbf{B}'^{-1}$ (slack-reduced), not the full admittance matrix. > 4. **Mixing operating states**: KTU expects the *5-state* Dy-Liacco model; using a 3-state or 4-state diagram loses the "restorative" or "extremis" definition marks. > 5. **Units confusion**: PTDF and LODF are *dimensionless ratios*; writing them in p.u. is correct for relative flows but not for absolute MW — clarify which. > 6. **No mention of "credible contingency"**: Always state that the list $\mathcal{C}$ is a *curated* subset of single-element outages (N-1), not all possible faults. --- ## 📘 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Define power system security. Differentiate between static and dynamic security analysis.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Model Answer:** **Power system security** is defined as the ability of a power system to withstand sudden disturbances such as the loss of a generator, transmission line, or transformer, while continuing to operate without violating equipment thermal, voltage, or frequency limits. | Aspect | Static Security | Dynamic Security | |---|---|---| | Time scale | Steady-state (post-disturbance equilibrium) | Transient (0.1 s – 30 s) | | Checked variables | Voltage magnitudes, line MVA flows | Rotor angles, frequency, damping | | Tools | DC / AC load flow, contingency analysis | Time-domain integration, EAC/EEAC | | Computational cost | Low | High | *(3 marks: 1.5 for definition + 1.5 for comparison)* ### **Q2. List the five operating states of a power system as per Dy-Liacco. What is the role of preventive control?** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The **five operating states** are: 1. **Normal (Secure)** — all constraints satisfied, no contingencies in list cause violations. 2. **Alert (Insecure)** — current state feasible, but post-contingency violations predicted. 3. **Emergency** — one or more constraints actively violated. 4. **Restorative** — load partially served, system recovering after emergency. 5. **Extremis (In-extremis)** — cascading failure, system disintegrating. **Preventive control** is the set of corrective actions (generation rescheduling, phase-shifter tap change, line switching) taken while the system is in the **Alert** state to bring it back to the **Normal** state, *before* the contingency actually occurs. *(3 marks: 2 for states list + 1 for preventive control definition)* --- ## 📕 Part B Questions — 14 Marks (Internal Choice) > **INSTRUCTION (KTU Standard):** *Answer ANY ONE full question from the Module 3 choice. Each question carries 14 marks divided into two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.* --- ### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, KTU Model Paper]` **Q. (a)** Explain the four stages of security analysis as performed in a modern Energy Management System (EMS). What is meant by a "contingency"? Why is *contingency selection* necessary? (7 marks) **(b)** For the 3-bus system shown below, compute the **LODF** of line 1–2 with respect to the outage of line 1–3. Given: $x_{12}=0.2$ p.u., $x_{13}=0.25$ p.u., $x_{23}=0.4$ p.u. Pre-outage flow $P_{12}^0 = 1.5$ p.u., $P_{13}^0 = 0.8$ p.u.
