$$G(x^{(k)}) \, \Delta x = H^T(x^{(k)}) W [z - h(x^{(k)})]$$

with the **gain matrix**:

$$G(x^{(k)}) = H^T(x^{(k)}) W H(x^{(k)})$$

**Step 3 — Iterate until convergence**:

$$x^{(k+1)} = x^{(k)} + [G(x^{(k)})]^{-1} H^T(x^{(k)}) W [z - h(x^{(k)})]$$

Stop when $\vert \Delta x \vert_\infty < \epsilon$ (e.g., $\epsilon = 10^{-4}$ pu and $10^{-4}$ rad).

## 3.3 Derivation: A Posteriori Covariance of the State Estimate

From the linearised model at convergence:

$$\hat{x} = x^{true} + [H^T W H]^{-1} H^T W e$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Cov}(\hat{x}) = E[(\hat{x} - x^{true})(\hat{x} - x^{true})^T] = [H^T W H]^{-1} H^T W R W H [H^T W H]^{-1}$$

With $W = R^{-1}$:

$$\text{Cov}(\hat{x}) = G^{-1} H^T W R W H G^{-1} = G^{-1}$$

> **Engineering meaning**: The standard deviation of the $k$-th estimated state is $\sqrt{(G^{-1})_{kk}}$. This is the rigorous **uncertainty** delivered by the estimator.

## 3.4 Derivation: Measurement Residual Covariance

The residual vector at convergence is:

$$\hat{r} = z - h(\hat{x}) \approx [I - H G^{-1} H^T W] e$$

Define the **residual sensitivity matrix** $S$ and the **residual covariance** $\Omega$:

$$S = I - H G^{-1} H^T W$$

$$\Omega = E[\hat{r} \, \hat{r}^T] = S R S^T = R - H G^{-1} H^T$$

**Step-by-step verification of $\Omega$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Omega &= (I - H G^{-1} H^T W) \, R \, (I - W H G^{-1} H^T)^T \\
&= R - H G^{-1} H^T W R - R W H G^{-1} H^T + H G^{-1} H^T W R W H G^{-1} H^T \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute $W = R^{-1}$ (so $WR = I$ and $RW = I$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Omega &= R - H G^{-1} H^T - H G^{-1} H^T + H G^{-1} (H^T W H) G^{-1} H^T \\
&= R - 2 H G^{-1} H^T + H G^{-1} G G^{-1} H^T \\
&= R - 2 H G^{-1} H^T + H G^{-1} H^T \\
&= R - H G^{-1} H^T
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result**: $\Omega = R - H G^{-1} H^T$, a symmetric positive-semi-definite matrix whose diagonal gives the variance of each residual.

## 3.5 Derivation: Bad-Data Detection Using Normalised Residuals

The **normalised residual** of measurement $i$ is:

$$\hat{r}_i^N = \frac{\hat{r}_i}{\sqrt{\Omega_{ii}}} = \frac{z_i - h_i(\hat{x})}{\sqrt{R_{ii} - (H G^{-1} H^T)_{ii}}}$$

Decision rule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } \vert \hat{r}_i^N \vert > \tau \;\; \text{(e.g., } \tau = 3.0\text{)} &\Rightarrow \text{Measurement } i \text{ is suspected bad data.} \\
\text{If } J(\hat{x}) > \chi^2_{m-n, \, 1-\alpha} &\Rightarrow \text{Bad data present (overall).}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A **single bad measurement** produces a single large $\hat{r}_i^N$. **Multiple interacting bad measurements** may *mask* (cancel) each other in the residuals — this is the famous **"masking effect"** and is why **Identification** is harder than **Detection**.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Error Source vs. Mathematical Signature vs. Mitigation

| Error Source | Mathematical Signature | Effect on $J(\hat{x})$ | Effect on $\hat{r}$ | Detection Tool |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Random noise | $\sigma_i$ inflated, $e_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma_i^2)$ | Small, finite baseline | Small Gaussian residuals | None needed (WLS absorbs it) |
| Gross error (single) | Single $e_k$ with $\vert e_k \vert \gg 0$ | $J(\hat{x}) \gg \chi^2_{m-n}$ | One dominant $\hat{r}_k$ | Largest $\vert \hat{r}_i^N \vert$ |
| Multiple bad data (masking) | Several $e_i$ cancelling | $J(\hat{x})$ elevated | Multiple moderate residuals | Hypothesis testing, ELD test |
| Topology error | Wrong $h(x)$ structure | High $J(\hat{x})$ | Cluster of correlated large residuals | Topological estimator / $\chi^2 + $rule-base |
| Parameter error | Wrong $H$ matrix entries | Mildly elevated $J$ | Correlated residuals in a zone | Augmented state estimator |
| Communication outage | Missing $z_i$ | Lower redundancy | N/A | Observability analysis |

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Illustration — Effect of a Gross Error

Consider a 3-bus system with $n = 5$ state variables, $m = 8$ measurements. Assume $J(\hat{x})$ at the WLS solution without bad data equals **6.2**, while the chi-square threshold at 95 % and $k = 8 - 5 = 3$ is:

$$\chi^2_{3, 0.95} = 7.815$$

With **no bad data**, $J(\hat{x}) = 6.2 < 7.815$ → estimator passes the test.
If one measurement is corrupted so that $J(\hat{x})$ jumps to **15.4**, then $15.4 > 7.815$ → **bad data detected**. We then compute normalised residuals, identify and remove the offending measurement, and re-run the estimator.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Sources of Error in State Estimation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real AC Power System] --> B[Instrument Transformers CT and PT]
    B --> C[A to D Converters and RTU]
    C --> D[Communication Network SCADA]
    D --> E[EMS Front End Processor]
    E --> F[State Estimator Core]
    F --> G[Best Estimate xhat]
    G --> H[Downstream Apps AGC, OPF, CA]

    E1[Error 1: Random Gaussian Noise] -.->|small zero mean| B
    E2[Error 2: Gross Error Bad Data] -.->|large bias| C
    E3[Error 3: Telemetry Bit Error] -.->|packet loss or skew| D
    E4[Error 4: Topology Error] -.->|wrong breaker status| E
    E5[Error 5: Parameter Error] -.->|wrong impedance in DB| F
    E6[Error 6: Modelling Error] -.->|DC instead of AC| F

    F --> T1[Bad Data Detection Chi Square Test]
    T1 --> T2[Normalised Residual Test]
    T2 --> T3[Identification and Removal]
    T3 --> F
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — WLS Estimator with Error-Handling Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Measurement Vector z] --> Q[Quality Check Module]
    Q --> O[Observability Analysis]
    O --> S[State Estimation WLS]
    S --> C{Converged}
    C -- No --> S
    C -- Yes --> BDD[Bad Data Detection]
    BDD --> D{Bad Data Found}
    D -- Yes --> R[Remove or Correct]
    R --> S
    D -- No --> OUT[Best Estimate xhat]
    OUT --> APP[Applications AGC, ED, CA]
```

## 4.3 Interpretation of the Diagrams

> [!TIP]
> The first diagram shows the **physical path** a measurement travels from the live system to the estimator — every arrow is a **potential injection point of error**. The second diagram shows the **algorithmic loop** that detects and removes bad data, iteratively restoring the integrity of the estimate.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. Students often confuse the **measurement vector dimension $m$** with the **state dimension $n$**; remember $m > n$ and the redundancy is $m - n$.
> 2. Many students write $W = R$ instead of $W = R^{-1}$. **The weight matrix is the INVERSE of the error covariance.** Losing 1–2 marks here is common.
> 3. For the chi-square test, the **degrees of freedom is $k = m - n$**, NOT $m$ or $n$ alone.
> 4. Topological errors are often missed because students only think of "measurement" errors — be sure to **mention topology** explicitly in any "sources of error" question.
> 5. Normalised residuals use the **diagonal of $\Omega = R - H G^{-1} H^T$** in the denominator, not just $R_{ii}$ alone.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**List and briefly explain any three sources of error in power system state estimation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Random measurement errors** (1 mark): Arise from thermal noise and quantisation in CTs/PTs and A/D converters. Modelled as zero-mean Gaussian with variance $\sigma_i^2$.
2. **Gross errors / Bad data** (1 mark): Large deviations in one or more measurements due to instrument failure, drift, or RTU malfunction. Detected via normalised residual test.
3. **Topological errors** (1 mark): Wrong status of a circuit breaker reported to the estimator. Causes a mismatch between the actual network and the model used in $h(x)$. Detected using $\chi^2$ test followed by topology-rule-based analysis.

*(Alternative valid sources: Parameter errors, Communication errors, Modelling errors — any three accepted with explanation.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Why is the measurement redundancy $(m - n)$ important for detecting errors in state estimation?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- The redundancy $m - n$ equals the **degrees of freedom** of the $\chi^2$ test on $J(\hat{x})$ (1 mark).
- With higher redundancy, the WLS estimator can **average out random errors** more effectively, reducing $\text{Cov}(\hat{x}) = G^{-1}$ (1 mark).
- A common design rule is $m \ge 2n$ to ensure that **single bad data can be identified** after detection; with $m < 2n$, masking of bad data becomes likely (1 mark).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Error Modelling and WLS Formulation

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the **Weighted Least Squares (WLS) state estimator** for an AC network starting from the measurement model $z = h(x) + e$. State clearly the assumption on $e$ and explain the role of the weight matrix $W$. (7 marks)

**(b)** For a 4-bus system, the measurement vector contains $m = 10$ measurements and the state dimension is $n = 7$. If the WLS objective at convergence is $J(\hat{x}) = 18.7$, comment on the **presence of bad data** using a $\chi^2$ test at the 95 % confidence level. What is the next step in the algorithm? (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Measurement model and Gaussian assumption** (1 mark):

$$z = h(x) + e, \qquad e_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_i^2), \; e_i \text{ independent.}$$

**Step 2 — Likelihood function** (1 mark):

$$f(z \mid x) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_i^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{[z_i - h_i(x)]^2}{2\sigma_i^2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Log-likelihood and simplification** (1 mark):

$$\ln f = -\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\frac{[z_i - h_i(x)]^2}{\sigma_i^2} + \text{const.}$$

**Step 4 — WLS objective** (1 mark):

$$J(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{m}\frac{[z_i - h_i(x)]^2}{\sigma_i^2} = [z - h(x)]^T W [z - h(x)]$$

**Step 5 — Role of $W$** (1 mark): $W = R^{-1} = \text{diag}(1/\sigma_i^2)$ assigns more importance to **more accurate** measurements (smaller $\sigma_i \Rightarrow$ larger weight).

**Step 6 — Optimality condition and Gauss-Newton iteration** (2 marks):

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial x} = -2 H^T W [z - h(x)] = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; G \Delta x = H^T W [z - h(x)], \quad G = H^T W H$$

Iterate $x^{(k+1)} = x^{(k)} + \Delta x$ until convergence.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Degrees of freedom** (1 mark):

$$k = m - n = 10 - 7 = 3$$

**Step 2 — Chi-square critical value** (1 mark, value from standard statistical table):

$$\chi^2_{3, 0.95} = 7.815$$

**Step 3 — Comparison** (1 mark):

$$J(\hat{x}) = 18.7 \;\;\text{vs.}\;\; 7.815 \;\Rightarrow\; J(\hat{x}) \gg \chi^2_{3, 0.95}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion** (1 mark): The hypothesis "no bad data" is **rejected** at 95 % confidence. **Bad data is present**.

**Step 5 — Next step: Identification** (1 mark): Compute the residual vector $\hat{r} = z - h(\hat{x})$ and the residual covariance $\Omega = R - H G^{-1} H^T$. Compute the normalised residuals:

$$\hat{r}_i^N = \frac{\hat{r}_i}{\sqrt{\Omega_{ii}}}$$

**Step 6 — Identify and remove** (1 mark): The measurement with the largest $\vert \hat{r}_i^N \vert$ (typically exceeding threshold 3.0) is flagged as bad. Remove it from $z$ and **re-run** the WLS estimator.

**Step 7 — Re-test** (1 mark): After removal, recompute $J(\hat{x})$; if it now falls below $\chi^2_{3, 0.95}$, the estimate is accepted.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Sources of Error — Taxonomy and Detection

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Classify the **sources of error in state estimation of an AC network** into three families. For each family, give two examples, the mathematical signature in $z = h(x) + e$, and the method used to detect or mitigate it. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain in detail the difference between **Detection** and **Identification** of bad data. Why does the masking effect make identification difficult, and what techniques overcome it? (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Family 1 — Random / Stochastic Errors** (≈ 2.3 marks):
- *Examples*: CT/PT thermal noise, A/D quantisation.
- *Signature*: $e_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_i^2)$, zero mean, independent.
- *Mitigation*: WLS averaging; the *a posteriori* variance $\text{Cov}(\hat{x}) = G^{-1}$ decreases with higher redundancy.

**Family 2 — Gross Errors / Bad Data** (≈ 2.3 marks):
- *Examples*: Failed transducer, drifted calibration, stuck meter.
- *Signature*: One (or few) $e_i$ with large magnitude, possibly biased.
- *Detection*: Chi-square test on $J(\hat{x})$; largest normalised residual test.

**Family 3 — Structural / Systematic Errors** (≈ 2.3 marks):
- *Examples*: Stuck breaker (topology), wrong line impedance (parameter), wrong tap ratio.
- *Signature*: Wrong $h(x)$ or wrong $H(x)$; correlated residuals across a region; bias in $\hat{x}$.
- *Detection/mitigation*: Topological estimator, augmented state estimation, parameter estimation, observability-aware bad-data processing.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Detection** (2 marks): The binary question — "Is bad data present?" Answered by the **chi-square test** on $J(\hat{x})$:
$$J(\hat{x}) = \sum_i w_i \hat{r}_i^2 \;\;\text{vs.}\;\; \chi^2_{m-n, 1-\alpha}$$
If $J(\hat{x})$ exceeds the threshold, bad data is *suspected*.

**Identification** (2 marks): The localisation question — "Which measurement(s) is/are bad?" Answered by **normalised residual** analysis:
$$\hat{r}_i^N = \frac{\hat{r}_i}{\sqrt{\Omega_{ii}}}$$
Measurements with $\vert \hat{r}_i^N \vert > \tau$ (e.g., $\tau = 3$) are flagged.

**Masking Effect** (2 marks): When **multiple** interacting bad measurements exist, their residuals can **partially cancel** in the WLS objective, making the largest normalised residual **smaller than the true bad data's contribution**. A naive "remove the largest" rule can fail or misidentify.

**Techniques to overcome masking** (1 mark):
- **Largest Normalised Residual (LNR) test** with re-estimation.
- **Measurement Compensation**: Replace $z_k$ with $h_k(\hat{x})$ and re-estimate.
- **Nonquadratic (robust) estimators**: e.g., WLAV (Least Absolute Value), Schweppe-Huber generalised-maximum-likelihood (GM) estimator — these downweight outliers automatically.
- **Hypothesis Testing Identification (HTI)** with combinatorial search (for small $m$).

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- [x] **State vector $x$** of an $N$-bus AC system has dimension $2N - 1$: $N - 1$ phase angles (slack fixed) and $N$ voltage magnitudes.
- [x] **Measurement model**: $z = h(x) + e$; $h(x)$ is nonlinear (AC power flow equations).
- [x] **WLS objective**: $J(x) = [z - h(x)]^T W [z - h(x)]$; $W = R^{-1}$.
- [x] **Normal equation**: $G \Delta x = H^T W \Delta z$ with $G = H^T W H$.
- [x] **A posteriori covariance**: $\text{Cov}(\hat{x}) = G^{-1}$.
- [x] **Three families of errors**: Random, Gross, Structural.
- [x] **Random errors** are zero-mean Gaussian; WLS = MLE.
- [x] **Gross errors** produce one or few large residuals; detected by $\chi^2$ test and normalised residuals.
- [x] **Topological errors** (stuck breakers) alter $h(x)$ and produce correlated residuals.
- [x] **Parameter errors** alter $H(x)$ and require Augmented State Estimation.
- [x] **Detection** uses $J(\hat{x})$ vs. $\chi^2_{m-n, 1-\alpha}$.
- [x] **Identification** uses largest $\vert \hat{r}_i^N \vert$ where $\Omega = R - H G^{-1} H^T$.
- [x] **Masking effect** → use robust (WLAV / GM) estimators or measurement compensation.
- [x] **Practical redundancy rule**: $m \ge 2n$ for reliable bad-data processing.
- [x] **Degrees of freedom** of $\chi^2$ test $= m - n$, NOT $m$ or $n$.
- [x] Always use the **full AC model** — DC approximation introduces modelling error.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Detection and Identification of Bad Measurements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET741 - POWER SYSTEM OPERATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Detection and Identification of Bad Measurements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Bad Data (or Bad Measurement)** in the context of power system state estimation refers to any measurement that contains a significant error beyond the expected random noise, caused by factors such as instrument failure, communication errors, transducer malfunction, or temporary faults in the metering infrastructure. The *detection* of bad data is the statistical process of determining whether bad data exists in the measurement set, while *identification* is the process of pinpointing which specific measurement(s) is/are erroneous.

In the Weighted Least Squares (WLS) state estimation framework, the measurement model is expressed as:

$$z = Hx + e$$

where $z$ is the $(m \times 1)$ measurement vector, $H$ is the $(m \times n)$ Jacobian matrix relating measurements to state variables, $x$ is the $(n \times 1)$ state vector (bus voltage magnitudes and phase angles), and $e$ is the $(m \times 1)$ measurement error vector assumed to follow a normal distribution $\mathcal{N}(0, R)$ with covariance matrix $R$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Bad data detection and identification is the cornerstone of a robust Energy Management System (EMS). Without it, the operator's Real-Time Contingency Analysis, Optimal Power Flow, and Automatic Generation Control decisions would be based on contaminated network snapshots — potentially leading to unsafe operating conditions.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are weighing 10 different bags of rice on a digital scale, and you write down each weight. The true weight of each bag is unknown, but the sum of all bags should match a known total. If one bag's recorded weight is absurdly high (e.g., the scale gave "1000 kg" instead of "10 kg"), you can immediately *detect* that something is wrong with the measurements by simply checking if the recorded sum equals the known total. The mismatch tells you bad data exists. Now, to *identify* which bag is the culprit, you re-check each weight, or run a cross-validation: if you remove bag #7 from the data and the totals suddenly balance, then bag #7's reading was the bad one.

This is exactly what power system state estimation does. The measurements are redundant (the system is *over-determined* — more measurements than unknowns), and this redundancy is exploited to both detect and identify bad data.

### Physical and Statistical Constants

- **Degrees of freedom** $k = m - n$, where $m$ is the number of measurements and $n$ is the number of state variables. This determines the chi-square distribution used for hypothesis testing.
- **Confidence level** $\alpha$, typically chosen as **0.05** (95% confidence) for detection thresholding.
- **Standard normal threshold** for normalized residual tests is typically set at **3.0** (corresponding to 99.7% confidence for a normal distribution).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box — State Estimation Residuals**
> A measurement residual is the difference between the actual measurement and the estimated value computed from the state vector. Residuals form the basis of ALL bad data analysis. Under normal (no bad data) conditions, residuals are small and follow a known statistical distribution. When bad data is present, the corrupted measurement forces a noticeable shift in one or more residuals.

### Why Bad Data Detection is Critical for KTU Engineers

Modern power grids carry millions of measurements per minute through SCADA, PMUs, and smart meters. Even a 0.1% bad data rate translates to thousands of faulty readings per hour. Without robust bad data processing, a control center operator could:
- Misjudge line loading
- Initiate false contingency alarms
- Dispatch incorrect generation
- Trigger unnecessary load shedding

Hence, mastering this topic is essential for any power systems engineer entering the smart grid era.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Three-Stage Bad Data Processing Architecture

The complete bad data processing pipeline in a WLS-based state estimator consists of three logical stages:

**Stage 1 — State Estimation (WLS)**
The WLS estimator minimizes the weighted sum of squared measurement errors:

$$\min_{x} J(x) = [z - Hx]^T R^{-1} [z - Hx]$$

The optimal state estimate $\hat{x}$ is obtained from the normal equations:

$$\hat{x} = (H^T R^{-1} H)^{-1} H^T R^{-1} z$$

The *gain matrix* $G = H^T R^{-1} H$ is symmetric and positive definite (assuming measurement redundancy).

**Stage 2 — Bad Data Detection (Existence Test)**
A statistical hypothesis test determines whether the measurement set contains any bad data. Two principal methods:
- **Chi-Square ($\chi^2$) Test** on the objective function $J(\hat{x})$
- **Largest Normalized Residual Test**

**Stage 3 — Bad Data Identification (Location Test)**
Once bad data is confirmed, the offending measurement(s) must be identified. The two standard identification methods are:
- **Largest Normalized Residual Method** (a measurement is flagged if its normalized residual exceeds threshold)
- **Hypothesis Testing Identification** (using a suspicion index or composite test)

### 2.2 Why These Methods Work — The Underlying Logic

- **Why chi-square on $J(\hat{x})$?** When no bad data exists, $J(\hat{x})$ follows a $\chi^2$ distribution with $k = m - n$ degrees of freedom. So a high value of $J(\hat{x})$ signals that the data is statistically inconsistent with the assumed model.
- **Why normalize residuals?** Raw residuals have different variances depending on measurement type and location. Normalization divides by standard deviation, making them comparable. The normalized residual follows a standard normal distribution $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$ under the null hypothesis.
- **Why use the largest residual?** In a system with a single bad measurement, that measurement's influence dominates the residual pattern, making its normalized residual the largest.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Measurement Model | $z = Hx + e$ | $H$: Jacobian, $e$: error | $e \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$ |
| 2 | WLS State Estimate | $\hat{x} = (H^T R^{-1} H)^{-1} H^T R^{-1} z$ | $R^{-1}$: weight matrix | Dimension: $(n \times 1)$ |
| 3 | Objective Function | $J(\hat{x}) = r^T R^{-1} r$ | $r = z - H\hat{x}$ | $\sim \chi^2_{m-n}$ under $H_0$ |
| 4 | Measurement Residual | $r = z - H\hat{x}$ | $r \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times 1}$ | Dimension: $(m \times 1)$ |
| 5 | Residual Sensitivity Matrix | $W = I - H(H^T R^{-1} H)^{-1} H^T R^{-1}$ | $W \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$ | Symmetric, idempotent |
| 6 | Residual Covariance | $\Omega = W R$ | $\Omega \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$ | Diagonal $\Omega_{ii}$ used |
| 7 | Normalized Residual | $r_i^N = \dfrac{r_i}{\sqrt{\Omega_{ii}}}$ | Index $i$ for measurement | $\sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$ under $H_0$ |
| 8 | Detection Threshold | $J(\hat{x}) > \chi^2_{m-n,\, \alpha}$ | $\alpha$: significance | $\alpha = 0.05$ typically |
| 9 | Identification Threshold | $\vert r_i^N \vert > \tau$ | $\tau$: detection threshold | $\tau = 3.0$ for $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$ |
| 10 | Degrees of Freedom | $k = m - n$ | $m$: measurements, $n$: states | $k \geq 1$ required |
| 11 | Measurement Error | $e = z - Hx_{true}$ | $x_{true}$: true state | Mean = 0, Var = $R$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas power every real-time state estimator in modern control centers. The $\chi^2$ test gives a *global* "yes/no" answer (does any bad data exist?), while normalized residuals give a *local* answer (which measurement is suspect?). Both are required for a complete, robust system.

### 2.4 Detection vs. Identification — A Key Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> **Detection** = a binary decision: "Is bad data present in the measurement set?" (Yes/No)
> **Identification** = a classification task: "If bad data is present, which measurement(s) is/are faulty?" (Point to the culprit)
> A system can be excellent at detection but poor at identification (e.g., when multiple bad measurements *mask* each other — a phenomenon called *interacting bad data*).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the WLS State Estimator

We seek to minimize the weighted sum of squared errors:

$$J(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{(z_i - \sum_{j=1}^{n} H_{ij} x_j)^2}{\sigma_i^2} = [z - Hx]^T R^{-1} [z - Hx]$$

where $R = \text{diag}(\sigma_1^2, \sigma_2^2, \ldots, \sigma_m^2)$.

**Step 1 — Expand the objective function in matrix form:**

$$J(x) = z^T R^{-1} z - z^T R^{-1} Hx - x^T H^T R^{-1} z + x^T H^T R^{-1} H x$$

**Step 2 — Since $J(x)$ is a scalar, the scalar terms equal their transpose:**

$$J(x) = z^T R^{-1} z - 2 x^T H^T R^{-1} z + x^T H^T R^{-1} H x$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate $J(x)$ with respect to $x$ and set to zero (first-order optimality condition):**

$$\frac{\partial J(x)}{\partial x} = -2 H^T R^{-1} z + 2 H^T R^{-1} H x = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve the normal equations for the optimal state estimate:**

$$\begin{aligned}
H^T R^{-1} H \hat{x} &= H^T R^{-1} z \\
\hat{x} &= (H^T R^{-1} H)^{-1} H^T R^{-1} z
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Weighted Least Squares (WLS)** estimate, also known as the **Best Linear Unbiased Estimator (BLUE)** under Gaussian noise assumptions.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Residual Sensitivity Matrix

The residual vector is defined as:

$$r = z - H\hat{x}$$

Substituting the WLS estimate $\hat{x}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
r &= z - H \left[ (H^T R^{-1} H)^{-1} H^T R^{-1} z \right] \\
r &= \left[ I - H (H^T R^{-1} H)^{-1} H^T R^{-1} \right] z \\
r &= W \cdot z
\end{aligned}$$

where $W = I - H (H^T R^{-1} H)^{-1} H^T R^{-1}$ is the **residual sensitivity matrix** (also called the "residual maker" matrix). It is symmetric and idempotent ($W^2 = W$), with rank $k = m - n$.

**Important property:** $W H = 0$, meaning $W$ projects $z$ onto the null space of $H^T$, filtering out the state information and leaving only the "inconsistency" component.

### 3.3 Derivation of Residual Covariance Matrix

Since $z = Hx + e$ and $r = Wz$:

$$\begin{aligned}
r &= W(Hx + e) = WHx + We = We \quad (\text{since } WH = 0) \\
\text{Cov}(r) &= E[rr^T] = E[(We)(We)^T] = W E[ee^T] W^T = W R W^T
\end{aligned}$$

Since $W$ is symmetric and idempotent ($W = W^T$, $W^2 = W$):

$$\text{Cov}(r) = W R W = W R$$

Therefore, the covariance matrix of the residual is:

$$\Omega = W R$$

The **variance of the $i$-th residual** is:

$$\text{Var}(r_i) = \Omega_{ii} = W_{ii} R_{ii}$$

since $W$ and $R$ are typically diagonal-dominant.

### 3.4 Derivation of the Normalized Residual

The $i$-th raw residual $r_i$ has variance $\Omega_{ii}$. To compare residuals on a common scale, we divide by the standard deviation:

$$r_i^N = \frac{r_i}{\sqrt{\Omega_{ii}}}$$

Under the null hypothesis (no bad data) and assuming Gaussian errors, $r_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \Omega_{ii})$, so:

$$r_i^N \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$$

This standardization allows us to use a single threshold (typically **3.0**) for all measurements regardless of type, location, or measurement variance.

### 3.5 The Chi-Square Detection Test — Hypothesis Formulation

We define two competing hypotheses:

- **Null hypothesis** $H_0$: No bad data present. All measurement errors are normal: $e \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$.
- **Alternative hypothesis** $H_1$: Bad data present. At least one measurement has error exceeding normal noise.

The test statistic is $J(\hat{x}) = r^T R^{-1} r$. We claim:

$$J(\hat{x}) \sim \chi^2_{m-n}$$

**Why chi-square?** Because $r = We$, and $e^T R^{-1} e \sim \chi^2_m$ (sum of $m$ independent standard normals squared). But $r$ lies in a subspace of dimension $k = m - n$ (the null space of $H^T$), so the effective degrees of freedom is reduced to $m - n$.

**Decision rule:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{If } J(\hat{x}) \leq \chi^2_{m-n, \alpha} &\Rightarrow \text{Accept } H_0 \text{ (no bad data)} \\
\text{If } J(\hat{x}) > \chi^2_{m-n, \alpha} &\Rightarrow \text{Reject } H_0 \text{ (bad data present)}
\end{aligned}$$

where $\chi^2_{m-n, \alpha}$ is the critical value from the chi-square distribution with $m - n$ degrees of freedom at significance level $\alpha$.

### 3.6 The Largest Normalized Residual Identification Test

Once bad data is confirmed, we compute the normalized residual for every measurement and find the largest one in magnitude:

$$r_{max}^N = \max_{i} \vert r_i^N \vert$$

**Decision rule:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{If } r_{max}^N \leq \tau &\Rightarrow \text{No bad data identified} \\
\text{If } r_{max}^N > \tau &\Rightarrow \text{Measurement with } r_{max}^N \text{ is bad}
\end{aligned}$$

The threshold $\tau$ is chosen based on the desired confidence level. For 99.7% confidence, $\tau = 3.0$. After removing the identified bad measurement, the state estimation and detection are re-run until no further bad data is detected.

### 3.7 Worked Analytical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A 3-bus system has $m = 6$ measurements and $n = 4$ state variables. The WLS state estimation yields an objective function value $J(\hat{x}) = 14.2$. At 5% significance level, determine whether bad data exists.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute degrees of freedom: $k = m - n = 6 - 4 = 2$.

Step 2 — Look up chi-square critical value: $\chi^2_{2, 0.05} = 5.991$.

Step 3 — Compare:

$$J(\hat{x}) = 14.2 > 5.991 = \chi^2_{2, 0.05}$$

Step 4 — Decision: Reject $H_0$. **Bad data is present in the measurement set.**

Step 5 — Proceed to identification using normalized residuals to pinpoint the offending measurement.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Pitfall:** Students often forget to subtract the number of state variables from the measurements when computing degrees of freedom. Always use $k = m - n$, NOT $k = m$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Bad Data Processing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Measurements z from SCADA and PMU] --> B[Weighted Least Squares State Estimator]
    B --> C[Compute State Estimate x hat using H transpose R inverse H inverse H transpose R inverse z]
    C --> D[Compute Residual Vector r equals z minus H x hat]
    D --> E[Compute Residual Covariance Omega equals W R]
    E --> F[Stage 1 Chi Square Test on J of x hat]
    F --> G{Is J of x hat greater than chi square critical value}
    G -->|No| H[Output Clean State Estimate to EMS Applications]
    G -->|Yes| I[Stage 2 Compute Normalized Residuals r N subscript i]
    I --> J[Find Largest Normalized Residual in absolute value]
    J --> K{Is r max N greater than threshold tau of 3.0}
    K -->|No| L[Multiple or Interacting Bad Data Suspected Use Advanced Methods]
    K -->|Yes| M[Identify Measurement with Largest r N as Bad Data]
    M --> N[Remove Bad Measurement from Data Set]
    N --> O[Re run State Estimation with Cleaned Data]
    O --> F
    L --> P[Apply Robust Estimator or Hypothesis Testing Identification]
    P --> Q[Final State Estimate to EMS]
    H --> Q
```

### 4.2 Detailed Process Flow for Single Bad Measurement Identification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input_Stage
        I1[Measurement Vector z] --> I2[Measurement Covariance R]
        I2 --> I3[Jacobian Matrix H from Network Topology]
    end
    
    subgraph WLS_Estimation
        I1 --> S1[Compute Gain G equals H transpose R inverse H]
        I3 --> S1
        S2[Compute x hat equals G inverse H transpose R inverse z] --> S3[Residual r equals z minus H x hat]
        S1 --> S2
        S3 --> S4[Objective J equals r transpose R inverse r]
    end
    
    subgraph Detection_Stage
        S4 --> D1[Chi Square Test J versus chi square k alpha]
        D1 --> D2{Decision on Bad Data Existence}
    end
    
    subgraph Identification_Stage
        S3 --> Id1[Compute W equals I minus H G inverse H transpose R inverse]
        Id1 --> Id2[Compute Omega equals W R]
        Id2 --> Id3[Normalize r i N equals r i over square root Omega ii]
        Id3 --> Id4[Find Index j of Maximum r i N]
        Id4 --> Id5[Flag Measurement j as Suspect]
    end
    
    D2 -->|Bad Data Detected| Id1
    Id5 --> Id6{Remove and Re estimate}
    Id6 --> S1
```

### 4.3 Hypothesis Testing Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Begin Hypothesis Test]) --> H0[Null Hypothesis H0: No bad data]
    H0 --> Test[Compute Test Statistic J of x hat]
    Test --> Compare{J of x hat versus chi square threshold}
    Compare -->|Less or Equal| Accept[Accept H0: System Clean]
    Compare -->|Greater| Reject[Reject H0: Bad Data Exists]
    Accept --> End1([End: Proceed with Normal Operation])
    Reject --> Identify[Proceed to Identification Stage]
    Identify --> End2([End: Apply Correction])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The flowcharts above depict the standard KTU-2024 prescribed bad data processing architecture. Note the iterative loop in the third diagram — bad data processing is rarely a one-shot operation. Identification and removal may need to be repeated multiple times for systems with multiple bad measurements.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define "bad data" in the context of power system state estimation. List any two sources of bad data.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

*Definition (2 marks):* Bad data refers to any measurement whose error is significantly larger than the standard deviation of the expected measurement noise, i.e., it lies outside the normal Gaussian error distribution. In the WLS state estimation framework, bad data corresponds to measurements that violate the assumed error model $e \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$.

*Two sources (1 mark):*
1. **Instrument/Transducer failure** — e.g., a faulty Potential Transformer (PT) or Current Transformer (CT) giving a grossly incorrect reading.
2. **Communication/Telemetry errors** — bit errors, dropped packets, or synchronization issues in the SCADA/PMU data link.

*Other valid sources include:* meter calibration drift, analog-to-digital conversion errors, topology errors, and cyber-attack data injection.

---

**Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is the difference between "detection" and "identification" of bad data?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Detection | Identification |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Determines whether bad data **exists** in the measurement set | Determines **which specific measurement(s)** is/are erroneous |
| Output | Binary: Yes / No | Index / Location of bad measurement(s) |
| Typical Method | Chi-square test on $J(\hat{x})$ | Largest normalized residual test |
| Level of Analysis | Global (entire system) | Local (per measurement) |

*Key insight (1 mark):* Detection is a necessary but not sufficient condition for robust state estimation. After detection, identification is essential to remove or correct the offending measurement before the state estimate is dispatched to downstream EMS applications.

---

### Part B — Full ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A (14 Marks).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the Weighted Least Squares (WLS) state estimate. Clearly state the necessary conditions and assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A power system has $m = 10$ measurements and $n = 6$ state variables. The WLS estimator yields $J(\hat{x}) = 18.5$. Using the chi-square test at 5% significance, determine whether bad data is present. If bad data is detected, explain the next step for identification. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Assumption (1 mark):** Measurement errors $e$ are independent, zero-mean Gaussian random variables with covariance matrix $R = \text{diag}(\sigma_1^2, \ldots, \sigma_m^2)$.

**Step 1 — Define the WLS objective function (1 mark):**

$$J(x) = [z - Hx]^T R^{-1} [z - Hx]$$

**Step 2 — Expand the quadratic form (1 mark):**

$$J(x) = z^T R^{-1} z - 2x^T H^T R^{-1} z + x^T H^T R^{-1} H x$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate and equate to zero (1 mark):**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial x} = -2 H^T R^{-1} z + 2 H^T R^{-1} H x = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the state estimate (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
H^T R^{-1} H \hat{x} &= H^T R^{-1} z \\
(H^T R^{-1} H) \hat{x} &= H^T R^{-1} z \\
\hat{x} &= (H^T R^{-1} H)^{-1} H^T R^{-1} z
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — State the condition for existence (1 mark):** The matrix $H^T R^{-1} H$ must be non-singular, which requires that $m \geq n$ (measurement redundancy) and that the network be *observable*.

---

**Part (b) — Detection Problem (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute degrees of freedom (1 mark):**

$$k = m - n = 10 - 6 = 4$$

**Step 2 — Look up the chi-square critical value (1 mark):** From standard chi-square tables at $\alpha = 0.05$ and $k = 4$:

$$\chi^2_{4, 0.05} = 9.488$$

**Step 3 — Compare test statistic with critical value (1 mark):**

$$J(\hat{x}) = 18.5 > 9.488 = \chi^2_{4, 0.05}$$

**Step 4 — Decision (1 mark):** Since $J(\hat{x})$ exceeds the critical value, **reject the null hypothesis $H_0$**. Bad data is present in the measurement set.

**Step 5 — Next step for identification (3 marks):**

Proceed to the **identification stage**:
1. Compute the residual vector: $r = z - H\hat{x}$
2. Compute the residual sensitivity matrix: $W = I - H(H^T R^{-1} H)^{-1} H^T R^{-1}$
3. Compute the residual covariance: $\Omega = WR$
4. Compute normalized residuals: $r_i^N = r_i / \sqrt{\Omega_{ii}}$
5. Find the largest normalized residual $r_{max}^N = \max_i \vert r_i^N \vert$
6. Compare with threshold $\tau = 3.0$
7. The measurement with $\vert r_i^N \vert > 3.0$ is flagged as bad, removed, and the state estimation is repeated.

---

**Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice).** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the chi-square test for bad data detection in state estimation. State the null and alternative hypotheses and the decision rule. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the normalized residual. Why is normalization necessary? Compute the normalized residual for a measurement where the raw residual is $r_3 = 4.2$ and $\Omega_{33} = 2.25$. Is this measurement flagged as bad data? **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Chi-Square Test Explanation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Why chi-square? (1 mark):** When all measurement errors are Gaussian and no bad data exists, the WLS objective function $J(\hat{x}) = r^T R^{-1} r$ follows a chi-square distribution with $k = m - n$ degrees of freedom.

**Step 2 — State the hypotheses (2 marks):**

$$H_0: \text{No bad data present; all errors follow } e \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$$
$$H_1: \text{Bad data present in at least one measurement}$$

**Step 3 — Test statistic and distribution (1 mark):**

$$J(\hat{x}) \sim \chi^2_{m-n}$$

**Step 4 — Decision rule (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{If } J(\hat{x}) \leq \chi^2_{m-n, \alpha} &\Rightarrow \text{Accept } H_0 \text{ (no bad data)} \\
\text{If } J(\hat{x}) > \chi^2_{m-n, \alpha} &\Rightarrow \text{Reject } H_0 \text{ (bad data present)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Threshold significance (1 mark):** The threshold $\chi^2_{m-n, \alpha}$ is obtained from chi-square tables. Typical choice: $\alpha = 0.05$ (95% confidence) or $\alpha = 0.01$ (99% confidence).

---

**Part (b) — Normalized Residual Derivation and Computation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Recall residual definition (1 mark):**

$$r = z - H\hat{x} = We \quad \text{where } W = I - H(H^T R^{-1} H)^{-1} H^T R^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Derive the variance of $r_i$ (2 marks):**

$$\text{Var}(r_i) = E[r_i^2] = W_{ii} R_{ii} = \Omega_{ii}$$

where $\Omega = WR$ is the residual covariance matrix (using idempotency of $W$).

**Step 3 — Define normalized residual (2 marks):**

$$r_i^N = \frac{r_i}{\sqrt{\Omega_{ii}}}$$

Under $H_0$, $r_i^N \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$, enabling a universal threshold.

**Why normalization is necessary (1 mark):** Raw residuals have different variances depending on measurement type, weighting, and network location. Normalization makes them statistically comparable on a uniform standard-normal scale.

**Step 4 — Numerical computation (1 mark):**

$$r_3^N = \frac{r_3}{\sqrt{\Omega_{33}}} = \frac{4.2}{\sqrt{2.25}} = \frac{4.2}{1.5} = 2.8$$

**Decision:** Since $\vert r_3^N \vert = 2.8 < 3.0$ (the typical threshold), this measurement is **NOT flagged as bad data** under the standard 99.7% confidence rule. (However, if a stricter threshold of $\tau = 2.5$ is used, it would be flagged — illustrating the trade-off between detection sensitivity and false alarm rate.)

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Where KTU Students Lose Marks in Bad Data Problems:**
> 1. **Degrees of Freedom Mistake:** Using $k = m$ instead of $k = m - n$. Always remember to subtract the state variables.
> 2. **Confusing Detection with Identification:** The chi-square test ONLY detects presence. Do not claim it identifies which measurement is bad.
> 3. **Threshold Value Error:** $\chi^2_{m-n, \alpha}$ is NOT the same as $\chi^2_{m-n, 1-\alpha}$. The critical value corresponds to the upper-tail at significance $\alpha$.
> 4. **Forgetting to Iterate:** Single bad data detection is rarely a one-pass process. If multiple bad measurements exist, re-estimation is required.
> 5. **Unit / Dimension Inconsistency:** In normalized residual derivations, the unit of $r_i$ (in pu or MW) cancels with the unit of $\sqrt{\Omega_{ii}}$, yielding a dimensionless ratio. Forgetting this dimensional check loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Not Stating Assumptions:** Always explicitly state that measurement errors are Gaussian and that the network is observable.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Points for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- [x] **Bad data** = measurement with error exceeding normal noise threshold; arises from PT/CT failure, telemetry errors, or cyber-attacks.
- [x] **State estimation model:** $z = Hx + e$, with $e \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$.
- [x] **WLS estimate:** $\hat{x} = (H^T R^{-1} H)^{-1} H^T R^{-1} z$.
- [x] **Residual:** $r = z - H\hat{x} = Wz$, where $W = I - H(H^T R^{-1} H)^{-1} H^T R^{-1}$.
- [x] **Residual covariance:** $\Omega = WR$ (since $W$ is symmetric and idempotent).
- [x] **Normalized residual:** $r_i^N = r_i / \sqrt{\Omega_{ii}} \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$ under $H_0$.
- [x] **Objective function:** $J(\hat{x}) = r^T R^{-1} r \sim \chi^2_{m-n}$ under $H_0$.
- [x] **Detection rule:** $J(\hat{x}) > \chi^2_{m-n, \alpha} \Rightarrow$ reject $H_0 \Rightarrow$ bad data present.
- [x] **Identification rule:** Identify measurement $i$ with $\vert r_i^N \vert > 3.0$ (or chosen $\tau$) as bad.
- [x] **Degrees of freedom:** $k = m - n$ (not $m$).
- [x] **Typical confidence levels:** $\alpha = 0.05$ for $\chi^2$ test; $\tau = 3.0$ for normalized residual test.
- [x] **Iteration required:** After removing a bad measurement, re-estimate and re-test.
- [x] **Masking effect:** Multiple bad measurements can mask each other; advanced methods (e.g., Estimation Index, Suspicion Index) or robust estimators may be needed.
- [x] **Engineering context:** This entire topic is applied in real-time Energy Management Systems (EMS) for SCADA-based grid monitoring and control.

---

> **End of KTU-Premier Engine V10 Notes for Module 4 — Detection and Identification of Bad Measurements.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Quantities Not Being Measured
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET741 - POWER SYSTEM OPERATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Quantities Not Being Measured

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of modern **Energy Management Systems (EMS)**, the **Estimation of Quantities Not Being Measured** refers to the mathematical process of computing the *complete operating state* of a power network from a limited, redundant, and noisy set of **real-time telemetered measurements** obtained through the **SCADA** (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition) system. The unmeasured quantities — bus voltage magnitudes, phase angles, line loadings, generator outputs at un-telemetered buses, transformer tap positions, etc. — are obtained indirectly by solving the **Power System State Estimation (PSSE)** problem.

The "state" of the system is conventionally defined by the complex bus voltage phasors. For an $N$-bus system, the state vector is:

$$
x = 
\begin{bmatrix}
\theta_2 \ \theta_3 \ \ldots \ \theta_N \ \vert V_1 \ \vert V_2 \ \ldots \ \vert V_N
\end{bmatrix}^{T}
$$

where bus 1 is the **slack (reference) bus** with $\theta_1 = 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The true power of state estimation is *not* merely noise filtering — it is the ability to **infer quantities that are not physically measured at all**, such as the loading of an un-instrumented tie-line, the voltage at a load-end bus with no PMU/SCADA, or the loss of life of a transformer. This is the *indirect observability* of the grid.

The fundamental measurement model governing the estimation is:

$$
z = h(x) + e
$$

where:
- $z \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ = measurement vector
- $h(\cdot)$ = nonlinear measurement function (AC power flow equations)
- $x \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ = state vector ($n \le m$ for redundancy)
- $e \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ = random measurement error vector with covariance $R = E[ee^{T}]$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — The Hospital Diagnosis:** Imagine a doctor who can only measure *body temperature* and *blood pressure*, but must infer *oxygen saturation, blood sugar, kidney function,* and *cardiac output*. By combining the limited measurements with the *anatomical model* of the human body (a set of known physical relationships), the doctor can accurately estimate the unmeasured vital signs. The power system does exactly the same: it uses **Kirchhoff's laws + Ohm's law** (the anatomical model of the grid) plus a few measurements to deduce *every* voltage, angle, and flow in the network.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — GPS Triangulation:** Your smartphone receives signals from only 3 or 4 satellites, yet pinpoints your exact 3D location. Similarly, an EMS uses ~2 to 3 measurements per state variable to triangulate the operating point of a network with thousands of buses.

## 1.3 Why Direct Measurement of *All* Quantities is Impossible

| Constraint | Implication |
|---|---|
| **Cost of transducers** | Installing CTs, PTs, and PMUs on every bus and every line is economically infeasible for large grids. |
| **Communication bandwidth** | SCADA can telemeter only a finite number of analog values every 2–4 seconds. |
| **Reliability** | If a single RTU fails, the system must still operate; hence, all important quantities should be *calculable* from surviving measurements. |
| **Topology errors** | Breaker status may be wrong; the estimator must deduce the *true* topology along with the state. |

Hence, estimation is not a luxury — it is an **operational necessity**.

## 1.4 Classification of Quantities in the EMS

| Category | Examples | Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Directly Measured (Real-time)** | $\vert V_i \vert$, $P_{ij}$, $Q_{ij}$, $P_i$, $Q_i$, $I_{ij}$ | RTU / PMU |
| **Pseudo-measurements** | Forecasted load at un-telemetered bus | Load forecasting module |
| **Virtual measurements (zero-injection)** | $P_k = 0,\ Q_k = 0$ at a pure load-less transit bus | Network topology (exact, no error) |
| **Status (logical) measurements** | Breaker open/close, tap position | Status flags from RTU |
| **Analog (continuous) measurements** | MW, MVAR, kV, A | Transducers |

The "**quantities not being measured**" are reconstructed from the *first* three categories via the **Weighted Least Squares (WLS)** estimator, which is the industry standard formalized by **Schweppe (1970)** and **Aboytes-Coke (1978)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Architecture of State Estimation

### Step 1 — The Measurement Function $h(x)$

For an AC model, the typical measurements are real/reactive **bus injections** and **branch flows**:

**Real power injection at bus $k$:**

$$
P_k = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \vert V_k \vert \vert V_j \vert \left( G_{kj} \cos\theta_{kj} + B_{kj} \sin\theta_{kj} \right)
$$

**Reactive power injection at bus $k$:**

$$
Q_k = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \vert V_k \vert \vert V_j \vert \left( G_{kj} \sin\theta_{kj} - B_{kj} \cos\theta_{kj} \right)
$$

**Real power flow from bus $i$ to bus $j$ (π-model line):**

$$
P_{ij} = \vert V_i \vert^{2} g_{ij} - \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \left( g_{ij} \cos\theta_{ij} + b_{ij} \sin\theta_{ij} \right)
$$

**Reactive power flow from bus $i$ to bus $j$:**

$$
Q_{ij} = -\vert V_i \vert^{2} \left( b_{ij} + \frac{b_{sh,i}}{2} \right) + \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \left( b_{ij} \cos\theta_{ij} - g_{ij} \sin\theta_{ij} \right)
$$

These are all *nonlinear* in the state $x$.

### Step 2 — The DC (Linear) Approximation

For high-voltage transmission networks where $G_{ij} \ll B_{ij}$ and $\theta_{ij} \approx 0$, we obtain the **DC power flow model**:

$$
P_{ij} = \frac{\theta_i - \theta_j}{X_{ij}} \quad ; \quad P_k = \sum_{j \in \Omega_k} B_{kj} \left( \theta_k - \theta_j \right)
$$

This gives a **linear** measurement equation:

$$
z = Hx + e
$$

where $H$ is the **measurement Jacobian** (constant for DC).

### Step 3 — Formulation of the WLS Estimator

The estimator $\hat{x}$ is the value of $x$ that **minimizes the weighted sum of squared measurement residuals**:

$$
\min_{x} \ J(x) = \left[ z - h(x) \right]^{T} W \left[ z - h(x) \right]
$$

where $W = R^{-1} = \text{diag}\left( \sigma_{1}^{-2}, \sigma_{2}^{-2}, \ldots, \sigma_{m}^{-2} \right)$ is the **weighting matrix** (inverse of measurement covariance), and $\sigma_i$ is the standard deviation of the $i^{th}$ measurement.

The first-order optimality condition $\frac{\partial J}{\partial x} = 0$ yields the **normal equation** (linear case):

$$
H^{T} W H \ \hat{x} = H^{T} W z
$$

### Step 4 — The Gain Matrix and Information

$$
G = H^{T} W H \quad \text{(Gain / Information matrix, } n \times n \text{)}
$$

The WLS estimate (DC, closed-form) is:

$$
\boxed{\hat{x} = \left( H^{T} W H \right)^{-1} H^{T} W z}
$$

For the **nonlinear AC** case, we apply the iterative **Gauss-Newton** method:

$$
G(x^{\nu}) \ \Delta x^{\nu} = H(x^{\nu})^{T} W \left[ z - h(x^{\nu}) \right]
$$

$$
x^{\nu+1} = x^{\nu} + \Delta x^{\nu}
$$

Iteration continues until $\vert \Delta x^{\nu} \vert_{\infty} < \varepsilon$ (typical $\varepsilon = 10^{-4}$ pu, 2–5 iterations).

## 2.2 Critical Theorem — Observability (KTU Hot Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Kron-reduced Observability):** *A power system is numerically observable if and only if the gain matrix $G = H^{T} W H$ is **non-singular**, i.e., $\det(G) \ne 0$.* Equivalently, the system must contain at least one **spanning tree of measurement-graph** that reaches every bus (topological observability).

A redundant, well-instrumented grid has **redundancy ratio** $\eta = m/n \ge 2$ (industry norm: $\eta \approx 2.5$).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **For all formulas below, use `\vert` for absolute value notation to preserve markdown table integrity.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Meaning / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $z = h(x) + e$ | Measurement model | $z$ in MW/MVAr/kV, $e$ ~ $\mathcal{N}(0, R)$ |
| 2 | $\hat{x} = (H^{T} W H)^{-1} H^{T} W z$ | DC WLS estimate | Vector of bus angles $\theta$ |
| 3 | $G = H^{T} W H$ | Gain matrix | $n \times n$, must be PD |
| 4 | $W_{ii} = 1/\sigma_{i}^{2}$ | Weighting | Inverse variance |
| 5 | $r = z - H\hat{x}$ | Measurement residual | $m \times 1$ |
| 6 | $\Omega = W^{-1} - H G^{-1} H^{T} W$ | Residual covariance | Used in bad-data tests |
| 7 | $r_{i}^{N} = \frac{\vert r_i \vert}{\sqrt{\Omega_{ii}}}$ | Normalized residual | Threshold $\ge 3.0$ |
| 8 | $P_{ij} = \dfrac{\theta_i - \theta_j}{X_{ij}}$ | DC power flow | MW (linearized) |
| 9 | $J(\hat{x}) = \sum W_{ii} r_i^{2}$ | Objective (chi-square test) | $\chi^{2}_{m-n,\ \alpha}$ |
| 10 | $\eta = m/n$ | Measurement redundancy | $\eta \ge 2$ for observability |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Real-Time Security Assessment:** Once $\hat{x}$ is known, the operator can compute $\hat{P}_{ij},\ \hat{Q}_{ij}$ for *every* line and compare with limits $\Rightarrow$ automatic contingency screening.
- **Loss Minimization & OPF:** The estimated state is the starting point of the **Optimal Power Flow** (Module 4 follow-up).
- **Locational Marginal Pricing (LMP):** Requires $\theta_i$ at every bus — only obtainable through state estimation.
- **Forensic analysis of blackouts:** Post-mortem reconstruction of pre-fault voltages and flows.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — The WLS Estimator from First Principles

We want to estimate $x \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ from $m$ noisy measurements $z \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ where $m > n$ and $z = h(x) + e$ with $e \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$.

### Step A — Probability Density of the Measurement Vector

Assuming errors are independent, zero-mean Gaussian:

$$
f(z \vert x) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma_i^{2}}} \exp\left( -\frac{\left[ z_i - h_i(x) \right]^{2}}{2 \sigma_i^{2}} \right)
$$

### Step B — The Maximum Likelihood Estimator (MLE)

The MLE maximizes $f(z \vert x)$, which is equivalent to **minimizing** the negative log-likelihood:

$$
\min_{x} \ \mathcal{L}(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{\left[ z_i - h_i(x) \right]^{2}}{2 \sigma_i^{2}}
$$

Dropping the constant factor ½, this is the **Weighted Least Squares** objective:

$$
J(x) = \left[ z - h(x) \right]^{T} W \left[ z - h(x) \right] = \sum_{i=1}^{m} W_{ii} \left[ z_i - h_i(x) \right]^{2}
$$

where $W = \text{diag}\left( \sigma_{1}^{-2}, \ldots, \sigma_{m}^{-2} \right)$.

### Step C — Linear Case (DC Model)

If $h(x) = Hx$, then:

$$
J(x) = (z - Hx)^{T} W (z - Hx) = z^{T} W z - 2 z^{T} W H x + x^{T} H^{T} W H x
$$

Taking the gradient and setting to zero (first-order optimality):

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial x} = -2 H^{T} W z + 2 H^{T} W H x = 0
$$

$$
H^{T} W H \ x = H^{T} W z
$$

The Hessian (second derivative) is $2 H^{T} W H$, which is positive definite whenever $H$ has full column rank — guaranteeing a **unique global minimum**. Hence:

$$
\boxed{\hat{x} = \left( H^{T} W H \right)^{-1} H^{T} W z}
$$

### Step D — Nonlinear Case (AC Model) — Gauss-Newton Iteration

We linearize $h(x)$ around the current iterate $x^{\nu}$:

$$
h(x) \approx h(x^{\nu}) + H(x^{\nu}) \Delta x
$$

Substituting into $J(x)$ and minimizing with respect to $\Delta x$:

$$
\min_{\Delta x} \ \left[ \Delta z - H \Delta x \right]^{T} W \left[ \Delta z - H \Delta x \right]
$$

where $\Delta z = z - h(x^{\nu})$. This gives the iterative normal equation:

$$
\underbrace{H(x^{\nu})^{T} W H(x^{\nu})}_{G(x^{\nu})} \ \Delta x^{\nu} = H(x^{\nu})^{T} W \left[ z - h(x^{\nu}) \right]
$$

Update rule: $x^{\nu+1} = x^{\nu} + \Delta x^{\nu}$. **Convergence** is quadratic (Newton-like) and is reached in 2–5 iterations for well-conditioned networks.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — DC State Estimation on a 3-Bus System

### System Data (3-bus, all line reactances in pu, base 100 MVA)

| From → To | Reactance $X_{ij}$ (pu) |
|---|---|
| 1 → 2 | 0.20 |
| 1 → 3 | 0.10 |
| 2 → 3 | 0.15 |

**Network susceptance matrix entries** (B-coefficients):

$$
B_{12} = \frac{1}{0.20} = 5.0 \quad ; \quad B_{13} = \frac{1}{0.10} = 10.0 \quad ; \quad B_{23} = \frac{1}{0.15} = 6.667
$$

$$
B_{bus} = 
\begin{bmatrix}
15.0 & -5.0 & -10.0 \\
-5.0 & 11.667 & -6.667 \\
-10.0 & -6.667 & 16.667
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Bus 1 is the slack, so the state vector is $x = [\theta_2, \theta_3]^{T}$.

### Available Measurements (in MW on 100 MVA base, i.e. in pu of 100 MW)

| # | Measurement | Value (MW) | $\sigma$ (MW) | Weight $W_{ii}$ (pu MW)$^{-2}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{12}$ | 50 | 5 | 0.04 |
| 2 | $P_{13}$ | 80 | 5 | 0.04 |
| 3 | $P_{23}$ | 30 | 5 | 0.04 |

### Form the Measurement Equations (DC Model)

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{12} &= B_{12}(\theta_1 - \theta_2) = -5.0\,\theta_2 \quad (\text{since } \theta_1 = 0) \\
P_{13} &= B_{13}(\theta_1 - \theta_3) = -10.0\,\theta_3 \\
P_{23} &= B_{23}(\theta_2 - \theta_3) = 6.667\,(\theta_2 - \theta_3)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1 — Build the $H$ Matrix ($3 \times 2$)

$$
H = 
\begin{bmatrix}
\partial P_{12}/\partial \theta_2 & \partial P_{12}/\partial \theta_3 \\
\partial P_{13}/\partial \theta_2 & \partial P_{13}/\partial \theta_3 \\
\partial P_{23}/\partial \theta_2 & \partial P_{23}/\partial \theta_3
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
-5.0 & 0 \\
0 & -10.0 \\
6.667 & -6.667
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2 — Build the Weighting Matrix

$$
W = 0.04 \times I_{3} = 
\begin{bmatrix}
0.04 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0.04 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0.04
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Gain Matrix $G = H^{T} W H$

$$
H^{T} W H = 0.04 \times 
\begin{bmatrix}
-5.0 & 0 & 6.667 \\
0 & -10.0 & -6.667
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
-5.0 & 0 \\
0 & -10.0 \\
6.667 & -6.667
\end{bmatrix}
$$

First compute $H^{T} H$ (a $2 \times 2$ matrix):

$$
H^{T} H = 
\begin{bmatrix}
(-5.0)^2 + (6.667)^2 & 0 + (6.667)(-6.667) \\
0 + (6.667)(-6.667) & (-10.0)^2 + (-6.667)^2
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
25.0 + 44.45 & -44.45 \\
-44.45 & 100.0 + 44.45
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
69.45 & -44.45 \\
-44.45 & 144.45
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Therefore:

$$
G = H^{T} W H = 0.04 \times 
\begin{bmatrix}
69.45 & -44.45 \\
-44.45 & 144.45
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
2.778 & -1.778 \\
-1.778 & 5.778
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute $H^{T} W z$

With $z = [50, 80, 30]^{T}$ MW:

$$
H^{T} z = 
\begin{bmatrix}
-5.0 \times 50 + 6.667 \times 30 \\
0 \times 50 + (-10.0) \times 80 + (-6.667) \times 30
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
-250.0 + 200.0 \\
0 - 800.0 - 200.0
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
-50.0 \\
-1000.0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

So $H^{T} W z = 0.04 \times [-50, -1000]^{T} = [-2.0, -40.0]^{T}$.

### Step 5 — Solve $G \hat{x} = H^{T} W z$

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
2.778 & -1.778 \\
-1.778 & 5.778
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
\theta_2 \\
\theta_3
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
-2.0 \\
-40.0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Determinant: $\det(G) = (2.778)(5.778) - (-1.778)^{2} = 16.05 - 3.16 = 12.89$ (non-zero $\Rightarrow$ observable).

Inverse:

$$
G^{-1} = \frac{1}{12.89}
\begin{bmatrix}
5.778 & 1.778 \\
1.778 & 2.778
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
0.4482 & 0.1379 \\
0.1379 & 0.2155
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 6 — State Estimate $\hat{x}$

$$
\hat{x} = G^{-1} \cdot H^{T} W z = 
\begin{bmatrix}
0.4482 & 0.1379 \\
0.1379 & 0.2155
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
-2.0 \\
-40.0
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
-0.8964 - 5.516 \\
-0.2758 - 8.620
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
-6.4124 \\
-8.8958
\end{bmatrix} \text{ (degrees if MW in actual units, else radians)}
$$

If we treat MW as pu on 100 MVA, $\theta$ is in **radians** but the magnitudes look large — confirming we should convert: $\theta_{\text{deg}} = \theta_{\text{rad}} \times (180/\pi) \times 100 \text{ (base angle divisor)}$. For exam purposes, **the numerical procedure is what matters**.

### Step 7 — Compute Estimated Unmeasured Quantities (THE KEY PAYOFF!)

Once $\hat{\theta}_2$ and $\hat{\theta}_3$ are known, **every** other power quantity is computable:

$$
\hat{P}_{21} = -\hat{P}_{12} = +6.412 \text{ MW}
$$

$$
\hat{P}_{31} = -\hat{P}_{13} = +80.0 \text{ MW}
$$

$$
\hat{P}_2 = \hat{P}_{21} + \hat{P}_{23} = 6.412 + 30 = 36.412 \text{ MW (estimated injection at Bus 2 — NOT directly measured!)}
$$

$$
\hat{P}_3 = \hat{P}_{31} + \hat{P}_{32} = 80.0 - 30 = 50.0 \text{ MW (estimated injection at Bus 3 — NOT directly measured!)}
$$

$$
\hat{P}_{\text{loss}} = \hat{P}_{12} + \hat{P}_{13} + \hat{P}_{23} \text{ (total system loss)}
$$

This is **exactly** how unmeasured quantities are estimated in a real EMS.

### Step 8 — Residual Check (Bad Data Detection)

$$
r = z - H\hat{x} = 
\begin{bmatrix}
50 \\ 80 \\ 30
\end{bmatrix}
-
\begin{bmatrix}
-5 \times (-6.41) \\
-10 \times (-8.90) \\
6.667 \times (-6.41 - (-8.90))
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
50 - 32.06 \\
80 - 89.0 \\
30 - 16.58
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
17.94 \\ -9.0 \\ 13.42
\end{bmatrix}
$$

The largest normalized residual flags a suspected bad measurement — to be eliminated and re-estimated.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — State Estimation inside an EMS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[SCADA RTUs and PMUs] -->|Analog and Status Data| B[Topology Processor]
    B -->|Network Model B_bus| C[Measurement Function Builder]
    A -->|Analog and Status Data| C
    C -->|H and h x| D[WLS State Estimator]
    D -->|Estimated State x_hat| E[Bus Voltages and Angles]
    D -->|Estimated Flow P_ij and Q_ij| F[Unmeasured Quantities]
    D -->|Residuals r| G[Bad Data Detector]
    G -->|Flagged measurement| H[Re-estimate]
    E --> I[Security Assessment and OPF]
    F --> I
    H --> D
    I --> J[Operator Displays and Alarms]
```

## 4.2 Flow Diagram — Iterative Gauss-Newton WLS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start([Start with x0 flat start]) --> build[Build H x and z]
    build --> gcomp[Compute Gain Matrix G]
    gcomp --> rhs[Form RHS H^T W r]
    rhs --> solve[Solve G Delta x equals RHS]
    solve --> update[x new equals x plus Delta x]
    update --> check{Converged epsilon 1e-4}
    check -->|No| build
    check -->|Yes| finish([x_hat is final estimate])
    finish --> post[Post-processing compute flows, voltages, losses]
```

## 4.3 Measurement Graph — Observability Tree for 3-Bus Example

```mermaid
graph TB
    bus1((Bus 1 Slack)) ---|P12 measured| bus2((Bus 2))
    bus1 ---|P13 measured| bus3((Bus 3))
    bus2 -.->|P23 measured virtual link| bus3
    style bus1 fill:#FFD700
    style bus2 fill:#90EE90
    style bus3 fill:#87CEEB
```

> [!NOTE]
> The graph above shows that **all three buses are reached from bus 1 by at least one measured branch** — the system is *topologically observable*. The dashed line shows a redundant (non-essential) measurement that improves the bad-data detection capability.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]`
**Define the Weighted Least Squares (WLS) state estimation problem. Why is "weighting" essential rather than ordinary least squares?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The WLS state estimation problem is the minimization of the weighted sum of squared residuals between telemetered measurements $z$ and their computed values $h(x)$:

$$
\min_{x} \ J(x) = \left[ z - h(x) \right]^{T} W \left[ z - h(x) \right]
$$

**Weighting is essential** because different measurements have different *accuracies* — a PMU measurement ($\sigma \approx 0.001$ pu) is far more accurate than a 1970s analog RTU ($\sigma \approx 0.02$ pu). Weighting by $W_{ii} = 1/\sigma_{i}^{2}$ ensures the **estimator is the Best Linear Unbiased Estimator (BLUE)** per the Gauss-Markov theorem, giving minimum variance and statistical consistency. Ordinary LS would treat a 1% and a 5% instrument as equally trustworthy — a serious engineering error. **(3 marks: 1 for equation, 1 for weight definition, 1 for BLUE reasoning)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary]`
**What is meant by "observability" of a power network? State the mathematical condition.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Observability* is the property of a power network by which the **complete state vector $x$ can be uniquely determined** from the available measurement set $z$.

**Mathematical condition:** A system is observable if and only if the gain matrix is non-singular:

$$
\det(G) = \det(H^{T} W H) \neq 0
$$

**Topological interpretation (1 mark):** A spanning measurement tree must exist — from any bus, there must be a path of *measured* branches (or *measured* injections) leading to the slack bus. If a bus is *islanded* from all measurements, it is *unobservable* and the state at that bus cannot be estimated. **(3 marks: 1 definition, 1 mathematical condition, 1 topological interpretation)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the WLS state estimator $\hat{x} = (H^{T}WH)^{-1} H^{T} W z$ starting from the Gaussian likelihood function. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 2-bus system with $X_{12} = 0.1$ pu, base = 100 MVA, the measurements are:
$P_{12} = 45 \pm 2$ MW, $P_{1} = -45 \pm 2$ MW, $V_{1} = 1.05 \pm 0.01$ pu.
Using DC state estimation, estimate $\theta_2$ and compute the unmeasured quantities $P_2$, $Q_{12}$, and the line current. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Assumption 1:** Measurement errors are independent, zero-mean Gaussian: $e \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$.

**Assumption 2:** The measurement function is linear: $z = Hx + e$ (DC approximation).

**Step 1:** Likelihood of the measurement vector for a given state $x$:

$$
f(z \vert x) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{m/2} \vert R \vert^{1/2}} \exp\left( -\tfrac{1}{2} (z - Hx)^{T} R^{-1} (z - Hx) \right)
$$

**Step 2:** Maximizing the likelihood $\equiv$ minimizing the negative log-likelihood:

$$
J(x) = (z - Hx)^{T} R^{-1} (z - Hx)
$$

**Step 3:** Expand and differentiate:

$$
J(x) = z^{T} W z - 2 z^{T} W H x + x^{T} H^{T} W H x
$$

where $W = R^{-1}$.

Setting $\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial x} = -2 H^{T} W z + 2 H^{T} W H x = 0$ gives the **normal equation**:

$$
H^{T} W H \, \hat{x} = H^{T} W z
$$

**Step 4:** Provided $H^{T}WH$ is non-singular (observability condition):

$$
\boxed{\hat{x} = (H^{T} W H)^{-1} H^{T} W z}
$$

**Step 5 — BLUE Property (1 mark):** $E[\hat{x}] = x$ (unbiased), and the covariance $\text{cov}(\hat{x}) = (H^{T}WH)^{-1}$ is the *minimum possible* variance among all linear unbiased estimators.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

*States:* $\theta_1 = 0$ (slack), unknown $\theta_2$. Only $V_1$ is measured; assume $V_1$ is given (not estimated in DC).

*Measurement equations:*

$$
P_{12} = \frac{\theta_1 - \theta_2}{X_{12}} = \frac{-\theta_2}{0.1} = -10 \theta_2
$$

$$
P_1 = -P_{12} = 10 \theta_2
$$

*Form $H$ and $W$:* With $\sigma_{1} = 2$ MW, $\sigma_{2} = 2$ MW, weights $W_{11} = W_{22} = 0.25$ MW$^{-2}$.

$$
H = \begin{bmatrix} -10 \\ 10 \end{bmatrix}, \quad z = \begin{bmatrix} 45 \\ -45 \end{bmatrix} \text{ MW}
$$

*Gain matrix:* $G = H^{T} W H = 0.25 \times [(-10)^2 + (10)^2] = 0.25 \times 200 = 50$.

*RHS:* $H^{T} W z = 0.25 \times [(-10)(45) + (10)(-45)] = 0.25 \times [-450 - 450] = -225$.

*Estimate:* $\hat{\theta}_2 = -225 / 50 = -4.5$ rad? **Sanity check** — angle is in radians but the value is too large; treating MW as pu on 100 MVA, $\theta_2 = -4.5$ pu × (180/π) ... Students should **report the symbolic answer** unless units are explicitly defined.

*Better approach (interpreting MW as pu × 100):* Then $\theta_2 = -4.5$ pu in rad — but realistically we keep $\theta_2$ in **radians** and report:

$$
\hat{\theta}_2 = -4.5 \text{ rad (on 100 MVA base, MW as pu)}
$$

Or for small angles, in **degrees** for a 100 MVA / 220 kV base: $\hat{\theta}_2 \approx -4.5 \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} \times \dfrac{1}{100} \approx -2.58°$.

*Unmeasured quantities:*
- $P_2 = -P_1 = +45$ MW
- $P_{21} = -P_{12} = -45$ MW (into bus 2)
- $Q_{12} \approx 0$ (DC model is real-power only)
- $I_{12} = \dfrac{P_{12}}{\sqrt{3}\, V_{LL} \cos\phi} = \dfrac{45}{\sqrt{3} \times 220 \times 1} \approx 0.118$ kA

**(7 marks: 1 setup, 2 H and W, 2 G and solve, 2 unmeasured quantities)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **observability** in power system state estimation. Discuss the topological observability algorithm and the role of **pseudo-measurements** in making a network observable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-bus system has measurements (DC model) of: $P_{12}$, $P_{23}$, $P_{34}$, and $P_{1}$ with standard deviations $\sigma = 5$ MW each. With bus 1 as slack, line reactances $X_{12} = 0.1, X_{23} = 0.2, X_{34} = 0.15$ pu. Test for observability using the determinant of the gain matrix. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution Sketch

**Part (a):** Discuss spanning-tree algorithm, fusion of pseudo-measurements (forecasted loads) to make weakly-instrumented buses observable. *(7 marks)*

**Part (b):** Build $H$ matrix from $B_{bus}$ submatrix, form $G = H^T W H$, compute $\det(G)$. Non-zero determinant $\Rightarrow$ observable. *(7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones — Examiner's Eye-View:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to exclude the slack bus** from the state vector (n should be $2N-1$, not $2N$). Penalty: up to **−1 mark**.
> 2. **Using the wrong weight:** Students frequently write $W = R$ instead of $W = R^{-1}$. The WLS is *minimization with inverse-variance weights* — any other choice loses the BLUE property. Penalty: **−2 marks** (entire objective is wrong).
> 3. **Mixing units:** DC state estimation in MW must be converted to per-unit on a common MVA base before forming $H$ and $W$, OR kept consistently in MW. Mixing MW with pu voltages will silently give wrong answers. Penalty: **−2 marks**.
> 4. **Ignoring observability:** Writing $\hat{x} = (H^T W H)^{-1} H^T W z$ without checking $\det(G) \ne 0$ is a common mistake. The examiner will explicitly look for the observability assertion. Penalty: **−1 mark**.
> 5. **Confusing state estimation with load flow:** Load flow has measurements equal to unknowns (deterministic); state estimation has $m > n$ with noise (statistical). Mixing the two methods loses conceptual credit. Penalty: **−2 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] The **state** of an $N$-bus system = $\big[\theta_2, \ldots, \theta_N, \vert V_1\vert, \ldots, \vert V_N \vert\big]^{T}$ — dimension $n = 2N - 1$.
- [x] **Measurement model:** $z = h(x) + e$ (AC) or $z = Hx + e$ (DC). Errors Gaussian, $E[ee^T] = R$.
- [x] **WLS objective:** $J(x) = (z - h(x))^T W (z - h(x))$, $W = R^{-1}$.
- [x] **WLS solution (DC):** $\hat{x} = (H^T W H)^{-1} H^T W z$ — closed-form, BLUE.
- [x] **WLS solution (AC):** Iterative Gauss-Newton using $G \Delta x = H^T W [z - h(x^\nu)]$.
- [x] **Observability condition:** $\det(H^T W H) \ne 0$ — topologically, a *measurement spanning tree* must exist.
- [x] **Redundancy ratio:** $\eta = m/n \ge 2$ for robust estimation and bad-data detection.
- [x] **Pseudo-measurements:** Forecasted loads, generator schedules, virtual zero-injections — they augment the real-time set to make weakly-instrumented networks observable.
- [x] **Bad data detection:** Largest normalized residual $r_i^N = \vert r_i \vert / \sqrt{\Omega_{ii}}$ — threshold $\ge 3.0$ flags bad data.
- [x] **Key engineering pay-off:** From a *limited* SCADA set, the estimator reconstructs **all** unmeasured voltages, angles, flows, injections, and losses — the foundation of every downstream EMS function (security assessment, OPF, LMP, ATC).
- [x] **Industry standard:** WLS with DC approximation is the workhorse; PMU-based linear state estimation (LSE) is the modern upgrade using synchronized phasor measurements.

---

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET741 - POWER SYSTEM OPERATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Network Observability and Pseudo-Measurements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Network Observability** is a topological and numerical property of a power system that determines whether the complete voltage state vector (magnitudes and phase angles at all buses) can be uniquely estimated from the available set of real-time measurements using the Weighted Least Squares (WLS) state estimator.

Mathematically, a power network is **observable** if and only if the measurement Jacobian matrix $\mathbf{H}$ has **full column rank**, i.e., $\text{rank}(\mathbf{H}) = n$, where $n$ is the number of state variables (typically $n = 2N - 1$ for an $N$-bus system with one reference bus).

> [!NOTE]
> **Pseudo-Measurements** are artificially generated or estimated quantities (such as forecasted loads, scheduled generation outputs, or zero-injection constraints at transformer-only buses) that are treated as additional measurements with lower confidence weights to enhance the observability of a network where physical real-time measurements are insufficient.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a detective trying to solve a crime in a city with 10 streets, but the CCTV cameras work only on 3 streets. Can you reconstruct what happened on all 10 streets? **Only if the 3 streets are strategically connected** so that information "flows" through witnesses and indirect clues. If not, certain streets remain in darkness — this darkness is **unobservability**.

**Pseudo-measurements** are like asking neighbors: "Did you see anything unusual around 8 PM?" Their answers are estimates — not as reliable as CCTV footage — but they help fill the gap so the detective can solve the case.

In power systems:
- **CCTV cameras** → Real-time measurements (PMUs, SCADA RTUs)
- **Witness statements** → Pseudo-measurements (load forecasts, scheduled generation, zero injections)
- **Crime reconstruction** → State estimation (computing voltages at every bus)

### 1.3 Core Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **State variables per bus**: Voltage magnitude $V_i$ and phase angle $\delta_i$
> - **Reference bus phase angle**: $\delta_{\text{ref}} = 0$ (set as reference, not estimated)
> - **Total state dimension**: $n = 2N - 1$ for an $N$-bus system
> - **Minimum measurements required**: $m \geq n$ for observability
> - **Redundancy level**: $\eta = \dfrac{m}{n}$ (typically $\eta \geq 1.5$ to $\eta \geq 2.0$ in practical Energy Management Systems)
> - **Pseudo-measurement weight**: $W_{ps} = \dfrac{1}{\sigma_{ps}^2}$ where $\sigma_{ps}$ is typically **5 to 20 times** larger than $\sigma$ of real measurements

### 1.4 Classification of Observability

| Type | Basis | Computational Approach |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Topological Observability** | Graph theory on measurement-to-branch mapping | Spanning tree, network reduction |
| **Numerical Observability** | Rank analysis of Jacobian / gain matrix | Triangular factorization of $\mathbf{G} = \mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{W}\mathbf{H}$ |
