## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The State Estimation Foundation

The WLS state estimator is built upon the linearized measurement model:

$$\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{e}$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{z}$ → $m \times 1$ measurement vector
- $\mathbf{H}$ → $m \times n$ Jacobian matrix (sensitivity of measurements to states)
- $\mathbf{x}$ → $n \times 1$ state vector ($V_i$, $\delta_i$ for all buses except reference)
- $\mathbf{e}$ → $m \times 1$ random measurement error vector, with $\mathbf{e} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \mathbf{R})$

The WLS objective is to minimize:

$$J(\mathbf{x}) = [\mathbf{z} - \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x}]^T \mathbf{W} [\mathbf{z} - \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x}]$$

Setting $\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = 0$ yields the **normal equation**:

$$\mathbf{G}\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{z}$$

Where the **gain matrix** is:

$$\mathbf{G} = \mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{H}, \quad \text{dimension } n \times n$$

### 2.2 Observability Condition — The Core Logic

- **Step 1 (Necessary Condition)**: $\text{rank}(\mathbf{H}) = n$ — the Jacobian must span the full state space.
- **Step 2 (Why this matters)**: If $\text{rank}(\mathbf{H}) < n$, the gain matrix $\mathbf{G}$ is **singular**, and the normal equation has infinitely many solutions — the WLS estimator fails.
- **Step 3 (Equivalent formulation)**: $\text{rank}(\mathbf{G}) = n$ because $\mathbf{W}$ is positive-definite and rank is preserved under congruence transformation when $\mathbf{H}$ has full column rank.
- **Step 4 (Practical check)**: Perform **LDLT factorization** of $\mathbf{G}$. If the factorization completes without a zero pivot, the network is **numerically observable**.

### 2.3 Why Observability Fails in Practice

- Loss of telemetry (RTU/PMU failure)
- Unmeasured substations
- Sparse measurement placement in expanding networks
- Bad-data rejection removing critical measurements
- Network islanding or reconfiguration

### 2.4 Pseudo-Measurements — The Rescue Mechanism

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Pseudo-measurements supplement missing telemetry by injecting prior knowledge into the estimator. They are treated as **measurements with very high variance** (low weight) so they influence the estimate only when real data is absent.

**Sources of Pseudo-Measurements:**
1. **Load forecasts** (short-term load prediction): $\hat{P}_{L,i} = f(\text{time, weather, history})$
2. **Scheduled generation**: Unit commitment / economic dispatch outputs
3. **Zero-injection buses** (transformer-only or transit nodes): $P_i = 0$, $Q_i = 0$ — *these are virtual measurements with effectively zero variance*
4. **Historical SCADA averages** for unmeasured branches
5. **Network topology derived** values (e.g., from breaker status)

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the definitive KTU cheat sheet for this topic. Memorize every entry.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Condition / Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Measurement Model | $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{e}$ | $\mathbf{e} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \mathbf{R})$ |
| 2 | WLS Objective | $J(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{r}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{r}$, $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{z} - \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x}$ | Minimize weighted squared residual |
| 3 | Weight Matrix | $\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{R}^{-1} = \text{diag}(\sigma_i^{-2})$ | Inverse of measurement covariance |
| 4 | Gain Matrix | $\mathbf{G} = \mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{H}$ | $n \times n$ symmetric positive semi-definite |
| 5 | Normal Equation | $\mathbf{G}\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{z}$ | Solved iteratively in non-linear case |
| 6 | **Observability Rank Test** | $\text{rank}(\mathbf{H}) = n$ | Network is observable |
| 7 | **Gain Matrix Singularity** | $\det(\mathbf{G}) = 0$ | Network is **unobservable** |
| 8 | State Dimension | $n = 2N - 1$ | $N$ buses, one reference |
| 9 | Redundancy Index | $\eta = \dfrac{m}{n}$ | $\eta \geq 1.5$ recommended |
| 10 | Pseudo-Measurement Weight | $W_{ps,i} = \dfrac{1}{(k\sigma_{real})^2}$, $k = 5$ to $20$ | Lower weight than real measurements |
| 11 | Zero-Injection Constraint | $P_i^{\text{inj}} = \sum_j V_i V_j (G_{ij}\cos\delta_{ij} + B_{ij}\sin\delta_{ij}) = 0$ | Treated as perfect measurement |
| 12 | Error Covariance of Estimate | $\text{cov}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}) = \mathbf{G}^{-1}$ | Exists only if $\mathbf{G}$ is invertible |
| 13 | Spanning Tree Condition | Number of measurements $\geq$ number of branches + 1 | Necessary for topological observability |
| 14 | Critical Measurement | Removing it makes network unobservable | $\text{diag}(\mathbf{R}) \subset \text{diag}(\mathbf{G}^{-1}\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{W}\mathbf{R}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{H}\mathbf{G}^{-1})$ |
| 15 | Observable Island | A maximal subgraph where all states are determinable | Used in topological algorithms |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EMS Implementation**: Every modern Energy Management System (e.g., Siemens Spectrum, GE PowerOn) runs an observability check **before** state estimation to ensure reliable operation.
- **PMU Placement**: Observability guides optimal PMU placement strategies for wide-area monitoring.
- **Cyber-Attack Detection**: Loss of observability can indicate false data injection attacks.
- **Renewable Integration**: Highly variable renewable generation creates time-varying observability needs handled by pseudo-measurements from forecasts.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: WLS State Estimator from First Principles

We start with the measurement model. We want to find $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ that best fits the measurements in a weighted least-squares sense.

**Step 1:** Define the residual vector:
$$\mathbf{r}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{z} - \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x}$$

**Step 2:** Define the scalar objective function as the weighted sum of squared residuals:
$$J(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{r}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{r} = (\mathbf{z} - \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x})^T \mathbf{W} (\mathbf{z} - \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x})$$

**Step 3:** Take the gradient with respect to $\mathbf{x}$:
$$\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = -2\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{W}(\mathbf{z} - \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x})$$

**Step 4:** Set gradient to zero for the optimal $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$:
$$-2\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{W}(\mathbf{z} - \mathbf{H}\hat{\mathbf{x}}) = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange to obtain the normal equation:
$$\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{W}\mathbf{H}\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{W}\mathbf{z}$$

**Step 6:** Identify the gain matrix and solution:
$$\mathbf{G} = \mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{H} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \hat{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{G}^{-1}\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{z}$$

**Step 7:** **Observability is required for $\mathbf{G}^{-1}$ to exist** — i.e., $\mathbf{G}$ must be non-singular, equivalent to $\mathbf{H}$ having full column rank.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Observability of a 3-Bus System (Worked Example)

Consider a 3-bus system. Let bus 1 be the reference. States: $V_2, V_3, \delta_2, \delta_3$ → $n = 4$.

Available measurements:
- $P_2^{\text{inj}}$ (real power injection at bus 2)
- $P_{12}$ (real power flow from bus 1 to 2)
- $V_3$ (voltage magnitude at bus 3)
- $Q_3^{\text{inj}}$ (reactive power injection at bus 3)

So $m = 4$, $n = 4$. We form the linearized Jacobian (in the DC approximation for clarity, or linearized around the flat start):

$$\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial P_2^{\text{inj}}}{\partial \delta_2} & \dfrac{\partial P_2^{\text{inj}}}{\partial \delta_3} & \dfrac{\partial P_2^{\text{inj}}}{\partial V_2} & \dfrac{\partial P_2^{\text{inj}}}{\partial V_3} \\ \dfrac{\partial P_{12}}{\partial \delta_2} & \dfrac{\partial P_{12}}{\partial \delta_3} & \dfrac{\partial P_{12}}{\partial V_2} & \dfrac{\partial P_{12}}{\partial V_3} \\ \dfrac{\partial V_3}{\partial \delta_2} & \dfrac{\partial V_3}{\partial \delta_3} & \dfrac{\partial V_3}{\partial V_2} & \dfrac{\partial V_3}{\partial V_3} \\ \dfrac{\partial Q_3^{\text{inj}}}{\partial \delta_2} & \dfrac{\partial Q_3^{\text{inj}}}{\partial \delta_3} & \dfrac{\partial Q_3^{\text{inj}}}{\partial V_2} & \dfrac{\partial Q_3^{\text{inj}}}{\partial V_3} \end{bmatrix}$$

By direct computation using the power flow Jacobian structure, and assuming all sensitivities are non-zero (network is connected with all branches in service), we get $\text{rank}(\mathbf{H}) = 4 = n$.

**Conclusion:** The network is **observable** with these 4 measurements.

> [!TIP]
> If we remove the measurement $V_3$, then $\text{rank}(\mathbf{H}) = 3 < 4$, and the network becomes **unobservable** at bus 3. We would need a **pseudo-measurement** (e.g., a forecasted $\hat{V}_3$) to restore observability.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Effect of Pseudo-Measurement on the Gain Matrix

Suppose we add a pseudo-measurement $z_{ps} = h_{ps}(x) + e_{ps}$ with variance $\sigma_{ps}^2$.

The augmented Jacobian becomes:
$$\mathbf{H}_{aug} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{H} \\ \mathbf{h}_{ps}^T \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{W}_{aug} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{W} & \mathbf{0} \\ \mathbf{0} & W_{ps} \end{bmatrix}$$

The augmented gain matrix:
$$\mathbf{G}_{aug} = \mathbf{H}_{aug}^T \mathbf{W}_{aug} \mathbf{H}_{aug} = \mathbf{G} + W_{ps}\mathbf{h}_{ps}\mathbf{h}_{ps}^T$$

By the **Sherman-Morrison rank-1 update theorem**:

$$\det(\mathbf{G}_{aug}) = \det(\mathbf{G})\left(1 + W_{ps}\mathbf{h}_{ps}^T \mathbf{G}^{-1} \mathbf{h}_{ps}\right)$$

**Key Insight:** If $\mathbf{G}$ is singular (unobservable), $\mathbf{G}^{-1}$ does not exist, but the rank-1 update may still make $\mathbf{G}_{aug}$ non-singular **only if** $\mathbf{h}_{ps}$ lies outside the null space of $\mathbf{G}$ — i.e., the pseudo-measurement is sensitive to the previously unobservable states.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Measurement Type vs. State Observability Contribution

| Measurement Type | Symbol | Observes | Pseudo-Measurement Substitute |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Real Power Injection | $P_i$ | $\delta_i$ primarily | Load forecast $P_L$ from ML/AI |
| Reactive Power Injection | $Q_i$ | $V_i$ primarily | Historical average $\bar{Q}$ |
| Real Power Flow | $P_{ij}$ | $\delta_i, \delta_j$ (difference) | Scheduled transaction |
| Reactive Power Flow | $Q_{ij}$ | $V_i, V_j$ magnitudes | Network reduction estimate |
| Voltage Magnitude | $V_i$ | $V_i$ directly | Forecasted voltage profile |
| Current Magnitude | $\vert I_{ij}\vert$ | $V_i, V_j$ both | Calculated from forecast |
| Phase Angle (PMU) | $\delta_{ij}$ | $\delta_i, \delta_j$ directly | Synchrophasor interpolation |
| Zero Injection | $P_i = 0, Q_i = 0$ | All neighboring states | **Perfect** virtual measurement |

---

### 3.5 Algorithm: Numerical Observability Test (Triangular Factorization)

The standard algorithm used in industrial EMS:

```
INPUT: G = H^T W H (n × n)
FOR k = 1 to n:
    d_k = G_{kk} - sum_{j=1}^{k-1} L_{kj}^2 d_j
    IF d_k ≤ ε_tol (e.g., 1e-9):
        NETWORK IS UNOBSERVABLE
        Identify unobservable state
    END IF
    FOR i = k+1 to n:
        L_{ik} = (G_{ik} - sum_{j=1}^{k-1} L_{ij} L_{kj} d_j) / d_k
    END FOR
END FOR
NETWORK IS OBSERVABLE
```

**Logic:** Each zero (or near-zero) pivot $d_k$ corresponds to an unobservable state. The location identifies which bus lacks measurements.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Observability & Pseudo-Measurement Integration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Telemetry Data] --> B[Measurement Pre-Processor]
    C[Load Forecast Module] --> D[Pseudo-Measurement Generator]
    E[Unit Commitment Scheduler] --> D
    F[Network Topology Processor] --> D
    G[Zero-Injection Detector] --> D
    D --> H[Pseudo-Measurement Bank]
    B --> I[Measurement Set Assembly]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Observability Analyzer]
    J --> K{Network Observable?}
    K -- Yes --> L[WLS State Estimator]
    K -- No --> M[Observable Island Identification]
    M --> N[Add Critical Pseudo-Measurement]
    N --> J
    L --> O[State Vector Estimate]
    O --> P[Bad Data Detection]
    P --> Q[Final Validated State]
```

**Description of the Architecture:**
- The **Measurement Pre-Processor** cleanses raw SCADA/PMU data.
- The **Pseudo-Measurement Generator** synthesizes virtual measurements from four independent sources.
- The **Observability Analyzer** performs the rank test on the Jacobian.
- The feedback loop at $N \to J$ represents iterative pseudo-measurement addition until observability is achieved.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Topological Observability via Spanning Tree

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[B1 Ref] -- P12 --> B[B2]
    B -- P23 --> C[B3]
    C -- P34 --> D[B4]
    A -- P13 --> C
    B -- P24 --> D
    M1((P inj B2)) --> B
    M2((P flow B1-B2)) --> A
    M3((Q inj B4)) --> D
    M4((V mag B3)) --> C
    M5((P flow B3-B4)) --> C
    subgraph Observable
        A
        B
        C
        D
    end
```

**Reading Guide:** The solid lines are power network branches. The colored circles are measurement locations. A **maximum spanning tree** can be formed using edges $P_{12}, P_{23}, P_{34}$ (length 3), which is $N-1 = 3$ edges for a 4-bus system. With measurements $P_2^{\text{inj}}, P_{12}, Q_4^{\text{inj}}, V_3, P_{34}$, all four bus states can be computed — hence **observable**.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Unobservable Network with Pseudo-Measurement Rescue

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Bus1 Ref] --> B[Bus2]
    B --> C[Bus3]
    C --> D[Bus4]
    M1((P2 inj)) -. Real Meas .-> B
    M2((P12 flow)) -. Real Meas .-> A
    MP((V4 forecast)) -. Pseudo Meas .-> D
    style MP fill:#f9f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style M1 fill:#9cf,stroke:#333
    style M2 fill:#9cf,stroke:#333
```

**Interpretation:** Without $M_P$ (the pseudo-measurement of $V_4$), buses 3 and 4 are in an unobservable island. Adding the pseudo-measurement of $V_4$ propagates observability back through $P_{34}$, $P_{23}$ flows to Bus 3.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define network observability. State the mathematical condition for a power network to be observable in the context of WLS state estimation.**

**Model Answer:**

Network observability is the property of a power system that determines whether the complete voltage state vector (magnitudes and phase angles at all buses) can be uniquely determined from the available set of real-time measurements using the WLS state estimator.

**Mathematical Condition:** A power network is observable if and only if the measurement Jacobian matrix $\mathbf{H}$ has full column rank:

$$\text{rank}(\mathbf{H}) = n = 2N - 1$$

Equivalently, the gain matrix $\mathbf{G} = \mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{H}$ must be non-singular so that $\mathbf{G}^{-1}$ exists, enabling the solution $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{G}^{-1} \mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{z}$.

If the network is not observable, the WLS estimator cannot converge to a unique solution because infinitely many state vectors can fit the same set of measurements.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for condition with equation)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What are pseudo-measurements? List any three sources of pseudo-measurements used in power system state estimation.**

**Model Answer:**

Pseudo-measurements are artificially generated or estimated quantities that are treated as additional measurements in the WLS state estimator. They are used to enhance the observability of a network where physical real-time measurements are insufficient. They are typically assigned a much higher variance (lower weight) than real measurements to reflect their reduced reliability.

**Three Sources of Pseudo-Measurements:**

1. **Load forecasts** — short-term predicted active and reactive power demands at unmeasured load buses.
2. **Scheduled generation outputs** — active and reactive power outputs from the unit commitment and economic dispatch modules.
3. **Zero-injection constraints** — at buses with no generation or load (e.g., transformer-only buses), $P_i = 0$ and $Q_i = 0$ are imposed as perfect virtual measurements.

Other acceptable answers: historical SCADA averages, network topology-derived estimates, PMU-interpolated values, renewable generation forecasts.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for three sources)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **topological observability analysis** of a power network. Describe the spanning tree method for determining observability and the role of critical measurements. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **numerical observability analysis** based on the LDLT factorization of the gain matrix. Show how a zero pivot in the factorization indicates unobservability and identify the corresponding unobservable state. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Topological Observability Analysis (7 marks)**

Topological observability analysis is a graph-theoretic approach that determines observability by examining the **structure** of the measurement network rather than numerical values.

**Definition (1 mark):** A power network is topologically observable if there exists at least one **spanning tree** of full rank formed by the measurement-instrumented branches.

**Spanning Tree Method (3 marks):**
- The power network is represented as a graph $\mathcal{G} = (\mathcal{B}, \mathcal{L})$ with bus set $\mathcal{B}$ and branch set $\mathcal{L}$.
- A **flow measurement** $P_{ij}$ or $Q_{ij}$ on a branch $(i,j)$ allows the spanning tree to include that branch.
- An **injection measurement** $P_i$ at bus $i$ "covers" all incident branches and can be used to "grow" a tree.
- A spanning tree with $N-1$ branches exists if and only if the network is topologically observable.

**Algorithm (2 marks):**
1. Start with an unobserved bus.
2. If it has an injection measurement, declare it observed and use the flow information on incident branches.
3. Propagate the observed state to neighboring buses through measured flow branches.
4. Repeat until all buses are observed or no further propagation is possible.

**Critical Measurements (1 mark):** A **critical measurement** is a measurement whose removal makes the network unobservable. The corresponding row of the normalized residual covariance matrix will be identically zero:
$$\text{diag}(\mathbf{R} - \mathbf{R} \mathbf{W}^{1/2} \mathbf{H} \mathbf{G}^{-1} \mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{W}^{1/2})$$

A zero diagonal entry on the $i$-th position indicates that measurement $z_i$ is critical.

---

**(b) Numerical Observability Analysis (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):** Numerical observability is determined by computing the rank of the Jacobian $\mathbf{H}$ or, equivalently, by performing triangular factorization of the gain matrix $\mathbf{G} = \mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{H}$.

**LDLT Factorization (3 marks):**
$$\mathbf{G} = \mathbf{L} \mathbf{D} \mathbf{L}^T$$

where $\mathbf{L}$ is lower triangular with unit diagonal and $\mathbf{D}$ is diagonal. The factorization proceeds as:

For $k = 1, 2, \ldots, n$:

$$d_k = G_{kk} - \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} L_{kj}^2 \, d_j$$

$$L_{ik} = \dfrac{1}{d_k}\left(G_{ik} - \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} L_{ij} L_{kj} d_j\right), \quad i = k+1, \ldots, n$$

**Zero Pivot Detection (2 marks):**
- If at any step $d_k \leq \varepsilon$ (numerical tolerance, typically $10^{-9}$), the factorization fails.
- The index $k$ corresponds to the **$k$-th state variable** in the ordering — i.e., the unobservable state is identified.
- Example: If $d_3 = 0$ and states are ordered as $[\delta_2, \delta_3, V_2, V_3]$, then $V_2$ is unobservable.

**Why LDLT and not LU (1 mark):** The gain matrix $\mathbf{G}$ is symmetric and positive semi-definite. LDLT preserves this structure, is computationally more efficient ($n^3/6$ flops vs $2n^3/3$ flops for general LU), and the diagonal pivots $d_k$ have direct physical interpretation as variance components.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** What are **pseudo-measurements**? Explain with examples the various sources of pseudo-measurements in power system state estimation. Discuss how the weighting of pseudo-measurements affects the accuracy of the state estimate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 4-bus power system with bus 1 as the reference, derive the **observability condition** using the rank criterion. If measurements $P_2^{\text{inj}}, P_3^{\text{inj}}, P_{23}, P_{34}, V_4$ are available, determine whether the network is observable. Justify your answer by computing the rank of the Jacobian for the linearized DC model. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Pseudo-Measurements (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):** Pseudo-measurements are estimates or prior information treated as measurements in the WLS state estimator. They supplement real-time measurements to make a network observable.

**Sources of Pseudo-Measurements (4 marks):**

1. **Load forecasts** — Predicted $P_L, Q_L$ at unmeasured load buses from short-term load forecasting models (regression, neural networks, time series). Example: $\hat{P}_{L,5} = 45.3 \pm 5.0$ MW.

2. **Generation schedules** — Day-ahead unit commitment and economic dispatch outputs provide $P_G, Q_G$ at unmeasured generator buses.

3. **Zero-injection constraints** — At transformer-only or transit buses with no load or generation, $P_i^{\text{calc}} = 0$ and $Q_i^{\text{calc}} = 0$ are imposed as **perfect** virtual measurements (zero variance).

4. **Historical data** — Time-averaged SCADA measurements from previous operating intervals used as estimates for currently unmeasured quantities.

5. **Network-topology-derived values** — Calculated from breaker/switch status and known transformer tap ratios.

**Weighting of Pseudo-Measurements (2 marks):**

The weight of a pseudo-measurement is $W_{ps} = 1/\sigma_{ps}^2$, where $\sigma_{ps}$ is much larger than the standard deviation of real measurements.

- **Effect on accuracy**: Lower weights (higher variance) make the estimator rely more on real measurements. If a pseudo-measurement is in error, its low weight limits the corruption of the estimate.
- **Trade-off**: A high-variance pseudo-measurement has minimal influence on $J(\mathbf{x})$ but also minimal ability to "anchor" the unobservable state.
- **Typical practice**: $\sigma_{ps} = 5$ to $20 \times \sigma_{real}$, e.g., $\sigma_{real} = 0.01$ pu for voltage, $\sigma_{ps} = 0.05$ to $0.20$ pu.

The augmented objective function becomes:
$$J_{aug}(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \dfrac{(z_i - h_i(\mathbf{x}))^2}{\sigma_i^2} + \sum_{j=1}^{m_{ps}} \dfrac{(z_{ps,j} - h_{ps,j}(\mathbf{x}))^2}{\sigma_{ps,j}^2}$$

---

**(b) Observability of 4-Bus System (7 marks)**

**System Setup (1 mark):**
- 4-bus system, bus 1 = reference
- States: $x = [\delta_2, \delta_3, \delta_4, V_2, V_3, V_4]^T$
- $n = 2N - 1 = 7$

**Available measurements (1 mark):** $P_2^{\text{inj}}, P_3^{\text{inj}}, P_{23}, P_{34}, V_4$ → $m = 5$

**DC Power Flow Model Approximation (1 mark):**
In the DC model, voltage magnitudes are constant at 1 pu, so only phase angles are state variables. Here we keep both $V$ and $\delta$ for rigor.

The Jacobian rows for each measurement:

**Row 1** ($P_2^{\text{inj}}$): Non-zero entries in columns corresponding to $\delta_2, \delta_3, \delta_4$ (all branches from bus 2), and to $V_2$.

**Row 2** ($P_3^{\text{inj}}$): Non-zero entries in columns $\delta_2, \delta_3, \delta_4$, and $V_3$.

**Row 3** ($P_{23}$): Non-zero entries in columns $\delta_2, \delta_3$, and $V_2, V_3$.

**Row 4** ($P_{34}$): Non-zero entries in columns $\delta_3, \delta_4$, and $V_3, V_4$.

**Row 5** ($V_4$): Non-zero entry in column $V_4$ only (a 1).

**Rank Analysis (3 marks):**

Form the $5 \times 7$ Jacobian $\mathbf{H}$:

$$\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} h_{11} & h_{12} & h_{13} & h_{14} & 0 & 0 \\ h_{21} & h_{22} & h_{23} & 0 & h_{25} & 0 \\ h_{31} & h_{32} & 0 & h_{34} & h_{35} & 0 \\ 0 & h_{42} & h_{43} & 0 & h_{45} & h_{46} \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

By inspection, the last row has a non-zero entry in column 6 (state $V_4$) only. Removing this row gives a $4 \times 7$ submatrix that must be analyzed.

**Conclusion:** With $m = 5 < n = 7$, the maximum possible rank of $\mathbf{H}$ is 5, but full column rank requires $\text{rank}(\mathbf{H}) = 7$. This is **impossible** with only 5 measurements.

**Final Verdict (1 mark):** The network is **NOT observable** with the given measurements. The states $V_2$ and $V_3$ are in an unobservable island, and at least 2 additional pseudo-measurements are required to make the system observable.

To restore observability, we could add: (i) pseudo-measurement of $V_2$ (forecasted), and (ii) pseudo-measurement of $P_2^{\text{inj}}$ or $Q_3^{\text{inj}}$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "observable" with "over-determined"** — A network can be over-determined ($m > n$) but still unobservable if measurements are poorly placed. *Always check rank, not just measurement count.*
>
> 2. **Forgetting the reference bus convention** — The phase angle at the reference bus is fixed ($\delta_1 = 0$), so the state vector has $n = 2N - 1$ elements, **not** $2N$. Students who write $n = 2N$ lose 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Treating pseudo-measurements as real measurements in weight** — A pseudo-measurement with $\sigma = 0.01$ (same as real) is effectively treated as truth. The whole point of pseudo-measurements is the high variance. *Always justify the weight choice.*
>
> 4. **Confusing "critical measurement" with "bad measurement"** — A critical measurement is one whose loss makes the network unobservable. A bad measurement is one with gross error. They are not the same.
>
> 5. **Skipping the convergence discussion** — In numerical observability, mention the LDLT factorization with explicit pivot computation. Writing only "rank = n" without showing the computation loses 2–3 marks in Part B.
>
> 6. **Ignoring zero-injection buses** — Zero-injection constraints are FREE observability. Always identify them first before adding forecast-based pseudo-measurements.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Network Observability & Pseudo-Measurements**

### **Core Concepts:**
- [ ] Observability is a **prerequisite** for state estimation, not a result of it.
- [ ] Mathematically: $\text{rank}(\mathbf{H}) = n$ where $n = 2N - 1$.
- [ ] Gain matrix singularity $\det(\mathbf{G}) = 0$ ↔ unobservable.
- [ ] LDLT factorization of $\mathbf{G}$ detects unobservable states via zero pivots.

### **Types of Observability:**
- [ ] **Topological** — graph-theoretic, uses spanning trees.
- [ ] **Numerical** — rank/factorization-based, more accurate.

### **Pseudo-Measurements — The 5 Sources:**
- [ ] Load forecasts
- [ ] Generation schedules
- [ ] Zero-injection constraints (MOST important — perfect virtual measurements)
- [ ] Historical SCADA averages
- [ ] Topology-derived estimates

### **Weighting Rules:**
- [ ] Real measurement $\sigma$: 0.01 to 0.02 pu
- [ ] Pseudo-measurement $\sigma_{ps}$: 5–20× $\sigma_{real}$
- [ ] Zero-injection: $\sigma \to 0$ (perfect measurement, infinite weight)

### **Key Numerical Benchmarks:**
- [ ] Practical redundancy $\eta \geq 1.5$ to $2.0$
- [ ] Minimum measurements $m_{\min} = N - 1$ (spanning tree)
- [ ] $\mathbf{G}^{-1}$ gives covariance of estimate

### **Algorithm Triggers (must know for KTU):**
- [ ] Loss of telemetry → run observability check
- [ ] Add pseudo-measurement if unobservable island detected
- [ ] Critical measurements → flag for protection/duplication

### **One-Line Mantra for Exam:**
> *"Observability = full rank H; Pseudo-measurements = high-variance fillers to restore rank."*

### **Common KTU Question Patterns:**
1. "Define observability and state the rank condition." → 3 marks
2. "Explain pseudo-measurements with sources." → 7–14 marks
3. "Determine observability of a given system." → 7–14 marks
4. "Discuss topological vs numerical methods." → 7–14 marks

**Total marks for this topic in KTU ESE:** Typically **14 marks (1 full question)**, sometimes combined with state estimation for **14 marks combined**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Use of Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET741 - POWER SYSTEM OPERATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** The Use of Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phasor Measurement Unit (PMU):** A PMU is a digital, time-synchronized measurement device that estimates the magnitude, phase angle, and frequency of voltage and current waveforms at a power system bus in real time, using a **Global Positioning System (GPS)**-referenced one-pulse-per-second (1-PPS) clock as the universal time reference.

A **synchrophasor** is the phasor value produced by a PMU, measured at a precise instant in time that is common across the entire interconnected grid. The phasor is reported as a complex number (real + imaginary) corresponding to the cosine reference synchronized to the **UTC (Coordinated Universal Time)** second rollover.

> [!NOTE]
> **Governing Standard (KTU 2024 syllabus reference):** IEEE C37.118.1 (2011) / IEEE Std C37.118.1a-2014 (Amendment), superseded by **IEEE/IEC 60255-118-1:2018** for synchrophasor measurement definitions, and **IEEE/IEC 60255-118-2:2018** for data transfer.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have **10 stopwatches scattered across a city**, each timing a runner in a 100-m race. If every stopwatch starts at a *different* unknown instant, you cannot tell which runner is *truly* ahead — the clocks are not synchronized. Now give **all 10 stopwatches an atomic-clock signal from a GPS satellite** so that all of them tick "0" at the *exact same global instant*. Suddenly, you can compare runners with microsecond precision, even though they are kilometres apart.

That is precisely what a **PMU does for the power grid**:
- **GPS 1-PPS pulse** = the universal starting gun
- **Voltage/Current phasor** = the "position" of the waveform at that instant
- **PMU network** = synchronized "stopwatches" at every bus

Without PMUs, an **Energy Management System (EMS)** only had **SCADA** snapshots every 2–4 seconds — slow, asynchronous, and unable to capture rapid dynamic phenomena. With PMUs, we get **30 to 120 synchronized snapshots per second** — fast enough to *see* rotor swings, voltage collapse, and inter-area oscillations as they unfold.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Must Remember)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Standard Bound |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reporting Rate** | $F_s$ | 10–120 frames/sec | 10, 25, 50, 60, 100, 120 fps |
| **Total Vector Error** | **TVE** | < 1% | ≤ 1% (under steady state) |
| **Time Stamp Accuracy** | $\tau_{UTC}$ | ±1 μs | ±1 μs (UTC alignment) |
| **Frequency Error** | **FE** | < 0.005 Hz | ≤ 0.005 Hz |
| **Rate of Change of Frequency** | **ROCOF** | < 0.01 Hz/s | ≤ 0.01 Hz/s |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Hint:** When asked *"Why are PMUs superior to conventional SCADA measurements?"*, the answer is **three-fold**: (1) Synchronized (common time reference), (2) High reporting rate (≥ 30 Hz vs. SCADA's ~0.25 Hz), and (3) Direct measurement of **phase angle** — which SCADA cannot provide.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Block Architecture of a PMU

A PMU is a layered digital instrument. From the analog power system to the communication network, the signal undergoes the following transformations:

**Step 1 — Signal Acquisition:**
- Voltage signals from **Potential Transformers (PTs)**
- Current signals from **Current Transformers (CTs)**
- Both scaled to low-level analog voltages (±10 V or 4–20 mA equivalents)

**Step 2 — Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF):**
- A low-pass filter (typically 4th-order Butterworth) with cutoff at $f_c \le F_s/2$ (Nyquist criterion)
- Prevents frequency aliasing before sampling

**Step 3 — Analog-to-Digital Conversion (A/D):**
- Sampling rate $F_s$ typically 96 to 2400 samples per nominal cycle
- 16-bit (or higher) resolution
- Sampling clock **disciplined by GPS 1-PPS** to keep absolute time

**Step 4 — Phasor Estimation Algorithm:**
- Most common: **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** over one or more cycles
- The estimated phasor at instant $t$ is:

$$X(k) = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(nT_s) \cdot e^{-j2\pi nk/N}$$

- The $\sqrt{2}$ factor converts RMS phasor representation to peak.

**Step 5 — Time-Stamping & Synchronization:**
- Each phasor is tagged with a **UTC time stamp** derived from the GPS receiver
- The 1-PPS rising edge marks the start of each UTC second

**Step 6 — Data Formatting & Transmission:**
- Output data frame per IEEE C37.118.2 / IEEE 60255-118-2
- Header + configuration block + data block + command frame

### 2.2 Why PMUs? — The "Why" Behind the Concept

> The classical EMS relied on the **DC power flow** model with measurements that were:
> 1. **Asynchronous** (different scan times),
> 2. **Low bandwidth** (2–4 s update),
> 3. **Not phasorial** (no phase angle).

PMUs directly address all three limitations by:
- Providing **time-synchronized** phasors → enables direct AC state estimation.
- Sampling at **30–120 Hz** → captures electromechanical dynamics (0.1–5 Hz).
- Reporting **voltage phase angle** → the most informative state variable for angle stability.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Physical Meaning / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Synchrophasor (general) | $X(t) = X_m(t) \, e^{j\phi(t)}$ | Complex phasor with magnitude and phase |
| 2 | Phase Angle from Frequency Deviation | $\theta(t) = 2\pi \int_0^t \left[ f(\tau) - f_{nom} \right] d\tau$ | Links bus frequency to phase angle drift |
| 3 | Frequency Estimation from Phase | $f = f_{nom} + \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt}$ | ROCOF-style frequency measurement |
| 4 | **Total Vector Error (TVE)** | $TVE = \frac{\vert \hat{X} - X_{true} \vert}{\vert X_{true} \vert} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(\hat{X}_r - X_{r,true})^2 + (\hat{X}_i - X_{i,true})^2}{X_{r,true}^2 + X_{i,true}^2}}$ | Composite accuracy metric (must ≤ 1%) |
| 5 | **Frequency Error (FE)** | $FE = \vert \hat{f} - f_{true} \vert$ | Hz error in measured frequency |
| 6 | **ROCOF Error (RFE)** | $RFE = \vert \widehat{df/dt} - (df/dt)_{true} \vert$ | Hz/s error in rate of change |
| 7 | DFT Phasor Estimate | $X_k = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot e^{-j 2\pi nk/N}$ | Single-frequency phasor extraction |
| 8 | PMU Reporting Latency | $t_{latency} = t_{stamp} - t_{acquisition}$ | Time from signal capture to UDP dispatch |
| 9 | Inter-Area Oscillation Frequency | $f_{osc} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{\omega_s}{2H}} \cdot \sin(\delta_{12}/2)$ | Dominant mode in wide-area swings |
| 10 | GPS Clock Skew Constraint | $\Delta t \le 1 \,\mu s \Rightarrow \Delta\theta \le 0.0216^\circ$ (at 60 Hz) | Phase angle uncertainty from time error |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | PMU-Based Application |
|---|---|
| **Transmission Operator (e.g., PGCIL, USA ISO/RTO)** | Real-time congestion & angle-difference monitoring |
| **Renewable Integration** | Phase-angle-based control of Type-4 wind/solar inverters |
| **Protection** | PMU-assisted out-of-step / distance protection backup |
| **Post-mortem Analysis** | Replay of disturbance at 30–60 fps to find root cause |
| **Model Validation** | Compare dynamic simulation against measured synchrophasors |
| **Wide-Area Control (WAMS/WACS)** | Damping of inter-area modes via FACTS / HVDC modulation |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Total Vector Error (TVE) — From First Principles

**Problem Setup:** A PMU estimates a synchrophasor $\hat{X}$ (complex number), but the **true** synchrophasor is $X_{true}$. Both lie on the complex plane. We want a single scalar metric of accuracy.

**Step 1 — Write both phasors in rectangular components:**

$$\hat{X} = \hat{X}_r + j\hat{X}_i, \quad X_{true} = X_{r,true} + jX_{i,true}$$

**Step 2 — The Euclidean distance between the two points on the complex plane is:**

$$d = \sqrt{(\hat{X}_r - X_{r,true})^2 + (\hat{X}_i - X_{i,true})^2}$$

This is the magnitude of the **error vector** $\hat{X} - X_{true}$.

**Step 3 — Normalize this distance by the magnitude of the true phasor to obtain a dimensionless (and hence scale-independent) accuracy index:**

$$TVE = \frac{d}{\vert X_{true} \vert} = \frac{\sqrt{(\hat{X}_r - X_{r,true})^2 + (\hat{X}_i - X_{i,true})^2}}{\sqrt{X_{r,true}^2 + X_{i,true}^2}}$$

**Step 4 — Square both numerator and denominator to get a useful form for error budgeting:**

$$TVE^2 = \frac{(\hat{X}_r - X_{r,true})^2 + (\hat{X}_i - X_{i,true})^2}{X_{r,true}^2 + X_{i,true}^2}$$

**Step 5 — Decompose into magnitude and phase errors (very useful in KTU problems):**

Let $\hat{X} = \hat{X}_m \angle \hat{\theta}$ and $X_{true} = X_m \angle \theta_{true}$. Then:

$$\hat{X} - X_{true} = (\hat{X}_m \cos\hat{\theta} - X_m \cos\theta_{true}) + j(\hat{X}_m \sin\hat{\theta} - X_m \sin\theta_{true})$$

Using trigonometric identities and Taylor expansion (small errors $\Delta X_m, \Delta \theta$), one obtains:

$$TVE \approx \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\Delta X_m}{X_m}\right)^2 + (\Delta \theta)^2}$$

This approximation shows that **TVE combines magnitude and phase errors in quadrature** — a clean engineering result for KTU derivations.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Past Exam Insight:** "Show that TVE = 1% means combined magnitude and phase error budget must lie on a quarter-circle in the (ΔX/X, Δθ) plane." This is the practical engineering use of the formula above.

### 3.2 Derivation of Phase Angle from Time-Synchronization Error

**Problem:** The GPS 1-PPS signal has a worst-case time error $\Delta t$ (typically $\pm 1 \,\mu s$). What is the resulting phase-angle error for a 50 Hz system?

**Step 1 — A sinusoid $v(t) = V_m \cos(\omega t + \theta)$ completes one full cycle in time $T = 1/f$.**

$$T = \frac{1}{50} = 0.02 \, \text{s} = 20 \, \text{ms}$$

**Step 2 — One full electrical cycle corresponds to a phase change of $2\pi$ radians = $360^\circ$.**

So phase change per unit time is:

$$\frac{d\theta}{dt} = 2\pi f \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \Delta\theta = 2\pi f \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\Delta t = 1 \, \mu s = 10^{-6}$ s and $f = 50$ Hz:**

$$\Delta\theta = 2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 10^{-6} = 100\pi \cdot 10^{-6} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{rad}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to degrees:**

$$\Delta\theta = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 0.018^\circ$$

**Step 5 — Engineering conclusion:** A 1 μs GPS timing error translates to only $0.018^\circ$ of phase-angle uncertainty in a 50 Hz grid — this is why GPS synchronization is **adequate** (not perfect, but compliant with the 1% TVE budget for angle stability monitoring).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sources of PMU Error and Their Mitigation

| Error Source | Symbol | Typical Magnitude | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Instrument Transformer (CT/PT) errors | $\varepsilon_{CT}, \varepsilon_{PT}$ | 0.3%–1% | Calibration, better-class CTs/PTs |
| Analog anti-aliasing filter phase shift | $\phi_{AAF}$ | 0.05°–0.2° | Software phase-compensation filter |
| A/D quantization | $q$ | 0.01% | 16-bit or 24-bit ADC |
| DFT leakage (off-nominal frequency) | $\varepsilon_{DFT}$ | 0.1%–2% | Use **IpDFT** or **windowed DFT** |
| GPS timing jitter | $\Delta t$ | ±50 ns typical | OCXO disciplined oscillator backup |
| Communication latency jitter | $t_{jitter}$ | 1–10 ms | UDP with IEEE C37.118.2 protocol |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Question:** A PMU measures a voltage phasor as $\hat{V} = 220.5 \angle 30.4^\circ$ V (line-to-neutral, RMS). The true value is $V_{true} = 220.0 \angle 30.0^\circ$ V. Compute the Total Vector Error in percent.

**Solution — Step 1: Convert both phasors to rectangular form.**

$$\hat{V}_r = 220.5 \cos(30.4^\circ) = 220.5 \times 0.8625 = 190.18 \, \text{V}$$

$$\hat{V}_i = 220.5 \sin(30.4^\circ) = 220.5 \times 0.5060 = 111.57 \, \text{V}$$

$$V_{r,true} = 220.0 \cos(30.0^\circ) = 220.0 \times 0.8660 = 190.52 \, \text{V}$$

$$V_{i,true} = 220.0 \sin(30.0^\circ) = 220.0 \times 0.5000 = 110.00 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 2: Apply the TVE formula.**

$$TVE = \frac{\sqrt{(190.18 - 190.52)^2 + (111.57 - 110.00)^2}}{\sqrt{190.52^2 + 110.00^2}}$$

$$= \frac{\sqrt{(-0.34)^2 + (1.57)^2}}{\sqrt{36298 + 12100}} = \frac{\sqrt{0.1156 + 2.4649}}{\sqrt{48398}} = \frac{\sqrt{2.5805}}{219.99}$$

$$= \frac{1.6064}{219.99} = 0.00730 = 0.730\%$$

**Step 3: Interpretation.**

$$TVE = 0.73\% < 1\% \quad \Rightarrow \text{Measurement is COMPLIANT with IEEE C37.118.1}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Valuation Trick:** Always carry 4 significant figures in intermediate steps. A 0.730% answer looks impressive; 0.7% may be undervalued in board marking. **Show the rectangular conversion explicitly** — that's where 2 of the 7 marks usually come from.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PMU Internal Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PS[Power System Bus] -->|Voltage| PT[PT Step-down]
    PS -->|Current| CT[CT Step-down]
    PT --> AAF[Anti-Aliasing Filter]
    CT --> AAF
    AAF --> ADC[16-bit A-D Converter]
    GPS[GPS Satellite Array] -->|1-PPS| GPSR[GPS Receiver Module]
    GPSR --> CLK[Disciplined Clock OCXO]
    CLK --> ADC
    CLK --> TS[UTC Time Stamp]
    ADC --> PE[Phasor Estimator DFT]
    TS --> PE
    PE --> FRAME[Data Frame Builder IEEE C37.118.2]
    FRAME -->|Ethernet| PDC[Phasor Data Concentrator]
    PDC --> EMS[Energy Management System]
    PDC --> WAMS[Wide Area Monitoring System]
    WAMS --> CTRL[Wide Area Control]
    EMS --> OP[System Operator Display]
```

### 4.2 Synchrophasor Reporting — Time-Aligned Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BusA[Substation A - 50 Hz Grid]
        PMUA[PMU at Bus A]
    end
    subgraph BusB[Substation B - 200 km away]
        PMUB[PMU at Bus B]
    end
    subgraph BusC[Substation C - 350 km away]
        PMUC[PMU at Bus C]
    end
    GPSX[GPS Constellation - UTC reference] -->|1-PPS| PMUA
    GPSX -->|1-PPS| PMUB
    GPSX -->|1-PPS| PMUC
    PMUA -->|Frame 12:00:00.000| PDCNET[Phasor Data Concentrator PDC]
    PMUB -->|Frame 12:00:00.000| PDCNET
    PMUC -->|Frame 12:00:00.000| PDCNET
    PDCNET --> ARCH[Archival Historian PI Server]
    PDCNET --> RTDMS[Real-Time Dynamics Monitoring System]
    PDCNET --> SE[State Estimator with PMU Measurements]
```

### 4.3 PMU vs SCADA — Reporting Timeline Comparison

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Grid as Power System
    participant SCADA as SCADA RTU
    participant EMS1 as SCADA-EMS
    participant PMU as PMU Device
    participant WAMS as WAMS Server
    Grid->>SCADA: Analog signal 1
    Note over SCADA: Latency 2 to 4 s
    SCADA->>EMS1: Measurement
    Grid->>PMU: Analog signal 2
    Note over PMU: GPS sync 1 PPS
    PMU->>WAMS: Phasor 1
    PMU->>WAMS: Phasor 2
    PMU->>WAMS: Phasor 3
    PMU->>WAMS: Phasor 4
    Note over WAMS: 30 to 60 phasors per second
```

### 4.4 PMU Data Frame Structure (IEEE C37.118.2)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FR[IEEE C37.118.2 Data Frame] --> SYNC[SYNC Word 2 bytes - AA 01]
    FR --> FRAMESIZE[Frame Size 2 bytes]
    FR --> ID[PMU ID Code 2 bytes]
    FR --> SOC[Second of Century 4 bytes UTC]
    FR --> FRACSEC[Fraction of Second 4 bytes]
    FR --> STAT[Status Word 2 bytes]
    FR --> PHAS[Phasor Block 8 bytes per phasor]
    FR --> FREQ[Frequency 4 bytes]
    FR --> DFREQ[DF-DT ROCOF 4 bytes]
    FR --> ANA[Analog Values optional]
    FR --> DIG[Digital Status optional]
    FR --> CRC[CRC-CCITT 2 bytes]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
*Define a Phasor Measurement Unit (PMU). List any two advantages of PMU-based measurement over conventional SCADA measurement.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Phasor Measurement Unit (PMU)** is a device that measures the voltage and current phasors (magnitude and phase angle) of an electrical signal at a power system bus in real time, using a **GPS-synchronized time reference** for the measurement instant.

**Two advantages over SCADA:**

1. **Synchronized phase-angle measurement** — PMUs give a common time-stamped phasor across the grid; SCADA cannot directly measure phase angle. (1½ marks)
2. **Higher reporting rate** — PMUs report at 30–120 frames/second versus SCADA's 2–4 s update interval, enabling capture of **electromechanical dynamics and oscillations**. (1½ marks)

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition = 1, Two advantages = 1 + 1.

---

#### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
*What is meant by a "synchrophasor"? Why is GPS synchronization essential for synchrophasor measurement?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **synchrophasor** is a phasor value (magnitude and phase) of a sinusoidal voltage or current waveform, **referenced to a common absolute time standard**, typically the **UTC (Coordinated Universal Time)** second rollover. (1½ marks)

**GPS synchronization is essential because:**

- Without a common time reference, the phase angles measured at different buses cannot be directly compared.
- GPS provides a **1-pulse-per-second (1-PPS)** signal with accuracy of **±1 μs**, allowing the entire grid to be measured against the same global instant. (1½ marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Module 4 Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the following (Question A or Question B). Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a PMU. (7 marks)

**(b)** A PMU measures a voltage phasor as $\hat{V} = 230.0 \angle 25.5^\circ$ V, while the true reference is $V_{true} = 228.0 \angle 25.0^\circ$ V. Compute the **Total Vector Error (TVE)** and comment on its compliance with the IEEE C37.118.1 standard. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A(a): PMU Functional Architecture (7 marks)**

The PMU consists of the following functional blocks (1 mark each for naming, 1 mark for the diagram's role and connectivity):

1. **Signal Conditioning (PT/CT)** — Step-down transformers scale the bus voltage and current to safe low-level signals. (1 mark)
2. **Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF)** — A low-pass filter prevents frequency aliasing by removing spectral content above $F_s/2$. (1 mark)
3. **A/D Converter** — Synchronously samples the analog signals at a clock disciplined by the GPS 1-PPS reference. Typical: 16-bit, $F_s$ = 96 samples/cycle. (1 mark)
4. **Phasor Estimation Block (DFT)** — Computes the discrete Fourier transform over one cycle to extract magnitude and phase. (1 mark)
5. **GPS Receiver & Time-Stamping Unit** — Provides 1-PPS reference and generates a UTC time tag for each phasor estimate. (1 mark)
6. **Data Formatting & Communication** — Packages the phasor into IEEE C37.118.2 frames and transmits via Ethernet to the Phasor Data Concentrator (PDC). (1 mark)
7. **Diagram** — A neat block diagram is to be drawn showing all the above blocks with arrows indicating signal flow from PT/CT input to Ethernet output. (1 mark)

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A(b): TVE Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:** $\hat{V} = 230.0 \angle 25.5^\circ$ V, $V_{true} = 228.0 \angle 25.0^\circ$ V.

**Step 1: Convert to rectangular form.** (1 mark for formula, 1 mark for computation)

$$\hat{V}_r = 230.0 \cos(25.5^\circ) = 230.0 \times 0.9026 = 207.59 \, \text{V}$$

$$\hat{V}_i = 230.0 \sin(25.5^\circ) = 230.0 \times 0.4305 = 99.02 \, \text{V}$$

$$V_{r,true} = 228.0 \cos(25.0^\circ) = 228.0 \times 0.9063 = 206.64 \, \text{V}$$

$$V_{i,true} = 228.0 \sin(25.0^\circ) = 228.0 \times 0.4226 = 96.35 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 2: Apply the TVE formula.** (1 mark for formula, 1 mark for substitution)

$$TVE = \frac{\sqrt{(207.59 - 206.64)^2 + (99.02 - 96.35)^2}}{\sqrt{206.64^2 + 96.35^2}}$$

**Step 3: Numerical evaluation.** (1 mark for numerator, 1 mark for denominator and division)

$$= \frac{\sqrt{(0.95)^2 + (2.67)^2}}{\sqrt{42700 + 9283}} = \frac{\sqrt{0.9025 + 7.1289}}{\sqrt{51983}} = \frac{\sqrt{8.0314}}{227.99}$$

$$= \frac{2.834}{227.99} = 0.01243 = 1.243\%$$

**Step 4: Comment on compliance.** (1 mark)

Since TVE = **1.243%** is **marginally greater than 1%**, the measurement **does NOT strictly comply** with the IEEE C37.118.1 standard for a 50 Hz reporting class. **Mitigation:** Recalibrate the PT or apply a software phase correction.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Wide Area Measurement System (WAMS)** built using PMUs. List any four applications of WAMS in modern power system operation. (7 marks)

**(b)** A GPS receiver used for PMU synchronization has a worst-case timing error of $\pm 1 \,\mu s$. Calculate the corresponding phase-angle uncertainty in a 60 Hz power system. Is this uncertainty acceptable for a PMU used in inter-area oscillation monitoring? (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B(a): WAMS Concept & Applications (7 marks)**

**WAMS Concept (3 marks):** A **Wide Area Measurement System (WAMS)** is a network of **time-synchronized PMUs** distributed across a power grid, whose measurements are aggregated at a **Phasor Data Concentrator (PDC)** and processed for **real-time visualization, control, and post-event analysis** of the power system. The hallmark of WAMS is that all measurements share a **common GPS time base**, enabling direct, simultaneous comparison of voltages, currents, and frequencies across geographically distant buses.

**Four Applications of WAMS (4 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Real-time voltage and angle stability monitoring** — operators observe phase-angle differences across the grid and detect impending instability.
2. **Inter-area oscillation detection and damping** — modal analysis of PMU data identifies low-frequency (~0.1–1 Hz) oscillations between control areas.
3. **State estimation enhancement** — PMU data is used as direct measurements in linear state estimators, improving convergence and accuracy.
4. **Post-disturbance analysis & model validation** — synchrophasor archives are replayed to identify the root cause of disturbances and to calibrate power-system simulation models.

*(Alternative valid applications: frequency response monitoring, renewable-integration control, adaptive protection, congestion management.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B(b): Phase-Angle Uncertainty from GPS Error (7 marks)**

**Given:** GPS timing error $\Delta t = \pm 1 \, \mu s = \pm 10^{-6}$ s, system frequency $f = 60$ Hz.

**Step 1: Identify the relationship between time and phase.** (1 mark)

The phase angle of a sinusoid advances at a rate of $2\pi f$ radians per second:

$$\theta(t) = 2\pi f t \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta\theta = 2\pi f \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 2: Substitute values and compute in radians.** (2 marks)

$$\Delta\theta = 2\pi \cdot 60 \cdot 10^{-6} = 120\pi \cdot 10^{-6} = 3.7699 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{rad}$$

**Step 3: Convert to degrees.** (1 mark)

$$\Delta\theta = 3.7699 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 0.0216^\circ$$

**Step 4: Equivalence to electrical degrees.** (1 mark)

In a 60 Hz system, $0.0216^\circ$ corresponds to:

$$\Delta\theta_{elec} = 0.0216 \times \frac{3600''}{1^\circ} \cdot \frac{1}{1} = 77.76'' \approx 78'' \, \text{arc-seconds}$$

**Step 5: Acceptability comment.** (2 marks)

For **inter-area oscillation monitoring** (mode frequencies 0.1–1 Hz, amplitude 1–5°), a phase-angle noise floor of $0.0216^\circ$ is **comfortably below 1% of typical oscillation amplitude** and is therefore **ACCEPTABLE**. PMU vendors guarantee the IEEE 1% TVE budget is met when GPS discipline is held within $\pm 1 \,\mu s$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting rectangular conversion in TVE problems** — direct subtraction of polar magnitudes gives the wrong answer. Always convert to $\hat{X}_r + j\hat{X}_i$ first. (–2 to –3 marks)
> 2. **Confusing FE/ROCOF with TVE** — these are *separate* error metrics per IEEE C37.118.1; the question must be read carefully. (–1 to –2 marks)
> 3. **Using $2\pi$ vs $\pi$** in phase-time conversion — for a 50 Hz system, $\Delta\theta = 2\pi f \Delta t$, NOT $\pi f \Delta t$. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Not stating units in TVE answer** — always write "TVE = 0.73% (compliant)" not just "0.73". (–0.5 mark)
> 5. **Calling PMU and PDC the same thing** — PMU is the *field* device, PDC is the *central concentrator*. This conflation costs a mark. (–1 mark)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision — PMUs (Module 4)**

- ✅ PMU = GPS-synchronized phasor measurement device — reports magnitude + phase angle + frequency + ROCOF.
- ✅ Synchrophasor = phasor referenced to **UTC** common time.
- ✅ GPS provides **1-PPS** (one pulse per second) with ±1 μs accuracy.
- ✅ Phase-time relationship: $\Delta\theta = 2\pi f \cdot \Delta t$ — know this formula cold.
- ✅ **TVE ≤ 1%** is the standard accuracy benchmark (IEEE C37.118.1 / IEEE 60255-118-1).
- ✅ Reporting rates: **10, 25, 50, 60, 100, 120 fps** — choose based on dynamic phenomena to be captured.
- ✅ PMU > SCADA: synchronized, faster, and provides phase angle.
- ✅ **WAMS = network of PMUs + PDC + visualization**; enables wide-area monitoring and control.
- ✅ Key applications: real-time stability monitoring, oscillation detection, linear state estimation, model validation, post-event analysis.
- ✅ Phasor estimation: usually via **DFT** over one nominal cycle; multi-cycle window improves accuracy but reduces reporting rate.
- ✅ Three standard error metrics: **TVE, FE, RFE** — all must be reported.
- ✅ Communication protocol: **IEEE C37.118.2 / IEEE 60255-118-2** (data format and transport).
- ✅ Remember the **Phasor Data Concentrator (PDC)** — aggregates, time-aligns, and forwards PMU data to higher-level applications.
- ✅ GPS timing error of 1 μs gives **0.018° at 50 Hz** and **0.0216° at 60 Hz** — always know this conversion.
- ✅ **DFT leakage** at off-nominal frequencies is the *biggest* practical source of PMU error — mitigated by **IpDFT** or windowed DFT.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of Power Systems State Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET741 - POWER SYSTEM OPERATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Application of Power Systems State Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Power System State Estimation (PSSE)** is a statistical data-processing algorithm that uses a redundant set of real-time telemetered measurements (bus voltage magnitudes, power injections, power flows, and branch currents) along with network topology information to compute the **best estimate of the current operating state** of an electric power system.

Mathematically, the state estimator solves an **overdetermined nonlinear system** of the form:

$$z = h(x) + e$$

where:
- $z$ = measurement vector (dimension $m$)
- $x$ = state vector (dimension $n$, typically all bus voltage magnitudes and angles, with one angle as reference)
- $h(x)$ = nonlinear vector function relating states to measurements
- $e$ = measurement error vector (assumed Gaussian with zero mean)

The "state" of the power system is conventionally defined by the complex bus voltages $\mathbf{V}_i \angle \delta_i$ for all buses, giving $n = 2N_{bus} - 1$ state variables (one angle is chosen as the slack/reference).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "GPS Analogy"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Understanding:** Imagine you are inside a large, foggy city (the power grid) and you want to know the exact GPS coordinates of your location (the system's state). You do not have a perfect GPS — instead, you have multiple rough indicators: a barometer (analogous to voltage magnitude), a compass (analogous to power flow direction), a pedometer (analogous to power injection), and visual landmarks (analogous to PMU measurements). Each individual indicator has **measurement noise**. 
>
> A **state estimator** acts like a *fused sensor processor* — it mathematically combines all the noisy, redundant data to produce a single, statistically optimal location estimate that is **far more accurate** than any single raw measurement. If one indicator (sensor) is faulty (bad data), the estimator can also detect and reject it.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in State Estimation

- **Real-time cycle time:** 1–5 seconds (for control center applications)
- **Measurement redundancy ratio:** $m/n \approx 1.5$ to $3.0$ (industry standard)
- **Number of state variables:** $n = 2N - 1$ for an $N$-bus system
- **Reference bus angle:** Typically $\delta_{slack} = 0^\circ$ (constraint)
- **Measurement error standard deviation:** Typically **1% to 2%** of full scale for conventional SCADA, and **0.1% to 0.5%** for PMU-based phasors
- **Weighted Least Squares (WLS) gain matrix:** $G = H^T R^{-1} H$ (sparse, symmetric, positive definite)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is State Estimation called the "Heart" of the Energy Management System (EMS)?** 
> Because virtually every downstream real-time application — security assessment, contingency analysis, economic dispatch, automatic generation control, and market clearing — requires a consistent, reliable, and complete snapshot of the system state. The raw SCADA measurements are noisy, may be missing, may contain gross errors, and may even have topology errors. State estimation filters and reconciles all of this into a clean, network-model-consistent database that the rest of the EMS can trust.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why State Estimation? — The Operational Motivation

The raw measurements arriving at a control center from Remote Terminal Units (RTUs) and Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs) suffer from several practical limitations:

1. **Measurement noise** — Transducer errors, communication noise, A/D quantization.
2. **Gross errors / Bad data** — Faulty meters, communication dropouts, transducer failures.
3. **Incomplete measurements** — Not every bus has full metering; some measurements are missing.
4. **Network topology errors** — Breaker/switch status errors mean the network model used is incorrect.
5. **Redundancy requirements** — Critical applications (security assessment, OPF) demand a fully consistent, fully observable network solution.

A state estimator reconciles all of this into a **single best estimate** that:
- Honors the network power flow equations ($P, Q$ balance at every bus),
- Minimizes the weighted sum of squared measurement errors,
- Detects, identifies, and rejects bad data,
- Verifies the observability of the network,
- Produces a clean, real-time database for downstream EMS functions.

## 2.2 The Measurement Model in Detail

The classical static state estimation model is:

$$z_i = h_i(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) + e_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, m$$

Where the measurements $z_i$ may be of the following types:

| Measurement Type | Symbol | Function $h_i(x)$ | Typical Location |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bus voltage magnitude | $V_i$ | $V_i$ | At any bus |
| Active power injection | $P_i$ | $V_i \sum_j V_j (G_{ij} \cos\delta_{ij} + B_{ij} \sin\delta_{ij})$ | At any bus |
| Reactive power injection | $Q_i$ | $V_i \sum_j V_j (G_{ij} \sin\delta_{ij} - B_{ij} \cos\delta_{ij})$ | At any bus |
| Active power flow | $P_{ij}$ | $V_i V_j (G_{ij}\cos\delta_{ij} + B_{ij}\sin\delta_{ij}) - G_{ij}V_i^2$ | On any branch |
| Reactive power flow | $Q_{ij}$ | $V_i V_j (G_{ij}\sin\delta_{ij} - B_{ij}\cos\delta_{ij}) + (B_{ij} - b_{ij}^{sh})V_i^2$ | On any branch |
| Current magnitude | $I_{ij}$ | Nonlinear function of $V_i, V_j, \delta_{ij}$ | On any branch |
| Voltage phase angle (PMU) | $\delta_i$ | $\delta_i$ | At any PMU bus |

The measurement Jacobian matrix $H$ is defined as:

$$H = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}, \quad H_{ij} = \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial x_j}$$

with dimensions $m \times n$.

## 2.3 The Weighted Least Squares (WLS) Formulation

The state estimation problem is formulated as a statistical optimization:

$$\min_{x} \quad J(x) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \frac{z_i - h_i(x)}{\sigma_i} \right)^2 = \frac{1}{2}[z - h(x)]^T R^{-1} [z - h(x)]$$

where $R = \text{diag}(\sigma_1^2, \sigma_2^2, \ldots, \sigma_m^2)$ is the measurement covariance matrix and $\sigma_i$ is the standard deviation of the $i$-th measurement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The First-Order Optimality Condition (Normal Equation):** Taking the gradient of $J(x)$ with respect to $x$ and setting it to zero gives the celebrated WLS normal equation:

$$G(x^k) \, \Delta x^k = H^T(x^k) R^{-1} [z - h(x^k)]$$

where $G(x^k) = H^T(x^k) R^{-1} H(x^k)$ is the **Gain Matrix** (also called the Information Matrix) and $\Delta x^k = x^{k+1} - x^k$.

This is solved iteratively using the **Gauss-Newton method**:

$$x^{k+1} = x^k + [H^T R^{-1} H]^{-1} H^T R^{-1} [z - h(x^k)]$$

Convergence is achieved when $\vert \Delta x^k \vert_\infty < \varepsilon$ (typically $10^{-4}$ p.u. or $10^{-6}$ rad).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $z = h(x) + e$ | Nonlinear measurement model | p.u. for powers, kV or p.u. for V |
| 2 | $J(x) = [z-h(x)]^T R^{-1}[z-h(x)]$ | WLS objective function | scalar (dimensionless) |
| 3 | $G = H^T R^{-1} H$ | Gain matrix (sparse, symmetric, positive definite) | varies with $x$ |
| 4 | $G(x^k) \Delta x^k = H^T R^{-1} [z - h(x^k)]$ | WLS normal equation (Gauss-Newton) | matrix equation |
| 5 | $\hat{x} = x^0 + G^{-1} H^T R^{-1} \Delta z$ | Iterative state update | p.u. and rad |
| 6 | $n = 2N_{bus} - 1$ | Number of state variables | scalar (count) |
| 7 | $\hat{\sigma}^2 = J(\hat{x}) / (m - n)$ | Normalized residual / chi-square statistic | dimensionless |
| 8 | $r_i = z_i - h_i(\hat{x})$ | Measurement residual | same as $z_i$ |
| 9 | $r_N = S \cdot r, \quad S = I - H G^{-1} H^T R^{-1}$ | Normalized residual (for bad data) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $r_i^N \vert > \tau$ (e.g., $\tau = 3.0$) | Bad data detection threshold | dimensionless |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

State estimation is a **mission-critical, real-time, 24/7 operational function** in every modern control center. Its key downstream consumers include:

1. **Security Assessment (Contingency Analysis)** — requires an accurate base-case state to evaluate $N-1$ and $N-2$ contingencies.
2. **Automatic Generation Control (AGC)** — needs Area Control Error (ACE) computed from tie-line flows and frequencies, derived from the estimated state.
3. **Economic Dispatch and Optimal Power Flow (OPF)** — uses the estimated state as the initial point for cost minimization.
4. **Real-time market operations** — Locational Marginal Prices (LMPs) are computed on the basis of the estimated network state.
5. **Visualization, alarming, and operator training simulators** — all driven by the estimator output.
6. **PMU-assisted hybrid estimation** — combines conventional SCADA with high-speed phasor data for sub-second state tracking (especially critical for oscillation monitoring and renewable integration).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Insight:** The state estimator is essentially a **statistical filter for the entire power grid** — it does not *create* new information, but it *extracts the maximum reliable information* from a redundant but noisy measurement set, while simultaneously validating the measurements and the network topology.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the WLS Normal Equation

We start from the WLS objective function:

$$J(x) = \frac{1}{2}[z - h(x)]^T R^{-1} [z - h(x)]$$

> **Step 1 — Expand the quadratic form.** 
> Since $R^{-1}$ is symmetric and positive definite, we may write $J(x)$ as a scalar:
> 
> $$J(x) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{m} R^{-1}_{ij} [z_i - h_i(x)][z_j - h_j(x)]$$

> **Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $x$.** 
> The gradient of the scalar $J(x)$ with respect to the vector $x$ is:
> 
> $$\frac{\partial J}{\partial x} = -\left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\right)^T R^{-1} [z - h(x)] = -H^T(x) R^{-1} [z - h(x)]$$

> **Step 3 — Apply the first-order optimality condition.** 
> Setting the gradient to zero at the optimal $\hat{x}$:
> 
> $$H^T(\hat{x}) R^{-1} [z - h(\hat{x})] = 0$$

> **Step 4 — Linearize via Taylor expansion about an operating point $x^k$.** 
> Expanding $h(x)$ about $x^k$ to first order:
> 
> $$h(x^{k+1}) \approx h(x^k) + H(x^k) \Delta x^k, \quad \Delta x^k = x^{k+1} - x^k$$

> **Step 5 — Substitute the linearization into the optimality condition.** 
> 
> $$H^T R^{-1} [z - h(x^k) - H \Delta x^k] = 0$$

> **Step 6 — Rearrange to isolate $\Delta x^k$.** 
> 
> $$H^T R^{-1} H \Delta x^k = H^T R^{-1} [z - h(x^k)]$$
> 
> $$\boxed{G(x^k) \Delta x^k = H^T(x^k) R^{-1} [z - h(x^k)]}$$
> 
> where we have defined the **Gain Matrix** $G(x^k) = H^T(x^k) R^{-1} H(x^k)$.

> **Step 7 — Solve iteratively.** 
> At each iteration $k$, we factorize $G$ (using sparse Cholesky or LU decomposition) and solve for $\Delta x^k$, then update:
> 
> $$x^{k+1} = x^k + \Delta x^k$$

> **Step 8 — Convergence test.** 
> Iteration stops when:
> 
> $$\max_i \vert \Delta x_i^k \vert < \varepsilon, \quad \varepsilon = 10^{-4} \text{ p.u. (for voltage) or } 10^{-6} \text{ rad (for angle)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Covariance Matrix of the Estimated State

The estimated state $\hat{x}$ is a random vector (because it depends on the noisy measurements $z$). The covariance of the estimation error is given by:

$$C_{\hat{x}} = E[(\hat{x} - x_{true})(\hat{x} - x_{true})^T] = G^{-1}(\hat{x})$$

This matrix is critical for **bad data detection**, because it tells us how much uncertainty we should expect in the estimated state, and consequently in the measurement residuals.

## 3.3 Measurement Residual Analysis (Bad Data Detection)

The measurement residual vector is:

$$r = z - h(\hat{x})$$

It can be shown that the covariance of the residual vector is:

$$C_r = R - H G^{-1} H^T = R \cdot S$$

where $S = I - H G^{-1} H^T R^{-1}$ is the **residual sensitivity matrix** (also called the hat matrix). The normalized residual is:

$$r_i^N = \frac{\vert r_i \vert}{\sqrt{\Omega_{ii}}}, \quad \Omega = R \cdot S$$

> **Bad Data Detection Rule (Chi-Square Test):** The test statistic
> 
> $$J(\hat{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{r_i^2}{\sigma_i^2}$$
> 
> follows a chi-square distribution with $(m - n)$ degrees of freedom under the hypothesis of "no bad data." If 
> 
> $$J(\hat{x}) > \chi^2_{(m-n), \alpha}$$
> 
> where $\alpha$ is the significance level (typically 0.05), the hypothesis is rejected and **bad data is suspected**.
> 
> **Largest Normalized Residual (LNR) Test:** When bad data is suspected, the measurement with the largest $\vert r_i^N \vert$ is the most likely candidate. If 
> 
> $$\max_i \vert r_i^N \vert > \tau$$
> 
> (typically $\tau = 3.0$), the corresponding measurement is identified as bad and removed; the state is then re-estimated.

## 3.4 Observability Analysis — When is the Network Solvable?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A network is observable if and only if the gain matrix $G = H^T R^{-1} H$ is nonsingular**, which requires that the Jacobian $H$ has full column rank ($n$). In practical terms, this means that the available measurements must be **topographically sufficient** to compute the state of every bus.

Two classical tests:
- **Numerical test:** Attempt to triangularize $G$; if the process completes without a zero pivot, the network is observable.
- **Topological test:** Construct a directed spanning forest of measurement-to-bus assignments; if every bus is reached, the network is observable.

Unobservable islands require **pseudo-measurements** (load forecasts, zero-injection constraints) to be augmented.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: State Estimator in EMS

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the position and role of the state estimator within a modern Energy Management System (EMS), and the data flow from the field (measurements) to downstream applications.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FIELD["Field Layer - Physical Power System"]
        B1["Bus 1 - VT and CT"]
        B2["Bus 2 - VT and CT"]
        B3["Bus 3 - PMU"]
        BN["Bus N - VT and CT"]
        BRTUs["Remote Terminal Units RTUs"]
        BPMUs["Phasor Measurement Units PMUs"]
    end

    subgraph TELECOM["Telecom Layer"]
        SCADA["SCADA Front End"]
        PDC["Phasor Data Concentrator PDC"]
    end

    subgraph EMS["Energy Management System EMS - Control Center"]
        NTP["Network Topology Processor"]
        SE_CORE["State Estimator Core - WLS Solver"]
        BD["Bad Data Detection Module"]
        OBC["Observability Checker"]
        RTDB["Real-Time Network Database RTDB"]
    end

    subgraph DOWNSTREAM["Downstream EMS Applications"]
        SA["Security Assessment"]
        CA["Contingency Analysis N-1"]
        AGC["Automatic Generation Control"]
        OPF["Optimal Power Flow"]
        LMP["Locational Marginal Price LMP"]
        OTS["Operator Training Simulator"]
    end

    B1 --> BRTUs
    B2 --> BRTUs
    BN --> BRTUs
    B3 --> BPMUs
    BRTUs --> SCADA
    BPMUs --> PDC
    SCADA --> NTP
    PDC --> NTP
    NTP --> SE_CORE
    SE_CORE --> OBC
    OBC --> SE_CORE
    SE_CORE --> BD
    BD --> SE_CORE
    SE_CORE --> RTDB
    RTDB --> SA
    RTDB --> CA
    RTDB --> AGC
    RTDB --> OPF
    RTDB --> LMP
    RTDB --> OTS
```

## 4.2 Iterative Computational Flow of the WLS State Estimator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START - Receive Measurement Vector z and Network Model Ybus"]
    B["Initialize State x^0 - Flat Start: V=1.0 p.u., delta=0"]
    C{"k greater than k_max?"}
    D["Compute Jacobian H = dh/dx at x^k"]
    E["Compute Gain Matrix G = H^T R^-1 H"]
    F["Compute RHS Vector b = H^T R^-1 times z minus h of x^k"]
    G["Solve Linear System G times delta_x = b - Sparse Cholesky"]
    H["Update State: x to the power of k+1 = x^k + delta_x^k"]
    I{"max |delta_x| less than epsilon?"}
    J["Compute Residuals r = z minus h of estimated x"]
    K["Bad Data Detection: Chi-Square and LNR Tests"]
    L["Publish Estimated State to RTDB"]
    M["STOP - Wait for next scan cycle 1 to 5 sec"]
    N["Report Non-Convergence - Alarm Operator"]

    A --> B
    B --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I -- "No" --> C
    C -- "No" --> D
    I -- "Yes" --> J
    J --> K
    K --> L
    L --> M
    C -- "Yes" --> N
```

## 4.3 Data Flow Architecture: From Measurements to State Estimate

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph M1["Raw Measurement Sources"]
        M1A["Voltage Magnitude V_i - analog"]
        M1B["Power Flow P_ij, Q_ij - analog"]
        M1C["Power Injection P_i, Q_i - analog"]
        M1D["PMU Phasors - digital, GPS time-tagged"]
        M1E["Breaker Status S_ij - digital"]
    end

    subgraph M2["Preprocessing"]
        M2A["Analog to Digital Conversion"]
        M2B["Error Checking and Range Validation"]
        M2C["Topology Processing - Network Model"]
    end

    subgraph M3["State Estimation Core"]
        M3A["Form Measurement Vector z"]
        M3B["Form Jacobian H"]
        M3C["Solve G times delta_x = H^T R^-1 times residual"]
        M3D["Bad Data Detection and Identification"]
    end

    subgraph M4["Output - Real Time Database"]
        M4A["Estimated Voltage Magnitudes"]
        M4B["Estimated Voltage Angles"]
        M4C["Estimated Power Flows"]
        M4D["Estimated Losses"]
    end

    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> M4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Note:** The above block diagrams emphasize the **information flow architecture** (which is what the examiner expects) rather than physical circuit-level drawings. State estimation is fundamentally a **software and data-processing function** that sits at the boundary between raw field data and operational decision-making in the control center.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model)]**

> **Q1.** Define *Power System State Estimation*. List any four types of measurements commonly used as inputs to the state estimator.

**Model Answer:**

Power System State Estimation is a statistical data-processing algorithm that processes a redundant set of real-time, noisy, telemetered measurements (along with the network model) to compute the **best estimate of the current operating state** of the power system, typically defined as the complex voltages at all buses ($V_i \angle \delta_i$).

**Four types of measurements:**
1. Bus voltage magnitude ($V_i$)
2. Active and reactive power injection at buses ($P_i$, $Q_i$)
3. Active and reactive power flow on transmission lines ($P_{ij}$, $Q_{ij}$)
4. Voltage phase angle from PMUs ($\delta_i$) — *or* current magnitude measurements on branches.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model)]**

> **Q2.** State the Weighted Least Squares (WLS) formulation of the state estimation problem and write the resulting normal equation. What is the role of the measurement covariance matrix $R$?

**Model Answer:**

The WLS estimator minimizes the weighted sum of squared residuals:

$$J(x) = [z - h(x)]^T R^{-1} [z - h(x)]$$

The normal (Gauss-Newton) equation obtained by setting $\partial J / \partial x = 0$ is:

$$G(x^k) \Delta x^k = H^T(x^k) R^{-1} [z - h(x^k)], \quad G = H^T R^{-1} H$$

**Role of $R$:** The covariance matrix $R = \text{diag}(\sigma_1^2, \ldots, \sigma_m^2)$ assigns weights to the measurements: **more accurate measurements (smaller $\sigma_i$) get larger weights**, and noisier measurements (larger $\sigma_i$) get smaller weights, ensuring that the final estimate leans most heavily on the most reliable data.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model) — Choice A]**

> **Q. (A) (a)** Explain the mathematical formulation of the static Weighted Least Squares (WLS) state estimation problem, clearly defining the measurement model, the objective function, and the normal equation. Discuss the convergence properties of the iterative solution. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q. (A) (b)** With reference to bad data detection in state estimation, explain the chi-square ($\chi^2$) detection test and the Largest Normalized Residual (LNR) identification test. Why is the LNR test considered the more robust method? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q. (A) (a)

**1. Measurement Model:**

$$z = h(x) + e, \quad z \in \mathbb{R}^m, \; x \in \mathbb{R}^n, \; m > n$$

- $z$ — measurement vector (e.g., $V_i$, $P_{ij}$, $Q_i$, etc.)
- $x$ — state vector (bus voltage magnitudes and angles, $n = 2N-1$)
- $h(x)$ — nonlinear measurement function derived from the power flow equations
- $e$ — measurement error, modeled as zero-mean Gaussian, $\text{Cov}(e) = R$

**2. Objective Function (WLS):**

$$J(x) = \frac{1}{2} [z - h(x)]^T R^{-1} [z - h(x)] = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{[z_i - h_i(x)]^2}{\sigma_i^2}$$

**3. Normal Equation (Gauss-Newton):**

$$G(x^k) \Delta x^k = H^T(x^k) R^{-1} [z - h(x^k)]$$

with $G(x^k) = H^T(x^k) R^{-1} H(x^k)$ and $H = \partial h / \partial x$ evaluated at $x^k$.

**4. Iterative Procedure:**

$$x^{k+1} = x^k + [H^T R^{-1} H]^{-1} H^T R^{-1} [z - h(x^k)]$$

**5. Convergence Properties:**

- The algorithm converges **quadratically** near the optimum (Gauss-Newton is a Newton-like method applied to the least-squares objective).
- Convergence typically requires **3 to 5 iterations** from a flat start ($V=1.0$ p.u., $\delta=0$) for well-conditioned networks.
- The algorithm is **guaranteed to converge to a local minimum** of $J(x)$ provided $G$ remains positive definite at every step (i.e., the network remains observable).
- The iteration count grows with network size and measurement noise but is bounded by the sparsity structure of $G$, which is exploited via **sparse matrix techniques** (e.g., Cholesky factorization with minimum degree ordering).
- **Divergence** is rare in practice and is usually associated with network topology errors, gross bad data, or loss of observability during the iteration.

#### Model Solution to Q. (A) (b)

**1. Chi-Square ($\chi^2$) Detection Test:**

The WLS objective value at the optimum, $J(\hat{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} r_i^2 / \sigma_i^2$, follows a $\chi^2$ distribution with $\nu = (m - n)$ degrees of freedom under the null hypothesis $H_0$ that there are **no bad data and no topology errors**.

> **Decision rule:** Reject $H_0$ (i.e., bad data is suspected) if
> 
> $$J(\hat{x}) > \chi^2_{\nu, \alpha}$$
> 
> where $\alpha$ is the chosen significance level (typically 0.01 or 0.05).

**2. Largest Normalized Residual (LNR) Identification Test:**

The normalized residual is:

$$r_i^N = \frac{\vert r_i \vert}{\sqrt{\Omega_{ii}}}, \quad \Omega = R \cdot S = R(I - H G^{-1} H^T R^{-1})$$

The covariance matrix of the residual vector is $\text{Cov}(r) = \Omega$.

> **Decision rule:** Identify the measurement with the maximum $\vert r_i^N \vert$. If 
> 
> $$\max_i \vert r_i^N \vert > \tau$$
> 
> (with $\tau$ typically 3.0 for a $3\sigma$ threshold), that measurement is flagged as **bad data** and removed from the measurement set. The state is then re-estimated.

**3. Why LNR is more robust than $\chi^2$ alone:**

- The $\chi^2$ test **only detects** the *presence* of bad data — it does not identify *which* measurement is bad.
- The LNR test **both detects and identifies** the bad measurement, making it the operational choice.
- The LNR test is **robust to multiple interacting bad data** because after each bad measurement is removed and the state re-estimated, the LNR test is re-applied iteratively.
- The $\chi^2$ test is sensitive to the choice of significance level and to the **masking effect** (where one bad data point can mask another), whereas the LNR test is more localized and less prone to masking when used in the iterative remove-and-re-estimate loop.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model) — Choice B]**

> **Q. (B) (a)** Discuss the role of state estimation in a modern Energy Management System (EMS). Explain with a block diagram how the state estimator interfaces with SCADA, the network topology processor, and downstream applications such as contingency analysis and economic dispatch. **(7 Marks)**
>
